topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Binding Energy - Fission Process - Radioactivity - Alpha, Beta and Gamma Rays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Binding Energy (BE)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
Binding Energy is defined as the *minimum amount of energy required to completely separate the nucleons (protons and neutrons) of a stable nucleus into individual, free, non-interacting particles*. Equivalently, it is the energy released when individual nucleons combine to form that nucleus. It is a direct measure of **nuclear stability**.

$$BE = \big[\,Z \cdot m_p + N \cdot m_n - m_{nucleus}\,\big] \cdot c^2$$

Where:
- $Z$ = Atomic number (number of protons)
- $N$ = Neutron number ($A - Z$)
- $m_p$ = Mass of a proton ≈ **1.007825 u**
- $m_n$ = Mass of a neutron ≈ **1.008665 u**
- $m_{nucleus}$ = Mass of the actual atomic nucleus
- $c^2$ = Squared speed of light (931.494 MeV/u)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** Binding Energy is always a **POSITIVE quantity** because the mass of a stable nucleus is *always less* than the sum of masses of its constituent nucleons. This missing mass is called the **mass defect** ($\Delta m$), and by Einstein's mass-energy equivalence ($E = \Delta m c^2$), it is converted into the binding energy.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a deep well.** Imagine you drop marbles (nucleons) one by one into a deep well, and they stick together at the bottom. The deeper the well, the more energy you'd need to climb out and pull each marble back up to the surface. That "depth of the well" is the **Binding Energy**. A deeper well = a more stable nucleus. The deepest part of this "well" sits around **Iron-56 (⁵⁶Fe)**, with a binding energy per nucleon of approximately **8.8 MeV**.

---

## 1.2 Fission Process

**Formal Definition:**
Nuclear fission is the process in which a *heavy, unstable nucleus splits into two (or rarely three) lighter nuclei of comparable mass*, accompanied by the release of **2–3 free neutrons**, a large amount of energy (typically **~200 MeV per fission**), and gamma radiation.

The general reaction is:

$$^{A}_{Z}X + n \rightarrow \,^{A_1}_{Z_1}F_1 + \,^{A_2}_{Z_2}F_2 + \nu \cdot n + Q$$

Where $\nu$ is the neutron yield (≈ 2.43 for U-235) and $Q$ is the energy released.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Fission is *not* a "spontaneous splitting." It is **induced** by the absorption of a slow (thermal) neutron in fissile materials such as **U-235, U-233, or Pu-239**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Wobbling Water Droplet Analogy:** Picture a large, water-filled balloon held together by surface tension (the *strong nuclear force*). If you poke it gently with a needle (a *neutron*), the droplet stretches into a dumbbell shape, then snaps violently into two smaller droplets. The *pinch* in the middle is where the **Coulomb repulsion** (positive charges pushing apart) finally overcomes the nuclear attraction. Energy is released because the *two smaller droplets sit at a deeper energy level* (higher BE per nucleon) than the original big droplet.

---

## 1.3 Radioactivity

**Formal Definition:**
Radioactivity is the *spontaneous, stochastic (random) emission of particles or electromagnetic radiation from an unstable atomic nucleus* as it transitions to a more stable lower-energy state. This property is exhibited naturally by isotopes of elements with atomic numbers **Z > 82** (Lead), and artificially by neutron-rich lighter isotopes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in **1896** while studying uranium salts. The Curies later coined the term. Radioactivity is *not* affected by temperature, pressure, or chemical state — it is a purely **nuclear** phenomenon.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The Leaky Faucet Analogy:** Imagine a tank filled with unstable water molecules sitting on a high shelf (high-energy state). They randomly "drip" down to a lower shelf (stable daughter nucleus) at unpredictable times. You cannot predict *which drop* will fall next, but statistically, you can predict *how many* drops fall per second (the **Activity**). The half-life is the time for *half* the drops in the tank to have fallen.

---

## 1.4 Alpha, Beta, and Gamma Rays

### Alpha (α) Particles
- **Composition:** Helium-4 nucleus ($^{4}_{2}\text{He}^{2+}$) — 2 protons + 2 neutrons
- **Charge:** +2e
- **Mass:** ~4 u
- **Penetration:** Very low (stopped by paper or a few cm of air)
- **Ionization:** Very high
- **Speed:** ~5–7% of the speed of light
- **Example Parent:** $^{238}_{92}\text{U} \rightarrow \,^{234}_{90}\text{Th} + \,^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A heavy, slow, charged bowling ball — it bulldozes through atoms causing massive damage but cannot go far.

### Beta (β) Particles
- **β⁻ (Negatron):** High-energy electron ($e^-$) emitted when a neutron converts to a proton: $n \rightarrow p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$
- **β⁺ (Positron):** Positively charged electron ($e^+$) emitted when a proton converts to a neutron: $p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e$
- **Charge:** −1e (β⁻) or +1e (β⁺)
- **Mass:** ~1/1836 u (negligible)
- **Penetration:** Moderate (stopped by a few mm of aluminium)
- **Speed:** Up to ~90% of the speed of light

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A light, fast, charged dart — travels far, moderate damage, and shows a continuous energy spectrum.

### Gamma (γ) Rays
- **Composition:** High-energy electromagnetic photons (no mass, no charge)
- **Charge:** 0
- **Mass:** 0
- **Penetration:** Very high (requires thick lead or concrete shielding)
- **Ionization:** Very low (indirect)
- **Speed:** Speed of light ($c$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A pure, massless, chargeless beam of light — like a powerful X-ray laser. It does not ionize directly but damages biological tissue via secondary electrons (Compton/photoelectric effect).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Mnemonic — "Alpha is Heavy, Beta is Speedy, Gamma is Ghostly":**
> - **Alpha** = Heavy (mass 4u), Strong ionizer, Weak penetrator
> - **Beta** = Light (electron mass), Medium penetrator
> - **Gamma** = No mass, Weak ionizer, Strong penetrator
> - Deflection in magnetic field: **α** deflects one way, **β** deflects the other way, **γ** goes straight.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Binding Energy — Step-by-Step Logic

The binding energy of a nucleus is the cornerstone concept for understanding *why* fission and fusion release energy.

1. **Start with isolated nucleons:** Take $Z$ protons and $N$ neutrons, each at rest, far apart. The total rest-mass energy is $E_i = [Z m_p + N m_n]c^2$.
2. **Bring them together to form the nucleus:** The strong nuclear force binds them. The resulting nucleus has rest-mass energy $E_f = m_{nucleus} \cdot c^2$.
3. **Compare:** $E_i > E_f$ because some mass has been "lost" (mass defect, $\Delta m$).
4. **The difference is the Binding Energy:** $BE = \Delta m \cdot c^2 = [Z m_p + N m_n - m_{nucleus}]c^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the curve highest at Fe-56?** Because iron sits at the **peak of the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve**. Nuclei lighter than iron can **FUSE** to climb up the curve (releasing energy). Nuclei heavier than iron can **FISSION** to climb up the curve (also releasing energy). Both processes move *toward* the peak.

## 2.2 Fission Process — Step-by-Step Logic

1. A **thermal neutron** ($n$, ~0.025 eV) strikes a fissile nucleus (e.g., U-235).
2. The neutron is absorbed, forming a **compound nucleus** in an excited state: $^{236}\text{U}^*$.
3. The compound nucleus oscillates and deforms (the **Bohr-Wheeler model**).
4. If the **neutron-induced energy (B.E. gain + neutron KE)** exceeds the **critical fission barrier energy** (≈ 5–6 MeV for U-235), the nucleus splits.
5. Two **fission fragments** (e.g., Ba-141 and Kr-92) are produced, along with **2–3 prompt neutrons** and **γ-rays**.
6. The fission fragments are neutron-rich and undergo **β⁻ decay chains** to reach stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU Insight:** The ~200 MeV energy is distributed as:
> - Kinetic Energy of fragments: **~165 MeV** (largest share)
> - Kinetic Energy of neutrons: **~5 MeV**
> - Prompt γ-rays: **~5 MeV**
> - β & γ from decay of fragments: **~15 MeV**
> - Neutrinos (unrecoverable): **~10 MeV**

## 2.3 Radioactivity — Step-by-Step Logic

1. The parent nucleus $X$ has $N$ atoms at time $t$.
2. The **decay constant $\lambda$** represents the probability per unit time that *any single atom* will decay. Units: **s⁻¹**.
3. The rate of decay (Activity) is $A = \lambda N$.
4. Solving the differential equation: $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
5. The **half-life** $T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$.
6. The **mean life** $\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars in tables use `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Description | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Mass Defect | $\Delta m = Z m_p + N m_n - m_{nuc}$ | Difference between sum of free nucleons and nucleus mass | atomic mass unit (u) |
| 2 | Binding Energy (Total) | $BE = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ | Energy equivalent of mass defect | MeV |
| 3 | Binding Energy per Nucleon | $BE/A = BE / A$ | Average energy per nucleon; measure of stability | MeV/nucleon |
| 4 | Mass-Energy Conversion | $1 \text{ u} = 931.494 \text{ MeV}/c^2$ | Standard conversion factor for nuclear calcs | MeV |
| 5 | Q-Value (Reaction Energy) | $Q = (\sum m_{reactants} - \sum m_{products})c^2$ | Net energy released in a reaction; $Q>0$ exothermic | MeV |
| 6 | Fission Energy | $Q \approx 200 \text{ MeV per fission}$ | Typical for U-235 | MeV |
| 7 | Fission Neutron Yield | $\nu \approx 2.43$ (U-235 thermal) | Avg. neutrons released per fission | dimensionless |
| 8 | Radioactive Decay Law | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Number of remaining parent atoms | atoms |
| 9 | Activity | $A = \lambda N$ | Decays per second | Becquerel (Bq) or Curie (Ci) |
| 10 | Half-Life | $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 \,/\, \lambda \approx 0.693/\lambda$ | Time for half the nuclei to decay | s, min, h, y |
| 11 | Mean Life | $\tau = 1/\lambda$ | Average lifetime of a radioactive atom | s |
| 12 | Activity vs. Time | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Decay of activity over time | Bq |
| 13 | Curie Definition | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Activity of 1 g of Ra-226 | Bq |
| 14 | Range of α in air | $R_\alpha \approx 0.318 \cdot E^{3/2}$ (Geiger's law) | Empirical range of alpha particles in air | cm (E in MeV) |
| 15 | Alpha Charge & Mass | $q_\alpha = +2e$, $m_\alpha = 4 \text{ u}$ | Helium-4 nucleus properties | e, u |
| 16 | Beta Decay (β⁻) | $n \rightarrow p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e$ | Neutron to proton conversion | — |
| 17 | Beta Decay (β⁺) | $p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e$ | Proton to neutron conversion | — |
| 18 | Gamma Decay | $X^* \rightarrow X + \gamma$ | Excited nucleus de-excites | — |

### Real-World Engineering Applications
- **Binding Energy Curve** → Used by nuclear engineers to select fuels (U-235, Pu-239) for reactors, and to evaluate fusion fuels (D, T) for future reactors.
- **Fission Q-value** → Determines the **power density** of a reactor core (e.g., a 1 GW reactor consumes ~3 kg of U-235 per day).
- **Decay Law & Half-life** → Critical for **nuclear waste management** (e.g., spent fuel from a reactor remains hazardous for **~10,000 years** due to Pu-239's 24,100-year half-life).
- **Alpha/Beta/Gamma properties** → Determines **shielding design**: paper for α, plastic/aluminium for β, thick lead/concrete for γ.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Binding Energy per Nucleon Curve Origin

**Step 1 — Start with the mass defect for a nucleus of mass number A:**

$$\Delta m = Z \cdot m_p + N \cdot m_n - m_{nuc}$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass defect to total binding energy using Einstein's equation:**

$$BE = \Delta m \cdot c^2 = \big[\,Z m_p + N m_n - m_{nuc}\,\big] c^2$$

**Step 3 — Express in energy units of MeV (1 u = 931.494 MeV/c²):**

$$BE(\text{MeV}) = \big[\,Z m_p + N m_n - m_{nuc}\,\big] \times 931.494$$

**Step 4 — Divide by mass number A to get BE per nucleon:**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{\big[\,Z m_p + N m_n - m_{nuc}\,\big] c^2}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
For very light nuclei (A < 20), $BE/A$ rises steeply (peaks at ~8.8 MeV for Fe-56), then gradually decreases for heavy nuclei (drops to ~7.5 MeV for U-235). This is *the* reason both fusion (light→medium) and fission (heavy→medium) release energy.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Q-Value of a Fission Reaction

Consider the generic fission of $^A_ZX$ by a neutron:

$$^{A}_{Z}X + n \;\longrightarrow\; ^{A_1}_{Z_1}F_1 + \,^{A_2}_{Z_2}F_2 + \nu n + Q$$

**Step 1 — Define the Q-value (energy released):**

$$Q = \big[\,m_X + m_n - m_{F_1} - m_{F_2} - \nu m_n\,\big] c^2$$

**Step 2 — Equivalently, in terms of binding energies (BE):**
Total BE of products is higher than BE of reactants because products are more stable.

$$Q = \big[\,BE_{F_1} + BE_{F_2} + \nu \cdot BE_{neutron} - BE_{X}\,\big]$$

(The neutron's BE in the parent is just its KE, negligible in comparison.)

**Step 3 — Numerical example for U-235:**

$$^{235}_{92}\text{U} + n \;\longrightarrow\; ^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} + ^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} + 3n + Q$$

Using atomic masses (in u):
- $m(^{235}\text{U}) = 235.043930$
- $m(n) = 1.008665$
- $m(^{141}\text{Ba}) = 140.914411$
- $m(^{92}\text{Kr}) = 91.926156$
- $m(n) = 1.008665$ (×3)

**Step 4 — Compute the total mass of reactants:**

$$m_{react} = 235.043930 + 1.008665 = 236.052595 \text{ u}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total mass of products:**

$$m_{prod} = 140.914411 + 91.926156 + 3(1.008665) = 235.866562 \text{ u}$$

**Step 6 — Compute mass defect:**

$$\Delta m = 236.052595 - 235.866562 = 0.186033 \text{ u}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to energy:**

$$Q = 0.186033 \text{ u} \times 931.494 \text{ MeV/u} \approx 173.3 \text{ MeV}$$

(Actual ~200 MeV is achieved because the average binding energy values used here are approximate; the principle is what matters.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Decay Law (Radioactivity)

**Step 1 — Experimental observation (Rutherford & Soddy):** The rate of decay is proportional to the number of *undecayed* atoms $N$ present at time $t$.

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

The negative sign indicates that $N$ *decreases* with time.

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$\ln\big[\,N(t)\,\big] - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{\,N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the half-life relation:** By definition, when $t = T_{1/2}$, we have $N(t) = N_0/2$:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln(2) = \lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Derive the activity equation:** Since $A = \lambda N$:

$$A(t) = \lambda N(t) = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Alpha, Beta, Gamma — Direct Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam tool. Memorize the 5 bolded properties for each ray.**

| Property | Alpha (α) | Beta (β) | Gamma (γ) |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Identity** | Helium-4 nucleus | High-speed electron (β⁻) or positron (β⁺) | High-energy photon |
| **Symbol** | $^{4}_{2}\text{He}^{2+}$ | $e^-$ or $e^+$ | $\gamma$ (or $^0_0\gamma$) |
| **Charge** | **+2e** | **−1e** or **+1e** | **0** |
| **Rest Mass** | **4 u** (≈ 4 × 1.66×10⁻²⁷ kg) | **~0.000549 u** (≈ 1/1836 u) | **0** |
| **Speed** | ~5–7% of $c$ | Up to ~90% of $c$ | Exactly $c$ |
| **Energy Spectrum** | Discrete (mono-energetic) | **Continuous** (0 to $E_{max}$) | Discrete (line spectrum) |
| **Penetrating Power** | Very low (paper, skin) | Moderate (few mm Al) | Very high (cm of Pb, m of concrete) |
| **Ionizing Power** | **Very high** | Moderate | Very low |
| **Deflection in E-field** | Toward negative plate | Toward positive plate (β⁻) | No deflection |
| **Deflection in B-field** | Large circle, one direction | Large circle, opposite direction | No deflection |
| **Biological Hazard** | Internal hazard (inhalation) | Skin & internal hazard | External whole-body hazard |
| **Typical Shielding** | Paper, skin | Plastic, aluminium | Lead, concrete, water |
| **Emission Process** | α decay of heavy nucleus | Weak interaction (n↔p) | Nuclear de-excitation |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binding Energy per Nucleon Curve — Conceptual Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram presents the binding energy per nucleon curve as a functional block, mapping the physics regions and the corresponding nuclear processes.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Light Nuclei A less than 20"] --> B["Low BE per nucleon less than 8 MeV"]
    C["Iron 56 Peak"] --> D["Maximum BE per nucleon 8.8 MeV"]
    B --> E["FUSION releases energy climbing toward peak"]
    D --> F["Most stable nucleus in universe"]
    G["Heavy Nuclei A greater than 235"] --> H["Lower BE per nucleon about 7.5 MeV"]
    H --> I["FISSION releases energy climbing toward peak"]
    E --> C
    I --> C
    F --> J["Cannot release energy by fusion or fission"]
```

## 4.2 Fission Process — Functional Flow Block Diagram

This Mermaid diagram walks through the chain of physical events from neutron capture to energy release.

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["Step 1: Thermal neutron n approaches U 235 nucleus"] --> step2["Step 2: Neutron absorbed compound nucleus U 236 star forms"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Nucleus oscillates and deforms dumbbell shape"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Coulomb repulsion overcomes strong force at scission point"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Nucleus splits into two fission fragments Ba 141 and Kr 92"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: 2 to 3 prompt neutrons released"]
    step5 --> step7["Step 7: Prompt gamma rays released"]
    step6 --> step8["Step 8: Fragments undergo beta decay chains to stable isotopes"]
    step5 --> step9["Step 9: Total Q value about 200 MeV released"]
    step6 --> step10["Step 10: Neutrons induce further fissions CHAIN REACTION"]
```

## 4.3 Radioactive Decay Chain Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1["Parent Nucleus X unstable"] --> p2["Decays via alpha or beta or gamma emission"]
    p2 --> p3["Daughter Nucleus Y may also be unstable"]
    p3 --> p4["Decays further until stable isotope Z reached"]
    p4 --> p5["Stable end point no further decay"]
    p2 -.-> p6["Rate given by A equals lambda N"]
    p2 -.-> p7["Half life T half equals 0.693 over lambda"]
```

## 4.4 Alpha, Beta, Gamma — Comparison Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    rad["Radioactive Emissions"]
    rad --> a1["Alpha ray Helium 4 nucleus"]
    rad --> b1["Beta ray High energy electron or positron"]
    rad --> c1["Gamma ray High energy photon"]

    a1 --> a2["Charge plus 2e"]
    a1 --> a3["Mass 4 u"]
    a1 --> a4["Stopped by paper"]
    a1 --> a5["Very high ionization"]

    b1 --> b2["Charge minus 1e or plus 1e"]
    b1 --> b3["Mass negligible 1 over 1836 u"]
    b1 --> b4["Stopped by few mm aluminium"]
    b1 --> b5["Moderate ionization"]

    c1 --> c2["Charge zero"]
    c1 --> c3["Mass zero"]
    c1 --> c4["Stopped by lead or concrete"]
    c1 --> c5["Very low ionization high penetration"]
```

## 4.5 Mass Defect & Binding Energy Computation — Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1["Input Nucleus X with Z protons and N neutrons"] --> s2["Calculate total free nucleon mass Z mp plus N mn"]
    s2 --> s3["Subtract actual nucleus mass m nucleus"]
    s3 --> s4["Mass defect delta m"]
    s4 --> s5["Multiply by 931.494 MeV per u"]
    s5 --> s6["Binding Energy BE in MeV"]
    s6 --> s7["Divide by mass number A"]
    s7 --> s8["Binding Energy per nucleon BE over A"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part A questions are short-answer, requiring crisp, definition-style answers. Each is to be answered in approximately **60–90 words** in the exam script.

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define binding energy per nucleon. What is its significance in nuclear reactions?**

**Model Answer:**
Binding energy per nucleon is the **average energy required to remove a single nucleon** from a nucleus, calculated as $BE/A$. It is a direct measure of **nuclear stability** — the higher the $BE/A$, the more stable the nucleus. The plot of $BE/A$ versus mass number $A$ peaks at **⁵⁶Fe (≈ 8.8 MeV)**. This curve's shape explains why both **fusion** (light nuclei combining) and **fission** (heavy nuclei splitting) release energy, as both processes move nuclei *toward* the iron peak. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between alpha, beta, and gamma radiation with respect to charge, mass, and penetrating power.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Alpha (α) | Beta (β) | Gamma (γ) |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| Charge | +2e | ±1e | 0 |
| Mass | 4 u | 1/1836 u | 0 |
| Penetrating Power | Low (stopped by paper) | Moderate (few mm Al) | High (cm of Pb required) |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions are long-answer, usually divided into sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. The student is expected to attempt **ONE of two choices** (either Choice A or Choice B). Each sub-part requires a full derivation or detailed analysis.

---

### CHOICE A (Question A — 14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Set B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define binding energy. Derive an expression for the binding energy of a nucleus. The mass of $^{16}_{8}\text{O}$ nucleus is **15.994915 u**. Calculate its binding energy per nucleon. Given: $m_p = 1.007825$ u, $m_n = 1.008665$ u, $1 \text{ u} = 931.494 \text{ MeV}/c^2$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
Binding energy is the energy equivalent of the **mass defect**, i.e., the energy released when individual nucleons combine to form a nucleus (or equivalently, the energy required to disintegrate the nucleus into its constituent nucleons).

**Step 2 — Derivation:**
For a nucleus $\,^{A}_{Z}X$ with $Z$ protons and $N = (A - Z)$ neutrons:

$$BE = \big[\,Z \cdot m_p + N \cdot m_n - m_{nuc}\,\big] \cdot c^2$$

**Step 3 — Identify parameters for $^{16}_{8}\text{O}$:**
- $Z = 8$ (oxygen has 8 protons)
- $N = A - Z = 16 - 8 = 8$ neutrons
- $m_{nuc} = 15.994915$ u

**Step 4 — Calculate total free nucleon mass:**

$$Z \cdot m_p + N \cdot m_n = 8(1.007825) + 8(1.008665)$$

$$= 8.062600 + 8.069320 = 16.131920 \text{ u}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate mass defect:**

$$\Delta m = 16.131920 - 15.994915 = 0.137005 \text{ u}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate total binding energy:**

$$BE = 0.137005 \text{ u} \times 931.494 \text{ MeV/u} = 127.62 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate binding energy per nucleon:**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{127.62 \text{ MeV}}{16} \approx 7.98 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

**Conclusion:** The BE/A for $^{16}\text{O}$ is approximately **7.98 MeV**, which is close to the curve's value (~8.0 MeV) and indicates that $^{16}\text{O}$ is a relatively stable, tightly bound nucleus. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** State the radioactive decay law. Derive the relationship between half-life and decay constant. A radioactive sample has a half-life of **30 years**. Calculate the time required for its activity to reduce to **1/8th** of its initial value.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Decay Law:**
The rate of decay of radioactive atoms at any instant is directly proportional to the number of atoms present at that instant:

$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (s⁻¹). On integration, $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 2 — Derive Half-Life Relation:**
At $t = T_{1/2}$, we have $N = N_0/2$:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\ln(2) = \lambda T_{1/2} \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\,T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the given problem:**
Given: $T_{1/2} = 30$ years, and we want $A(t) = A_0/8$.

**Step 4 — Use the activity decay formula:**

$$\frac{A(t)}{A_0} = \frac{1}{8} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5 — Take natural log:**

$$\ln(1/8) = -\lambda t \;\Rightarrow\; -\ln(8) = -\lambda t \;\Rightarrow\; \ln(8) = \lambda t$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of $T_{1/2}$:**

$$t = \frac{\ln(8)}{\lambda} = \frac{\ln(2^3)}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \ln 2}{\lambda} = 3 \cdot \frac{0.693}{\lambda} = 3 \cdot T_{1/2}$$

**Step 7 — Compute numerically:**

$$t = 3 \times 30 \text{ years} = \boxed{\,90 \text{ years}\,}$$

**Note:** The "1/8 = (1/2)³" shortcut tells us the activity drops to 1/8th in **exactly 3 half-lives**. **(7 marks)**

---

### CHOICE B (Question B — 14 Marks — Internal Alternative)

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper, Set A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the binding energy per nucleon curve. Discuss the significance of the peak at mass number 56. Explain why energy is released in both fission and fusion.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Description of the Curve:**
The binding energy per nucleon ($BE/A$) versus mass number $A$ is plotted. For very light nuclei (e.g., $^{2}\text{H}$, $^{4}\text{He}$), $BE/A$ is low (~1–7 MeV). It rises steeply, reaching a **maximum of ~8.8 MeV at $^{56}\text{Fe}$**, then **gradually decreases** to ~7.5 MeV for $^{235}\text{U}$.

**Step 2 — Sketch (textual representation):**

```
   BE/A (MeV)
    9 |                  . . . . . .
    8 |            . .                 . .
    7 |       .                             . .
    6 |   .                                     . .
    5 |.                                            . .
      +-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|-----> A
          20  40  60  80 100 120 160 200 240
                    ↑ Peak at A=56 (Fe-56)
```

**Step 3 — Significance of the Peak at A = 56:**
Iron-56 is the **most stable nucleus in the universe** because it has the highest binding energy per nucleon. No further nuclear rearrangement (fusion or fission) can extract energy from it. Heavier stars, after exhausting hydrogen, ultimately fuse elements *up to* iron, but cannot go further via fusion.

**Step 4 — Why Fusion Releases Energy:**
Light nuclei (left of the peak, e.g., $^{2}\text{H}$ and $^{3}\text{H}$) have low $BE/A$. When they fuse to form a heavier nucleus (e.g., $^{4}\text{He}$), the resulting nucleus has a *higher* $BE/A$. The increase in binding energy is **released as kinetic energy** of the products.

$$\,^{2}_{1}\text{H} + \,^{3}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow \,^{4}_{2}\text{He} + n + 17.6 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 5 — Why Fission Releases Energy:**
Heavy nuclei (right of the peak, e.g., $^{235}\text{U}$, $BE/A \approx 7.5$ MeV) split into medium-mass fragments (e.g., Ba, Kr) which have *higher* $BE/A$ (~8.5 MeV). The increase in binding energy is released as the **Q-value (~200 MeV per fission)**.

**Conclusion:** Both fusion (light→medium) and fission (heavy→medium) cause nuclei to migrate *up the curve toward the iron peak*, releasing the binding energy difference. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is radioactivity? State the laws of radioactive disintegration. The half-life of $^{226}\text{Ra}$ is **1600 years**. How long will it take for **75%** of a given sample to decay?

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Radioactivity:**
Radioactivity is the **spontaneous, random emission of particles (α, β) or electromagnetic radiation (γ) from unstable atomic nuclei** as they transition to a more stable configuration. It is a purely nuclear process unaffected by external physical/chemical conditions.

**Step 2 — Laws of Radioactive Disintegration:**

1. **Law of Decay Rate:** $dN/dt = -\lambda N$ (rate proportional to the number of undecayed atoms).
2. **Law of Exponential Decay:** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
3. **Soddy's Group Displacement Law:** In α decay, the daughter nucleus has $Z-2$ and $A-4$. In β⁻ decay, $Z$ increases by 1, $A$ unchanged.
4. **Law of Successive Disintegration:** In a decay chain, the activity of the parent-daughter system follows Bateman equations.

**Step 3 — Identify the problem:**
- $T_{1/2} = 1600$ years
- 75% has decayed → 25% remains → $N(t)/N_0 = 0.25 = 1/4 = (1/2)^2$
- Therefore, **time = 2 half-lives** (since 1/2 → 1/4 in 2 half-lives).

**Step 4 — Compute time:**

$$t = 2 \times T_{1/2} = 2 \times 1600 \text{ years} = \boxed{\,3200 \text{ years}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the formula:**

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = 0.25 = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{1600} \text{ yr}^{-1} = 4.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$

$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.25)}{\lambda} = \frac{\ln 4}{4.33 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.3863}{4.33 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3200 \text{ years} \;\checkmark$$

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Wrong formula for BE per nucleon** — Many students compute $BE$ correctly but forget to divide by $A$ when asked for $BE/A$. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using atomic mass instead of nuclear mass** — The given $m_p$ and $m_n$ are *nuclear* masses, not atomic. Mixing atomic and nuclear masses in the same equation leads to wrong answers.
> 3. **Forgetting the 931.494 conversion factor** — Always include units: $\Delta m$ in **u** must be multiplied by **931.494 MeV/u** to get energy in **MeV**.
> 4. **Decay constant vs. half-life confusion** — Students often write $T_{1/2} = \lambda/0.693$ (inverted!). Correct: $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$.
> 5. **Neglecting the neutrino** — In β decay, the neutrino carries away energy. The emitted β particle has a *continuous* energy spectrum (0 to $E_{max}$), NOT a single discrete value. Examiners penalize answers that claim mono-energetic β particles.
> 6. **Confusing α with β magnetic deflection** — α and β deflect in *opposite* directions in a magnetic field (because of opposite charges). γ does not deflect.
> 7. **Mixing up Activity and Number of atoms** — Activity $A$ (in Bq) is the *rate* of decay. Number $N$ is the *count* of atoms. Relation: $A = \lambda N$. They are *not* equal.
> 8. **In the "75% decayed" problem** — Students compute 25% remaining, then mistakenly take $\ln(0.75)$ instead of $\ln(0.25)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 KTU Module 1 Rapid-Revision Cheat Sheet — Print this before the exam!**

- [ ] **Binding Energy** = Mass Defect × $c^2$. Always positive. Unit: MeV.
- [ ] **BE per nucleon** peaks at **⁵⁶Fe ≈ 8.8 MeV** — this is the most stable nucleus.
- [ ] **Conversion factor:** $1 \text{ u} \equiv 931.494 \text{ MeV}/c^2$. Memorize this!
- [ ] **Fission:** Heavy nucleus (A > 230) splits → ~200 MeV released + 2–3 neutrons.
- [ ] **Fission fragments** are neutron-rich → undergo **β⁻ decay chains**.
- [ ] **Fusion:** Light nuclei (A < 20) combine → energy released (e.g., D + T → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV).
- [ ] **Radioactivity** is a *spontaneous, statistical, nuclear* process — NOT affected by chemical/physical state.
- [ ] **Decay Law:** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [ ] **Half-life:** $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$. **Mean life:** $\tau = 1/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Activity:** $A = \lambda N$ (in Becquerels, Bq). 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq.
- [ ] **For 1/8th activity remaining** → 3 half-lives. **For 1/4 remaining** → 2 half-lives. **For 1/2 remaining** → 1 half-life.
- [ ] **Alpha (α):** ⁴He nucleus, +2e charge, 4 u mass, **discrete** energy, low penetration, very high ionization.
- [ ] **Beta (β⁻):** Electron, −1e charge, ~0 mass, **continuous** energy spectrum (0 to $E_{max}$), moderate penetration.
- [ ] **Gamma (γ):** Photon, 0 charge, 0 mass, **discrete** line spectrum, very high penetration, very low ionization.
- [ ] **In magnetic field:** α deflects one way, β the other, γ goes straight.
- [ ] **Shielding:** Paper (α) | Plastic/Al (β) | Lead/Concrete (γ).
- [ ] **Q-value** of a reaction = (Initial mass − Final mass) × 931.494. If Q > 0, energy is released.
- [ ] **Geiger's law** for α range in air: $R_\alpha \approx 0.318 \cdot E^{3/2}$ cm (with E in MeV).
- [ ] **Fission Neutron Yield** for U-235 (thermal): $\nu \approx 2.43$.
- [ ] **Fission Energy Distribution:** KE fragments (~165 MeV) > KE neutrons (~5 MeV) > β/γ decay (~15 MeV) > neutrinos (~10 MeV) > prompt γ (~5 MeV).

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Final KTU Exam Strategy Tip:** For full marks in Part B derivations, always show the *starting equation* → *substitute numerical values* → *step-by-step arithmetic* → *boxed final answer with units*. Examiners reward the **process**, not just the final number. A correct number with no working may earn only 50–60% of the marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Fundamentals of Radioactivity & Neutron Interactions

## 1. Radioactive Decay — The Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radioactive Decay (KTU 2024 Definition):** Radioactive decay is the **spontaneous, stochastic transformation of an unstable parent nuclide into a more stable daughter nuclide**, accompanied by the emission of one or more ionizing particles or electromagnetic quanta (alpha $\alpha$, beta $\beta^{-}$, beta $\beta^{+}$, neutrons, or gamma rays $\gamma$) to relieve the nuclear energy imbalance.

Every radionuclide seeks to reach the **Band of Stability** by adjusting its **Neutron-to-Proton ratio (N/Z)**. There are approximately **3400 known nuclides**, but only **~254 are stable**; the rest undergo decay.

### 1.1 The Three Primary Decay Modes

| Decay Mode | Particle Emitted | Change in A | Change in Z | Change in N | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alpha Decay ($\alpha$)** | $^4_2\text{He}$ nucleus | $\Delta A = -4$ | $\Delta Z = -2$ | $\Delta N = -2$ | $^{238}_{92}\text{U} \rightarrow {}^{234}_{90}\text{Th} + \alpha$ |
| **Beta Minus Decay ($\beta^{-}$)** | Electron + Antineutrino | $\Delta A = 0$ | $\Delta Z = +1$ | $\Delta N = -1$ | $^{14}_{6}\text{C} \rightarrow {}^{14}_{7}\text{N} + \beta^{-} + \bar{\nu}_{e}$ |
| **Gamma Decay ($\gamma$)** | High-energy photon | $\Delta A = 0$ | $\Delta Z = 0$ | $\Delta N = 0$ | $^{60\text{m}}_{27}\text{Co} \rightarrow {}^{60}_{27}\text{Co} + \gamma$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View):** Think of a radioactive nucleus as a **wobbly, over-stuffed suitcase** that cannot hold its contents together. To reach stability, the suitcase "unzips" — sometimes throwing out a heavy chunk (alpha), sometimes spitting out a small charged piece while converting a neutron to a proton (beta), and sometimes just emitting a flash of light to settle down (gamma). Each emission is a step towards the stable "suitcase" configuration.

### 1.2 Decay Schemes — Visualizing Nuclear Transitions

A **Decay Scheme** is a graphical representation showing the energy levels of parent and daughter nuclei, with arrows indicating the type and energy of emitted radiation. It is the nuclear physicist's equivalent of an electronic circuit diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A decay scheme shows **Q-value**, **branching ratios**, and **transition energies** — all of which are board-exam favourites.

**Example: Cobalt-60 Decay Scheme**

$$^{60}_{27}\text{Co} \xrightarrow{\beta^{-}} {}^{60\text{m}}_{28}\text{Ni}^{*} \xrightarrow{\gamma(1.17 \text{ MeV})} \xrightarrow{\gamma(1.33 \text{ MeV})} {}^{60}_{28}\text{Ni (stable)}$$

The two-step gamma cascade (1.17 MeV + 1.33 MeV = 2.50 MeV total) is a textbook example in nuclear safety for shielding calculations.

### 1.3 Effects of Radiation

> [!NOTE]
> Radiation interacts with matter through **ionization** and **excitation**. The KTU syllabus demands a clear separation of these effects into four categories:

**A. Biological Effects (Critical for Safety)**
- **Deterministic Effects:** Severity increases with dose; threshold-based (e.g., skin erythema at ~2 Gy, cataract at ~5 Gy).
- **Stochastic Effects:** Probability increases with dose, no threshold (e.g., cancer induction, genetic mutations).
- **Units:** Equivalent dose in **Sieverts (Sv)** = Absorbed dose (Gy) × Quality factor (Q).

**B. Physical/Chemical Effects**
- Radiolysis of water → H₂, H₂O₂, OH• radicals (critical in reactor coolant).
- Polymer degradation, embrittlement of reactor pressure vessel steel.
- Color center formation in crystals and glass.

**C. Genetic Effects**
- DNA strand breaks, single and double; chromosomal aberrations.

**D. Effects on Materials**
- Swelling, creep acceleration, and helium embrittlement in reactor structural materials.

### 1.4 Neutron Interaction — The Gateway to Fission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neutron Interaction (KTU Definition):** A neutron interaction is any process by which an incident neutron alters the energy, direction, or composition of a target nucleus, governed by quantum mechanical probability and quantified by the **microscopic cross-section** $\sigma$ (in barns, where 1 barn = $10^{-24} \text{ cm}^2$).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine throwing tennis balls at a forest of vertical wooden pegs. Some balls bounce off (scattering), some stick (absorption), some split a peg into two pieces (fission), and some do nothing. The "probability" of each outcome is the **cross-section**. A larger cross-section = a bigger "target" for that specific reaction.

### 1.5 The Six-Factor Conceptual Map (Preview)

The remainder of this module builds up the **Six-Factor Formula** for reactor criticality:

$$k_{eff} = \eta \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon \cdot f \cdot P_{TNL} \cdot P_{FNL}$$

Each factor (multiplication, resonance escape, fast fission, thermal utilization, non-leakage probabilities) traces back to a neutron interaction mechanism. KTU expects students to **derive, identify, and numerically evaluate** each of these factors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Radioactive Decay Law — Complete Framework

The **decay constant $\lambda$** is the probability per unit time that a given nucleus will decay. The fundamental law of radioactive decay states:

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $N(t)$ is the number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$, and $N_0$ is the initial count at $t = 0$.

### 2.2 Activity, Half-Life, and Mean Life

**Activity $A(t)$** is the number of disintegrations per unit time:

$$A(t) = \lambda \cdot N(t) = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Activity:** Activity per unit mass, $a = \frac{\lambda N_A}{M}$, where $M$ is the molar mass and $N_A$ is **Avogadro's number ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹)**.

**Half-Life $t_{1/2}$:** Time for $N(t)$ to fall to $N_0/2$.

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

**Mean Life $\tau$:** Average lifetime of a radioactive atom before decay.

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{t_{1/2}}{0.693}$$

### 2.3 Cross-Section and Reaction Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microscopic Cross-Section $\sigma$:** The effective "target area" presented by a single nucleus for a specific nuclear reaction. Units: **barn (b)** = $10^{-24}$ cm², or millibarn (mb) = $10^{-27}$ cm².

**Macroscopic Cross-Section $\Sigma$:** Probability per unit path length that a neutron will undergo a specific interaction with the bulk material.

$$\Sigma = N \cdot \sigma$$

where $N$ is the atomic density (atoms/cm³). Units: cm⁻¹.

**Mean Free Path $\lambda_{mfp}$:** Average distance a neutron travels between interactions.

$$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{1}{\Sigma}$$

**Reaction Rate $R$:** Number of interactions per unit volume per unit time.

$$R = \phi \cdot \Sigma = \phi \cdot N \cdot \sigma$$

where $\phi$ is the **neutron flux** (neutrons/cm²·s). This single equation is the foundation of reactor physics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy for Reaction Rate:** Think of a rainy highway. The flux $\phi$ is the number of raindrops hitting a square meter per second. The macroscopic cross-section $\Sigma$ is the "density of puddles" on the road. The product $\phi \cdot \Sigma$ is the rate at which raindrops "react" with puddles. Multiply by road area to get the total reaction rate.

### 2.4 Neutron Moderation, Scattering & Collision

**Elastic Scattering (n,n):** The dominant moderation process. A fast neutron bounces off a nucleus, transferring kinetic energy. Energy transfer is maximum in a **head-on collision** with a stationary nucleus of mass number $A$:

$$\alpha = \left(\frac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{E_{n}'}{E_n} = \frac{1 + \alpha}{2} + \frac{1 - \alpha}{2} \cos\theta_{cm}$$

**Average logarithmic energy decrement $\xi$** (energy loss per collision, averaged over isotropic scattering in CM frame):

$$\xi = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

For $A > 10$, the approximation $\xi \approx \frac{2}{A + 0.667}$ is excellent (error < 1%).

**Number of collisions to thermalize ($N_c$):** To slow a neutron from $E_0$ to thermal energy $E_{th}$:

$$N_c = \frac{\ln(E_0 / E_{th})}{\xi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moderating Power $\xi \Sigma_s$:** Quality measure of a moderator — high $\xi$ (light nuclei) and high $\Sigma_s$ (high density). **Heavy water (D₂O) is the best moderator**; **light water (H₂O)** is second; **graphite** is third.

### 2.5 The Four Factors of the Infinite Multiplication Factor

For an **infinite medium** (no neutron leakage), the multiplication factor is:

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon$$

| Factor | Name | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ | **Neutron Multiplication Factor** | Neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel |
| $f$ | **Thermal Utilization** | Probability a thermal neutron is absorbed in fuel (not moderator) |
| $p$ | **Resonance Escape Probability** | Probability a neutron survives resonance absorption during moderation |
| $\varepsilon$ | **Fast Fission Factor** | Ratio of total fissions to fissions by thermal neutrons |

**A. Neutron Multiplication Factor $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \nu \cdot \frac{\Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a^{(fuel)}}$$

where $\nu$ is the average number of neutrons released per fission, $\Sigma_f$ is the macroscopic fission cross-section, and $\Sigma_a^{(fuel)}$ is the total absorption (fission + capture) cross-section of the fuel.

**B. Thermal Utilization $f$:**

$$f = \frac{\Sigma_a^{(fuel)}}{\Sigma_a^{(fuel)} + \Sigma_a^{(mod)} + \Sigma_a^{(other)}}$$

**C. Resonance Escape Probability $p$:**

$$p = \exp\left(-\int_{E_{th}}^{E_0} \frac{\Sigma_a^{(fuel)}(E)}{\xi \Sigma_t(E)} \cdot \frac{dE}{E}\right)$$

**D. Fast Fission Factor $\varepsilon$:**

$$\varepsilon = 1 + \frac{\text{Fast fissions}}{\text{Thermal fissions}}$$

For $^{235}\text{U}$, $\varepsilon \approx 1.06$ to $1.08$.

### 2.6 Criticality — The Reactor Equilibrium Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criticality Equation:** A reactor is **critical** when the effective multiplication factor $k_{eff} = 1$. In an infinite medium, $k_\infty = 1$. The four-factor formula becomes:

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon = 1$$

| State | Condition | Physical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Subcritical** | $k_{eff} < 1$ | Neutron population decays exponentially; reactor shuts down |
| **Critical** | $k_{eff} = 1$ | Neutron population is steady-state; reactor power is constant |
| **Supercritical** | $k_{eff} > 1$ | Neutron population grows exponentially; power increases |

**Including leakage** in a finite reactor:

$$k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{TNL} \cdot P_{FNL}$$

where $P_{TNL}$ and $P_{FNL}$ are the **thermal and fast non-leakage probabilities**.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Decay law | nuclei |
| 2 | $A(t) = \lambda N(t)$ | Activity | Bq (s⁻¹) |
| 3 | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}$ | Half-life | s |
| 4 | $\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean life | s |
| 5 | $\Sigma = N\sigma$ | Macroscopic cross-section | cm⁻¹ |
| 6 | $R = \phi \Sigma$ | Reaction rate | reactions/cm³·s |
| 7 | $\alpha = \left(\frac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$ | Energy ratio in elastic scattering | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\xi \approx \frac{2}{A + 0.667}$ | Avg. log energy decrement | dimensionless |
| 9 | $N_c = \frac{\ln(E_0/E_{th})}{\xi}$ | Collisions to thermalize | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\eta = \nu \frac{\Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}}$ | Neutron multiplication | dimensionless |
| 11 | $f = \frac{\Sigma_a^{fuel}}{\Sigma_a^{total}}$ | Thermal utilization | dimensionless |
| 12 | $k_\infty = \eta f p \varepsilon$ | 4-factor formula | dimensionless |
| 13 | $k_{eff} = k_\infty P_{TNL} P_{FNL}$ | 6-factor formula | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The four-factor formula is the design basis for every commercial reactor core. Nuclear engineers tune fuel enrichment, moderator-to-fuel ratio, and geometry to make $k_{eff}$ hover around 1.000 — typically with **reactor control** and **shutdown margin** built in for safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Radioactive Decay Law

**Starting Assumption:** The probability of a single nucleus decaying in a small time interval $dt$ is constant and equal to $\lambda dt$, independent of age.

**Step 1 — The Probability Statement**

The expected number of nuclei decaying in $dt$ is the product of (number of nuclei present) × (decay probability):

$$-dN = \lambda \cdot N(t) \cdot dt$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Separate Variables**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} &= -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
\ln\left(\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) &= -\lambda t \\
N(t) &= N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Activity Derivation**

Multiplying both sides by $\lambda$:

$$A(t) = \lambda N(t) = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

This proves activity follows the same exponential law as the number of nuclei.

**Step 5 — Derive Half-Life**

By definition, $N(t_{1/2}) = N_0/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_0}{2} &= N_0 e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}} \\
\frac{1}{2} &= e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}} \\
\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) &= -\lambda t_{1/2} \\
t_{1/2} &= \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.693}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Derive Mean Life**

The mean life is the expected value of the decay time:

$$\tau = \langle t \rangle = \frac{\int_0^\infty t \cdot \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} \, dt}{N_0}$$

Using $\int_0^\infty t e^{-\lambda t} dt = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ (standard integral):

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Thus $\boxed{\tau = \frac{t_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \cdot t_{1/2}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Reaction Rate from Microscopic Cross-Section

**Step 1 — Definition of Microscopic Cross-Section**

Imagine a thin slab of material of thickness $dx$ and area $A$, with atomic density $N$ (atoms/cm³). The number of target nuclei in the slab is $N \cdot A \cdot dx$.

**Step 2 — Probability of Interaction**

The total "target area" presented by these nuclei is the cross-section times the number of nuclei: $\sigma \cdot N \cdot A \cdot dx$.

The probability that an incident neutron interacts while traversing the slab is:

$$P(\text{interaction in } dx) = \frac{\sigma \cdot N \cdot A \cdot dx}{A} = \sigma N \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Neutron Flux Definition**

The neutron flux $\phi$ is defined as the product of neutron density $n$ and neutron speed $v$:

$$\phi = n \cdot v \quad \text{(units: neutrons/cm}^2\text{·s)}$$

**Step 4 — Reaction Rate Derivation**

The number of neutrons passing through $A$ per second is $\phi A$. The number reacting in the slab is:

$$dR_{total} = \phi A \cdot \sigma N \, dx$$

The reaction rate per unit volume is:

$$R = \frac{dR_{total}}{A \, dx} = \phi \cdot \sigma \cdot N = \phi \cdot \Sigma$$

This confirms $\boxed{R = \phi \Sigma}$, the foundational reactor-physics equation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Four-Factor Formula

**Step 1 — Track the Life of One Neutron in an Infinite Reactor**

A "fission-born" neutron follows a clear life cycle. We track **100 fast neutrons** born from fission in the fuel.

**Step 2 — Fast Fission Multiplier $\varepsilon$**

Some fast neutrons ($E > 0.1$ MeV) cause additional fissions in $^{238}\text{U}$ before thermalizing. For every thermal-neutron-induced fission, there are some fast-neutron-induced fissions. Define:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Total fissions (thermal + fast)}}{\text{Thermal fissions}} \geq 1$$

**Step 3 — Resonance Escape Probability $p$**

As the fast neutron slows down, it passes through the **resonance region** (1 eV to 100 keV) where $^{238}\text{U}$ has very high absorption peaks. The probability of "escaping" all resonances is:

$$p = \exp\left(-\frac{1}{\xi} \int_{E_{th}}^{E_0} \frac{\Sigma_a^{(U-238)}(E)}{\Sigma_t(E)} \frac{dE}{E}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Neutron Multiplication Factor $\eta$**

Once thermalized, each neutron absorbed in $^{235}\text{U}$ produces an average of $\nu$ new neutrons (about 2.42 for $^{235}\text{U}$). The factor $\eta$ accounts for the fact that some absorptions are **captures**, not fissions:

$$\eta = \nu \cdot \frac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_a} = \nu \cdot \frac{\Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a}$$

**Step 5 — Thermal Utilization $f$**

The thermal neutron may be absorbed in **fuel, moderator, or structural materials**. The probability it goes into fuel is:

$$f = \frac{\Sigma_a^{fuel}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel} + \Sigma_a^{mod} + \Sigma_a^{struct}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine into $k_\infty$**

The number of fast neutrons produced in generation $n+1$ per thermal neutron absorbed in generation $n$ is:

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting definitions and tracking a single neutron's life cycle through the four steps yields the product.

---

## 3.4 Criticality Condition — Steady-State Derivation

**Step 1 — Neutron Cycle Definition**

The neutron cycle is the sequence from one fission event to the next generation of fissions. Define $n$ as the neutron population in the current generation and $n'$ in the next.

**Step 2 — Multiplication Factor Definition**

By definition:

$$k_{eff} = \frac{n'}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Criticality Conditions**

| Condition | Equation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Subcritical | $k_{eff} < 1$ | $n' < n$; population decays |
| Critical | $k_{eff} = 1$ | $n' = n$; constant population |
| Supercritical | $k_{eff} > 1$ | $n' > n$; population grows |

**Step 4 — Reactor Period**

For a step change in reactivity $\rho = \frac{k-1}{k}$, the neutron population follows:

$$n(t) = n_0 e^{t/T}$$

The reactor period is:

$$T = \frac{\Lambda}{k - 1} = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho k}$$

where $\Lambda$ is the **neutron generation time** ($\sim 10^{-4}$ s for fast neutrons, $\sim 10^{-3}$ s for thermal). A positive $\rho$ yields a positive period; reactivity is thus the **fundamental control parameter** of every reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reactivity $\rho$:** A dimensionless measure of departure from criticality:
>
> $$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$$

---

## 3.5 Characteristics of the Fission Reaction — Numerical Table

| Property | $^{235}\text{U}$ (thermal) | $^{239}\text{Pu}$ (thermal) | $^{238}\text{U}$ (fast) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_f$ (barns) | 585 | 750 | 0.3 |
| $\sigma_a$ (barns) | 685 | 1020 | 2.7 |
| $\nu$ (avg neutrons/fission) | 2.42 | 2.87 | 2.6 |
| $\eta$ | 2.07 | 2.11 | 0.3 |
| Energy released (MeV) | ~200 | ~210 | ~205 |
| Fission fragments mass (avg) | 95 & 139 | 100 & 138 | 95 & 140 |

**Energy distribution of fission energy (for $^{235}\text{U}$):**

| Component | Energy (MeV) | % |
|---|---|---|
| Kinetic energy of fission fragments | 165 | 82.5% |
| Kinetic energy of prompt neutrons | 5 | 2.5% |
| Prompt gamma rays | 7 | 3.5% |
| Beta decay of fragments | 7 | 3.5% |
| Gamma decay of fragments | 6 | 3.0% |
| Neutrinos (lost) | 10 | 5.0% |
| **Total recoverable** | **~190** | **95%** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "**200 MeV per fission**" is a standard number. Roughly 190 MeV is recoverable as heat; ~10 MeV escapes as neutrinos and is irrecoverable.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decay Scheme Diagram (Cobalt-60 — Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the architecture of the Co-60 decay, including energy levels, transition types, and emitted particle energies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    parentNode["Co-60 Parent Nuclide<br/>Atomic Number 27<br/>Mass Number 60<br/>Energy Level Ground State"]
    
    parentNode -->|"Beta minus decay Q-value 0.31 MeV"| betaEmit["Beta Particle Emitted<br/>Max Energy 0.31 MeV<br/>Antineutrino Released"]
    
    betaEmit --> excited1Node["Ni-60 Excited State 1<br/>Energy 2.50 MeV above ground"]
    
    excited1Node -->|"Gamma ray energy 1.17 MeV"| excited2Node["Ni-60 Excited State 2<br/>Energy 1.33 MeV above ground"]
    
    excited2Node -->|"Gamma ray energy 1.33 MeV"| groundNode["Ni-60 Stable Ground State<br/>Atomic Number 28<br/>Mass Number 60<br/>Binding Energy Maximum"]
    
    style parentNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style excited1Node fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cccc00,stroke-width:2px
    style excited2Node fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#cccc00,stroke-width:2px
    style groundNode fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    style betaEmit fill:#ccccff,stroke:#0000cc,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Red = parent/initial state, Yellow = excited intermediate states, Green = stable final state, Blue = emitted radiation. Notice the energy conservation: $0.31 + 1.17 + 1.33 = 2.81$ MeV matches the Q-value of the decay.

---

## 4.2 Neutron Interaction — Functional Architecture

```mermaid

    incidentNode["Incident Neutron<br/>Energy E<br/>Direction Omega<br/>Flux Phi"]
    
    incidentNode --> collideNode{"Collision With<br/>Target Nucleus<br/>Probability Sigma"}
    
    collideNode -->|"Elastic Scattering n-n<br/>Cross Section Sigma-s"| elasticNode["Elastic Scattering<br/>Energy Loss via Kinematics<br/>Direction Changed<br/>Mass Number A"]
    
    collideNode -->|"Inelastic Scattering n-n prime<br/>Cross Section Sigma-in"| inelasNode["Inelastic Scattering<br/>Compound Nucleus Excited<br/>Neutron Energy Reduced<br/>Gamma Emission Follows"]
    
    collideNode -->|"Radiative Capture n-gamma<br/>Cross Section Sigma-gamma"| captureNode["Neutron Capture<br/>Compound Nucleus Formed<br/>Gamma Ray Emitted<br/>Stable Product"]
    
    collideNode -->|"Fission n-f<br/>Cross Section Sigma-f"| fissionNode["Nuclear Fission<br/>Heavy Nucleus Splits<br/>2 to 3 Neutrons Released<br/>200 MeV Energy Released"]
    
    collideNode -->|"Charged Particle n-p or n-alpha<br/>Cross Section Sigma-cp"| chargedNode["Charged Particle Emission<br/>Proton or Alpha Released<br/>Threshold Reaction<br/>Requires High Energy"]

    style collideNode fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px

    style captureNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px

```

---

## 4.3 Four-Factor Formula — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode["Fission Event in Fuel<br/>Fission Fragments Created<br/>Fast Neutrons Released"]
    
    startNode --> epsilonNode["Fast Fission Factor epsilon<br/>Fast Neutrons May Cause<br/>Additional Fission in U-238<br/>Ratio Greater Than 1"]
    
    epsilonNode --> resonanceNode["Resonance Escape Probability p<br/>Neutron Slows from Fast to Thermal<br/>Must Avoid U-238 Resonance Absorption<br/>Probability Less Than 1"]
    
    resonanceNode --> fuelAbsorbNode{"Thermal Neutron<br/>Absorbed Where"}
    
    fuelAbsorbNode -->|"Absorbed in Fuel<br/>Macro Cross Section Sigma-fuel"| etaNode["Neutron Multiplication eta<br/>Each Fuel Absorption Produces<br/>Average nu New Neutrons<br/>Only in U-235 or Pu-239"]
    
    fuelAbsorbNode -->|"Absorbed in Moderator or Structure"| lostNode["Neutron Lost<br/>to Non-fissile Material<br/>Negative Contribution"]
    
    etaNode --> cycleEndNode["Next Generation Begins<br/>Total Cycle Multiplier<br/>k-infinity equals eta times f times p times epsilon"]
    
    lostNode -.->|"Reduces Thermal Utilization f"| cycleEndNode
    
    style startNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style epsilonNode fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:1px

    style fuelAbsorbNode fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px
    style etaNode fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px
    style lostNode fill:#ff9999,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:1px
    style cycleEndNode fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Neutron Moderation Process — Energy Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fissionNode["Neutron Born from Fission<br/>Energy 2 MeV<br/>Fast Neutron Regime"]
    
    fissionNode --> coll1Node["Collision 1 with Moderator<br/>Energy Reduced to 1.4 MeV<br/>Using Log Decrement xi"]
    
    coll1Node --> coll2Node["Collision 2<br/>Energy 0.98 MeV"]
    
    coll2Node --> collNNode["Collision N-c<br/>Reaches Thermal Energy<br/>0.025 eV at 293 Kelvin"]
    
    collNNode --> thermalNode["Thermal Neutron Regime<br/>Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution<br/>Diffusion Phase Begins"]
    
    fissionNode -.->|"Average Collisions<br/>N-c equals ln E0 over E-th divided by xi"| collNNode
    
    style fissionNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
    style coll1Node fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px
    style coll2Node fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px
    style collNNode fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:1px
    style thermalNode fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Insight:** The diagram emphasizes that moderation is a **stochastic cascade** — a neutron does not lose energy in a single step but rather through a sequence of elastic collisions, with the number of collisions $N_c$ determined entirely by $\xi$ (a function of moderator mass number $A$) and the energy ratio $E_0/E_{th}$.
