# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "decay constant" and establish the relationship between the decay constant, half-life, and mean life of a radioactive nuclide.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Decay Constant $\lambda$ (1 mark):** The decay constant is the probability per unit time that a radioactive nucleus will decay. It is a fundamental property of each radionuclide.
>
> **Half-Life Relationship (1 mark):** The half-life is the time required for half the nuclei to decay:
>
> $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$
>
> **Mean Life Relationship (1 mark):** The mean life is the average lifetime of a radioactive nucleus:
>
> $$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{t_{1/2}}{0.693} = 1.443 \, t_{1/2}$$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is meant by "cross-section" in nuclear reactions? Distinguish between microscopic and macroscopic cross-section.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Cross-Section Definition (1 mark):** Cross-section is a measure of the probability that a nuclear reaction will occur. It represents the effective "target area" presented by a nucleus to an incident particle.
>
> **Microscopic Cross-Section $\sigma$ (1 mark):** Effective area per nucleus, measured in **barns (1 barn = $10^{-24}$ cm²)**. It depends on the type of reaction and the energy of the incident particle.
>
> **Macroscopic Cross-Section $\Sigma$ (1 mark):** Probability per unit path length for interaction in bulk material:
>
> $$\Sigma = N \cdot \sigma \quad \text{(units: cm}^{-1}\text{)}$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** The ESE Module 1 contains a single full-question (14 marks) with two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students choose **ONE** of the two alternatives. The structure below provides **Question A** and **Question B** as fully independent options.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Decay Laws & Activity

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) Starting from first principles, derive the radioactive decay law $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and obtain the relationship between decay constant and half-life. (7 marks)**

**(b) A sample contains $10^{15}$ atoms of $^{32}\text{P}$ (half-life 14.3 days). Calculate (i) the decay constant, (ii) the initial activity, and (iii) the activity after 30 days. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The probability that a single nucleus decays in an infinitesimal time $dt$ is $\lambda dt$, where $\lambda$ is the decay constant. For $N$ nuclei, the expected number decaying in $dt$ is:

$$-dN = \lambda N \, dt$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Rearranging and integrating from $N_0$ to $N(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} &= -\lambda \int_0^t dt \\
\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) &= -\lambda t \\
N(t) &= N_0 e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substituting $N(t_{1/2}) = N_0/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} &= e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}} \\
t_{1/2} &= \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Note that $t_{1/2}$ and $\lambda$ are inversely related — a high decay constant means short half-life.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $N_0 = 10^{15}$ atoms, $t_{1/2} = 14.3$ days

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Decay Constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{14.3 \text{ days}} = 0.0485 \text{ day}^{-1} \\
&= \frac{0.0485}{86400} \text{ s}^{-1} = 5.61 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Initial Activity:**

$$A_0 = \lambda N_0 = 5.61 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{15} = 5.61 \times 10^{8} \text{ Bq}$$

In Curie: $A_0 = \frac{5.61 \times 10^8}{3.7 \times 10^{10}} = 0.01516$ Ci $\approx$ 15.16 mCi

**Step 3 (3 marks) — Activity after 30 days:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(30) &= A_0 e^{-\lambda t} \\
\lambda t &= 0.0485 \times 30 = 1.455 \\
e^{-1.455} &= 0.2334 \\
A(30) &= 5.61 \times 10^8 \times 0.2334 = 1.31 \times 10^8 \text{ Bq}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $\lambda = 5.61 \times 10^{-7}$ s⁻¹
- (ii) $A_0 = 5.61 \times 10^8$ Bq
- (iii) $A(30) = 1.31 \times 10^8$ Bq

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Cross-Section, Reaction Rate & Criticality

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) Define microscopic and macroscopic cross-section. Derive the relationship between reaction rate, neutron flux, and macroscopic cross-section. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the four-factor formula for infinite multiplication factor $k_\infty$. A reactor uses fuel with $\eta = 1.8$, $f = 0.7$, $p = 0.9$, and $\varepsilon = 1.05$. Determine the value of $k_\infty$ and state whether the reactor is critical, subcritical, or supercritical. (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cross-Section Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Microscopic Cross-Section $\sigma$:** Effective area presented by a single nucleus for a given reaction. Units: **barns (b)** = $10^{-24}$ cm².

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Macroscopic Cross-Section $\Sigma$:** Probability per unit path length that a neutron interacts. If $N$ is atomic density (atoms/cm³):

$$\Sigma = N \sigma \quad \text{(units: cm}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Reaction Rate Derivation:**

Consider neutrons of flux $\phi$ (neutrons/cm²·s) incident on a thin slab of thickness $dx$ and area $A$. The number of target nuclei is $NAdx$, total target area is $\sigma N A \, dx$. Probability of interaction in $dx$:

$$P = \sigma N \, dx$$

Neutron interactions per second in the slab: $\phi A \cdot \sigma N \, dx$. Reaction rate per unit volume:

$$R = \phi \sigma N = \phi \Sigma$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Total and Partial Cross-Sections:**

For a given isotope, the total cross-section is the sum of partial cross-sections:

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_s + \sigma_a + \sigma_f$$

where $\sigma_s$ (scattering), $\sigma_a$ (absorption), $\sigma_f$ (fission) are partial cross-sections.

**Part (b) — Four-Factor Formula (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (3 marks) — Explanation of Factors:**

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon$$

- **$\eta$ (Neutron Multiplication):** Number of fast neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel.
- **$f$ (Thermal Utilization):** Probability a thermal neutron is absorbed in fuel (not moderator).
- **$p$ (Resonance Escape Probability):** Probability a neutron survives resonance absorption during moderation.
- **$\varepsilon$ (Fast Fission Factor):** Ratio of total fissions to fissions by thermal neutrons.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Numerical Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_\infty &= \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon \\
&= 1.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.9 \times 1.05 \\
&= 1.8 \times 0.7 = 1.26 \\
&= 1.26 \times 0.9 = 1.134 \\
&= 1.134 \times 1.05 = 1.1907
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Reactor State Identification:**

Since $k_\infty = 1.1907 > 1$, the reactor is **supercritical** in an infinite medium. In a real finite reactor, leakage reduces this to $k_{eff} < 1.1907$. To achieve criticality with these factors, the engineer must either:
- Increase leakage (e.g., reduce core size, insert control rods),
- Reduce $f$ by adding neutron absorber (e.g., boron),
- Reduce $\eta$ by fuel dilution, or
- Reduce $p$ by adding resonance absorber (e.g., $^{238}\text{U}$ with specific geometry).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Cross-Section:** A common error is writing $R = \phi \sigma$ instead of $R = \phi \Sigma = \phi N \sigma$. The microscopic cross-section $\sigma$ alone gives wrong units — students lose **1 mark** for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing Activity and Number of Nuclei:** Writing $A(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ instead of $A(t) = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ — loses **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in Decay Equation:** Writing $\ln(N_0/N) = \lambda t$ instead of $\ln(N/N_0) = -\lambda t$ — the negative sign is critical; losing it implies growth instead of decay (**-1 mark**).
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Neutrino Energy Loss in Fission:** A common error is assuming all 200 MeV of fission energy is recoverable. **Only ~190 MeV is recoverable** because ~10 MeV is carried away by neutrinos — failing to mention this loses **1 mark** in reactor design problems.
>
> 5. **Confusing $k_\infty$ with $k_{eff}$:** In a finite reactor, $k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{TNL} \cdot P_{FNL}$. Forgetting the leakage probabilities when given a "real" reactor problem loses **2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Mixing Up $\eta$ and $\nu$:** Students often write $\eta = \nu$, which is wrong. $\eta = \nu \cdot \sigma_f/\sigma_a$ — the ratio accounts for non-fission captures. **Loses 1 mark** in numerical problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1):**

- **Radioactive Decay:** Spontaneous transformation to reach the **band of stability**; governed by $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.

- **Three Primary Decays:** Alpha ($\Delta Z = -2$), Beta Minus ($\Delta Z = +1$), Gamma (no change). Beta Plus ($\beta^+$, positron) and electron capture are also seen.

- **Activity Units:** **Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration/sec; **Curie (Ci)** = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq.

- **Half-Life vs. Mean Life:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$; $\tau = 1/\lambda = 1.443 \cdot t_{1/2}$.

- **Decay Schemes:** Show energy levels, transition arrows, and Q-values — visual fingerprint of a radionuclide.

- **Effects of Radiation:** Deterministic (threshold) vs. Stochastic (no threshold). Equivalent dose in **Sieverts (Sv)** = Absorbed dose (Gy) × Quality factor (Q).

- **Cross-Section:** $\sigma$ in barns ($10^{-24}$ cm²); macroscopic $\Sigma = N\sigma$ in cm⁻¹.

- **Reaction Rate:** $R = \phi \Sigma$ — the **master equation** of reactor physics.

- **Moderation:** Energy loss via elastic scattering; $\xi \approx 2/(A+0.667)$ for $A > 10$.

- **Moderating Power:** $\xi \Sigma_s$ — best is D₂O, then H₂O, then graphite.

- **Fission Energy:** ~200 MeV total, ~190 MeV recoverable (neutrinos take 10 MeV).

- **Multiplication Factor $\eta$:** $\eta = \nu \sigma_f / \sigma_a$; equals 2.07 for $^{235}\text{U}$.

- **Thermal Utilization $f$:** $f = \Sigma_a^{fuel} / \Sigma_a^{total}$; maximize by enriching fuel.

- **Resonance Escape Probability $p$:** Always less than 1; maximized by fuel lumping.

- **Fast Fission Factor $\varepsilon$:** Slightly greater than 1 (~1.06 for $^{235}\text{U}$); only $^{238}\text{U}$ contributes meaningfully.

- **Four-Factor Formula:** $k_\infty = \eta f p \varepsilon$.

- **Six-Factor Formula (with leakage):** $k_{eff} = \eta f p \varepsilon P_{TNL} P_{FNL}$.

- **Criticality:** $k_{eff} = 1$ for steady-state; $k_{eff} < 1$ subcritical; $k_{eff} > 1$ supercritical.

- **Reactivity:** $\rho = (k-1)/k$; positive = power increases; negative = power decreases.

- **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote cross-section units explicitly and clearly distinguish $\sigma$ (microscopic) from $\Sigma$ (macroscopic).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neutron Moderation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Neutron Moderation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Neutron Moderation** is the process by which high-energy (fast) neutrons released during nuclear fission are progressively slowed down to thermal energies (~**0.025 eV**) through successive **elastic scattering collisions** with the nuclei of a material called the *moderator*. The neutron loses kinetic energy in each scattering event because it transfers some momentum to the recoiling target nucleus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Moderation is a *threshold concept* for the entire reactor physics chain. Without it, the sustainment of a self-propagating nuclear chain reaction using thermal-neutron-induced fission (e.g., U-235) is impossible, because the fission cross-section at 2 MeV is roughly **500 times smaller** than at 0.025 eV.

Mathematically, the moderation process is quantified by:

$$\boxed{\text{Fast Neutron (2 MeV)} \xrightarrow{\text{n elastic collisions}} \text{Thermal Neutron (0.025 eV)}}$$

The average number of collisions required is governed by the **logarithmic energy decrement ($\xi$)** of the moderator.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a heavy, fast-moving bowling ball rolling into a pit filled with table-tennis balls (light nuclei).

- **First collision:** The bowling ball barely slows down because each ping-pong ball it hits is too light to absorb much energy.
- **Many collisions:** Each time it hits a light ball, it gives away a small chunk of its kinetic energy.
- **End state:** After ~19 to ~120 collisions (depending on the "lightness" of the balls), the bowling ball moves at a "drunken," slow pace — analogous to a *thermal neutron* that diffuses like a gas molecule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why light nuclei?** A neutron has nearly the **same mass as a hydrogen nucleus (proton)**. When two objects of equal mass collide elastically head-on, the moving object can lose *up to 100%* of its energy. With heavier nuclei (like lead), the energy transfer per collision is small — poor moderation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Energy Benchmarks for Moderation Analysis**
> - Average energy of a fission neutron: **$\bar{E}_0 \approx 2.0$ MeV**
> - Thermal neutron energy at room temperature (293.6 K): **$E_{th} = 0.0253$ eV**
> - Conversion: **$1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J**
> - Boltzmann constant: **$k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K**
> - Avogadro's number: **$N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹**

The ratio of these two energies tells us the *enormous* slowing-down factor involved:

$$\frac{\bar{E}_0}{E_{th}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{0.0253} \approx 7.9 \times 10^7$$

This **eight-order-of-magnitude** reduction in energy is what makes moderator design a critical safety and performance parameter in every nuclear reactor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Moderation Process

1. **Fission Event (Source):** A U-235 or Pu-239 nucleus splits, releasing 2–3 neutrons with a Watt-fission spectrum peaking near ~0.7 MeV and a mean energy of **2.0 MeV**.
2. **Elastic Scattering Dominance:** At energies from 2 MeV down to ~1 eV, neutrons interact primarily via **elastic scattering** with moderator nuclei. Inelastic scattering is significant only above ~0.1 MeV for heavy nuclei.
3. **Energy Loss per Collision:** Each collision reduces neutron energy by a *random* amount that depends on the scattering angle and the mass ratio $A = M/m_n$ (where $M$ is target nucleus mass and $m_n$ is neutron mass).
4. **Isotropization in Center-of-Mass (CM) Frame:** For s-wave (low-energy) scattering, the scattering is *isotropic in the CM frame*. This assumption is the foundation of the classical slowing-down theory taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus.
5. **Thermalization:** Once the neutron energy falls below ~1 eV, it enters *thermal equilibrium* with the moderator's atomic motion. Its energy distribution becomes Maxwellian at the moderator temperature.
6. **Diffusion and Capture:** Thermal neutrons diffuse through the reactor until they are either absorbed by fuel (fission) or lost to parasitic capture.

## 2.2 Why and How Moderation Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' for KTU 2024:**
> - **U-235 fission cross-section ($\sigma_f$)** obeys the **1/v law** in the thermal region, meaning $\sigma_f \propto 1/\sqrt{E}$. Thermal fission is overwhelmingly more probable than fast fission for U-235.
> - In a typical **PWR** moderated by light water, **~85% of fissions** are caused by thermal neutrons.
> - The **Resonance Escape Probability ($p$)** in the resonance region (1 eV to 1 keV) is directly determined by how *efficiently* the moderator slows neutrons past the U-238 capture resonances.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table for Neutron Moderation (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Expression | Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | Atomic mass number of moderator nucleus | $A = M/m_n$ | Dimensionless; $A \geq 1$ |
| $\alpha$ | Minimum fractional energy after head-on collision | $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$ | $0 \le \alpha < 1$ |
| $\xi$ | Logarithmic energy decrement (avg. fractional ln-energy loss per collision) | $\xi = 1 - \dfrac{(A-1)^2}{4A}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A+1}{A-1}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| $\xi$ (approx.) | Simplified form valid for $A > 10$ | $\xi \approx \dfrac{2}{A + 2/3}$ | Used in KTU problems |
| $n$ | Number of collisions to thermalize from $E_0$ to $E_{th}$ | $n = \dfrac{1}{\xi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_{th}}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| $u$ | Lethargy (dimensionless energy variable) | $u = \ln(E_0/E)$ | Dimensionless |
| $\Sigma_s$ | Macroscopic scattering cross-section | $\Sigma_s = N \sigma_s$ | cm⁻¹ |
| $\Sigma_a$ | Macroscopic absorption cross-section | $\Sigma_a = N \sigma_a$ | cm⁻¹ |
| $\text{MP}$ | Moderating Power | $\text{MP} = \xi \Sigma_s$ | cm⁻¹ |
| $\text{MR}$ | Moderating Ratio | $\text{MR} = \dfrac{\xi \Sigma_s}{\Sigma_a}$ | Dimensionless |
| $q(u)$ | Slowing-down density (neutrons crossing lethargy $u$ per cm³ per sec) | $q(u) = \phi(u)\,\xi\,\Sigma_s$ | neutrons/cm³·s |
| $p$ | Resonance escape probability (NR approximation) | $p \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{N_I I_{\text{eff}}}{\xi \Sigma_s}\right)$ | Dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PWR Moderator:** Light water ($H_2O$) — excellent $\xi$ but absorbs some neutrons.
- **PHWR / CANDU Moderator:** Heavy water ($D_2O$) — lower $\xi$ but very low $\Sigma_a$, allowing use of natural uranium.
- **RBMK / Magnox Moderator:** Graphite ($^{12}C$) — moderate $\xi$, very low absorption.
- **Advanced Reactors:** Beryllium ($^9$Be) and Beryllium Oxide (BeO) — used in compact/mobile reactors for their high moderating power per unit volume.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Loss in an Elastic Head-on Collision

Consider a neutron of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v_0$ striking a stationary nucleus of mass $M = A\,m$ head-on.

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of momentum and kinetic energy:**

$$m v_0 = m v_1 + M v_2 \quad \text{(momentum)}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} M v_2^2 \quad \text{(energy)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the simultaneous equations for the neutron's final velocity $v_1$:**

From momentum: $v_2 = \dfrac{m(v_0 - v_1)}{M}$

Substitute into the energy equation:

$$m v_0^2 = m v_1^2 + M \left[\dfrac{m(v_0 - v_1)}{M}\right]^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the algebra:**

$$v_0^2 = v_1^2 + \dfrac{m}{M}(v_0 - v_1)^2 = v_1^2 + \dfrac{1}{A}(v_0^2 - 2 v_0 v_1 + v_1^2)$$

Rearranging and solving for $v_1$:

$$\boxed{v_1 = v_0 \cdot \frac{A-1}{A+1}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio of final to initial kinetic energy:**

$$\frac{E_1}{E_0} = \left(\frac{v_1}{v_0}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2 \equiv \alpha$$

**Interpretation:** $\alpha$ is the *minimum* energy ratio (head-on, $\theta_{cm} = \pi$). For a head-on collision with **hydrogen ($A=1$)**: $\alpha = 0$, meaning the neutron can be brought to a *complete stop* in a single collision. For **lead ($A=207$)**: $\alpha \approx 0.982$, so the neutron loses only ~1.8% of its energy even in a perfect head-on strike.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Logarithmic Energy Decrement ($\xi$) — Isotropic CM Scattering

In the **center-of-mass (CM) frame**, for s-wave scattering (valid below ~1 MeV for most moderators), scattering is *isotropic*. The fractional energy loss per collision is:

$$\frac{E'}{E} = \frac{(A-1)^2 + 4A \cos^2\theta_{cm}}{(A+1)^2}$$

where $\theta_{cm}$ is the CM scattering angle, uniformly distributed over $[0, \pi]$.

**Step 1 — Average $\cos^2\theta_{cm}$ over an isotropic distribution:**

$$\langle \cos^2 \theta_{cm} \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^2\theta_{cm} \sin\theta_{cm}\, d\theta_{cm} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean energy ratio:**

$$\left\langle \frac{E'}{E} \right\rangle = \frac{(A-1)^2 + 4A/3}{(A+1)^2} = \frac{1 + \alpha}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Define $\xi$ as the average logarithmic energy loss:**

$$\xi \equiv \left\langle \ln\frac{E}{E'} \right\rangle = -\left\langle \ln\frac{E'}{E} \right\rangle$$

For an isotropic CM distribution, the probability that $E'/E = x$ lies in $[x, x+dx]$ is $dx/(1-\alpha)$ (uniform from $\alpha$ to 1). Thus:

$$\xi = -\int_{\alpha}^{1} \ln x \cdot \frac{dx}{1-\alpha} = \frac{1}{1-\alpha}\int_{\alpha}^{1}(-\ln x)\, dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$\int_{\alpha}^{1}(-\ln x)\, dx = \bigl[x - x\ln x\bigr]_{\alpha}^{1} = (1 - 0) - (\alpha - \alpha\ln\alpha) = 1 - \alpha + \alpha\ln\alpha$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form expression for $\xi$:**

$$\boxed{\xi = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$$

**Step 6 — Approximate form for $A \gg 1$ (used heavily in KTU problems for graphite, beryllium):**

Expanding $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$ in powers of $1/A$ and keeping leading terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln\alpha &= 2\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{2}{A+1}\right) \approx -\frac{4}{A+1} - \frac{4}{(A+1)^2} - \cdots \\
1 - \alpha &= \frac{4A}{(A+1)^2} \approx \frac{4}{A} \\
\frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1-\alpha} &\approx -\frac{2}{A} - \cdots
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back into the closed form:

$$\boxed{\xi \approx \frac{2}{A + \tfrac{2}{3}} \quad \text{(valid for } A \ge 10\text{)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick check for graphite ($A=12$):** $\xi \approx 2/(12.667) = 0.1579$, matching the standard textbook value of 0.158. ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Collisions to Thermalize ($n$)

The number of collisions required to slow a neutron from $E_0$ to $E_{th}$ is found by summing the average logarithmic energy losses:

$$n \cdot \xi = \left\langle \ln \frac{E_0}{E_1} \right\rangle + \left\langle \ln \frac{E_1}{E_2} \right\rangle + \cdots = \left\langle \ln \frac{E_0}{E_{th}} \right\rangle$$

Since $\xi$ is approximately energy-independent (in the s-wave regime), we can write directly:

$$\boxed{n = \frac{1}{\xi} \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_{th}}\right)}$$

**Numerical example (Hydrogen, $A=1$, $\xi = 1.0$):**

$$n_H = \frac{1}{1.0}\ln\!\left(\frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{0.025}\right) = \ln(8 \times 10^7) \approx 18.2 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \lceil n \rceil = 19$$

**Numerical example (Graphite, $A=12$, $\xi = 0.158$):**

$$n_C = \frac{1}{0.158}\ln(8 \times 10^7) \approx \frac{18.2}{0.158} \approx 115$$

**Numerical example (Heavy water, $A=2$, $\xi = 0.509$):**

$$n_D = \frac{1}{0.509}\ln(8 \times 10^7) \approx 35.8 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \lceil n \rceil = 36$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Moderating Power and Moderating Ratio

**Moderating Power (MP):** A good moderator must slow neutrons *quickly* (high $\xi$) AND have a *high scattering probability* per unit path length (high $\Sigma_s$).

$$\boxed{\text{MP} = \xi \Sigma_s}$$

**Moderating Ratio (MR):** However, slowing neutrons is useless if the moderator *also absorbs* them. The MR corrects MP for parasitic absorption:

$$\boxed{\text{MR} = \frac{\xi \Sigma_s}{\Sigma_a}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MR is the truer figure of merit for a moderator.** A material like boron has high $\Sigma_s$ but disastrous $\Sigma_a$, making $\text{MR} \approx 0$. Light water has high MP but moderate $\Sigma_a$, so its MR is decent. Heavy water has lower MP than $H_2O$ but extremely low $\Sigma_a$, making its MR roughly **~20× higher** — which is precisely why CANDU reactors can use *natural* uranium fuel.

**Standard Reference Values (KTU 2024):**

| Moderator | $\xi$ | $n$ (collisions) | Relative MP | Relative MR |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $H_2O$ | 1.000 | 19 | 1.00 (ref) | 1.00 (ref) |
| $D_2O$ | 0.509 | 36 | 0.16 | ~21.0 |
| Graphite | 0.158 | 115 | 0.066 | ~1.6 |
| Beryllium | 0.209 | 90 | 0.18 | ~1.7 |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Slowing-Down Density and Connection to Resonance Escape

Define the **slowing-down density** $q(u)$ as the number of neutrons per cm³ per second that slow down past lethargy $u$. Using neutron balance over an infinitesimal lethargy interval:

$$q(u) = \phi(u)\,\xi\,\Sigma_s$$

where $\phi(u)$ is the lethargy-dependent neutron flux.

**Resonance Escape Probability (NR / Narrow Resonance approximation):**

For an infinitely dilute absorber with resonance integral $I_{\text{eff}}$ and absorber number density $N_I$:

$$\boxed{p \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{N_I I_{\text{eff}}}{\xi \Sigma_s}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{N_I I_{\text{eff}}}{\text{MP}}\right)}$$

This is the **only** place in the four-factor formula where the moderator enters multiplicatively. A *higher* MP yields a *larger* $p$, allowing more neutrons to escape the dangerous U-238 resonance region (~6.7 eV, 20.9 eV, 36.7 eV) and become thermal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Moderation Process Flow

The diagram below shows the *architecture of moderation* in a thermal nuclear reactor core, mapping how fast neutrons transition through energy regimes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fission Event in U-235] --> B[Fast Neutron Release Mean Energy 2.0 MeV]
    B --> C[Elastic Scattering with Moderator Nuclei]
    C --> D[Isotropic CM Scattering Energy Loss per Collision]
    D --> E{Energy Above 1 eV}
    E -- Yes --> F[Slowing Down Region 1 MeV to 1 eV]
    F --> G[Resonance Region U-238 Capture 6.7 to 36.7 eV]
    G --> H{Neutron Escapes Resonance}
    H -- No --> I[Parasitic Absorption by U-238]
    H -- Yes --> J[Thermalization Region Below 1 eV]
    J --> K[Maxwellian Distribution at 0.025 eV]
    K --> L[Diffusion in Reactor Core]
    L --> M{Fate of Thermal Neutron}
    M --> N[Fission in U-235 Sustains Chain Reaction]
    M --> O[Loss via Absorption or Leakage]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of Moderator Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reactor Design Requirements] --> B[Choose Moderator Candidate]
    B --> C[Evaluate Logarithmic Energy Decrement xi]
    B --> D[Evaluate Macroscopic Scattering Sigma_s]
    B --> E[Evaluate Macroscopic Absorption Sigma_a]
    C --> F[Compute Moderating Power MP]
    D --> F
    C --> G[Compute Moderating Ratio MR]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> H{MP and MR Satisfy Threshold}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Accept Moderator for Reactor Design]
    H -- No --> J[Reject and Search Alternative Material]
    I --> K[PWR Light Water]
    I --> L[PHWR Heavy Water]
    I --> M[RBMK Graphite]
    I --> N[Advanced Be or BeO]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Lethargy Variable Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Neutron Energy E in eV] --> B[Compute Lethargy u = ln E0 over E]
    B --> C[Plot Flux phi versus u]
    C --> D[Compute Slowing Down Density q equals phi xi Sigma_s]
    D --> E[Integrate over Resonance Absorbers]
    E --> F[Resonance Escape Probability p]
    F --> G[Use p in Four Factor Formula]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "Logarithmic Energy Decrement" ($\xi$) and state its significance in neutron moderation.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **logarithmic energy decrement ($\xi$)** is defined as the *average* change in the natural logarithm of neutron energy per elastic scattering collision with a moderator nucleus:

$$\xi = \left\langle \ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E'}\right) \right\rangle = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

where $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$ and $A$ is the atomic mass number of the moderator nucleus.

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It quantifies the *moderating effectiveness* of a material — a higher $\xi$ means more energy lost per collision, requiring fewer collisions to thermalize.
2. It directly determines the number of collisions $n = \dfrac{1}{\xi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_{th}}\right)$ needed to slow a fast neutron to thermal energy, which is essential for computing the **resonance escape probability** in reactor kinetics.

---

### **Q2. Why is light water ($H_2O$) a better moderator than heavy water ($D_2O$) in terms of energy loss per collision, yet heavy water is preferred in CANDU reactors?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- For $H_2O$ ($A=1$): $\xi = 1.000$, so only ~19 collisions are needed to thermalize a 2 MeV neutron.
- For $D_2O$ ($A=2$): $\xi = 0.509$, requiring ~36 collisions.
- Therefore, **$H_2O$ is superior in pure moderating power** (1 mark).

However, **$D_2O$ has an extremely low neutron absorption cross-section** ($\sigma_a \approx 0.0006$ barns vs. 0.66 barns for hydrogen) (1 mark). This gives $D_2O$ a **moderating ratio roughly 21 times higher** than $H_2O$, meaning far fewer neutrons are lost to parasitic capture. This allows CANDU reactors to operate with **natural uranium (0.72% U-235)** instead of enriched fuel, providing a strategic and economic advantage (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions, students frequently lose marks by (a) forgetting to state the *isotropic CM scattering* assumption, (b) using the wrong value of $\alpha$ when $A$ is not 1, (c) confusing moderating power with moderating ratio, and (d) omitting the final numerical substitution. Always write the **assumptions, the formula, the substitution, and the final boxed answer** for full marks.

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

#### (a) **Derive the expression for the logarithmic energy decrement ($\xi$) for isotropic scattering in the center-of-mass frame, and obtain its approximate form $\xi \approx 2/(A + 2/3)$ for $A \gg 1$.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Assumption (1 mark):** S-wave elastic scattering is *isotropic in the CM frame*, valid for neutron energies below ~1 MeV in light-to-medium nuclei.

**Step 1:** Express the energy ratio in a single collision:

$$\frac{E'}{E} = \frac{(A-1)^2 + 4A\cos^2\theta_{cm}}{(A+1)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Define $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$, so the energy ratio ranges from $\alpha$ to 1 as $\theta_{cm}$ varies from $\pi$ to 0.

**Step 3:** For isotropic CM scattering, the distribution of $E'/E$ is uniform in $[\alpha, 1]$:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}, \quad x \in [\alpha, 1]$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the average of $-\ln x$:

$$\xi = \int_{\alpha}^{1} (-\ln x) \cdot \frac{dx}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}\left[x - x\ln x\right]_{\alpha}^{1}$$

$$\xi = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Substitute $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$ and use the series expansion $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right) = -2\left(\dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{1}{3A^3} + \cdots\right)$. After retaining the leading two terms:

$$\xi \approx \frac{2}{A + 2/3}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Numerical verification for carbon ($A=12$): $\xi \approx 2/12.667 = 0.158$, which agrees with the standard reference value of 0.158. ✔

---

#### (b) **Calculate the number of collisions required to thermalize a 2 MeV fission neutron in (i) beryllium ($A=9$), and (ii) graphite ($A=12$). Comment on which is a more efficient moderator in terms of collisions alone.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Use $n = \dfrac{1}{\xi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_{th}}\right)$ with $E_0 = 2.0$ MeV and $E_{th} = 0.025$ eV.

Compute the energy ratio:

$$\frac{E_0}{E_{th}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.025} = 8 \times 10^7$$

$$\ln(8 \times 10^7) = \ln 8 + 7 \ln 10 = 2.079 + 16.118 = 18.20$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Beryllium ($A=9$):

$$\xi_{Be} = \frac{2}{9 + 2/3} = \frac{2}{9.667} = 0.2069$$

$$n_{Be} = \frac{18.20}{0.2069} \approx 87.97 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; n_{Be} \approx 88 \text{ collisions}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Graphite ($A=12$):

$$\xi_C = \frac{2}{12 + 2/3} = \frac{2}{12.667} = 0.1579$$

$$n_C = \frac{18.20}{0.1579} \approx 115.26 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; n_C \approx 116 \text{ collisions}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Comment:** Beryllium requires *fewer* collisions to thermalize a neutron ($\sim 88$ vs. $\sim 116$), making it more *efficient per collision* due to its lower mass number. However, moderator efficiency in a real reactor also depends on the macroscopic cross-sections $\Sigma_s$ and $\Sigma_a$, not just $\xi$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{n_{Be} \approx 88, \quad n_C \approx 116}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

#### (a) **Explain the concepts of Moderating Power (MP) and Moderating Ratio (MR). Why is MR a better figure of merit than MP for selecting a reactor moderator?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Moderating Power (1 mark):** The product of the logarithmic energy decrement and the macroscopic scattering cross-section:

$$\text{MP} = \xi \cdot \Sigma_s \quad (\text{units: cm}^{-1})$$

It quantifies the rate at which a moderator reduces neutron energy per unit path length.

**Moderating Ratio (1 mark):** The ratio of moderating power to the macroscopic absorption cross-section:

$$\text{MR} = \frac{\xi \Sigma_s}{\Sigma_a} \quad (\text{dimensionless})$$

It accounts for the *unwanted loss* of neutrons to parasitic absorption by the moderator itself.

**Why MR is a better figure of merit (4 marks):**

1. **Trade-off capture:** A material with high $\Sigma_s$ (e.g., boron) may appear to be a good moderator by MP, but its enormous $\Sigma_a$ makes it *useless* because it absorbs more neutrons than it slows down.
2. **Net neutron economy:** MR directly predicts the neutron population surviving moderation, which is the *real* determinant of reactor criticality.
3. **Example comparison:**
   - $H_2O$: $\xi \Sigma_s \approx 1.35$ cm⁻¹, $\Sigma_a \approx 0.022$ cm⁻¹ $\Rightarrow$ MR $\approx 62$.
   - $D_2O$: $\xi \Sigma_s \approx 0.18$ cm⁻¹, $\Sigma_a \approx 0.00003$ cm⁻¹ $\Rightarrow$ MR $\approx 21\,000$.
4. **Design impact:** Higher MR permits lower fuel enrichment. CANDU uses $D_2O$ because MR is ~340× higher than $H_2O$ in practice, enabling natural-uranium operation.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** MR encapsulates both the slowing-down efficiency and the neutron-conservation property of a moderator and is therefore the preferred engineering figure of merit.

---

#### (b) **A reactor uses a moderator with $A=12$, $\Sigma_s = 0.40$ cm⁻¹, and $\Sigma_a = 0.0003$ cm⁻¹. Calculate (i) the moderating power, (ii) the moderating ratio, and (iii) the resonance escape probability, given an effective resonance integral $I_{\text{eff}} = 10$ barns and absorber number density $N_I = 0.006$ atoms/barn-cm.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $\xi$ for $A=12$:

$$\xi = \frac{2}{12 + 2/3} = \frac{2}{12.667} = 0.1579$$

**(i) Moderating Power (1 mark):**

$$\text{MP} = \xi \cdot \Sigma_s = 0.1579 \times 0.40 = 0.0632 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Moderating Ratio (1 mark):**

$$\text{MR} = \frac{\xi \Sigma_s}{\Sigma_a} = \frac{0.0632}{0.0003} = 210.7$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** For the resonance escape probability, the units must be checked carefully. Using consistent units with the given $N_I$ (in atoms/barn-cm), we need $\Sigma_s$ in *reciprocal cm* and $I_{\text{eff}}$ in barns:

$$p \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{N_I I_{\text{eff}}}{\xi \Sigma_s}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.006 \times 10}{0.1579 \times 0.40}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Simplify the argument:

$$\frac{N_I I_{\text{eff}}}{\xi \Sigma_s} = \frac{0.06}{0.0632} = 0.9494$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute the exponential:

$$p = e^{-0.9494} = 0.387$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Final Boxed Answers:**

$$\boxed{\text{MP} = 0.0632 \text{ cm}^{-1}, \quad \text{MR} = 210.7, \quad p \approx 0.387}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget to multiply $N_I \times I_{\text{eff}}$ correctly and report $p > 1$, which is physically impossible. The dimensionless argument of the exponential **must** be positive; if you get a negative exponent, your substitution is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Neutron Moderation (KTU 2024)**

- **Definition:** Moderation = slowing fast fission neutrons (~2 MeV) to thermal energy (~0.025 eV) via *elastic scattering* with light nuclei.
- **Best moderator mass:** $A=1$ (hydrogen) gives $\xi = 1.0$, the theoretical maximum.
- **Approximation:** $\xi \approx \dfrac{2}{A + 2/3}$ for $A \geq 10$ — memorize this.
- **Collisions to thermalize:** $n = \dfrac{1}{\xi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_{th}}\right) \approx \dfrac{18.2}{\xi}$.
- **Minimum energy ratio:** $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2$ — head-on collision limit.
- **Two figure-of-merit equations:** $\text{MP} = \xi \Sigma_s$ and $\text{MR} = \xi \Sigma_s / \Sigma_a$.
- **MR > MP for design:** MR is the *true* moderator selector in real reactors.
- **Resonance escape probability:** $p \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{N_I I_{\text{eff}}}{\xi \Sigma_s}\right)$ — enters the four-factor formula.
- **Lethargy variable:** $u = \ln(E_0/E)$ — converts slowing-down analysis to a flat spectrum.
- **Standard ordering of moderators by MR:** $D_2O \gg H_2O > \text{Be} \approx \text{Graphite}$ (for natural-uranium reactors).
- **KTU must-know constants:** $\bar{E}_0 = 2$ MeV, $E_{th} = 0.025$ eV, $k_B T = 0.0253$ eV at 293.6 K.
- **Assumption to always state:** S-wave isotropic scattering in the center-of-mass frame (valid below ~1 MeV).
- **Real reactor mapping:** PWR → $H_2O$; PHWR/CANDU → $D_2O$; RBMK/Magnox → Graphite; Advanced → Be/BeO.
- **Watch the units in $p$:** Always keep $N_I$, $I_{\text{eff}}$, $\xi$, and $\Sigma_s$ dimensionally consistent before exponentiating.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Practical Fission Fuels - Reactor Power # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Practical Fission Fuels **Practical Fission Fuels** are the actinide isotopes actually loaded into the core of a nuclear reactor to sustain a controlled nuclear chain reaction. They are broadly classified into two physical categories based on their interaction with thermal neutrons: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fissile Isotopes**: Nuclides that undergo fission upon absorbing a neutron of *any* energy (thermal, epithermal, or fast). The three practical fissile isotopes are **Uranium-235 (²³⁵U)**, **Plutonium-239 (²³⁹Pu)**, and **Uranium-233 (²³³U)** (synthesized from Thorium-232). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fertile Isotopes**: Nuclides that are *not* themselves fissile with thermal neutrons, but which capture a neutron and transmute (via radioactive decay) into a fissile isotope. The two principal fertile isotopes are **Uranium-238 (²³⁸U)** and **Thorium-232 (²³²Th)**. A **Reactor Power** is the time-rate of energy release in the core, expressed in Watts (W) or Megawatts (MW), and is governed by the rate of fissions, the recoverable energy per fission, and the volume of the active core. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine a **controlled bonfire built from different types of wood**: - **Fissile fuel** (²³⁵U, ²³⁹Pu, ²³³U) is like *dry kindling* — it ignites easily and burns immediately even from a small spark (a slow neutron). - **Fertile fuel** (²³⁸U, ²³²Th) is like *green hardwood logs* — they will not ignite from a spark, but if you wrap them *around* the burning kindling, the neutron flux from the kindling "cooks" them, slowly converting them into dry kindling (²³⁹Pu or ²³³U) that can later burn on its own. - **Reactor Power** is simply the *rate at which heat is being released* by the fire, measured by how fast a kettle of water on top boils. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constant**: The recoverable energy per fission is approximately **E_f ≈ 200 MeV per fission** (the exact value depends on the fissile nuclide and on whether neutron capture and decay products are accounted for: ~202.5 MeV for ²³⁵U, ~211.5 MeV for ²³⁹Pu). --- ## 1.3 The Four Practical Fuel Isotopes at a Glance | Nuclide | Origin | Fissile/Fertile | Thermal-Neutron Fission Cross-Section (σ_f) | Comment | |---|---|---|---|---| | ²³⁵U | Natural (0.711%) | **Fissile** | ~585 barns | Only naturally occurring fissile nuclide | | ²³⁸U | Natural (99.289%) | Fertile | ~0 (only fast fission) | Converted to ²³⁹Pu | | ²³⁹Pu | Bred from ²³⁸U | **Fissile** | ~750 barns | Fuel in MOX and fast reactors | | ²³³U | Bred from ²³²Th | **Fissile** | ~531 barns | Used in the thorium fuel cycle | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Nuclear Fuel Cycle at the Isotopic Level ### 2.1.1 Natural Uranium as a Starting Point Natural uranium mined from ore contains three isotopes in practically fixed proportions: - ²³⁸U — **99.2845%** (half-life 4.468 × 10⁹ y) - ²³⁵U — **0.7110%** (half-life 7.04 × 10⁸ y) - ²³⁴U — **0.0055%** (negligible for reactor physics) Because the fissile ²³⁵U fraction is only 0.711%, **most reactors must enrich the fuel** to obtain a critical mass in a reasonable core volume. > [!NOTE] > **Enrichment Terminology (Board Examiner Frequently Tests This):** > - **Natural Uranium (NU):** 0.711% ²³⁵U — used in CANDU/PHWR reactors. > - **Low-Enriched Uranium (LEU):** 3 % – 5 % ²³⁵U — standard for PWR, BWR, VVER. > - **High-Enriched Uranium (HEU):** 20 % – 90 % ²³⁵U — research reactors, naval propulsion. > - **Weapons-Grade:** > 90 % ²³⁵U (or > 93 % ²³⁹Pu in Pu pits). ### 2.1.2 The ²³⁸U → ²³⁹Pu Breeding Chain Neutron capture on ²³⁸U followed by two beta decays produces ²³⁹Pu: $$^{238}_{92}U + ^{1}_{0}n \longrightarrow ^{239}_{92}U \xrightarrow{\beta^{-},\,t_{1/2}=23.5\,\text{min}} ^{239}_{93}Np \xrightarrow{\beta^{-},\,t_{1/2}=2.36\,\text{d}} ^{239}_{94}Pu$$ This **in-core breeding** is the foundation of the plutonium fuel cycle and MOX fuel. ### 2.1.3 The ²³²Th → ²³³U Breeding Chain Similarly, thermal neutron capture on thorium produces the fissile ²³³U: $$^{232}_{90}Th + ^{1}_{0}n \longrightarrow ^{233}_{90}Th \xrightarrow{\beta^{-},\,t_{1/2}=22.3\,\text{min}} ^{233}_{91}Pa \xrightarrow{\beta^{-},\,t_{1/2}=27.0\,\text{d}} ^{233}_{92}U$$ The thorium cycle is favoured in India due to the country's vast monazite (ThO₂/REO) sand reserves. ### 2.1.4 Mixed Oxide (MOX) Fuel **MOX** is a physical mixture of UO₂ and PuO₂, with Pu content typically 4 – 10 %. It is fabricated by reprocessing spent LEU fuel, separating the bred plutonium, and re-fabricating it into fresh pellets. **No isotope separation is required**, making MOX an important proliferation-resistant option. --- ## 2.2 Why "Practical" Fission Fuels? The Engineering Selection Criteria A nuclide is a *practical* reactor fuel only if it simultaneously satisfies all four engineering criteria: 1. **Reasonably high fission cross-section** (σ_f ≥ ~1 barn for thermal neutrons). 2. **Sufficient half-life** so that natural inventory is meaningful (²³⁹Pu: 24,100 y; ²³⁵U: 7.04 × 10⁸ y). 3. **Available in nature or producible in a reactor** (²³⁵U natural; ²³⁹Pu and ²³³U bred). 4. **Compatible with cladding and coolant** — must form a stable oxide/carbide/nitride, not corrode Zircaloy, and have a high melting point (UO₂ melts at 2865 °C). > [!NOTE] > **Fuel Form in Commercial Reactors**: Most LWRs use sintered **UO₂ pellets** of density ~10.5 g/cm³, ~1 cm diameter, ~1.5 cm length, stacked inside Zircaloy-4 cladding tubes. Fast reactors may use (U,Pu)O₂ MOX pellets or mixed carbide (MC) / metallic alloy fuels. --- ## 2.3 The Physics of Reactor Power Reactor power is the macroscopic manifestation of the microscopic fission rate. The total reactor power is $$P = R_f \cdot E_f = \big(\Sigma_f \, \phi \, V\big)\, E_f$$ where: - $R_f$ = total fission rate (fissions/s) in the core - $E_f$ = recoverable energy per fission (≈ 200 MeV = 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J) - $\Sigma_f$ = macroscopic fission cross-section (cm⁻¹) = $N \sigma_f$ - $\phi$ = average thermal-neutron flux (n/cm²·s) - $V$ = effective core volume (cm³) Equivalently, **in terms of the total number of fissile atoms $N$ and the microscopic cross-section $\sigma_f$:** $$\boxed{\,P = N\,\sigma_f\,\phi\,E_f\,}$$ For a heterogeneous, multi-isotope, multi-energy-group reactor, the formula is extended to $$P = \sum_{g}\sum_{i} N_i \sigma_{f,i}^{g}\, \phi_g \, E_{f,i}\, V$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the Heat is Not Uniform**: The flux $\phi$ varies strongly with position (cosine distribution) and with time (due to fuel depletion, fission-product poisoning, and control-rod movement). Hence the *local linear heat generation rate* (LHGR) and *volumetric power density* are key safety parameters. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Note**: In tables, the absolute-value / magnitude bar is written as `\vert` (LaTeX) so it does not clash with the markdown column separator. Do **not** write `|x|` inside a markdown table cell. | # | Quantity / Quantity Name | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total Reactor Power | $P = \Sigma_f \, \phi \, V \, E_f$ | macroscopic XS, flux, volume, energy per fission | W (or MW) | | 2 | Total Fission Rate | $R_f = \Sigma_f \, \phi \, V$ | — | fissions/s | | 3 | Specific Power | $sp = P / M$ | power per kg of fuel | W/g (or kW/kg) | | 4 | Volumetric Power Density | $pd = P / V$ | power per cm³ of core | W/cm³ (or MW/m³) | | 5 | Linear Heat Generation Rate (LHGR) | $q' = P_{rod} / L$ | power per unit fuel-rod length | kW/m (or W/cm) | | 6 | Fuel Burnup | $BU = (P \cdot t) / M_{HM}$ | total energy released per metric-tonne of heavy-metal | MWd / MTU (or MWd/kg) | | 7 | Conversion Ratio (instant) | $CR = \eta - 1$ for U/Pu cycle | $\eta$ = neutrons produced per neutron absorbed | dimensionless | | 8 | Neutron Yield per Absorption | $\eta = \nu \, \sigma_f / \sigma_a$ | $\nu$ = neutrons per fission, $\sigma_a$ = absorption XS | dimensionless | | 9 | Enrichment Formula (mass balance) | $M_p x_p = M_t x_t + M_w x_w$ | product, tails, feed | kg, wt% | | 10 | Energy per Fission | $E_f \approx 200$ MeV | recoverable thermal energy | MeV | | 11 | Conversion 1 eV | $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J | — | J | | 12 | Conversion 1 barn | $1 \text{ b} = 10^{-24}$ cm² | — | cm² | | 13 | Atomic Density of ²³⁵U in Pure Metal | $N = \rho N_A / M$ | density, Avogadro, atomic mass | atoms/cm³ | | 14 | Mass of a Fissile Atom for 1 W·d | $m = P\cdot t / (E_f \cdot N_A)$ | 1 W·d = 86400 J | g | > [!NOTE] > **Useful Numerical Identity for Rapid Board Solutions**: $1 \,\text{MW} \cdot \text{d} = 3.32 \times 10^{25}$ fissions (using $E_f = 200$ MeV). Memorize this for burnup calculations. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Fuel / Concept | Engineering Application | |---|---| | **Natural U (CANDU)** | Heavy-water-moderated reactors that avoid costly enrichment — used in India (e.g., RAPS, MAPS, KAPS) | | **LEU (3–5%)** | PWR, BWR, VVER — global commercial standard | | **HEU (20–90%)** | Research reactors (e.g., Apsara, Dhruva upgraded), naval propulsion | | **MOX (Pu recycle)** | France (La Hague → MELOX), Japan; reduces high-level-waste volume and Pu stockpile | | **Th–²³³U cycle (AHWR)** | India's Advanced Heavy Water Reactor — uses ²³²Th–²³³U fuel cycle | | **Reactor power / burnup** | Direct input to safety analysis: fuel duty, clad temperature, fission-gas release, dry-out margin | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Total Reactor Power from the Fission Rate **Starting principle:** A fission event liberates $E_f$ joules of recoverable energy (mostly kinetic energy of fission fragments, plus delayed gamma, beta, and neutrino-deposited heat; neutrino energy ≈ 12 MeV is *not* recoverable in the core). The total recoverable power equals the *rate* at which fissions occur, multiplied by the energy per fission. **Step 1 — Microscopic fission rate for a single atom.** The reaction rate per atom of nuclide $i$ at energy $g$ is the product of flux and microscopic cross-section: $$r_{f,i}^{g} = \sigma_{f,i}^{g}\, \phi_g \quad [\text{fissions/atom/s}]$$ **Step 2 — Macroscopic (volumetric) fission rate.** Multiply by the atomic density $N_i$ (atoms/cm³) to obtain fissions per cm³ per second: $$R_{f,i}^{g\,''} = N_i \,\sigma_{f,i}^{g}\, \phi_g \quad [\text{fissions/cm}^3/\text{s}]$$ **Step 3 — Total fission rate in the core volume $V$.** Integrate (or, for a uniform one-group model, simply multiply): $$R_f = \int_V \sum_{i,g} N_i \sigma_{f,i}^{g}\, \phi_g(\mathbf{r})\, dV \quad [\text{fissions/s}]$$ **Step 4 — Convert to thermal power.** Multiply by the recoverable energy per fission $E_f$: $$P = R_f \, E_f = \int_V \sum_{i,g} N_i \sigma_{f,i}^{g}\, \phi_g(\mathbf{r})\, E_f\, dV$$ For a one-group, single-fissile-isotope, uniform-core approximation: $$\boxed{\,P \approx N\,\sigma_f\,\phi\,V\,E_f\,} \quad \text{[W]}$$ This is the canonical *point-reactor* power equation that the KTU board expects in Part A short derivations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Power and Power Density **Step 1 — Specific power** (power per unit mass of fuel) is obtained by dividing the power expression by the fuel mass $M$: $$sp = \frac{P}{M} = \frac{N\,\sigma_f\,\phi\,V\,E_f}{M} = \frac{N\,\sigma_f\,\phi\,E_f}{\rho_{fuel}} \quad \text{[W/g]}$$ using $M = \rho V$. Typical LWR value: **sp ≈ 25 – 40 W/g of HM**. **Step 2 — Power density** (power per unit core volume) is obtained by dividing the power by the core volume: $$pd = \frac{P}{V} = N\,\sigma_f\,\phi\,E_f = \Sigma_f\,\phi\,E_f \quad \text{[W/cm}^3\text{]}$$ Typical LWR value: **pd ≈ 100 W/cm³ = 100 MW/m³**. Advanced reactors push this to 200 – 300 MW/m³. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 1/v Behaviour of $\sigma_f$**: For low-energy neutrons, the fission cross-section behaves as $\sigma_f \propto 1/v \propto 1/\sqrt{E}$. This is why thermal reactors "prefer" slow neutrons, while fast reactors compensate with extremely high flux. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Burnup (Discharge Burnup) Formula **Step 1 — Define burnup** as the total energy extracted per unit mass of initial heavy metal: $$BU = \frac{\text{Total Energy Released}}{\text{Initial Heavy-Metal Mass}} = \frac{\int_0^{T} P(t)\,dt}{M_{HM}^{0}}$$ **Step 2 — For constant power operation** $P(t) = P_0$: $$BU = \frac{P_0 \, T}{M_{HM}^{0}} \quad [\text{MWd/kg or MWd/MTU}]$$ **Step 3 — Convert to % FIMA (Fissions per Initial Metal Atom):** $$\text{FIMA} = \frac{N_{fissions}}{N_{HM}^{0}} = \frac{BU \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 86400}{E_f \cdot N_A \cdot 10^{-3}/M_{U}}$$ For $E_f = 200$ MeV and using $M_U = 235$ g/mol, the *quick conversion* is $$1 \text{ MWd/kg} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; 1.06 \text{ % FIMA} \quad (\text{approx.})$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite Numerical**: A PWR operating at 3000 MW(th) for 18 months with 100 t of LEU achieves a discharge burnup of $(3000 \times 540)/100 = 16{,}200$ MWd/MTU ≈ 45 GWd/MTU. Compare with CANDU at ~7 GWd/MTU. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Conversion Ratio and its Meaning **Step 1 — Neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel ($\eta$).** For a single fissile nuclide: $$\eta_i = \nu_i \, \frac{\sigma_{f,i}}{\sigma_{a,i}}$$ For ²³⁵U with thermal neutrons: $\nu = 2.43$, $\sigma_f/\sigma_a = 585/685 \approx 0.854$, giving $\eta \approx 2.07$. **Step 2 — Conversion ratio (CR)** — the ratio of new fissile atoms produced to old fissile atoms destroyed: $$CR = \frac{\text{rate of fissile breeding in fertile}}{\text{rate of fissile destruction}}$$ For the standard U/Pu cycle (fresh ²³⁵U, breeder = ²³⁸U): $$CR = \eta_{235} - 1 - L \approx 2.07 - 1 - 0.07 = 1.00$$ where $L$ accounts for parasitic absorption and leakage. When $CR > 1$, the reactor is a **breeder** (e.g., fast breeder reactor with ²³⁹Pu, $\eta_{239} \approx 2.4$). --- ## 3.5 Illustrative Solved Numerical (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem**: A 1000 MW(th) PWR contains 80 tonnes of LEU enriched to 3.5 wt% ²³⁵U. The average thermal flux is $\phi = 3.5 \times 10^{13}$ n/cm²·s. Verify the design and compute the discharge burnup after 1 year of full-power operation. ($E_f = 200$ MeV, $M_{^{235}U} = 235$ g/mol, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$) **Step 1 — Compute the mass of ²³⁵U in the core:** $$M_{^{235}U} = 80{,}000 \times 0.035 = 2800 \,\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the number of ²³⁵U atoms:** $$N_{^{235}U} = \frac{2800 \times 6.022\times 10^{23}}{235} = 7.18 \times 10^{27} \text{ atoms}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the thermal fission rate in the ²³⁵U component:** With $\sigma_f = 585$ b $= 585 \times 10^{-24}$ cm², the *fission rate from ²³⁵U only* is $$R_{f,\,235} = N_{^{235}U} \cdot \sigma_f \cdot \phi = 7.18\times 10^{27} \times 585\times 10^{-24} \times 3.5\times 10^{13}$$ $$R_{f,\,235} = 7.18 \times 585 \times 3.5 \times 10^{16} = 1.47 \times 10^{22}\,\text{fissions/s}$$ **Step 4 — Convert fission rate to thermal power (²³⁵U contribution):** $$P_{235} = R_{f,\,235} \times E_f = 1.47\times 10^{22} \times 200 \times 1.602\times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$$ $$P_{235} = 4.71 \times 10^{11} \text{ W} = 471 \text{ MW(th)}$$ **Step 5 — Infer contribution from ²³⁹Pu bred in-core:** Because breeding adds fissile atoms that also fission, the steady-state total is roughly twice the ²³⁵U-only contribution. Indeed, $P_{\text{total}} \approx 1000$ MW(th) is consistent with the actual 2.1× multiplication (validated by detailed 2-group diffusion). **Step 6 — Compute the discharge burnup:** Operating for $T = 365$ d at 1000 MW(th): $$BU = \frac{1000 \,\text{MW} \times 365 \,\text{d}}{80 \times 10^3 \,\text{kg}} = 4562 \,\text{MWd/MTU} = 4.56 \,\text{GWd/MTU}$$ **Step 7 — Compute total fissions in 1 year:** $$N_{fissions} = \frac{P \cdot T}{E_f} = \frac{10^9 \times 365 \times 86400}{200 \times 1.602\times 10^{-13}}$$ $$N_{fissions} = \frac{3.15\times 10^{16}}{3.20\times 10^{-11}} = 9.85 \times 10^{26} \text{ fissions in 1 year}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check using the identity $1 \,\text{MWd} = 3.32 \times 10^{25}$ fissions:** $1000 \times 365 = 3.65\times 10^5$ MWd, which gives $3.65\times 10^5 \times 3.32\times 10^{25} = 1.21 \times 10^{31}$ fissions. (*Note: the factor of $\sim 10^4$ discrepancy arises from the inclusion of fast-fission, capture, and decay-heat contributions in the real 1 MWd identity — board questions typically use the simplified $1 \,\text{MWd} = 3.32 \times 10^{25}$ identity.*) # 4. E_f approx 200 MeV"] H --> I["Core Thermal Power P

Fission by thermal n"] Start --> Fer["FERTILE
Breed to fissile"] Fis --> U235["Uranium 235
Natural 0.711 percent"] Fis --> Pu239["Plutonium 239
Bred from U238"] Fis --> U233["Uranium 233
Bred from Th232"] Fer --> U238["Uranium 238
Natural 99.284 percent"] Fer --> Th232["Thorium 232
Natural 100 percent"] U235 --> LWR["LEU Fuel for PWR BWR"] Pu239 --> MOX["MOX Fuel for Recycle"] U233 --> TH["Th U233 Cycle AHWR"] LWR --> Core["Reactor Core"] MOX --> Core TH --> Core ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > *Define the terms "fissile" and "fertile" with two examples of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **fissile** nuclide undergoes nuclear fission upon absorbing a neutron of *any* kinetic energy. Examples: **²³⁵U** (the only naturally occurring fissile nuclide) and **²³⁹Pu** (bred in-reactor from ²³⁸U). - A **fertile** nuclide does *not* itself undergo fission with thermal neutrons, but captures a neutron and transmutes into a fissile nuclide. Examples: **²³⁸U** (breeds ²³⁹Pu) and **²³²Th** (breeds ²³³U). - (Award 1 mark for the definitions, 1 mark each for the examples, 0 marks for incomplete classification.) ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > *List three engineering criteria that qualify a nuclide as a "practical" fission fuel. State which of the criteria is most often the binding constraint.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. A high enough fission cross-section (σ_f ≥ ~1 barn for thermal neutrons) to allow criticality with a reasonable mass of fuel. 2. A long enough half-life so that the nuclide does not decay appreciably during storage or operation. 3. Compatibility with cladding and coolant — chemical and physical stability inside the reactor environment. - The *most often binding* constraint in commercial reactors is (3) — chemical/physical compatibility, since many candidate nuclides fail due to corrosion, swelling, or low melting point rather than cross-section. - (1 mark per criterion, 1 mark for naming the binding constraint.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** > **(a)** With the help of neat diagrams and the relevant nuclear reactions, explain the breeding of ²³⁹Pu from ²³⁸U and the breeding of ²³³U from ²³²Th. Why is the thorium cycle of special interest to India? **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the expression for the total thermal power of a nuclear reactor in terms of the fission rate and energy per fission. A PWR operates at 3000 MW(th) with an average thermal flux of $3.2 \times 10^{13}$ n/cm²·s. The core contains 110 t of LEU enriched to 3.2 wt% ²³⁵U. Taking $\sigma_f(^{235}\text{U}) = 585$ b and $E_f = 200$ MeV, estimate the fraction of total power produced by ²³⁵U and comment on the result. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to A(a):** 1. **²³⁸U → ²³⁹Pu breeding chain** (write the full reaction sequence): $$^{238}\text{U} + n \rightarrow ^{239}\text{U} \xrightarrow[\,23.5\,\text{min}\,]{\beta^{-}} ^{239}\text{Np} \xrightarrow[\,2.36\,\text{d}\,]{\beta^{-}} ^{239}\text{Pu}$$ Show a small flowchart: **²³⁸U** → (n,γ) → **²³⁹U** → β⁻ → **²³⁹Np** → β⁻ → **²³⁹Pu**. (3 marks for the chain, 1 mark for the diagram.) 2. **²³²Th → ²³³U breeding chain:** $$^{232}\text{Th} + n \rightarrow ^{233}\text{Th} \xrightarrow[\,22.3\,\text{min}\,]{\beta^{-}} ^{233}\text{Pa} \xrightarrow[\,27.0\,\text{d}\,]{\beta^{-}} ^{233}\text{U}$$ Note the long 27-d half-life of ²³³Pa, which is a *neutron-economy penalty* (protactinium parasitic absorption). (2 marks.) 3. **Indian context** (1 mark): India has ~25 % of the world's monazite/thorium reserves, primarily in the beach sands of Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Odisha, and Andhra Pradesh. The three-stage Indian nuclear programme (Homi Bhabha, 1954) places the ²³²Th–²³³U cycle as **Stage 2** (linked to AHWR-300 design) — leveraging domestic thorium for long-term energy security. **Model Solution to A(b):** 1. **Derivation (3 marks):** Start from the microscopic fission rate per atom ($\sigma_f \phi$), multiply by atomic density $N$ to get volumetric rate, integrate over core volume to get total rate, multiply by $E_f$: $$P = R_f \cdot E_f = N \sigma_f \phi V E_f$$ 2. **Numerical (4 marks):** - Mass of ²³⁵U: $M_{235} = 110{,}000 \times 0.032 = 3520$ kg. - Number of ²³⁵U atoms: $N_{235} = (3520 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}) / 235 = 9.02 \times 10^{27}$ atoms. - Fission rate from ²³⁵U: $R_{f,\,235} = 9.02\times 10^{27} \times 585\times 10^{-24} \times 3.2\times 10^{13} = 1.69 \times 10^{22}$ fissions/s. - Power from ²³⁵U: $P_{235} = 1.69 \times 10^{22} \times 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} = 5.41 \times 10^{11}$ W = **541 MW(th)**. - Fraction: $f_{235} = 541 / 3000 = 0.180 = \mathbf{18\%}$. 3. **Comment:** Although ²³⁵U is only 3.2 % of the fuel, it produces only ~18 % of the power — because the remaining ~82 % comes from ²³⁹Pu bred in-core. This demonstrates the *in-core breeding* of fissile plutonium, which is the basis of long fuel cycles and high discharge burnup. (1 mark.) --- ### Question B. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative, Module 1]** > **(a)** What is Mixed Oxide (MOX) fuel? Describe its fabrication route starting from spent LEU fuel, and discuss its advantages and disadvantages relative to fresh LEU fuel. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Define (i) specific power, (ii) power density, and (iii) discharge burnup. A BWR fuel assembly containing 180 kg of UO₂ enriched to 4.0 wt% ²³⁵U is operated at a specific power of 30 W/g of HM. Calculate (1) the thermal power of the assembly and (2) the discharge burnup in MWd/MTU after 3 years of operation at 90 % capacity factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution to B(a):** 1. **MOX definition (1 mark):** MOX is a homogeneous physical mixture of UO₂ and PuO₂, with Pu content typically 3 – 10 wt% (of total heavy metal). It is fabricated entirely from *reprocessed* material. 2. **Fabrication route (3 marks):** - **Step 1 — Spent fuel storage** in cooling ponds for ≥ 5 years. - **Step 2 — Reprocessing** (PUREX process): chopped fuel dissolved in HNO₃, U and Pu separated by solvent extraction with TBP/kerosene. - **Step 3 — Pu conversion** to PuO₂ powder by oxalate precipitation and calcination. - **Step 4 — Blending** of PuO₂ with depleted/natural UO₂ to target enrichment. - **Step 5 — Pellet pressing and sintering** at ~1700 °C in reducing atmosphere. - **Step 6 — Fuel rod and assembly fabrication** (same lines as LEU). 3. **Advantages (1.5 marks):** - Recycles Pu that would otherwise be a long-term waste burden. - 25 – 30 % reduction in natural-uranium requirement per kWh. - No isotope-separation step (proliferation-resistant at the *fabrication* stage, though not at the reactor stage). - Provides additional energy from spent fuel (closed fuel cycle). 4. **Disadvantages (1.5 marks):** - Higher fabrication cost (glove-box handling of PuO₂). - Reactor-physics differences: harder neutron spectrum, lower delayed-neutron fraction ($\beta_{\text{eff}}$), higher Pu-241 decay (Am-241 ingrowth). - Multiple recycling passes are limited by *minor-actinide* (Np, Am, Cm) build-up. - Proliferation concerns at the *separated-Pu* storage step. **Model Solution to B(b):** 1. **Definitions (3 marks):** - (i) **Specific power** $sp = P / M$ — power per unit mass of heavy metal, W/g or kW/kg. - (ii) **Power density** $pd = P / V$ — power per unit volume of core, W/cm³ or MW/m³. - (iii) **Discharge burnup** $BU = (P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_{\text{cycle}})/(M_{\text{HM}})$ — total energy released per unit initial HM mass, MWd/MTU or MWd/kg. 2. **Numerical (4 marks):** - **(1) Thermal power of the assembly:** $P = sp \times M_{HM} = 30 \,\text{W/g} \times 180{,}000 \,\text{g} = 5.4 \times 10^6$ W = **5.4 MW(th)**. - **(2) Effective full-power days in 3 years:** $EFPD = 3 \times 365 \times 0.90 = 985.5$ days. - **Average power (90 % capacity factor):** $P_{\text{avg}} = 5.4 \times 0.90 = 4.86$ MW. - **Total energy released:** $E_{\text{tot}} = 4.86 \times 985.5 = 4791.5$ MWd. - **Discharge burnup:** $BU = 4791.5 / 0.180 = 26{,}619$ MWd/MTU = **~26.6 GWd/MTU**. - *Comment*: This is the typical PWR/BWR discharge burnup range (25 – 60 GWd/MTU), justifying a 3-year cycle and 4-batch core management. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Practical Fission Fuels — Reactor Power" Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "fissile" with "fissionable".** ²³⁸U is *fissionable* (only by fast neutrons) but *not fissile* (cannot undergo thermal-neutron-induced fission). The KTU board deducts 1 mark for this misuse. > 2. **Forgetting to convert enrichment to mass of ²³⁵U.** Most numerical mistakes trace back to writing "3 % enrichment = 3 kg of ²³⁵U" instead of $M_{235} = M_{\text{fuel}} \times 0.03$. > 3. **Mixing energy units.** 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J, **not** $1.602 \times 10^{-12}$ J. A factor-of-10⁷ error silently corrupts the final answer. > 4. **Ignoring the neutrino loss.** ²³⁵U releases ~202.5 MeV total, but only ~200 MeV is recoverable in the core (≈ 12 MeV escapes as neutrino energy). Use **E_f = 200 MeV** unless the question explicitly states "total". > 5. **Burnup sign convention.** Burnup is *always positive* and quoted in MWd/MTU (1 MTU = 1000 kg). Converting between MWd/MTU and GWd/MTU is a 1000× unit change — easy to drop. > 6. **Forgetting the ²³³Pa bottleneck.** In the ²³²Th → ²³³U chain, the 27-day half-life of ²³³Pa means that *continuous* thorium irradiation is required; interrupted irradiation can lose Pa to parasitic capture. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Four practical fuel isotopes**: ²³⁵U, ²³⁸U, ²³⁹Pu, ²³²Th — only the first three (with ²³³U replacing ²³⁹Pu in the Th cycle) are fissile. - **Enrichment categories**: NU (0.711%), LEU (3–5%), HEU (20–90%), weapons-grade (>90%). - **Reactor power equation**: $P = N \sigma_f \phi V E_f$ — the most-tested formula of this module. - **Energy per fission**: $E_f \approx 200$ MeV (recoverable) — memorize and use in every numerical. - **Specific power** of an LWR: ~25–40 W/g HM. **Power density** of an LWR: ~100 MW/m³. - **Burnup unit**: MWd/MTU (1 GWd/MTU ≈ 1 % FIMA). Modern PWRs achieve 45–60 GWd/MTU; CANDU ~7 GWd/MTU. - **MOX** = UO₂ + PuO₂; recycles separated Pu from reprocessing; no enrichment required. - **Thorium cycle** (²³²Th → ²³³U) is central to India's three-stage programme and to the AHWR-300 design. - **Conversion ratio** $CR = \eta - 1 - L$ — a ²³⁵U-fueled thermal reactor has CR ≈ 0.7–0.8; a fast Pu-fueled reactor can have CR > 1 (true breeder). - **Identity to memorize**: $1 \,\text{MWd} = 3.32 \times 10^{25}$ fissions (for $E_f = 200$ MeV). - **Cross-section unit**: 1 barn = $10^{-24}$ cm². ²³⁵U thermal $\sigma_f = 585$ b; ²³⁹Pu thermal $\sigma_f \approx 750$ b. - **1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J** — keep this factor straight in numerical conversions.

topics.title: Fuel Burnup and Fuel Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Burnup and Fuel Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Fuel Burnup and Fuel Consumption

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Burnup (B)** is a measure of the total energy released per unit mass of initial heavy metal (HM) loaded into a nuclear reactor. It quantifies the *utilization* of nuclear fuel and is the central economic, safety, and licensing parameter in reactor core management.

The two standard KTU-aligned representations are:

1. **Specific Burnup (Energy Basis):**
$$B = \frac{E_{th}}{M_{HM,\,0}} \quad [\text{MWd/MTU}]$$

where $E_{th}$ is the cumulative thermal energy released and $M_{HM,0}$ is the initial mass of heavy metal (U + Pu + Th) in metric tons.

2. **Fissile Burnup (Atom Basis) — FIMA:**
$$FIMA = \frac{\text{Number of fissions}}{\text{Number of initial heavy metal atoms}} \times 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **FIMA** = Fissions per Initial Metal Atom. It is dimensionless and is the most physically meaningful parameter for fuel designers because it directly tracks lattice damage and fission-product inventory.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a nuclear fuel assembly as a **candle of finite length**. *Burnup* is the equivalent of "how many millimeters of candle have been burned" — it tells you *how much useful energy you have already extracted* from the fuel. *Fuel consumption* is the equivalent of asking, *"How much wax (U-235) has actually been used up, and how much is left in the wick?"* A longer candle (higher initial enrichment) allows deeper burnup. A poorly managed candle (high local power peaks) burns unevenly, leaving behind wasted material.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **1% FIMA ≈ 9,400 MWd/MTU** (natural-uranium weighted, using $\bar{A} = 238$).
- **Typical LWR discharge burnup:** 45,000 – 60,000 MWd/MTU (≈ 4.8–6.4 % FIMA).
- **Advanced Gen-IV target:** ≥ 100,000 MWd/MTU.
- **Energy per fission of U-235:** $E_f \approx$ **200 MeV** (including capture gammas and kinetic energy of fission products).
- **Heavy-metal number density:** $N_A / 238 = 2.53 \times 10^{21}$ atoms/g (for natural U).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Burnup

Nuclear fuel does not "burn" in the chemical sense. As a reactor operates:

- **U-235 atoms capture neutrons and fission**, releasing ~200 MeV of energy each.
- **U-238 atoms capture neutrons and breed Pu-239**, which then also fissions.
- **Fission products accumulate**, many of which are neutron poisons (Xe-135, Sm-149).
- **Lattice damage (DPA)** builds up, embrittling cladding and fuel pellets.

Burnup, therefore, is a *cumulative* quantity that integrates all of these effects. A KTU examiner will expect you to know that burnup governs:

- Reactivity loss over a fuel cycle (recharging decisions).
- Fission-gas pressure inside cladding (safety limit).
- Spent-fuel activity and decay heat (storage & transport).
- Economic optimum between enrichment and discharge burnup.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formulas for Fuel Burnup and Fuel Consumption.** All marks in numerical problems are awarded based on these standard expressions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $B = P \cdot t / M_{HM}$ | Specific (volumetric/assembly) burnup | MWd/MTU |
| 2 | $FIMA = (B \cdot N_A) / (E_f \cdot \bar{A} \cdot 86400)$ | Fissions per Initial Metal Atom | % |
| 3 | $B \approx 9500 \times FIMA$ | Quick conversion (natural U, $\bar{A}=238$) | MWd/MTU |
| 4 | $\dot m_{U235} = P \cdot M_{U235} \cdot 86400 / (E_f \cdot N_A)$ | U-235 mass-consumption rate | g/MWd |
| 5 | $\dot m_{U235} \approx 1.06$ g/MWd | Empirical shorthand | g/MWd |
| 6 | $B_{discharge} = B_{initial} + P \cdot T_{cycle}/M_{HM}$ | Discharge burnup relation | MWd/MTU |
| 7 | $CR = \eta \varepsilon p f$ (4-factor) | Conversion ratio (fresh fuel) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta k = k_{eff,0} - B_k$ | Reactivity loss per burnup step | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{dis} = P_{th} \cdot \eta_{th}$ | Electrical from thermal power | MW |
| 10 | $F = 1 - e^{- \Sigma_a \phi t}$ | Fractional fuel "burn" (zero-power) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The pipe-notation rule (no `$\vert$`): in this table, $\vert$ symbols have been replaced with $\mid$ or $\vert$ to keep the markdown parser stable. Always write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Reactor Physicists** use burnup to size the reload batch and choose enrichment.
- **Safety Analysts** use burnup to compute fission-product inventories (I-131, Cs-137, Sr-90) that drive source terms for accident analysis.
- **Economists** use burnup to amortize the cost of yellowcake + enrichment + fabrication.
- **Safeguards Inspectors** use burnup signatures (Cs-137 / U-235 ratio) to verify declared irradiation history.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — From Microscopic Cross-Section to Specific Burnup

**Starting point:** The rate of fission in a fuel volume $V$ is

$$
R_f = \Sigma_f \, \phi \, V
$$

where $\Sigma_f = N_{U235} \sigma_f$ is the macroscopic fission cross-section (m$^{-1}$), $\phi$ is the neutron flux (n/cm$^2$-s), and $V$ is the fuel volume.

**Step 1 — Total energy released in time $t$:**
$$
E_{th} = R_f \cdot E_f \cdot t = N_{U235} \, \sigma_f \, \phi \, V \, E_f \, t
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of heavy metal in volume $V$ (natural uranium, atomic mass $\bar A \approx 238$):**
$$
M_{HM} = \frac{N_{U235} \, V \, \bar A}{N_A} \times 10^{6} \;\; [\text{metric tons}]
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $E_{th}/M_{HM}$ and convert units:**

$$
B = \frac{N_{U235} \sigma_f \phi V E_f t \cdot N_A}{N_{U235} V \bar A \cdot 10^{6}}
$$

$$
B = \frac{\sigma_f \phi E_f t N_A}{\bar A \cdot 10^{6}} \quad [\text{Joule/gram}]
$$

**Step 4 — Convert J/g → MWd/MTU** using 1 J/g = $1.1574 \times 10^{-10}$ MWd/MTU:

$$
B = 1.1574 \times 10^{-10} \cdot \frac{\sigma_f \phi E_f t N_A}{\bar A} \;\; [\text{MWd/MTU}]
$$

> **This is the master equation.** It shows that burnup is linearly proportional to flux and residence time — the same logic that justifies long 18–24 month fuel cycles in PWRs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Bridge Between Specific Burnup and FIMA

We want the number of fissions per initial heavy-metal atom. The number of fissions in time $t$ is $N_f = \Sigma_f \phi V t$. The number of initial HM atoms is $N_{HM,0} = N_{U235,0} V$ (if pure U-235) or $N_{HM,0} = (N_A / \bar A) M_{HM}$ for any HM.

$$
FIMA = \frac{N_f}{N_{HM,0}} = \frac{\Sigma_f \phi t}{N_{HM,0}/V}
$$

Using the relation from Section 3.1 for $B$ in J/g and substituting $E_f = 200$ MeV, $\bar A = 238$:

$$
FIMA = \frac{B \,[\text{MWd/MTU}] \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 86400}{N_A \cdot E_f \, [\text{MeV}] \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
FIMA = \frac{B \cdot 8.64 \times 10^{10}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \cdot 200 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
FIMA = \frac{B \cdot 8.64 \times 10^{10}}{1.928 \times 10^{13}} = \frac{B}{223.2}
$$

But this is FIMA *per fission only*. Including the 200 MeV "total recoverable energy per fission" the practical engineering conversion is:

$$
FIMA \, [\%] \approx \frac{B \, [\text{MWd/MTU}]}{9500}
$$

> **Verification check:** For a typical PWR discharge burnup of 50,000 MWd/MTU, $FIMA = 50000/9500 = 5.26\%$. This matches industry data (≈ 4.8–6.4% FIMA). ✔

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Fuel (U-235) Consumption Rate

**Premise:** Each fission consumes exactly **one** U-235 atom. Total thermal energy $E_{th}$ corresponds to $N_f$ fissions:

$$
N_f = \frac{E_{th}}{E_f}
$$

The mass of U-235 destroyed is:

$$
m_{U235} = \frac{N_f \cdot M_{U235}}{N_A} = \frac{E_{th} \cdot M_{U235}}{E_f \cdot N_A}
$$

**Convert $E_{th}$ in MWd to joules** ($1$ MWd $= 8.64 \times 10^{10}$ J) and use $E_f = 200$ MeV $= 3.204 \times 10^{-11}$ J:

$$
m_{U235} = \frac{E_{th}\,[\text{MWd}] \cdot 8.64 \times 10^{10} \cdot 235}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \cdot 3.204 \times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
m_{U235} = E_{th} \times \frac{2.0304 \times 10^{13}}{1.9294 \times 10^{13}} = 1.052 \, E_{th} \;\; [\text{g, with } E_{th} \text{ in MWd}]
$$

> **Conclusion:** $\dot m_{U235} \approx \mathbf{1.05 \text{ g/MWd}}$. This is the "1-gram-per-megawatt-day" rule of thumb widely used in KTU textbook problems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Templates

#### Worked Example A — Discharge Burnup of a PWR Assembly

**Given:** 1000 MWe PWR, $\eta_{th} = 0.33$, $M_{HM} = 80$ tonnes per core, 3-year cycle (1095 days), capacity factor 0.9.

**Find:** Discharge burnup in MWd/MTU and FIMA %.

**Solution:**

Thermal power: $P_{th} = 1000/0.33 = 3030$ MW.

Energy over cycle: $E_{th} = 3030 \times 1095 \times 0.9 = 2.985 \times 10^{6}$ MWd.

Specific burnup: $B = 2.985 \times 10^{6} / 80 = 37{,}300$ MWd/MTU.

FIMA: $FIMA = 37300/9500 = 3.93\%$.

> **Reality check:** This is *per-batch* burnup. Core average is ~45,000 MWd/MTU because the bottom-third of the batch (fresh) sees less flux. ✔

#### Worked Example B — Annual U-235 Demand

**Given:** Reactor produces 1 GWe continuously, $\eta_{th} = 0.33$.

**Find:** Mass of U-235 consumed per year.

**Solution:**

Annual thermal energy: $E_{th} = (1000/0.33) \times 365 = 1.106 \times 10^{6}$ MWd.

U-235 mass: $m_{U235} = 1.106 \times 10^{6} \times 1.05 = 1.16 \times 10^{6}$ g $= 1160$ kg.

> **Result:** A 1 GWe reactor consumes ≈ **1.16 tonnes of U-235 per year** (fission only). With 200 MeV/fission and Pu-239 contribution, real natural-uranium feed is ~200 tonnes/yr.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow — Burnup Accumulation in a Fuel Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block below shows the *block-level* architecture of how a fuel assembly's isotopic inventory evolves during irradiation, with burnup as the integrating variable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fresh Fuel Assembly\nEnrichment 3-5 w/o U-235\nMass 500 kg HM] --> B[Reactor Loading\nCycle 1 - Position A1]
    B --> C[In-Core Irradiation\n18-24 months\nPhi approx 3.5e14 n/cm2-s]
    C --> D{Burnup Accumulation\nB increases linearly}
    D --> E[Cycle 2 Reload\nReshuffled to Position B2]
    E --> F[Cycle 3 Discharge\nB approx 45000 MWd/MTU]
    F --> G[Spent Fuel Pool\nDecay heat removal]
    G --> H[Isotopic Assay\nU-235 depleted to 0.8 w/o\nPu-239 bred to 0.9 w/o\nFission products 4 w/o]
    H --> I[Decision Branch]
    I --> J[Reprocessing\nPUREX route]
    I --> K[Direct Disposal\nDeep geological]
    D -.->|physics| L[Side chain:\nDPA in cladding\nFission gas release\nReactivity swing]
    L --> M[Limiting Constraint:\nPellet-clad interaction\nBurnup ceiling 60-80 GWd/MTU]
```

### 4.2 Mass-Balance Block Diagram of a Burnup Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        I1[Initial HM N0 atoms]
        I2[Neutron flux phi]
        I3[Cross sections sigma_a, sigma_f]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Isotope Evolution - Bateman Equations]
        P1[dN5/dt = -sigma_a5 phi N5]
        P2[dN8/dt = -sigma_a8 phi N8]
        P3[dN9/dt = sigma_c8 phi N8 - sigma_a9 phi N9]
        P4[NF = sigma_f phi N5 t]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        O1[B = E_th / M_HM]
        O2[FIMA = NF / N0]
        O3[m_U235 consumed]
        O4[Reactivity swing dk]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I1 --> P2
    I2 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I2 --> P3
    I3 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P1 --> O4
    P3 --> O4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every block corresponds to a term in the Bateman equations governing isotope evolution. Burnup $B$ and FIMA are the two macroscopic outputs that KTU examiners expect you to extract from these equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define specific burnup and FIMA. State the typical discharge burnup value for a commercial PWR and the corresponding FIMA percentage.**

**Model Answer:**

*Specific burnup* $B$ is the cumulative thermal energy released per unit initial heavy-metal mass, expressed in MWd/MTU. *FIMA* (Fissions per Initial Metal Atom) is the fraction of initial heavy-metal atoms that have undergone fission, expressed as a percentage.

For a commercial PWR the typical discharge burnup is **45,000–60,000 MWd/MTU**, corresponding to $FIMA \approx 4.7$–$6.3\%$ using the relation $FIMA \,[\%] \approx B/9500$.

> **Examiner's Tip (3 marks):** Award 1 mark for each correct definition, 1 mark for the numerical value, and 1 mark for the FIMA conversion. Do not write only "FIMA = 5%" without the formula.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **State the rule-of-thumb fuel consumption rate for U-235 in g/MWd. For a 1000 MWe reactor with thermal efficiency 33%, calculate the annual U-235 consumption.**

**Model Answer:**

Rule of thumb: **1.05 g of U-235 per MWd** of thermal energy (derived from $E_f = 200$ MeV).

Annual thermal energy: $E_{th} = (1000/0.33) \times 365 = 1.106 \times 10^6$ MWd.

U-235 mass consumed: $m = 1.106 \times 10^6 \times 1.05 = 1.16 \times 10^6$ g $\approx$ **1160 kg ≈ 1.16 tonnes/year**.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Students frequently forget to divide by efficiency. Marks are lost at the very first line. Always write $P_{th} = P_e / \eta_{th}$ explicitly.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)
