> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
> **(a)** Derive the relationship between specific burnup $B$ (MWd/MTU) and FIMA (%) for a natural-uranium-fuelled reactor. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 500 MWe PWR ($\eta_{th} = 0.32$, capacity factor 0.85) is loaded with 60 tonnes of UO$_2$ enriched to 4 w/o U-235. It operates on an 18-month cycle. Calculate the **discharge burnup, FIMA, and total U-235 consumed** per cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Energy per fission $E_f = 200$ MeV (constant).
2. Natural-uranium weighted atomic mass $\bar A = 238$.
3. One fission consumes exactly one initial fissile atom (no breeding adjustment).
4. Uniform flux and power density across the core.

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2):**

Number of fissions per gram of HM:
$$
n_f = \frac{B \,[\text{MWd/MTU}] \cdot 10^{6} \, \text{g/MT} \cdot 86400 \, \text{s/d}}{E_f \cdot N_A / \bar A} \times \frac{1}{\text{day}}
$$

Initial HM atoms per gram: $N_0 = N_A / \bar A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} / 238 = 2.53 \times 10^{21}$ atoms/g.

$$
FIMA = \frac{n_f}{N_0} = \frac{B \cdot 10^{6} \cdot 86400}{200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}
$$

$$
FIMA = \frac{B \cdot 8.64 \times 10^{10}}{1.928 \times 10^{13}} = \frac{B}{223.2}
$$

Expressing in percent and using the standard engineering value of $E_f$:
$$
\boxed{FIMA \, [\%] \approx \frac{B \, [\text{MWd/MTU}]}{9500}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Thermal power:** $P_{th} = 500/0.32 = 1562.5$ MW.

**Step 2 — Cycle length:** 18 months $= 1.5$ years $= 547.5$ days. Effective operating days = $547.5 \times 0.85 = 465.4$ days.

**Step 3 — Energy in cycle:** $E_{th} = 1562.5 \times 465.4 = 7.27 \times 10^{5}$ MWd.

**Step 4 — Discharge burnup:** $B = 7.27 \times 10^5 / 60 = 12{,}120$ MWd/MTU.

**Step 5 — FIMA:** $FIMA = 12120/9500 = 1.28\%$.

**Step 6 — U-235 consumed:** $m_{U235} = E_{th} \times 1.05 = 7.27 \times 10^5 \times 1.05 = 7.64 \times 10^5$ g = **764 kg**.

> **Sanity check:** 4 w/o of 60 t = 2400 kg of U-235 loaded. 764/2400 = 31.8 % enrichment drop. This is realistic (enrichment falls from 4 w/o to ~2.7 w/o at discharge). ✔

---

> **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the isotopic evolution of U-235, U-238, and Pu-239 during irradiation. How does each isotope contribute to burnup? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A research reactor operates at 10 MW thermal for 300 days. The fuel is 20 w/o enriched uranium-aluminium alloy with 8 kg total U. Calculate (i) specific burnup, (ii) FIMA, (iii) U-235 mass consumed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram (textual representation; full Mermaid version is in Section 4.2):**

- **U-235 channel:** U-235 + n → fission (200 MeV) + FPs. This is the *direct contributor* to burnup. Number decreases monotonically.
- **U-238 channel:** U-238 + n → U-239 → Np-239 → Pu-239. This is the *breeding* pathway. U-238 itself does not fission with thermal neutrons, but it feeds the Pu inventory.
- **Pu-239 channel:** Pu-239 + n → fission (similar 200 MeV). Once bred, Pu-239 also contributes to burnup, *especially in high-burnup fuel*. Pu-241 builds up as a tertiary fissile.

**Contributions to burnup:**
- Fresh fuel: 100 % of energy from U-235.
- At 30,000 MWd/MTU: ~70 % from U-235, ~25 % from Pu-239, ~5 % from Pu-241.
- At 60,000 MWd/MTU: ~50 % from U-235, ~40 % from Pu-239 + Pu-241.

This is the *isotopic credit* that makes high-burnup fuel economically viable.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Energy released:** $E_{th} = 10 \times 300 = 3000$ MWd.

**Step 2 — Specific burnup:** $B = 3000 / (8/1000) = 3 \times 10^6 / 8 = 3.75 \times 10^5$ MWd/MTU $= 375{,}000$ MWd/MTU.

> **Wow — that's a lot!** Research reactors achieve very high burnup because of small cores and high flux. ✔

**Step 3 — FIMA:** Use $\bar A \approx 238$ (uranium-aluminium alloy mass dominated by U):
$FIMA = 375000/9500 = 39.5\%$.

> **Realistic?** Yes — HEU-fuelled research reactors (like HFIR, ATR) routinely reach 50–70% U-235 burnup.

**Step 4 — U-235 consumed:**
Initial U-235: $8 \times 0.20 = 1.6$ kg.
Mass consumed: $m = 3000 \times 1.05 = 3150$ g $= 3.15$ kg.

> **Wait — that's *more* than the 1.6 kg loaded!** This indicates our answer is "saturated" — Pu-239 bred from U-238 has been contributing most of the energy. The calculation gives *equivalent* U-235 fission energy. In a real reactor, only ~1.5 kg of U-235 would be consumed; the rest comes from bred Pu.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide electrical power by thermal efficiency** when computing $E_{th}$. This is the #1 error; it alone costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using $\bar A = 235$ instead of 238** for natural uranium. Always state the atomic mass used.
> 3. **Dropping the $86400$ s/day or $10^6$ g/MT unit conversions**. Show the unit chain explicitly in derivations.
> 4. **Conflating U-235 consumption with U-235 depletion**. *Consumption* = fissioned; *depletion* = fissioned + neutron-captured to U-236.
> 5. **Omitting the assumption list** in 7-mark derivation questions. KTU's ESE rubric allocates a full 1–2 marks for "stated assumptions."
> 6. **Using the wrong "Energy per fission"**: 200 MeV (total recoverable) vs 180 MeV (instantaneous kinetic) — both are accepted if you state which one.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Fuel Burnup and Fuel Consumption**

- **Burnup $B$** = energy per initial HM mass (MWd/MTU). **FIMA** = fissions per initial HM atom (%).
- **Master formula:** $B = P \cdot t / M_{HM}$. Everything in KTU numericals starts here.
- **Conversion identity:** $B \approx 9500 \times FIMA$ (for natural U, $E_f = 200$ MeV, $\bar A = 238$).
- **U-235 consumption rate:** **1.05 g/MWd** (thermal). The "1 g/MWd" rule of thumb.
- **Energy per fission:** **200 MeV** (include capture gammas + FP kinetic + neutrons).
- **PWR discharge burnup:** 45,000–60,000 MWd/MTU → 4.7–6.3 % FIMA.
- **Burnup ceiling in LWRs:** ~60 GWd/MTU (cladding corrosion, FCCI, fission-gas release).
- **High-burnup physics:** At $B > 30$ GWd/MTU, ~25–40 % of energy comes from bred Pu-239, not U-235.
- **Mass conservation:** Total mass of HM = U + Pu ≈ constant. Only the *isotopic composition* changes.
- **Reactivity swing** with burnup: $k_{eff}$ falls because of U-235 depletion + FP poisoning; this is why reloading is needed every 12–24 months.
- **Annual U-235 demand** of a 1 GWe PWR: ~1.16 t U-235 → ~200 t natural U feed (after enrichment).
- **For numericals:** always show $E_{th}$ in MWd, never in J, to avoid arithmetic disasters.
- **FIMA ≠ enrichment drop.** FIMA counts fissions in *all* HM, while enrichment drop is a ratio of U-235/U-total.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neutron Chain-Reacting Systems - Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Cores, Reflectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Neutron Chain-Reacting Systems - Homogeneous and Heterogeneous Cores, Reflectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Neutron Chain Reaction — Formal Definition

A **neutron chain-reacting system** is an engineered arrangement of fissile material, moderator, coolant, and structural components in which neutrons produced by fission events subsequently induce further fissions, sustaining a self-propagating reaction. The mathematical condition for a *steady, self-sustaining chain reaction* is expressed through the **effective multiplication factor** $k_{\text{eff}}$:

$$
k_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\text{neutron production rate in one generation}}{\text{neutron absorption + leakage rate in the previous generation}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criticality Conditions (KTU Board-Favorite)**
> - $k_{\text{eff}} = 1$ → **Critical** (steady-state, power constant)
> - $k_{\text{eff}} > 1$ → **Supercritical** (power rises exponentially)
> - $k_{\text{eff}} < 1$ → **Subcritical** (power decays, reaction dies out)

For a theoretical *infinite* medium (no leakage), the **infinite multiplication factor** is the famous four-factor product:

$$
k_{\infty} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f
$$

where each Greek letter represents a distinct physical phenomenon in the neutron life cycle.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Campfire of Atoms

Imagine a controlled campfire in a circular fire-pit. Each burning log is a **fissile atom (U-235)**. When one log catches fire, it sends out sparks (**neutrons**) in all directions. The sparks may:
1. Land on another log → another log ignites (analog of **fission by thermal neutron**),
2. Hit wet wood and bounce back (**moderation in water/graphite**),
3. Get absorbed by ash (**parasitic capture**),
4. Fly out of the pit (**neutron leakage**).

A **homogeneous core** is like finely chopped wood mixed uniformly with the damp twigs — every spark sees the *average* mixture. A **heterogeneous core** is like solid logs placed in a ring with damp twigs in the middle — sparks slow down in the wet region *before* striking the logs, dramatically improving the chance of a hit. A **reflector** is the stone wall around the pit that throws escaping sparks back in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize for KTU 2024**
> - $1 \text{ barn} = 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^2$
> - $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$
> - Average neutron energy in thermal equilibrium: **0.025 eV** at **293 K**
> - Energy released per fission of U-235: $\approx$ **200 MeV**

## 1.3 Why Cores are Classified — Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous

| Property | Homogeneous Core | Heterogeneous Core |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Fuel and moderator uniformly mixed (solution, molten salt) | Fuel in discrete rods/lumps separated by moderator |
| **Example Reactors** | Aqueous homogeneous, MSRE | PWR, BWR, PHWR, GCR, LMFBR |
| **Resonance absorption** | High (all neutrons see fuel resonances) | Low (resonance self-shielding by lumped fuel) |
| **Thermal utilization** | Lower | Higher (neutrons slowed in pure moderator) |
| **Engineering practicality** | Limited (corrosion, pumping) | Universal in commercial power reactors |
| **Multiplication factor ($k_\infty$)** | Lower for same fuel | Significantly higher |

## 1.4 Reflector — Formal Definition

A **reflector** is a region of material with high scattering cross-section and low absorption cross-section placed around the active core to redirect escaping neutrons back into the reacting region, thereby reducing neutron leakage and shrinking the critical size of the reactor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Reflector Materials (descending effectiveness)**
> 1. **Beryllium (Be)** — best, $\Sigma_a \approx 0.0015 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, high scattering
> 2. **Heavy water (D₂O)** — excellent in CANDU
> 3. **Ordinary water (H₂O)** — good in PWR/BWR
> 4. **Graphite (C)** — used in Magnox, AGR, RBMK
> 5. **Lead / Steel** — poor absorbers but heavy, used in fast reactors
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Factor Formula — Decomposed Step by Step

The infinite multiplication factor is the product of four dimensionless probabilities, each representing a stage in the neutron life cycle:

### Step 1 — Neutron Production (η — Eta)
When a neutron is absorbed by a fuel nucleus, the *average* number of new fast neutrons released per **absorption** in the fuel (not per fission) is:

$$
\eta = \nu \cdot \frac{\Sigma_f^{\text{fuel}}}{\Sigma_a^{\text{fuel}}}
$$

where $\nu$ is the average number of neutrons per fission ($\nu \approx 2.42$ for U-235 thermal fission).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick**: $\eta$ is *not* the same as $\nu$. The distinction is absorption vs. fission. Always use $\eta$ in the four-factor formula.

### Step 2 — Fast Fission (ε — Epsilon)
Some neutrons cause fission in **fertile** isotopes (e.g., U-238) before thermalizing. The fast fission factor captures this:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\text{total fast fissions in fuel}}{\text{fissions due to thermal neutrons alone}} \geq 1
$$

Typical $\varepsilon \approx 1.03$ for PWR fuel pins.

### Step 3 — Resonance Escape (p — Probability)
Neutrons slow down through the resonance region of U-238 (notably the 6.67 eV and 20.9 eV resonances). The probability of *avoiding* capture:

$$
p \approx \exp\!\left( -\frac{N_I \cdot I_A}{\xi \Sigma_s} \right)
$$

where $N_I$ is the absorber number density, $I_A$ is the resonance integral, $\xi$ is the average logarithmic energy decrement per collision, and $\Sigma_s$ is the scattering cross-section of the moderator.

### Step 4 — Thermal Utilization (f — Fraction)
Probability that a thermal neutron is absorbed in *fuel* rather than moderator or structure:

$$
f_{\text{homogeneous}} = \frac{\Sigma_a^{\text{fuel}}}{\Sigma_a^{\text{fuel}} + \Sigma_a^{\text{mod}} + \Sigma_a^{\text{struct}}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{heterogeneous}} = \frac{\Sigma_a^{\text{fuel}} \cdot V_{\text{fuel}}}{\Sigma_a^{\text{fuel}} \cdot V_{\text{fuel}} + \Sigma_a^{\text{mod}} \cdot V_{\text{mod}} + \Sigma_a^{\text{struct}} \cdot V_{\text{struct}}}
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> In the heterogeneous case, the **disadvantage factor** $\zeta$ (ratio of average flux in moderator to average flux in fuel) modifies $f$ — exam questions often test this!

## 2.2 Criticality with Leakage

For a *finite* reactor, leakage must be included. Define the **non-leakage probability** $P_{NL}$:

$$
P_{NL} = P_{\text{fast}} \cdot P_{\text{thermal}} = \exp(-B^2 \tau) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + L^2 B^2}
$$

where $B^2$ is the **buckling**, $\tau$ is the **Fermi age** (slowing-down length squared), and $L$ is the **diffusion length**. The effective multiplication factor becomes:

$$
k_{\text{eff}} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a bare spherical reactor of radius $R$, the **geometric buckling** is $B_g^2 = (\pi/R)^2$, and criticality requires $B_m^2 = B_g^2$, where $B_m^2 = (k_\infty - 1)/L^2$ is the **material buckling**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $k_\infty$ | Infinite multiplication factor | $k$ in absence of leakage | 1.10 – 1.30 | dimensionless |
| $k_{\text{eff}}$ | Effective multiplication factor | $k$ in real reactor | 1.000 (critical) | dimensionless |
| $\eta$ | Neutron yield per absorption | Fast neutrons released per fuel absorption | 2.07 (U-235) | dimensionless |
| $\varepsilon$ | Fast fission factor | Multiplication by fast fission | 1.00 – 1.10 | dimensionless |
| $p$ | Resonance escape probability | Fraction surviving resonance capture | 0.70 – 0.95 | dimensionless |
| $f$ | Thermal utilization factor | Fraction of thermal n's absorbed in fuel | 0.70 – 0.95 | dimensionless |
| $B^2$ | Buckling | Curvature of neutron flux | 0.001 – 0.1 | cm$^{-2}$ |
| $\tau$ | Fermi age | Slowing-down length squared | 27 – 350 cm$^2$ | cm$^2$ |
| $L^2$ | Diffusion area | Thermal diffusion length squared | 50 – 5000 cm$^2$ | cm$^2$ |
| $M^2$ | Migration area | $L^2 + \tau$ | 100 – 5500 cm$^2$ | cm$^2$ |
| $\delta$ | Reflector savings | Critical radius reduction | 5 – 50 cm | cm |
| $\rho$ | Reactivity | $(k_{\text{eff}} - 1)/k_{\text{eff}}$ | 0 (critical) | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Heterogeneous Core — Physical Mechanisms Behind Improved Performance

A heterogeneous lattice gives a *higher* $k_\infty$ than a homogeneous mixture of the same composition for three deep reasons:

1. **Resonance self-shielding** — In a fuel lump, the outer surface absorbs the bulk of resonance neutrons; the interior is "shielded." The effective resonance integral becomes:

   $$
   I_{\text{eff}} = I_\infty \left( 1 + \frac{\mu}{S/M} \right)^{-1/2}
   $$

   where $\mu$ is a constant, $S$ is the lump surface area, $M$ is the lump mass. Smaller lumps have *higher* $I_{\text{eff}}$ (worse). Larger lumps (rods) have *lower* $I_{\text{eff}}$ (better).

2. **Flux depression in fuel** — In a lattice, the thermal flux inside a fuel rod is depressed relative to the moderator. This is quantified by the **disadvantage factor** $\zeta = \bar{\phi}_m / \bar{\phi}_f > 1$.

3. **Dancoff correction** — In closely-packed lattices, neutrons escaping one rod may strike a neighbour. The **Dancoff factor** $C$ corrects resonance absorption for rod-to-rod shadowing.

## 2.5 Reflector Theory — Quantitative

For a spherical reactor of bare critical radius $R_b$ surrounded by an infinite reflector, the critical radius with reflector $R_r$ is smaller. The **reflector savings** is:

$$
\delta = R_b - R_r
$$

A common engineering approximation for an infinite reflector is:

$$
R_r = R_b - \delta, \quad \delta \approx 0.71 \lambda_{\text{tr}} \quad \text{(for thick reflector)}
$$

For a finite reflector of thickness $T$, the **extrapolation distance** $d$ to the outer boundary modifies the effective radius:

$$
\frac{1}{R_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{\pi}{R_r + \delta}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility**: Reflector savings directly translates to **reduced fissile inventory**. The 60-tonne beryllium reflector at the Hanford K-Reactor saved roughly 1.5 tonnes of enriched uranium.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Four-Factor Formula ($k_\infty$)

We trace the fate of $N_0$ fast fission neutrons born in one generation. We will follow a single neutron step by step.

**Step 1**: Of the $N_0$ fast neutrons produced, a fraction is multiplied by fast fission. The number reaching thermal energies from all sources is:

$$
N_1 = N_0 \cdot \varepsilon
$$

Here, $\varepsilon$ accounts for the extra neutrons born when U-238 (or other fertile material) fissions on a fast neutron.

**Step 2**: While slowing down, neutrons traverse the resonance region of U-238. A fraction $p$ escapes resonance capture:

$$
N_2 = N_1 \cdot p = N_0 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p
$$

**Step 3**: At thermal energy, a fraction $f$ of neutrons is absorbed in the fuel (rather than moderator/structure):

$$
N_3 = N_2 \cdot f = N_0 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f
$$

**Step 4**: Each thermal absorption in fuel produces $\eta$ new fast neutrons. Hence, the next generation begins with:

$$
N_0' = N_3 \cdot \eta = N_0 \cdot \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f
$$

By definition, the infinite multiplication factor is the ratio of successive generations:

$$
k_\infty \equiv \frac{N_0'}{N_0} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f \qquad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Radius for a Bare Spherical Reactor

We start from the **one-group diffusion equation** in steady state:

$$
\nabla^2 \phi + B^2 \phi = 0
$$

where $B^2$ is the buckling. For a bare spherical core of extrapolated radius $R$, the boundary condition is $\phi(R) = 0$. The lowest eigenfunction is:

$$
\phi(r) = \frac{A}{r} \sin\!\left( \frac{\pi r}{R} \right)
$$

Applying the zero-flux boundary condition at $r = R$ gives:

$$
\sin\!\left( \frac{\pi R}{R} \right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\pi R}{R} = \pi \quad \text{(automatically satisfied)}
$$

The general eigenvalue requirement is:

$$
B_g^2 = \left( \frac{\pi}{R} \right)^2
$$

The material buckling for a thermal reactor is defined as:

$$
B_m^2 = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2}
$$

Setting $B_g^2 = B_m^2$ for criticality:

$$
\left( \frac{\pi}{R_c} \right)^2 = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2}
$$

Solving for the critical radius:

$$
R_c = \frac{\pi L}{\sqrt{k_\infty - 1}} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a *favorite* derivation in KTU exams. Memorise the limiting cases:
> - $k_\infty \to 1$ ⇒ $R_c \to \infty$ (no critical system possible with $k_\infty \leq 1$)
> - $k_\infty$ large ⇒ small critical size (e.g., pure U-235 metal)

## 3.3 Derivation of Reflector Savings (Simplified Slab Case)

Consider a one-dimensional slab reactor of half-thickness $a$ with an infinite reflector of thickness $T$ on each side. Inside the core, the flux satisfies:

$$
\frac{d^2 \phi_c}{dx^2} + B_c^2 \phi_c = 0 \quad \text{with} \quad B_c^2 = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2}
$$

In the reflector, $k_\infty = 1$ (no fuel), so:

$$
\frac{d^2 \phi_r}{dx^2} - \kappa^2 \phi_r = 0 \quad \text{with} \quad \kappa^2 = \frac{1}{L_r^2}
$$

The general solutions are:

$$
\phi_c(x) = A \cos(B_c x)
$$

$$
\phi_r(x) = C \cosh(\kappa (T - x)) \quad \text{for } a \leq x \leq a+T
$$

The interface conditions at $x = a$ are:

$$
\phi_c(a) = \phi_r(a) \quad \text{(continuity of flux)}
$$

$$
D_c \left. \frac{d\phi_c}{dx} \right|_a = D_r \left. \frac{d\phi_r}{dx} \right|_a \quad \text{(continuity of current)}
$$

Applying these conditions, the transcendental criticality equation for a reflected slab is:

$$
B_c \tan(B_c a) = \frac{D_r}{D_c} \kappa \tanh(\kappa T)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an *infinite* reflector, $\tanh(\kappa T) \to 1$, and the **reflector savings** $\delta$ satisfies:

$$
\tan\!\left( \frac{\pi(a - \delta)}{a_{\text{bare}}} \right) = \frac{D_r}{D_c} \kappa \cot(\kappa \delta)
$$

> An approximate solution for thin reflectors is $\delta \approx D_r / D_c \cdot L_r$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> **Problem**: A spherical reactor has $k_\infty = 1.20$, diffusion length $L = 5$ cm. Find the critical radius for a bare sphere and the new critical radius if a thick beryllium reflector ($\delta = 7$ cm) is added.

**Solution**:

Bare sphere critical radius (from Section 3.2):

$$
R_b = \frac{\pi L}{\sqrt{k_\infty - 1}} = \frac{\pi \times 5}{\sqrt{1.20 - 1.00}} = \frac{15.708}{\sqrt{0.20}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.20} = 0.4472
$$

$$
R_b = \frac{15.708}{0.4472} = 35.13 \text{ cm}
$$

With reflector savings $\delta = 7$ cm:

$$
R_r = R_b - \delta = 35.13 - 7.00 = 28.13 \text{ cm}
$$

**Volume ratio** (savings in fissile material $\propto$ volume):

$$
\frac{V_r}{V_b} = \left( \frac{R_r}{R_b} \right)^3 = \left( \frac{28.13}{35.13} \right)^3 = (0.8008)^3 = 0.5134
$$

So the reflected core requires only **51.3%** of the fissile mass of the bare core — a **48.7% saving in critical mass**. This is why every commercial reactor has a reflector.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-ready result format**: Always state the bare value, then the reflected value, then the percentage saving — KTU examiners reward this presentation.

## 3.5 Comparison of Thermal Utilization: Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous

Consider a lattice with fuel volume fraction $\beta = V_f / V_{\text{total}}$ and moderator volume fraction $1 - \beta$. For the *homogeneous* equivalent (same $\Sigma_a$ smeared out):

$$
f_{\text{hom}} = \frac{\beta \Sigma_a^f}{\beta \Sigma_a^f + (1-\beta) \Sigma_a^m}
$$

For the *heterogeneous* lattice, the actual thermal utilization is:

$$
f_{\text{het}} = \frac{\Sigma_a^f \bar{\phi}_f V_f}{\Sigma_a^f \bar{\phi}_f V_f + \Sigma_a^m \bar{\phi}_m V_m}
$$

Using the disadvantage factor $\zeta = \bar{\phi}_m / \bar{\phi}_f$:

$$
f_{\text{het}} = \frac{\beta \Sigma_a^f}{\beta \Sigma_a^f + (1-\beta) \Sigma_a^m \zeta}
$$

Because $\zeta > 1$ for a typical PWR lattice, $f_{\text{het}} < f_{\text{hom}}$ for the *same* volume fractions — but the gain in $p$ and $\varepsilon$ more than compensates, giving a higher net $k_\infty$.

> [!CAUTION]
> KTU trap: students often state "$f$ is higher in heterogeneous" — *wrong* in isolation. The correct statement is: "$k_\infty$ is higher in heterogeneous because the gain in $p$ and $\varepsilon$ outweighs the loss in $f$."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Neutron Life Cycle in a Reactor Core

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fission Event in U-235 Fuel] --> B[Fast Neutrons Released: Energy ~2 MeV]
    B --> C{Fast Fission in U-238?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Extra Neutron Multiplication: Factor epsilon]
    C -->|No| E[Continue to Slowing-Down]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Moderation by H2O or D2O or Graphite]
    F --> G[Resonance Region of U-238: 6 to 200 eV]
    G --> H{Resonance Capture?}
    H -->|Captured: p fraction lost| Z1[Parasitic Absorption: End of Line]
    H -->|Escaped: factor p| I[Thermalized Neutron: 0.025 eV]
    I --> J{Absorbed by Fuel or Moderator?}
    J -->|Fuel: fraction f| K[New Fission Event: eta new neutrons]
    J -->|Moderator/Structure: 1-f lost| Z2[Parasitic Loss]
    K --> A
    Z1 --> END1[Cycle Terminated]
    Z2 --> END2[Cycle Terminated]
```

## 4.2 Architectural Block Diagram — Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous Core

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOM["HOMOGENEOUS CORE"]
        direction TB
        H1[U-235 Fuel Molecules] 
        H2[H2O or D2O Moderator]
        H3[Uniformly Dissolved]
        H1 --- H2
        H2 --- H3
    end

    subgraph HET["HETEROGENEOUS CORE"]
        direction TB
        F1[Fuel Rod 1: UO2 Pellets]
        F2[Fuel Rod 2: UO2 Pellets]
        F3[Fuel Rod 3: UO2 Pellets]
        M1[Moderator Channel 1]
        M2[Moderator Channel 2]
        F1 --- M1
        F2 --- M1
        F2 --- M2
        F3 --- M2
    end

    REF[Reflector Region: Be or D2O] --> HET
    SHIELD[Shield: Concrete + Boron] --> REF
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Reflector Action on Neutrons

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Neutron Born in Core] --> B{Direction of Travel}
    B -->|Inward| C[Continues Fission Chain in Core]
    B -->|Outward toward Boundary| D[Enters Reflector Region]
    D --> E[Scattering Collision in Be or D2O]
    E --> F{Lost to Reflector Absorption?}
    F -->|Yes, prob 1 minus P_R| G[Absorbed in Reflector]
    F -->|No, survives| H[Direction Randomized: Isotropic Scatter]
    H --> I{Now Directed Back into Core?}
    I -->|Yes| C
    I -->|No, escapes outer boundary| J[Permanent Leakage Loss]
    G --> K[Net Effect: Reduced Leakage]
    J --> K
    C --> L[Net Effect: Increased k_eff and Reduced Critical Size]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Matrix — Fuel Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMP["CORE GEOMETRY COMPARISON"]
        direction LR
        subgraph L1["HOMOGENEOUS SLAB VIEW"]
            S1[F]:::fuel --> S2[M]:::mod
            S2 --> S3[F]:::fuel
            S3 --> S4[M]:::mod
            S4 --> S5[F]:::fuel
        end
        subgraph L2["HETEROGENEOUS LATTICE VIEW"]
            T1[M]:::mod --> T2[F]:::fuel
            T2 --> T3[M]:::mod
            T3 --> T4[F]:::fuel
            T4 --> T5[M]:::mod
        end
    end

    classDef fuel fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A87800,color:#000
    classDef mod fill:#A8D8FF,stroke:#003D7A,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend**: Yellow `F` blocks = Fuel, Blue `M` blocks = Moderator. In the heterogeneous lattice, neutrons spend time in pure moderator regions *between* encounters with fuel, improving thermalization and reducing resonance absorption in fuel.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the four-factor formula. State the physical significance of each factor.
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** `Module 1` `CO1` `Cognitive Level: Remember/Understand` `Marks: 3`

**Model Answer**:

The four-factor formula gives the infinite multiplication factor:

$$
k_\infty = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f
$$

- **$\eta$ (eta) — neutron yield per absorption in fuel**: average number of fast neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel ($\eta = \nu \Sigma_f / \Sigma_a$).
- **$\varepsilon$ (epsilon) — fast fission factor**: ratio of total fast fissions to thermal fissions, accounting for extra neutrons from U-238 fast fission ($\varepsilon \geq 1$).
- **$p$ — resonance escape probability**: probability that a neutron slows through the U-238 resonance region without being captured.
- **$f$ — thermal utilization factor**: probability that a thermal neutron is absorbed in fuel rather than moderator or structure.

Together, $k_\infty$ represents the neutron multiplication in an infinite, leak-free medium. **[3 marks]**

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a homogeneous and a heterogeneous reactor core. Give one example of each.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** `Module 1` `CO1` `Cognitive Level: Understand` `Marks: 3`

**Model Answer**:

| Feature | Homogeneous Core | Heterogeneous Core |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel-moderator arrangement** | Uniformly mixed at molecular level | Fuel as discrete lumps/rods in a moderator matrix |
| **Resonance absorption** | High — every neutron sees resonances | Low — self-shielding in fuel lumps |
| **Thermal utilization** | Moderate | Higher effective $k_\infty$ |
| **Example** | Aqueous Homogeneous Reactor (AHR), Molten Salt Reactor (MSRE) | Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR), CANDU, AGR |
| **Engineering use** | Research, specialised concepts | Universal in commercial power reactors |

**[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[Module Internal Choice — KTU Regulation 2024]**: Answer **either** Question A *or* Question B in full.

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`** `Module 1` `CO1, CO2` `Cognitive Level: Apply / Analyze` `14 Marks`

#### (a) Derive the four-factor formula for the infinite multiplication factor $k_\infty$ in a thermal reactor. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

We trace a single fast neutron from birth to the next generation in an infinite medium. Let $N_0$ be the initial number of fast neutrons.

**Step 1 — Fast Fission (1 mark)**
Some fast neutrons cause fission in U-238, producing additional fast neutrons. The number becomes:

$$
N_1 = \varepsilon \, N_0
$$

**Step 2 — Resonance Escape (2 marks)**
While slowing down, neutrons may be captured at U-238 resonances (6.67 eV, 20.9 eV, etc.). A fraction $p$ escapes:

$$
N_2 = p \, N_1 = \varepsilon p \, N_0
$$

**Step 3 — Thermal Utilization (2 marks)**
At thermal energies, neutrons are absorbed in fuel with probability $f$, and in moderator/structure with probability $(1-f)$:

$$
N_3 = f \, N_2 = \varepsilon p f \, N_0
$$

**Step 4 — Neutron Production (2 marks)**
Each fuel absorption produces $\eta$ new fast neutrons, beginning the next generation:

$$
N_0' = \eta \, N_3 = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f \, N_0
$$

By definition:

$$
\boxed{k_\infty = \frac{N_0'}{N_0} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

**[7 marks]**

#### (b) A spherical reactor has $k_\infty = 1.25$, thermal diffusion length $L = 6$ cm. Calculate: (i) the critical radius for a bare sphere, and (ii) the new critical radius when a thick reflector with savings $\delta = 8$ cm is added. Comment on the engineering significance. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**(i) Bare critical radius** (from $B_g^2 = B_m^2$):

$$
R_b = \frac{\pi L}{\sqrt{k_\infty - 1}} = \frac{\pi \times 6}{\sqrt{1.25 - 1.00}} = \frac{18.850}{\sqrt{0.25}} = \frac{18.850}{0.5} = 37.70 \text{ cm}
$$

**[3 marks]**

**(ii) Reflected critical radius**:

$$
R_r = R_b - \delta = 37.70 - 8.00 = 29.70 \text{ cm}
$$

Critical volume ratio:

$$
\frac{V_r}{V_b} = \left( \frac{29.70}{37.70} \right)^3 = (0.7878)^3 = 0.4889
$$

**Engineering significance**: The reflected core requires only **48.9%** of the bare-core fissile mass. This represents a **51.1% reduction in critical mass**, a substantial economic and safety benefit. Reflectors are therefore standard in every commercial reactor, reducing both fuel inventory cost and the consequence of any accidental criticality excursion. **[4 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — Common errors:
> 1. Confusing $\eta$ with $\nu$ (loses 1 mark per occurrence).
> 2. Using $R_c = \pi L / (k_\infty - 1)$ instead of $\sqrt{k_\infty - 1}$ — **wrong, must be under the square root**.
> 3. Forgetting to convert critical radius to volume using $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ before commenting on mass savings.
> 4. Writing $k_\infty$ in place of $k_{\text{eff}}$ when discussing the reflected case — in practice, $k_{\text{eff}} = 1$ is what determines criticality, and the reflector changes the leakage term.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`** `Module 1` `CO1, CO2` `Cognitive Level: Understand / Apply` `14 Marks`

#### (a) With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the function of a reflector in a thermal nuclear reactor. Discuss the criteria for choosing a reflector material. (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Definition and Function (3 marks)**: A reflector is a layer of material with low absorption and high scattering cross-section placed around the active core. Its primary function is to redirect neutrons that would otherwise leak out, back into the core, thereby:
- Reducing neutron leakage,
- Decreasing the critical mass/volume,
- Improving the neutron economy,
- Flattening the power distribution.

**Block Diagram (2 marks)**: 

```
            ┌─────────────────────────┐
            │  REFLECTOR (Be / D2O)   │
            │  ← neutrons scattered → │
            │  ┌─────────────────┐    │
            │  │   FUEL CORE     │    │
            │  │ (Fission + Heat)│    │
            │  └─────────────────┘    │
            └─────────────────────────┘
            ┌─────────────────────────┐
            │  SHIELD (Concrete)      │
            └─────────────────────────┘
```

**Material Selection Criteria (2 marks)**: A good reflector must have:
- **High scattering cross-section** $\Sigma_s$ → increases probability of bouncing neutrons back.
- **Low absorption cross-section** $\Sigma_a$ → does not remove neutrons parasitically.
- **High atomic mass ratio with hydrogen (or be hydrogenous)** → good moderation.
- **Mechanical and chemical stability** under irradiation, heat, and coolant.
- **Engineering availability** (Be is best but expensive; H₂O is most common).

**Ranking of reflectors (descending)**: Be > D₂O > H₂O > Graphite > Fe.

**[7 marks]**

#### (b) A PWR fuel assembly has the following cell-averaged properties: fuel volume fraction $\beta = 0.30$, $\Sigma_a^{\text{fuel}} = 10$ cm$^{-1}$, $\Sigma_a^{\text{mod}} = 0.05$ cm$^{-1}$, $\eta = 2.07$, $\varepsilon = 1.04$, $p = 0.85$, disadvantage factor $\zeta = 1.25$. Compute $k_\infty$ for the lattice and compare with the homogeneous case (same $\beta$, $\eta$, $\varepsilon$, $p$). (7 marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Heterogeneous thermal utilization**:

$$
f_{\text{het}} = \frac{\beta \, \Sigma_a^f}{\beta \, \Sigma_a^f + (1-\beta) \Sigma_a^m \, \zeta}
$$

Numerator: $0.30 \times 10 = 3.00$ cm$^{-1}$

Denominator: $0.30 \times 10 + 0.70 \times 0.05 \times 1.25 = 3.00 + 0.04375 = 3.04375$ cm$^{-1}$

$$
f_{\text{het}} = \frac{3.00}{3.04375} = 0.9856
$$

**Heterogeneous $k_\infty$**:

$$
k_\infty^{\text{het}} = \eta \varepsilon p f = 2.07 \times 1.04 \times 0.85 \times 0.9856 = 1.8024
$$

**[2 marks]**

**Homogeneous thermal utilization** ($\zeta = 1$, all mixed):

$$
f_{\text{hom}} = \frac{3.00}{3.00 + 0.70 \times 0.05} = \frac{3.00}{3.035} = 0.9885
$$

**Homogeneous $k_\infty$**:

$$
k_\infty^{\text{hom}} = 2.07 \times 1.04 \times 0.85 \times 0.9885 = 1.808
$$

**[2 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **Surprising result trap** — In this *idealised* case the homogeneous value is *slightly higher* because the moderator absorption term is already very small. In real reactors, the gain in $p$ (resonance escape) for the heterogeneous case overwhelms any loss in $f$. KTU board examiners will deduct marks if you state unconditionally that "heterogeneous is always better" — it depends on the energy-dependence of cross sections and the lattice pitch.

**Engineering comment (1 mark)**: The small numerical difference confirms that for high-burnup PWR lattices, both $f_{\text{het}}$ and $f_{\text{hom}}$ approach unity (most thermal neutrons go to fuel), and the dominant benefit of heterogeneity is in $p$, not $f$. For natural-uranium, heavy-water lattices (CANDU), the gain in $p$ is dramatic and is the reason heterogeneous designs are universal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall** — Common errors for Q.B:
> 1. Forgetting to multiply moderator $\Sigma_a$ by the disadvantage factor $\zeta$ — gives 1 mark deduction.
> 2. Using total $\Sigma_a$ values without multiplying by volume fractions — error of principle.
> 3. Not stating the assumption of equal $\eta$, $\varepsilon$, $p$ when comparing — examiners will mark this as "incomplete comparison."
> 4. Stating "heterogeneous is always better" without numerical justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Neutron Chain-Reacting Systems**

**Core Definitions**
- ☐ $k_\infty = \eta \varepsilon p f$ — infinite multiplication factor
- ☐ $k_{\text{eff}} = k_\infty P_{NL}$ — accounts for leakage
- ☐ $k_{\text{eff}} = 1$ critical, $>1$ supercritical, $<1$ subcritical
- ☐ $\eta = \nu \Sigma_f / \Sigma_a$ (per **absorption**, not per fission)
- ☐ $P_{NL} = P_{\text{fast}} \cdot P_{\text{thermal}} = e^{-B^2 \tau} / (1 + L^2 B^2)$

**Homogeneous vs Heterogeneous**
- ☐ Heterogeneous gives higher $k_\infty$ mainly because of higher $p$ (resonance self-shielding)
- ☐ Heterogeneous actually has *lower* $f$ due to disadvantage factor $\zeta > 1$
- ☐ Disadvantage factor: $\zeta = \bar{\phi}_m / \bar{\phi}_f$
- ☐ Heterogeneous: $f = \beta \Sigma_a^f / [\beta \Sigma_a^f + (1-\beta) \Sigma_a^m \zeta]$
- ☐ Resonance integral in lumped fuel: $I_{\text{eff}} = I_\infty (1 + \mu/(S/M))^{-1/2}$

**Reflectors**
- ☐ Purpose: reduce leakage, reduce critical size, improve neutron economy
- ☐ Best reflector: **Beryllium** (low $\Sigma_a$, high $\Sigma_s$, high $\rho$ for $\gamma$-shielding)
- ☐ Reflector savings: $\delta = R_b - R_r$ (typical 5–50 cm)
- ☐ Critical radius with infinite reflector: $R_r = \pi L_r / \sqrt{k_\infty - 1} - \delta$ (slab/sphere variations)
- ☐ Engineering value: every commercial reactor uses H₂O/D₂O/Be reflector; critical mass reduced by 30–50%

**Formula Flashcards for KTU 2024**
- ☐ $R_c = \pi L / \sqrt{k_\infty - 1}$ (bare sphere)
- ☐ $B_m^2 = (k_\infty - 1)/L^2$ (material buckling)
- ☐ $B_g^2 = (\pi/R)^2$ (geometric buckling, sphere)
- ☐ $\rho = (k_{\text{eff}} - 1)/k_{\text{eff}}$ (reactivity)

**Common KTU Traps to Avoid**
- ☐ Don't confuse $\eta$ and $\nu$ — always use $\eta$ in the four-factor formula.
- ☐ Don't forget the **square root** in the critical radius formula.
- ☐ Don't claim heterogeneous is *unconditionally* better — must justify with $p$ vs $f$ trade-off.
- ☐ Don't include leakage in $k_\infty$ — leakage belongs in $P_{NL}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics**:
> - "**E**psilon **P**hi **F**rom **A**bsorptions" → $\varepsilon p f \eta$ (E-P-F-A)
> - "**H**ot **P**izza **F**eeds **A**ngry neutrons" → $\eta p f \varepsilon$ (note order in $k_\infty$)
> - Criticality: "$k$ equals **1** is the **One** and only safe number" — never operate at $k > 1.001$ in steady state.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Kinetics and Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reactor Kinetics and Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reactor Kinetics** is the branch of nuclear reactor physics that studies the time-dependent behavior of the neutron population and the concentrations of delayed-neutron precursors inside a nuclear reactor core. It is mathematically formulated through a set of coupled, non-linear ordinary differential equations known as the **Point Kinetics Equations (PKE)**.

**Reactor Dynamics** extends kinetics by incorporating the *feedback effects* of changing core conditions (fuel temperature, moderator temperature/ density, void fraction, xenon concentration, and control-rod motion) on reactivity, leading to a coupled, multi-physics description of the reactor as a **self-regulating system**.

Mathematically, the instantaneous neutron balance is governed by:

$$\frac{dn(t)}{dt} = \frac{\rho(t) - \beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i \, C_i(t) + S(t)$$

where every symbol has a precise physical meaning explained in the subsequent sections.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Bonfire of Matches" Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bonfire Analogy for KTU Students**
>
> Imagine a small bonfire that is just stable because a thin boy (a *delayed-neutron precursor*) is carefully feeding it one extra match every few seconds. **Prompt neutrons** behave like the fire being blown by the wind — they are instantaneous and fierce. **Delayed neutrons** are the slow matches that prevent the fire from exploding.
>
> Without the boy, even a tiny puff of wind would either extinguish the fire or blow it up catastrophically. With the boy, small puffs are absorbed safely. This is exactly why a reactor can be controlled: **delayed neutrons provide a "slow lever"** to manage a system that would otherwise be controlled by an extremely fast, dangerous "instant lever" (prompt neutrons only).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Numerical Values to Memorize for KTU Examinations**
>
> - **Effective neutron generation time** $\Lambda \approx 10^{-4}$ s (thermal reactors, e.g., PWR) and $\Lambda \approx 10^{-8}$ s (fast reactors).
> - **Total delayed-neutron fraction** $\beta \approx 0.0065$ (≈ 0.65 %) for U-235, $0.002$ for Pu-239.
> - **Mean neutron lifetime** $\ell = \Lambda / k \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ s in thermal reactors.
> - **Reactivity unit** 1 dollar (\$) = 100 cents = effective $\beta$ worth of reactivity.
> - **Effective multiplication factor** at criticality: $k_{eff} = 1.0$ (steady state).
> - **1 dollar of added reactivity** corresponds to $\rho = \beta \approx 0.0065$.
> - **Six delayed-neutron groups** (commonly used): with half-lives ranging from ~0.2 s to ~55 s.

### 1.4 Why These Two Subjects Matter in Nuclear Safety

| Aspect | Kinetics | Dynamics |
|---|---|---|
| Time scale | Microseconds to minutes | Seconds to hours |
| Main variable | Neutron population n(t) | Reactivity $\rho(t)$ feedback |
| Controlling equation | Point Kinetics Equation | Coupled PKE + feedback ODEs |
| Safety relevance | Predicts reactor period (T) | Prevents prompt-critical excursions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Hook:** Module 1 of PESFT417 explicitly maps the four- and six-group delayed neutron formulation, the Inhour equation, the Nordheim–Fuchs excursion model, and reactivity feedback coefficients to **Course Outcome CO1** of the 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Roadmap (Bulleted Logic Flow)

- **Step 1 — Neutron Source Term.** A fission event releases ~2.43 neutrons on average for U-235. These are split into *prompt* (released within $10^{-14}$ s) and *delayed* (released from 0.2 s to 55 s later) categories.
- **Step 2 — Multiplication Factor (k).** The ratio of neutrons in one generation to the previous generation. Criticality: $k=1$, subcritical $k<1$, supercritical $k>1$.
- **Step 3 — Reactivity $\rho$.** A *dimensionless* measure of departure from criticality: $\rho = (k-1)/k$. Positive $\rho$ means power rises; negative $\rho$ means power falls.
- **Step 4 — Generation Time $\Lambda$.** Average time between successive neutron generations (≈ $10^{-4}$ s thermal, $10^{-8}$ s fast).
- **Step 5 — Delayed Neutron Precursors.** Six fission-product nuclides (e.g., Br-87, I-137) that $\beta$-decay and emit a neutron. They are *slow neutron sources* that make reactors controllable.
- **Step 6 — Point Kinetics Equations (PKE).** A system of 7 coupled ODEs (1 neutron + 6 precursor equations) describing the system.
- **Step 7 — Inhour Equation.** Roots of the characteristic equation give the *stable* and *transient* periods of power variation after a reactivity insertion.
- **Step 8 — Nordheim–Fuchs Model.** A simplified analytical solution for rapid *power excursions* (e.g., control-rod ejection accident).
- **Step 9 — Reactivity Feedback Coefficients.** $\alpha_F, \alpha_M, \alpha_V$ — negative in a *self-regulating* reactor (inherently safe).
- **Step 10 — Reactor Scram.** Emergency insertion of large negative $\rho$ (≈ -10 \$ or more) to shut down the chain reaction.

### 2.2 The Six-Group Delayed Neutron Data (U-235 — Memorize for KTU)

| Group $i$ | Half-life $T_{1/2,i}$ (s) | Decay const. $\lambda_i$ (s$^{-1}$) | Yield $\beta_i$ | Fraction $\beta_i / \beta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 55.72 | 0.0124 | 0.000215 | 0.033 |
| 2 | 22.72 | 0.0305 | 0.001424 | 0.219 |
| 3 | 6.22 | 0.1110 | 0.001274 | 0.196 |
| 4 | 2.30 | 0.3010 | 0.002568 | 0.395 |
| 5 | 0.610 | 1.1400 | 0.000748 | 0.115 |
| 6 | 0.230 | 3.0100 | 0.000273 | 0.042 |
| **Total** | — | — | **0.006500** | **1.000** |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet for Reactor Kinetics & Dynamics — Master These 14 Equations**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reactivity definition | $\rho = \dfrac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Reactivity in dollars | $\rho_{\$} = \rho / \beta$ | dollars (\$) |
| 3 | Reactivity in cents | $\rho_{\Phi} = 100\,\rho / \beta$ | cents (¢) |
| 4 | One-group PKE (neutron) | $\dfrac{dn}{dt} = \dfrac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \lambda C$ | n in n/cm³/s, $\Lambda$ in s |
| 5 | One-group PKE (precursor) | $\dfrac{dC}{dt} = \dfrac{\beta}{\Lambda} n - \lambda C$ | C in atoms/cm³/s |
| 6 | Six-group PKE (neutron) | $\dfrac{dn}{dt} = \dfrac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i$ | same as above |
| 7 | Six-group PKE (precursor) | $\dfrac{dC_i}{dt} = \dfrac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n - \lambda_i C_i, \quad i=1,\dots,6$ | per group |
| 8 | Effective generation time | $\Lambda = \dfrac{\ell}{k_{eff}}$ | s |
| 9 | Prompt-neutron period | $T_p = \dfrac{\Lambda}{k - 1} = \dfrac{\Lambda}{\rho k}$ | s |
| 10 | Stable (asymptotic) period | $T = \dfrac{1}{\omega}$, where $\omega$ is smallest positive root of Inhour eq. | s |
| 11 | Inhour equation | $\rho = \Lambda \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \dfrac{\beta_i \omega}{\omega + \lambda_i}$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Reactor period vs reactivity (small $\rho$) | $T \approx \dfrac{\beta - \rho}{\rho \lambda_{eff}}$ | s |
| 13 | Nordheim–Fuchs peak power | $P_{max} = P_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\rho_0 - \beta}{2\,\alpha\, \tau_0}\right)$ | W |
| 14 | Fuel-temperature feedback | $\dfrac{d\rho}{dT_F} = \alpha_F$ | $\Delta$k/k per °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $\Lambda$ (generation time) with $\ell$ (mean neutron lifetime). They are related by $\Lambda = \ell / k_{eff}$, so $\Lambda \approx \ell$ only when $k_{eff} \approx 1$. Using one in place of the other at high reactivity is a common valuation mistake.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Control-Rod Calibration:** The Inhour equation is used daily to convert measured reactor period $T$ into the required control-rod reactivity worth in dollars.
- **Safety Analysis Licensing:** Every nuclear power plant in India (NPCIL — RAPS, MAPS, KAPS, KKNPP) submits a *Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR)* containing Nordheim–Fuchs and Doppler-feedback calculations to AERB.
- **Prompt-Criticality Avoidance:** Reactor physics design ensures that *all anticipated* operational reactivity insertions remain below $\rho = \beta$ ($\rho_{\$} < 1.0$).
- **Xenon Transient Prediction:** Reactor dynamics equations include $^{135}$Xe and $^{135}$I dynamics to predict power oscillations after shutdown — critical for planning reactor restart in Kerala's KGS-1, KGS-2, and KGS-3 (Kaiga).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the One-Group Point Kinetics Equation (PKE)

#### Step 1: Define the neutron balance

Consider a uniform multiplying medium. The rate of change of neutron density $n(t)$ equals the *net production rate* per unit volume per second.

**Production** comes from fission, both prompt and delayed:

- Prompt production: each fission releases $(1 - \beta)\, k_\infty$ neutrons per neutron absorbed (approximately).
- Delayed production: precursor decay emits $\sum_i \lambda_i C_i$ neutrons per cm³ per second.

**Loss** is by absorption and leakage, with characteristic time $\ell$:

- Loss rate: $n / \ell$.

#### Step 2: Write the balance equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dn(t)}{dt} &= \underbrace{\text{Production}}_{\text{from fission}} - \underbrace{\text{Loss}}_{\text{absorption + leakage}} \\
&= \left[\frac{(1-\beta)\,k_{eff} - 1}{\ell}\right] n(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic note:** $k_{eff}/\ell$ is the *effective* production rate per neutron, and the term $(k_{eff}-1)/\ell$ is the net reactivity contribution. Subtracting $\beta$ removes the prompt fraction from the production term because delayed neutrons are handled separately by the precursor term.

#### Step 3: Introduce $\Lambda = \ell / k_{eff}$ and $\rho = (k_{eff}-1)/k_{eff}$

Substitute these definitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dn}{dt} &= \frac{(1-\beta)\,k_{eff} - 1}{\ell}\, n + \sum_i \lambda_i C_i \\
&= \frac{k_{eff} - 1 - \beta k_{eff}}{\Lambda\, k_{eff}}\, n + \sum_i \lambda_i C_i \\
&= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\, n + \sum_i \lambda_i C_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic note:** We used $\ell = \Lambda k_{eff}$ and $\rho k_{eff} = k_{eff} - 1$. After algebraic simplification, the prompt contribution is $\rho - \beta$.

**Result — Neutron PKE:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\, n(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i(t)\;}$$

#### Step 4: Derive the precursor balance equation

Each precursor group $i$ is produced at rate $\beta_i k_{eff} n / \ell$ and decays at rate $\lambda_i C_i$. Substituting $\Lambda = \ell/k_{eff}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_i(t)}{dt} &= \frac{\beta_i k_{eff}}{\ell}\, n(t) - \lambda_i C_i(t) \\
&= \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda}\, n(t) - \lambda_i C_i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic note:** Each fission of the $i$-th precursor family produces a delayed neutron with branching fraction $\beta_i / k_{eff}$ (approximately $\beta_i$ since $k_{eff} \approx 1$).

**Result — Precursor PKE (six coupled equations):**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda}\, n(t) - \lambda_i C_i(t), \quad i = 1,2,\ldots,6\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Inhour Equation

#### Step 1: Assume an exponential solution

For a *step insertion* of constant reactivity $\rho$ (no feedback), assume the neutron density and precursors evolve as $e^{\omega t}$:

$$n(t) = n_0 e^{\omega t}, \qquad C_i(t) = C_{i,0}\, e^{\omega t}$$

#### Step 2: Substitute into the precursor equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega C_i &= \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n - \lambda_i C_i \\
C_i (\omega + \lambda_i) &= \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n \\
C_i &= \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda (\omega + \lambda_i)}\, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic note:** We have expressed the precursor concentration as a function of the neutron density — eliminating the precursor ODEs from the system.

#### Step 3: Substitute into the neutron equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega n &= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \sum_i \lambda_i C_i \\
\omega n &= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \sum_i \lambda_i \cdot \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda (\omega + \lambda_i)} n
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide both sides by $n$ (non-zero during transients):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \frac{1}{\Lambda} \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \lambda_i}{\omega + \lambda_i} \\
\Lambda \omega + \beta &= \rho + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \lambda_i}{\omega + \lambda_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Rearrange to get the Inhour equation

**Logic note:** We move $\beta$ to the right side using the identity $\beta = \sum_i \beta_i$, and the equation becomes a transcendental relation between $\rho$ and $\omega$.

$$
\boxed{\;\rho = \Lambda \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \omega}{\omega + \lambda_i}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The seven roots $\omega_0, \omega_1, \ldots, \omega_6$ of this equation correspond to the seven *periods* $T_j = 1/\omega_j$ of the reactor. The smallest positive root $\omega_0$ gives the **stable period** that the reactor asymptotically approaches; the other six are *transient* periods (decaying exponentials).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Nordheim–Fuchs Model (Power Excursion)

Used for *very fast* reactivity insertions where feedback cannot be ignored. Let $P(t)$ be power, $T_F$ fuel temperature, $\alpha = \alpha_F$ the *fuel-temperature coefficient* (negative).

#### Step 1: Coupling assumption

The reactivity drops linearly with fuel temperature:

$$\rho(t) = \rho_0 - \alpha \, \Delta T_F(t)$$

where $\rho_0$ is the initial inserted reactivity and $\Delta T_F$ is the temperature rise.

#### Step 2: Adiabatic fuel heating (no cooling)

The fuel energy equation in a short excursion:

$$m_F c_F \frac{dT_F}{dt} = P(t)$$

#### Step 3: Approximate the prompt-supercritical PKE

For excursions on the order of milliseconds, neglect delayed neutrons ($\beta \ll \rho$):

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\rho(t)}{\Lambda} P(t) = \frac{\rho_0 - \alpha (T_F - T_{F,0})}{\Lambda} P
$$

#### Step 4: Eliminate time using $dP/dT_F$

**Logic note:** Divide $dP/dt$ by $dT_F/dt = P/(m_F c_F)$ to remove time from the problem.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dT_F} &= \frac{dP/dt}{dT_F/dt} = \frac{\rho_0 - \alpha (T_F - T_{F,0})}{\Lambda} \cdot m_F c_F \\
\frac{dP}{dT_F} &= \frac{m_F c_F}{\Lambda}\left[\rho_0 - \alpha (T_F - T_{F,0})\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Integrate to find peak power

Integrate from $T_{F,0}$ to $T_{F,peak}$ where $\rho(t) = 0$ (i.e., $T_{F,peak} = T_{F,0} + \rho_0/\alpha$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{P_0}^{P_{max}} \frac{dP}{P} &= \frac{m_F c_F}{\Lambda} \int_{T_{F,0}}^{T_{F,peak}} \left[\rho_0 - \alpha (T_F - T_{F,0})\right] dT_F \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_0}\right) &= \frac{m_F c_F}{\Lambda} \cdot \frac{\rho_0^2}{2\alpha} \\
P_{max} &= P_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{m_F c_F\, \rho_0^2}{2\alpha \Lambda}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compact form:**

$$\boxed{\;P_{max} = P_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{\rho_0^2}{2 \alpha \, \tau_0}\right)\;}$$

where $\tau_0 = \Lambda / (m_F c_F)$ is the characteristic fuel time constant.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases of Inhour Equation

| Case | Reactivity Range | Approximation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho \ll \beta$ (small) | $T \approx (\beta - \rho)/(\rho \lambda_{eff})$ | Slow controllable period |
| 2 | $\rho = 0$ (critical) | $\omega = 0 \Rightarrow T = \infty$ | Steady power (no time variation) |
| 3 | $0 < \rho < \beta$ (delayed supercrit.) | $T \approx \beta/(\rho \lambda_{eff})$ | Delayed-neutron dominated period |
| 4 | $\rho = \beta$ ($\rho_\$ = 1$) | $T = T_p = \Lambda / (k-1)$ | Prompt critical (boundary) |
| 5 | $\rho > \beta$ (prompt supercrit.) | $T \approx \Lambda/\rho$ k | Explosive — must SCRAM |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** Always check whether the given reactivity is in *dollars* or *absolute* $\Delta k/k$. Convert using $\rho = \rho_{\$} \times \beta$ before substituting into the Inhour equation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Reactor Kinetics & Dynamics Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactivity Insertion rho_in] --> B[Point Kinetics Solver]
    C[Delayed Neutron Precursors C1 to C6] --> B
    D[External Neutron Source S] --> B
    B --> E[Neutron Density n and Power P]
    E --> F[Fuel Temperature T_F]
    E --> G[Moderator Temperature T_M]
    E --> H[Void Fraction and Xenon Xe135]
    F --> I[Feedback Reactivity rho_F]
    G --> J[Feedback Reactivity rho_M]
    H --> K[Feedback Reactivity rho_V]
    I --> L[Total Reactivity rho_sum]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> B
    M[Control Rod Drive] --> A
    N[SCRAM Trip Signal] --> M
    O[Reactor Period T meter] --> N
    B --> O
```

**Explanation of the Mermaid Diagram:**

- **Block A → B:** External reactivity (control rod movement, boron dilution) enters the PKE solver.
- **Blocks C and D:** Precursor concentrations and any external neutron source (e.g., start-up source) feed the same solver.
- **Block B → E:** Solver outputs the *neutron density* and *thermal power*.
- **Blocks F, G, H:** Power deposition heats the fuel, moderator, and changes the void/xenon state.
- **Blocks I, J, K:** Each of these states generates a *feedback reactivity* through the corresponding temperature coefficient $\alpha_F, \alpha_M, \alpha_V$.
- **Block L:** Total reactivity $\rho_{sum} = \rho_{in} + \rho_F + \rho_M + \rho_V$ closes the loop and re-enters the solver.
- **Blocks M and N:** Period meter and safety logic trigger control-rod motion and emergency SCRAM.

### 4.2 State-Transition Diagram — Reactivity Regimes in a Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1[Subcritical rho less than zero] -->|Rods Withdrawn| state2[Critical rho equals zero]
    state2 -->|Delayed Supercritical| state3[Delayed Supercritical zero less than rho less than beta]
    state3 -->|Rho crosses beta| state4[Prompt Critical rho equals beta]
    state4 -->|Further Insertion| state5[Prompt Supercritical rho greater than beta]
    state5 -->|SCRAM Activated| state2
    state1 -->|SCRAM Activated| state1
```

**Explanation:**

- **state1 → state2 → state3:** Normal start-up sequence, rods withdrawn, reactivity rises through 0 to a small positive value.
- **state3 → state4:** Boundary condition; reactor period drops sharply from seconds to milliseconds.
- **state4 → state5:** Power rises *promptly* — region of severe accident concern.
- **state5 → state2:** SCRAM (rapid negative-reactivity insertion) returns the reactor to critical and then subcritical.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PKE Solver (Six-Group)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input rho t, n_t, C1_t to C6_t] --> solverA[Solver computes dn_dt]
    inputA --> solverB[Solver computes dC1_dt]
    inputA --> solverC[Solver computes dC2_dt]
    inputA --> solverD[Solver computes dC3_dt]
    inputA --> solverE[Solver computes dC4_dt]
    inputA --> solverF[Solver computes dC5_dt]
    inputA --> solverG[Solver computes dC6_dt]
    solverA --> integrator[Numerical Integrator Runga Kutta 4]
    solverB --> integrator
    solverC --> integrator
    solverD --> integrator
    solverE --> integrator
    solverF --> integrator
    solverG --> integrator
    integrator --> outputA[Output n_t_plus_dt]
    integrator --> outputB[Output C1_t_plus_dt to C6_t_plus_dt]
    outputA --> inputA
    outputB --> inputA
```

**Explanation:** This is a *closed-loop iterative solver*. At each time step, the current state $\{n, C_1, \ldots, C_6\}$ is used to compute the seven time derivatives, which a 4th-order Runge–Kutta integrator then advances to the next time step. The output becomes the new input. This is the architecture used in industry codes such as **PARCS**, **SIMULATE-3K**, and **MOCADI**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define *reactivity* and state its units. What is meant by *one dollar* of reactivity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Reactivity is the dimensionless measure of departure of the multiplication factor from unity:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$$

- It is **dimensionless** (often expressed in pcm = $10^{-5}$ or per cent mille).
- **One dollar (\$1)** of reactivity is defined as an amount of reactivity equal to the *effective delayed-neutron fraction* $\beta_{eff}$. Numerically, for a U-235 fueled thermal reactor, $1\,\$ \approx 0.0065$ in $\Delta k/k$.
- **One cent (¢)** is one-hundredth of a dollar: $1\,\cent = 0.01\, \$ \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-5}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why are *delayed neutrons* essential for the *controllability* of a thermal nuclear reactor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Delayed neutrons constitute a small fraction ($\beta \approx 0.0065$ for U-235) but are emitted over seconds, in contrast to prompt neutrons released in $10^{-14}$ s.
2. They effectively *lengthen* the reactor period from ~$10^{-4}$ s to several seconds, allowing mechanical control rods to insert negative reactivity in time.
3. If the reactor operated on prompt neutrons only ($\beta = 0$), the period would be ~$10^{-4}$ s, making the chain reaction uncontrollable — every perturbation would lead to either a runaway or a shutdown within microseconds.
4. Therefore, **delayed neutrons are the *safety* and *control* lifeline** of all thermal fission reactors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 1 Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Derive the one-group point kinetics equations for a thermal nuclear reactor starting from the neutron balance, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal reactor operating at a steady power of 100 MW experiences a step insertion of reactivity $\rho = +0.001$ ($\beta = 0.0065$, $\Lambda = 10^{-4}$ s, $\lambda_{eff} = 0.08$ s$^{-1}$). Calculate the **stable reactor period** using the small-reactivity approximation. Comment on the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. Start from the neutron balance: rate of change of neutron density = production – loss.
2. Production: prompt fissions + delayed precursor decay.
3. Loss: absorption + leakage, parameterized by mean neutron lifetime $\ell$.
4. Substitute $\Lambda = \ell/k_{eff}$ and $\rho = (k_{eff} - 1)/k_{eff}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dn}{dt} &= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\, n + \lambda\, C \\
\frac{dC}{dt} &= \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\, n - \lambda\, C
\end{aligned}
$$

5. State all assumptions (point model, one delayed group, no spatial dependence, no external source, constant $\rho$). **(Full marks awarded for stating assumptions explicitly — see valuation warning below.)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Logic note:** The reactivity $\rho = 0.001$ is much smaller than $\beta = 0.0065$, so the *delayed-critical approximation* applies.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &\approx \frac{\beta - \rho}{\rho \, \lambda_{eff}} \\
&= \frac{0.0065 - 0.001}{0.001 \times 0.08} \\
&= \frac{0.0055}{8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 68.75 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Safety implication (2 marks):** A period of 68.75 s is comfortably within the controllable range — operators have approximately 1–2 minutes to take corrective action (rod insertion or boron addition). This confirms that $\rho = +0.001$ is an *acceptable* step insertion for a thermal reactor.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is the **Inhour equation**? Derive it from the point kinetics equations for a one-delayed-group model and explain the physical significance of its roots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Nordheim–Fuchs model, calculate the **peak power** during a control-rod ejection accident in which $\rho_0 = +0.01$ (i.e., 1.5 dollars), the fuel temperature coefficient is $\alpha = -5 \times 10^{-5}$ $\Delta k/k$ per °C, the effective generation time is $\Lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ s, the fuel mass $m_F = 1.0 \times 10^{5}$ kg, and the specific heat $c_F = 200$ J/(kg·°C). Initial power $P_0 = 1$ MW. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. Define Inhour equation as the *characteristic equation* of the linearized PKE:

$$\rho = \Lambda \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \, \omega}{\omega + \lambda_i}$$

2. **Derivation steps (one-group):**
   - Assume $n(t), C(t) \propto e^{\omega t}$.
   - Substitute into precursor equation: $C = \dfrac{\beta}{\Lambda(\omega + \lambda)}\, n$.
   - Substitute into neutron equation and solve for $\rho$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega n &= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \lambda C \\
\omega &= \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \frac{\lambda \beta}{\Lambda(\omega + \lambda)} \\
\rho &= \Lambda \omega + \frac{\beta \omega}{\omega + \lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Physical significance of roots:** The Inhour equation is a 7th-degree polynomial in $\omega$ (six-group), so it has **seven roots** $\omega_0, \omega_1, \ldots, \omega_6$:
   - The **smallest positive root** $\omega_0$ gives the *stable* (asymptotic) period $T = 1/\omega_0$.
   - The other **six roots are negative** — they correspond to decaying transients.
   - When $\rho = 0$ (critical), $\omega_0 = 0 \Rightarrow T_0 = \infty$ (steady power).
   - When $\rho = \beta$ (prompt critical), $\omega_0$ jumps to the *prompt* time scale.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Logic note:** First compute the fuel time constant $\tau_0 = \Lambda / (m_F c_F)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_0 &= \frac{\Lambda}{m_F c_F} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{5} \times 200} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{7}} \\
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s/°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the exponential argument:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\rho_0^2}{2 \alpha \tau_0} &= \frac{(0.01)^2}{2 \times (-5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 2.5 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times (-1.25 \times 10^{-16})} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{-2.5 \times 10^{-16}} \\
&= -4.0 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL ALGEBRAIC TRAP:** The fuel temperature coefficient $\alpha$ is **negative** in a self-regulating reactor. The exponential in the Nordheim–Fuchs formula therefore has a *negative* argument, and the peak power equation must be applied carefully. The sign convention is:
> $$\frac{\rho_0^2}{2 \alpha \tau_0}$$ is **negative** when $\alpha < 0$, leading to $P_{max} < P_0$ — *which is unphysical*. This indicates that the *simple* Nordheim–Fuchs model breaks down for negative $\alpha$ at the time scale considered, and a *full* PKE-with-feedback simulation is needed. Students should write this conclusion explicitly in the answer.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** A negative feedback coefficient makes the reactor *self-limiting* — power growth is automatically arrested. The simple Nordheim–Fuchs formula is only valid for $\alpha > 0$ (e.g., a *prompt-burst* experiment such as Godiva or kinetics experiments in fast burst reactors).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost on This Module**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** when deriving PKE — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $\Lambda$ and $\ell$** at non-critical conditions — KTU checkers actively look for this.
> 3. **Forgetting that $T = 1/\omega$, not $T = \omega$** — appears in 1 out of 4 answer sheets.
> 4. **Using absolute value of $\alpha$** in Nordheim–Fuchs without considering sign — this loses the entire 2-mark "safety interpretation" subpart.
> 5. **Failing to convert reactivity** between *dollars* and *absolute* form before using the Inhour equation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1: Reactor Kinetics & Dynamics**

- **Reactivity:** $\rho = (k-1)/k$ — dimensionless; 1 \$ = $\beta \approx 0.0065$ for U-235.
- **Point Kinetics Equations:** Two coupled ODEs (1 neutron + 1 or 6 precursor equations) — must be derived from neutron balance.
- **Inhour Equation:** $\rho = \Lambda \omega + \sum_i \beta_i \omega / (\omega + \lambda_i)$ — transcendental, **7 roots** for 6-group formulation.
- **Stable period:** smallest positive root $T_0 = 1/\omega_0$.
- **Prompt critical:** $\rho = \beta$ — the boundary between controllable and uncontrollable regimes.
- **Nordheim–Fuchs:** $P_{max} = P_0 \exp(\rho_0^2 / (2 \alpha \tau_0))$ — applicable only for *fast* excursions with *positive* $\alpha$; for $\alpha < 0$, the result is unphysical, indicating self-regulation.
- **Reactivity coefficients:** $\alpha_F$ (Doppler, always negative for UO₂), $\alpha_M$ (moderator — negative for LWRs, positive for BWRs at low pressure), $\alpha_V$ (void — context dependent).
- **Generation time:** $\Lambda \approx 10^{-4}$ s (thermal), $10^{-8}$ s (fast). Memorize the order of magnitude.
- **Six-group delayed neutron data:** Memorize the half-lives ($\sim$0.2 s to 55 s) and the rough yield fractions.
- **Reactor scram:** Emergency insertion of $\rho \approx -10\,\$$ or more; trip setpoints based on period (e.g., $T < 5$ s in PWRs) and high neutron flux.
- **Safety philosophy:** *Defense in depth*, *single failure criterion*, *redundancy* — the Inhour equation and PKE are the *theoretical backbone* of all safety analyses.
- **KTU hot topics (most frequently asked):** Prompt criticality, control-rod worth calibration, Inhour equation solution, Nordheim–Fuchs model, Doppler feedback sign, delayed neutron groups.
- **Numerical short-cuts:** For *small* $\rho$: $T \approx (\beta - \rho)/(\rho \lambda_{eff})$; for *large* $\rho$: $T \approx \Lambda/\rho$ k.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactivity Feedback, Core Composition Changes During Reactor Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reactivity Feedback, Core Composition Changes During Reactor Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reactivity (ρ) – Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactivity (ρ)** is a dimensionless measure of the departure of the effective neutron multiplication factor ($k_{eff}$) of a reactor core from unity (criticality). It quantifies the fractional change in neutron population per neutron generation.

The mathematical expression as per the KTU 2024 syllabus is:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$$

Equivalently, in terms of excess reactivity:

$$\rho = \frac{\Delta k}{k_{eff}}$$

Where:
- $k_{eff} = 1$ → Critical (ρ = 0)
- $k_{eff} > 1$ → Supercritical (ρ > 0)
- $k_{eff} < 1$ → Subcritical (ρ < 0)

Reactivity is conventionally expressed in units of:
- **Absolute reactivity**: $\Delta k/k$
- **Per cent mille (pcm)**: $1\,pcm = 10^{-5}\,\Delta k/k$
- **Dollar ($)**: Defined relative to the effective delayed neutron fraction $\beta_{eff}$
- **Nile**: Defined as $\rho / \beta_{eff}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reactivity Matters in Nuclear Safety:**
> Any unplanned, uncontrolled insertion of positive reactivity can lead to a **power excursion**. Conversely, large negative reactivity insertions can cause **reactor shutdown** or **xenon-induced poisoning** problems. The science of *reactivity feedback* is therefore the cornerstone of inherent reactor safety.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Thermostat in the Reactor"

Imagine a **room heater** controlled by a thermostat. If the room gets too warm, the thermostat automatically *reduces* the heating. If it gets cold, it increases heating. This is called **negative feedback**, and it is inherently *self-stabilizing*.

A nuclear reactor is built with the same principle. The reactor designer embeds *physical mechanisms* within the fuel, moderator, and coolant such that:
- If the fuel or moderator **temperature rises**, the chain reaction *automatically slows down* (negative reactivity is inserted).
- If the temperature **falls**, the chain reaction speeds up slightly.

This is **reactivity feedback** – the reactor's built-in *passive safety thermostat*.

In stark contrast, if a reactor possessed *positive* temperature coefficients, a small rise in temperature would accelerate the chain reaction, generating more heat, which in turn raises the temperature further – a **thermal runaway** (analogous to thermal explosion). Modern reactor designs rigorously avoid this scenario.

## 1.3 Core Composition Changes – Plain English View

As a reactor operates, the **atomic inventory** inside the core continuously changes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core composition changes** refer to the time-dependent evolution of nuclide concentrations in the reactor core due to neutron-induced transmutations. These changes alter $k_{eff}$ and hence reactivity throughout the fuel cycle.

The principal physical processes are:

1. **Fissile depletion** – The "fuel" atoms (chiefly $^{235}$U, sometimes $^{239}$Pu) are consumed by fission.
2. **Fertile conversion** – The "fertile" atoms ($^{238}$U, $^{232}$Th) absorb neutrons to become new fissile atoms ($^{239}$Pu, $^{233}$U).
3. **Fission product accumulation** – Fission creates lighter elements, some of which are strong neutron absorbers (*poisons*).
4. **Actinide buildup** – Higher-mass transuranic elements (Am, Cm, Np) gradually form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A typical 1000 MWe PWR operates on an **18-month fuel cycle**, beginning with an *excess reactivity* of roughly **+10% Δk/k** (controlled by soluble boron and control rods), which gradually falls as $^{235}$U depletes. The longest-lived reactivity sink is **fissile depletion**, and the most volatile short-term reactivity perturbation is **Xenon-135 poisoning**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants (Bolded per KTU Standard)

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal neutron absorption cross-section of $^{135}$Xe | $\sigma_{a,Xe}$ | **2.6 × 10⁶ barns** |
| Thermal neutron absorption cross-section of $^{149}$Sm | $\sigma_{a,Sm}$ | **4.1 × 10⁴ barns** |
| Fission yield of $^{135}$Xe (direct + from decay) | $\gamma_{Xe}$ | **~0.006** |
| Fission yield of $^{135}$I (precursor of Xe) | $\gamma_{I}$ | **~0.061** |
| Half-life of $^{135}$I | $T_{1/2, I}$ | **6.57 hours** |
| Half-life of $^{135}$Xe | $T_{1/2, Xe}$ | **9.17 hours** |
| Fission yield of $^{149}$Sm | $\gamma_{Sm}$ | **~0.0135** |
| Decay constant of $^{135}$I | $\lambda_{I}$ | **2.92 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹** |
| Decay constant of $^{135}$Xe | $\lambda_{Xe}$ | **2.10 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reactivity Feedback Mechanisms – A Structured Walk-Through

The reactivity of a reactor core is a function of numerous state variables: fuel temperature $T_f$, moderator temperature $T_m$, coolant density $\rho_c$, and the boron concentration $C_B$, etc. The **total reactivity** can be expanded as a **Taylor series** about an operating point:

$$\rho(T_f, T_m, \rho_c, C_B) = \rho_0 + \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T_f}\right)\Delta T_f + \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T_m}\right)\Delta T_m + \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial \rho_c}\right)\Delta \rho_c + \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial C_B}\right)\Delta C_B + \cdots$$

The partial derivatives are called **reactivity coefficients**. The most important ones are:

### 2.1.1 Fuel Temperature Coefficient (Doppler Effect)
- **Cause:** As fuel temperature rises, $^{238}$U resonance absorption peaks broaden (Doppler broadening).
- **Result:** More neutrons are parasitically captured in the resonance region before they can cause fission.
- **Sign:** Always **negative** in LWRs.
- **Magnitude:** $\alpha_{Doppler} \approx -2$ to $-4\,pcm/K$ (prompt, instantaneous response).
- **Safety importance:** *First* feedback to act; credited in the **EBR-II** and **PHEBUS** passive safety tests.

### 2.1.2 Moderator Temperature Coefficient (MTC)
- **Cause:** Higher moderator temperature → lower density → fewer water molecules per unit volume → reduced moderation and increased neutron leakage.
- **Result:** Lower thermal neutron flux, fewer fissions.
- **Sign:** **Negative** in PWRs (and in BWRs with proper void-fraction design). Positive in small, under-moderated reactors (a serious safety concern).
- **Magnitude:** $\alpha_{MTC} \approx -1$ to $-8\,pcm/^{\circ}C$ (PWR; depends strongly on boron concentration).

### 2.1.3 Void Coefficient (BWR Specific)
- **Cause:** Steam voids in BWR coolant reduce moderation efficiency.
- **Result:** Large negative reactivity insertion in modern BWRs (e.g., ABWR).
- **Sign:** Strongly **negative** in well-designed BWRs.

### 2.1.4 Boron Concentration Coefficient
- **Cause:** Soluble boron (as boric acid $H_3BO_3$) in PWR coolant absorbs neutrons via $^{10}B(n,\alpha)^{7}Li$.
- **Result:** Boron concentration changes directly translate to reactivity changes.
- **Sign:** Always **negative** (more boron → less reactivity).

### 2.1.5 Power Coefficient of Reactivity
The combined effect of all temperature-dependent feedbacks at constant pressure:

$$\alpha_P = \frac{d\rho}{dP} \quad \text{(pcm/% power)}$$

For a PWR: $\alpha_P \approx -10$ to $-25\,pcm/\%$. This single coefficient is what gives the reactor its **inherent stability** – if power rises, reactivity automatically drops.

## 2.2 Core Composition Changes – Detailed Mechanisms

### 2.2.1 Fissile Isotope Depletion
The rate of change of fissile atom density $N_f$:

$$\frac{dN_f}{dt} = -N_f \sigma_{a,f} \phi$$

Solution:

$$N_f(t) = N_f(0) \exp(-\sigma_{a,f} \phi t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exponential decay of fissile density with **fluence** ($\phi t$) is the dominant long-term reactivity sink.

### 2.2.2 Fertile-to-Fissile Conversion
For $^{238}$U capturing a neutron and decaying to $^{239}$Pu:

$$\frac{dN_{Pu}}{dt} = N_{U8} \sigma_{c,U8} \phi - N_{Pu} \sigma_{a,Pu} \phi$$

The **conversion ratio** is:

$$CR = \frac{\text{Rate of fissile atom production}}{\text{Rate of fissile atom destruction}}$$

When $CR > 1$ → **breeding** (possible in fast reactors with fertile blanket).

### 2.2.3 Fission Product Poisons
Two poisons dominate reactor physics:

**(a) Xenon-135 (the volatile poison):**
- $^{135}$I (precursor) → $^{135}$Xe → disappears by decay + neutron absorption.
- After a power reduction, **xenon concentration temporarily rises** ("xenon peak") because iodine continues to decay into xenon, while xenon decay and burnup are reduced. This is the famous **xenon transient** that operators must manage during load follow.

**(b) Samarium-149 (the stable poison):**
- $^{149}$Pm (precursor, half-life **53.1 hours**) → $^{149}$Sm (stable, huge cross-section).
- Reaches **equilibrium** at all power levels but the equilibrium value rises with neutron flux.
- After shutdown, Sm concentration grows from Pm decay, reaching a maximum ~10-15 days later (**samarium peak**).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Master every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units / Sign | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reactivity | $\rho = (k_{eff}-1)/k_{eff}$ | dimensionless; $\Delta k/k$ | Core criticality assessment |
| 2 | Reactivity in dollars | $\rho_{\$} = \rho / \beta_{eff}$ | $ | Delayed-neutron based safety analysis |
| 3 | Reactivity in pcm | $1\,pcm = 10^{-5}\,\Delta k/k$ | pcm | Small reactivity worth measurement |
| 4 | Doppler reactivity | $\Delta \rho_D \propto -\ln(T_f)$ | negative; $-2$ to $-4\,pcm/K$ | Transient safety (RIA) |
| 5 | MTC | $\alpha_{MTC} = \partial \rho/\partial T_m$ | $pcm/^{\circ}C$ | PWR load-follow capability |
| 6 | Power coefficient | $\alpha_P = d\rho/dP$ | $pcm/\%$ | Inherent stability criterion |
| 7 | Fissile depletion | $N_f(t)=N_f(0)\,e^{-\sigma_a\phi t}$ | atoms/cm³ | Fuel cycle length |
| 8 | Xe-135 eq. conc. | $X_{eq} = [(\gamma_I + \gamma_X)\Sigma_f \phi]/(\lambda_X + \sigma_{a,X}\phi)$ | atoms/cm³ | Peak xenon calculation |
| 9 | Xe reactivity | $\Delta \rho_{Xe} = -\sigma_{a,X} X / \Sigma_a^{fuel}$ | $\Delta k/k$ (negative) | Shutdown margin |
| 10 | Sm-149 eq. conc. | $Sm_{eq} = \gamma_{Pm} \Sigma_f \phi / \sigma_{a,Sm} \phi = \gamma_{Pm} \Sigma_f / \sigma_{a,Sm}$ | atoms/cm³ (independent of flux) | Samarium poisoning |
| 11 | Conversion ratio | $CR = R_{prod}/R_{dest}$ | dimensionless | Breeding feasibility |
| 12 | Burnup | $Bu = \Sigma_f \phi t \,(MWd/MTU)$ | MWd/MTU | Fuel performance |

> [!NOTE]
