# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]`
**Define the term "Nuclear System Lifetime" and distinguish between the "Design Life" and "Licensed Life" of a nuclear power plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Nuclear system lifetime is the total duration from initial design through decommissioning during which a nuclear plant, along with its SSCs, remains fit to perform its safety and power-generation functions under all operational states and accident conditions, in compliance with the regulatory operating licence.

- **Design Life (1 mark):** This is the engineering baseline used at the design stage to size components, select materials, and compute fatigue and corrosion allowances. For Gen II PWRs/BWRs, the design life is typically **40 years**.

- **Licensed Life (1 mark):** This is the operational period formally authorized by the regulatory body (AERB in India, NRC in the USA) through an **Operating Licence**. While usually equal to the design life initially, it can be **extended** through a formal **Licence Renewal Application (LRA)**, subject to demonstration of an adequate **Aging Management Program**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set B]`
**List any four degradation mechanisms that limit the operational lifetime of a nuclear power plant. For each, name one safety-significant component affected.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Degradation Mechanism | Component Affected |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Neutron irradiation embrittlement | Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) |
| 2 | Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC) | Steam Generator tubes / Piping |
| 3 | Thermal fatigue | Surge line, pressurizer spray nozzles |
| 4 | Creep | High-temperature piping, turbine rotors |
| 5 | (Bonus) Radiation-induced swelling | Fast-reactor core cladding |

(1 mark for listing mechanisms; 1 mark for correct component linkage; 1 mark for completeness/clarity.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Pattern):** All Part-B questions carry **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A.** (a) Explain the four phases of a nuclear power plant's lifetime in detail, with approximate durations for a typical 1000 MWe PWR. Discuss the role of the **Periodic Safety Review (PSR)** in extending the operational life.

**(b)** A PWR operates at a thermal power of $3000$ MW with an initial fuel loading of $80$ tonnes of Uranium. The reactor operates continuously at full power for **18 months (540 days)**. Calculate the **specific burnup** at the end of this cycle. If the regulatory limit for discharge burnup is **45,000 MWd/tU**, how many such cycles can the fuel complete before mandatory discharge?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Phase I — Pre-Operational (1.5 marks):** Site selection, environmental impact assessment, licensing (AERB/NRC), construction, commissioning. Typical duration: **8 – 12 years**.

- **Phase II — Operational (1.5 marks):** Power operation under licence. Typically **40 years** for the original licence, extendable to **60 or 80 years**. Subject to PSR every **10 years**.

- **Phase III — Decommissioning (1.5 marks):** Permanent shutdown, defuelling, and dismantling. Two main strategies:
  - **DECON** (immediate dismantling): 5 – 10 years
  - **SAFSTOR** (safe enclosure): 30 – 60 years

- **Phase IV — Site Release (1 mark):** Final radiological survey, license termination, and unrestricted site release.

- **Role of PSR (1.5 marks):** The PSR is a comprehensive re-evaluation conducted every 10 years covering **safety, ageing, emergency planning, radiation protection, and operational feedback**. Its outcome determines whether the plant may continue operation for the next PSR interval. The PSR is the cornerstone of the **Long-Term Operation (LTO)** framework, enabling licence renewal by demonstrating continued compliance with modern safety standards.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute total thermal energy released in one cycle.**

$$
E = P_{\text{rated}} \times t = 3000 \; \text{MW} \times 540 \; \text{days} = 1{,}620{,}000 \; \text{MWd}
$$

**Step 2: Compute specific burnup at end of cycle.**

$$
\text{BU} = \frac{E}{M_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{1{,}620{,}000 \; \text{MWd}}{80 \; \text{tU}} = 20{,}250 \; \text{MWd/tU}
$$

**Step 3: Determine number of cycles before discharge limit.**

Let $N$ = number of cycles. Then:
$$
N \times 20{,}250 \; \text{MWd/tU} \leq 45{,}000 \; \text{MWd/tU}
$$

$$
N \leq \frac{45{,}000}{20{,}250} \approx 2.22
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, the fuel can complete **2 full cycles** (giving $2 \times 20{,}250 = 40{,}500 \; \text{MWd/tU}$) but **cannot complete a third cycle** (which would require an additional $20{,}250$ MWd/tU, exceeding the limit by $15{,}750$ MWd/tU).

**Conclusion:** The fuel assembly will be **discharged at the end of the 2nd cycle** with a burnup of **≈ 40,500 MWd/tU** (slightly below the limit, which is regulatorily acceptable as it preserves safety margin). The third cycle would require a **partial burnup credit** or a lower power operation.

**Answer boxed:**
$$
\boxed{\text{BU}_{\text{cycle}} = 20{,}250 \;\text{MWd/tU}, \quad N_{\max} = 2 \;\text{cycles}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**B.** (a) Discuss in detail the **Aging Management Program (AMP)** as a regulatory framework for extending nuclear plant life. List the **ten elements** of a comprehensive AMP and explain how the **Inservice Inspection (ISI)** supports it.

**(b)** Using the **sigmoidal embrittlement model** $\Delta \text{RT}_{\text{NDT}} = A[1 - \exp(-\alpha \Phi)]$, compute the shift in reference temperature for a reactor pressure vessel steel with $A = 110 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-19}$ cm²/n, after a fast neutron fluence of $\Phi = 6 \times 10^{18}$ n/cm². Given that the initial $\text{RT}_{\text{NDT}} = -30 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the AERB regulatory upper limit is $132 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$, verify whether the vessel satisfies the safety margin.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Definition and Purpose (2 marks):** The AMP is a structured, documented set of activities that ensure all safety-significant **Structures, Systems, and Components (SSCs)** remain capable of performing their intended safety functions throughout the plant's licensed life. It is mandated by AERB under safety codes aligned with **IAEA SALTO (Safety Aspects of Long Term Operation)** and **10 CFR 54** (USA).

- **The 10 Elements of AMP (3 marks):**
  1. **Scope** of SSCs
  2. **Preventive Actions**
  3. **Parameters Monitored**
  4. **Detection of Aging Effects**
  5. **Trending and Monitoring**
  6. **Acceptance Criteria**
  7. **Corrective Actions**
  8. **Confirmation Process**
  9. **Administrative Controls**
  10. **Operating Experience Feedback**

- **Role of ISI (2 marks):** In-Service Inspection is the **eyes and ears** of the AMP. It uses Non-Destructive Examination (NDE) techniques — **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**, **Eddy Current Testing (ECT)**, **Visual Testing (VT)**, and **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** — to detect flaws (cracks, wall thinning, corrosion) during plant outages. ISI results feed directly into the **trending and acceptance criteria** of the AMP, allowing timely corrective actions (e.g., plugging of leaking steam generator tubes).

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $A = 110 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$
- $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-19}$ cm²/n
- $\Phi = 6 \times 10^{18}$ n/cm²
- $\text{RT}_{\text{NDT, initial}} = -30 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Regulatory limit: $132 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$

**Step 1: Compute the dimensionless exponent.**

$$
\alpha \Phi = 1.2 \times 10^{-19} \times 6 \times 10^{18} = 7.2 \times 10^{-1} = 0.72
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the exponential term.**

$$
\exp(-0.72) = 0.4868 \quad \text{(from standard tables)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the shift.**

$$
\Delta \text{RT}_{\text{NDT}} = 110 \times (1 - 0.4868) = 110 \times 0.5132 = 56.45 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the end-of-life reference temperature.**

$$
\text{RT}_{\text{NDT, EOL}} = \text{RT}_{\text{NDT, initial}} + \Delta \text{RT}_{\text{NDT}} = -30 + 56.45 = 26.45 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with regulatory limit.**

$$
26.45 \;^{\circ}\text{C} \;\ll\; 132 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 6: State the available margin.**

$$
\text{Margin} = 132 - 26.45 = 105.55 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel easily satisfies the AERB safety margin. The large available margin (≈ 105.55 °C) implies that the vessel could tolerate **approximately twice the current fluence** (i.e., operation well beyond the current licence period) before approaching the regulatory limit, provided the sigmoidal model remains valid. This is a typical quantitative basis for justifying **licence renewal for Long-Term Operation (LTO)**.

**Answer boxed:**
$$
\boxed{\Delta \text{RT}_{\text{NDT}} = 56.45 \;^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad \text{RT}_{\text{NDT, EOL}} = 26.45 \;^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad \text{Margin} = 105.55 \;^{\circ}\text{C} \;\; \checkmark}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing burnup units:** Students often mix up **MWd/kg** vs. **MWd/tonne**. Remember, **1 MWd/kg = 1000 MWd/tonne**. The KTU convention is **MWd/tU**.
> 2. **Ignoring the safety margin in Part-B solutions:** The model answer always compares the result against the regulatory limit (132 °C). Forgetting this comparison **loses 2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to state units:** Always carry **units through** the derivation. A number without units is incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping Taylor expansion explanation:** When asked about low-fluence behaviour, KTU expects the **Taylor expansion derivation** (Step 2 in Section 3.2), not just a statement.
> 5. **Mixing up Plant Lifetime and Component Lifetime:** *Plant lifetime* (40–80 yrs) ≠ *Fuel assembly lifetime* (4–6 yrs in-core) ≠ *Decay heat removal time* (months to years). Distinguish clearly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Nuclear System Lifetime**

- ☐ **Nuclear System Lifetime** = total duration (design → decommissioning) during which SSCs remain safety-fit.
- ☐ The lifetime has **four tiers**: *Design Life* (40 yrs), *Licensed Life* (40 yrs initially), *Technical Life* (40–80+ yrs), *Economic Life* (30–60 yrs).
- ☐ **Five aging mechanisms** (the "Big Five"): Embrittlement, Thermal Aging, SCC, Fatigue, Creep/Swelling.
- ☐ **Burnup** is the energy extracted per tonne of initial heavy metal: $\text{BU} = \frac{\int P \,dt}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ [MWd/tU].
- ☐ **Discharge burnup limit** for LWR fuel: **45,000 – 60,000 MWd/tU**.
- ☐ **Neutron fluence** $\Phi = \phi \cdot t$ governs irradiation damage (dpa, embrittlement).
- ☐ **DPA (Displacements per Atom)** is the universal metric for neutron-induced material damage.
- ☐ **Embrittlement model** (sigmoidal): $\Delta \text{RT}_{\text{NDT}} = A[1 - \exp(-\alpha \Phi)]$; **linear** at low $\Phi$, **saturates** at $A$ for high $\Phi$.
- ☐ **RPV safety criterion:** $\text{RT}_{\text{NDT, EOL}} + \text{Margin} \leq 132 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ (AERB/NRC).
- ☐ **Miner's Rule:** cumulative fatigue damage $D = \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i}$; failure at $D \geq 1.0$.
- ☐ **Paris Law** for crack growth: $\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$.
- ☐ **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP):** $T[C + \log_{10}(t_r)]$ — used to predict creep rupture life.
- ☐ **AMP** has **10 elements** (scope, prevention, monitoring, detection, trending, acceptance, correction, confirmation, administration, OPEX).
- ☐ **PSR (Periodic Safety Review)** is conducted every **10 years**; it is the regulatory gateway for **Long-Term Operation (LTO)** and **Licence Renewal**.
- ☐ **Licence Renewal** can extend plant life from **40 → 60 → 80 years**, with each extension requiring a dedicated LRA submission.
- ☐ **Decommissioning strategies:** DECON (5–10 yrs) vs. SAFSTOR (30–60 yrs) vs. ENTOMBMENT (in-situ burial, very long term).
- ☐ **Constants to memorize:** PWR primary pressure **155 bar**, PWR primary temperature **321 °C**, fast-neutron E > 1 MeV for embrittlement, RPV end-of-license fluence ≈ **1 × 10¹⁹ n/cm²**.
- ☐ **Three pillars of lifetime safety:** Public Safety, Economic Viability, Waste Management Planning.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 must-remember line:** *"Lifetime is not extended by the AMP; rather, the AMP demonstrates that the inherent lifetime is being preserved within safety bounds."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Requirements in Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control Requirements in Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Control Requirements in Design Considerations** refer to the systematic set of engineering, operational, and regulatory design mandates imposed on nuclear reactor systems, structures, and components (SSCs) to ensure that reactivity, power distribution, coolant inventory, pressure, temperature, and radiation fields are maintained within prescribed safety limits throughout all operational states and postulated accident conditions.

These requirements are derived from the fundamental **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** philosophy and are codified under regulatory frameworks such as the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) Safety Code AERB/NPP-1/2004**, **IAEA Safety Standards (SSR-2/1, NS-R-1)**, and the **US NRC 10 CFR 50 Appendix A (General Design Criteria - GDC)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Control requirements are NOT an afterthought — they are **built into the very first engineering drawing** of a nuclear plant. The principle is *"Safety must be designed in, not added on."*

The control requirements typically govern:
- **Reactivity Control** (control rods, boron, burnable poisons)
- **Inventory Control** (pressurizer, CVCS, ECCS)
- **Heat Removal Control** (RCS flow, cooldown rates, residual heat removal)
- **Containment Control** (isolation, spray, ventilation)
- **Instrumentation & Control (I&C)** (sensors, trip systems, control rooms)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Cockpit" View

Think of a nuclear reactor as a **commercial passenger jet flying at 35,000 feet**.

| Aircraft Equivalent | Nuclear Plant Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Autopilot | Reactor Regulating System (RRS) |
| Co-pilot override | Backup Control Rod Drive Mechanism |
| Engine fire suppression | Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) |
| Black box + cockpit alarms | Safety Injection System + Annunciation |
| Crash-resistant fuel tanks | Containment Building |
| Pre-flight inspection checklist | Defence-in-Depth Level-1 (Conservative Design) |

> A pilot does not rely on a **single altimeter** to avoid crashing a mountain. Similarly, a nuclear designer does not rely on a **single temperature sensor** or a **single control rod** to prevent a core melt. **Multiple, independent, and diverse** control channels must be present — each capable of safely shutting the reactor down, even if all others fail.

---

## 1.3 The "Three Pillars" of Control Design

1. **Functional Capability** — The system *can* perform the required safety function (e.g., SCRAM).
2. **Reliability** — The system *will* perform when called upon (high reliability, demonstrated through testing).
3. **Independence** — The system is *isolated* from common-mode failures (electrical, physical, logical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standards Referenced in this Module:**
> - **AERB Safety Classification:** Class-1, 2, 3 systems (based on safety significance)
> - **Single Failure Criterion (SFC):** System must tolerate **any single failure** without loss of safety function
> - **Unavailability Target for Class-1 systems:** < **10⁻³** per demand (for initiating events)
> - **Core Damage Frequency (CDF) Goal:** < **10⁻⁵ per reactor-year**
> - **Large Early Release Frequency (LERF):** < **10⁻⁶ per reactor-year**

---

## 1.4 Why "Control Requirements" Are a Standalone Topic

Unlike a chemical plant where over-pressure can be handled by a relief valve, a nuclear reactor's **delayed neutron precursors** give a *finite but small time window* (seconds to minutes) to act before fuel damage escalates. Hence, the **speed, reliability, and independence** of control actions are not negotiable — they are **mathematically deterministic** safety criteria.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Control Requirements

The control requirements in nuclear design are structured into a **five-tier hierarchy** (mapped to the **five layers of Defence-in-Depth**):

### Tier 1 — Conservative Design (DiD Level 1)
- Inherent safety features (e.g., **negative void coefficient** in PWRs, **negative temperature coefficient** in all LWRs)
- High quality of materials, components, and fabrication
- **Margin to design limits** (e.g., DNBR ≥ 1.30 for PWR, MDNBR limit)
- Conservative selection of **Design Basis Accidents (DBAs)**

### Tier 2 — Operational Control (DiD Level 2)
- Operational limits and conditions (OLCs)
- Surveillance, testing, and maintenance programs
- Operating procedures, training of reactor operators
- **Reactor Regulating System (RRS)** — automatic power regulation to match load demand (in load-following mode)

### Tier 3 — Engineered Safety Features (DiD Level 3 — Active)
- Reactor Protection System (RPS) / **SCRAM** (Safety Control Rod Activation Mechanism)
- Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) — High-Pressure Injection, Low-Pressure Injection, Accumulators
- Containment Spray System
- Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) closure
- **Auxiliary Feedwater System (AFWS)** — for PWRs

### Tier 4 — Accident Management (DiD Level 4)
- Severe Accident Management Guidelines (SAMGs)
- Hydrogen recombiners (Passive autocatalytic recombiners - PARs)
- Core catchers, ex-vessel cooling
- Long-term cooling and fission product retention

### Tier 5 — Emergency Response (DiD Level 5)
- Off-site emergency plans
- Public protective actions (evacuation, sheltering, iodine prophylaxis)
- Environmental monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pearl:** "Name the 5 levels of Defence-in-Depth" — a direct 2-mark short-answer question in nearly every KTU university exam. Memorize the order: **Conservative Design → Operational → ESF → Accident Management → Emergency Response**.

---

## 2.2 The Five Key Engineering Principles for Control System Design

| # | Principle | Engineering Meaning | Practical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Redundancy** | Multiple identical components performing the same function (typically N+1 or 2-out-of-4) | Four independent RPS trip channels in a PWR |
| 2 | **Diversity** | Different physical principles or technologies to perform the same function (protects against common-mode failure) | Both borated water AND control rods for shutdown; both high-pressure and low-pressure ECCS pumps |
| 3 | **Independence** | Physical and electrical separation between redundant channels | Separate cable trays, separate I&C cabinets, separate power supplies |
| 4 | **Single Failure Criterion (SFC)** | System must perform its safety function despite loss of *any single active component* | RPS must SCRAM with 3 of 4 channels operable (loss of 1 channel tolerated) |
| 5 | **Fail-Safe Design** | System moves to a safe state upon loss of power or signal | Control rods fall in by gravity on loss of power; MSIVs close on loss of air/power |

---

## 2.3 Reactivity Control — The Heart of Reactor Design

Reactor control is fundamentally about managing the **effective multiplication factor (k_eff)**. The control requirements translate into:

$$k_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon}{1 + \text{(leakage and non-fission losses)}}$$

For long-term operation, the requirement is:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}} \quad \text{(reactivity)}$$

The **total reactivity worth of all control systems** must exceed the **maximum excess reactivity** of the core, with a sufficient **shutdown margin**.

### Reactivity Control Methods (Comparative)

| Method | Used In | Speed | Typical Worth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control Rods (Ag-In-Cd or B₄C) | PWR, BWR, PHWR | Fast (~2 sec for full insertion) | ~5–10 % Δk/k |
| Soluble Boron (H₃BO₃) | PWR | Slow (chemical shim) | Up to ~15–20 % Δk/k |
| Burnable Poisons (Gd₂O₃, Er₂O₃) | PWR, BWR | Passive (burns out) | ~2–8 % Δk/k |
| Liquid Poison (Gd nitrate) | BWR (standalone) | Medium | ~1–2 % Δk/k |
| Shim/Safety Rods | PHWR | Medium | Distributed worth |
| Adjustable Rods | PHWR (adjuster rods) | Slow | Fine control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reactivity Shutdown Margin (RSD) Requirement (AERB):**
> - **Hot zero power, all rods inserted EXCEPT the single rod of highest worth withdrawn:** $k_{\text{eff}} \leq 0.99$
> - This ensures **sub-criticality ≥ 1 % Δk/k** even in the worst-case stuck-rod condition.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective Multiplication Factor | $k_{\text{eff}} = \eta f p \varepsilon P_{NL} P_{FT}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Reactivity | $\rho = (k_{\text{eff}} - 1) / k_{\text{eff}}$ | Δk/k or "dollars" (1 \$ = β_eff) |
| 3 | Subcriticality Margin | $\Delta k = 1 - k_{\text{eff}}$ | ≥ 0.01 for cold shutdown |
| 4 | Reactivity Insertion Rate Limit (PWR) | $d\rho/dt \leq 0.5 \text{ \$/sec}$ | Prevents excessive peak power |
| 5 | Shutdown Rod Group Drop Time | $t_{\text{drop}} \leq 2.0$ sec | AERB/ASTM requirement |
| 6 | Single Failure Allowed | Loss of any *one* active component | N+1 redundancy rule |
| 7 | Unavailability Target (Class-1) | $Q \leq 10^{-3}$ per demand | Demonstrated by reliability block diagram |
| 8 | DNBR (Departure from Nucleate Boiling Ratio) | $q'' / q''_{\text{DNB}}$ | Limit ≥ 1.30 (W-3 correlation) |
| 9 | Pressurizer Pressure Band | $P \in [P_{\text{nom}} \pm 0.21 \text{ MPa}]$ | PWR typical ~15.5 MPa |
| 10 | Cooldown Rate Limit | $dT/dt \leq 55 \text{ °C/hr}$ | Prevents thermal shock to RPV |
| 11 | Containment Design Pressure | $P_{\text{design}} = 0.45 \text{ MPa (g)}$ | Mark-III containment typical |
| 12 | LERF Goal | $\leq 10^{-6} \text{ ry}^{-1}$ | Safety goal metric |
| 13 | CDF Goal | $\leq 10^{-5} \text{ ry}^{-1}$ | Core damage frequency |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These control requirements are not academic exercises. They directly drive:
- **Reactor Protection System architecture** (e.g., Westinghouse Eagle, ABB ACCU-100, Siemens Teleperm XS)
- **Reactor Control Console (Main Control Room)** human-factors engineering
- **Safety Analysis Report (SAR) Chapter 15** — Accident Analysis
- **Probabilistic Safety Assessment (PSA) — Level-1 & Level-2**

In a 1000 MWe PWR, the **RPS alone** uses **~4000 sensors, 2000 cables, and 4 redundant trip channels** — all governed by these control requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shutdown Margin and Reactivity Balance

The fundamental reactivity control requirement is that **all control mechanisms combined** must be able to keep the reactor subcritical even in the most reactive configuration. We will derive the **Shutdown Reactivity Requirement** formally.

### Step 1: Define Core Excess Reactivity

After a fresh refueling, the clean cold core has the **maximum excess reactivity** $\rho_{\text{ex}}$:

$$\rho_{\text{ex}} = \frac{k_{\text{eff,clean,cold}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff,clean,cold}}}$$

For a typical PWR, $\rho_{\text{ex}}$ can reach **+0.20 to +0.25** in $\Delta k/k$ (depending on enrichment and burnable poison loading).

### Step 2: Reactivity Worths Available for Control

The reactor designer allocates *total* negative reactivity worth across multiple control systems:

$$\rho_{\text{control}} = \rho_{\text{rods}} + \rho_{\text{boron}} + \rho_{\text{burnable}}$$

with the constraint that the **most-reactivity-needed condition** is met with margins.

### Step 3: Apply AERB/ASTM Stuck-Rod Criterion

The reactor must remain subcritical with the **single most reactive control rod stuck in the fully withdrawn position** (AERB Safety Guide on Shutdown Systems, AERB/SG/D-24). The required **shutdown margin** is:

$$\Delta \rho_{\text{margin}} = (\rho_{\text{rods,total}} - \rho_{\text{rods,highest-worth}}) + \rho_{\text{boron,min}} - \rho_{\text{ex,max}}$$

This must satisfy:

$$\Delta \rho_{\text{margin}} \geq 0.01 \quad (\text{i.e., } k_{\text{eff}} \leq 0.99)$$

### Step 4: Derive Minimum Boron Concentration

For a PWR with given $\rho_{\text{ex,max}}$ and $\rho_{\text{rods,total}}$:

$$\rho_{\text{boron,required}} = \rho_{\text{ex,max}} - \rho_{\text{rods,effective}} + \Delta \rho_{\text{margin}}$$

Using the boron reactivity coefficient $\alpha_{\text{B}}$ (in pcm/ppm), the **minimum boron concentration** is:

$$C_{\text{B,min}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{boron,required}}}{\alpha_{\text{B}}}$$

with typical $\alpha_{\text{B}} \approx -8$ to $-12$ pcm/ppm (negative, since boron absorbs neutrons).

### Step 5: Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- $\rho_{\text{ex,max}} = +0.18 \, \Delta k/k = 18,000$ pcm
- Control rod total worth: $\rho_{\text{rods,total}} = 8,000$ pcm
- Highest-worth single rod: $\rho_{\text{rods,1}} = 1,200$ pcm
- Boron coefficient: $\alpha_{\text{B}} = -10$ pcm/ppm
- Required shutdown margin: $\Delta \rho_{\text{margin}} = 1,000$ pcm

**Calculation:**

\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{boron,required}} &= \rho_{\text{ex,max}} - \rho_{\text{rods,effective}} + \Delta \rho_{\text{margin}} \\
&= 18{,}000 - (8{,}000 - 1{,}200) + 1{,}000 \\
&= 18{,}000 - 6{,}800 + 1{,}000 \\
&= 12{,}200 \text{ pcm}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{B,min}} &= \frac{12{,}200 \text{ pcm}}{10 \text{ pcm/ppm}} \\
&= 1{,}220 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}

**Interpretation:** The boron concentration in the primary coolant must never fall below **1,220 ppm** at hot zero power (HZP), regardless of operational state. This is a hard interlock in the plant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Single Failure Criterion & Reliability

### Step 1: Define Unavailability Q

The **unavailability** of a safety system (probability of failure on demand) is modeled as:

$$Q = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t \quad \text{(for } \lambda t \ll 1\text{)}$$

For a 1-out-of-2 (1oo2) redundant system with identical channels (failure rate $\lambda$):

$$Q_{1oo2} = \frac{2 \lambda^2 t^2}{1} \approx 2 \lambda^2 t^2$$

For a 2-out-of-4 (2oo4) voted system:

$$Q_{2oo4} = \binom{4}{0} \lambda^4 t^4 + \binom{4}{1} \lambda^4 t^4 \cdot \text{(correction)} \approx 6 \lambda^2 t^2$$

### Step 2: Compare Architectural Redundancy

A **2oo4** architecture (typical for RPS trip logic) is preferred over 1oo2 because:
- Higher **safety** (rejects spurious trips better with 2oo4 logic)
- Tolerates single failure without compromising protection

For demonstration, let $\lambda = 10^{-5}/\text{hr}$, $t = 8760 \text{ hr (1 yr)}$:

\begin{aligned}
Q_{1oo1} &= \lambda t = 10^{-5} \times 8760 \approx 8.76 \times 10^{-2} \\
Q_{1oo2} &\approx 2 \lambda^2 t^2 = 2 \times (8.76 \times 10^{-2})^2 \approx 1.53 \times 10^{-2} \\
Q_{2oo4} &\approx 6 \lambda^2 t^2 \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}

(Note: Actual reliability block diagrams use **Markov modeling** with test intervals; the above is a simplified approximation for illustration.)

### Step 3: Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) Verification

For the **ECCS** (engineered safety feature), the target PFD is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{ECCS}} \leq 10^{-3} \text{ per demand}$$

This corresponds to the **Safety Class-1** threshold per AERB classification.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Control Requirements vs. Plant State

| Plant State | Primary Control Action | Backup Control Action | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Operation** | RRS adjusts rods/boron | Manual operator action | $k_{\text{eff}} = 1.0000 \pm 0.0001$ |
| **Load Following** | Rod motion + boron dilution | Generator load setpoint | Power < 110 % nominal |
| **Step Load Change (10 %)** | Automatic rod insertion | ARI (Axial Rod Index) | ARI within ±5 % |
| **Small LOCA** | HPIS (High-Pressure Injection) | Accumulators (passive) | Peak cladding T < 1200 °C |
| **Large LOCA** | LPIS + Accumulators | Core flood tanks | Zr-H₂O reaction < 1 % |
| **SBO (Station Blackout)** | AFWS (turbine-driven) | Batteries for I&C | RCS pressure < 17.2 MPa |
| **ATWS (Anticipated Transient Without Scram)** | Backup liquid poison (BWR) | Diverse rod insertion | $k_{\text{eff}} < 0.95$ within 60 s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defence-in-Depth Layered Control Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    did1["LEVEL 1 - CONSERVATIVE DESIGN\nInherent Safety - Quality Assurance\nNegative reactivity coefficients"]
    did2["LEVEL 2 - OPERATIONAL CONTROL\nLimits and Conditions - RRS\nOperating Procedures - Surveillance"]
    did3["LEVEL 3 - ENGINEERED SAFETY FEATURES\nRPS - SCRAM - ECCS - MSIV\nContainment Isolation - AFWS"]
    did4["LEVEL 4 - SEVERE ACCIDENT MANAGEMENT\nSAMGs - PARs - Core Catcher\nHydrogen Mitigation"]
    did5["LEVEL 5 - EMERGENCY RESPONSE\nOffsite Plans - Public Protection\nEnvironmental Monitoring"]

    did1 --> did2
    did2 --> did3
    did3 --> did4
    did4 --> did5

    pwr["PWR CONTROL SYSTEMS"] --> rrs["Reactor Regulating System"]
    pwr --> rps["Reactor Protection System\n2 out of 4 Trip Logic"]
    pwr --> esfs["Engineered Safety Features\nHPIS - LPIS - ACC - CSS"]
    pwr --> rcs["Reactor Control Rods\nAg In Cd or B4C"]
    pwr --> cvs["Chemical and Volume Control\nBoron Adjustment CVCS"]
    pwr --> afws["Auxiliary Feedwater System"]

    rps --> scram["SCRAM Function\nGravity Drop Insertion"]
    scram --> sdmargin["Shutdown Margin Verification\nk_eff below 0.99"]

    rrs --> reactivity["Reactivity Balance\nrho equals zero steady state"]
    cvs --> reactivity
    rcs --> reactivity
```

## 4.2 Reactor Protection System — Four-Channel Redundant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensorA["Sensor Channel A\nNeutron Flux and T_hot"] --> tripA["Trip Channel A\nCoherent Logic"]
    sensorB["Sensor Channel B"] --> tripB["Trip Channel B"]
    sensorC["Sensor Channel C"] --> tripC["Trip Channel C"]
    sensorD["Sensor Channel D"] --> tripD["Trip Channel D"]

    tripA --> vote["2 out of 4 Voter\nLogic Matrix"]
    tripB --> vote
    tripC --> vote
    tripD --> vote

    vote --> scramRelay["SCRAM Relays\nUndervoltage Trip"]
    scramRelay --> rodDrive["Rod Drive Mechanism\nPower Removed"]
    rodDrive --> gravity["Gravity Insertion\nFail Safe"]
    gravity --> safe["REACTOR SUBCRITICAL\nShutdown Achieved"]

    test["Periodic Channel Bypass\nSurveillance Test"] -.-> tripA
    test -.-> tripB
    test -.-> tripC
    test -.-> tripD
```

## 4.3 Reactivity Control Feedback Loop (Point Kinetics Perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    demand["Load Demand\nReactor Power Setpoint"] --> comp["Comparator\nNegative Feedback"]
    flux["Measured Neutron Flux\nExcore Detectors"] --> comp

    comp --> rrs["Reactor Regulating System\nPID Controller"]
    rrs --> rods["Control Rod Motion\nDrive Mechanism"]
    rrs --> boron["Boron Dilution or Boration\nCVCS Actuation"]

    rods --> core["Reactor Core\nk_eff Modulation"]
    boron --> core
    core --> flux

    temp["Reactor Coolant T avg"] --> coeff["Reactivity Feedback\nNegative Temperature Coefficient"]
    coeff --> core

    void["Void Fraction in BWR\nor Moderator Density"] --> coeffB["Void Coefficient\nNegative in PWR BWR"]
    coeffB --> core
```

## 4.4 Summary Block: Control Requirement Mapping to DiD Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sys["Nuclear Plant Safety Functions"]
    sys --> f1["1. Reactivity Control"]
    sys --> f2["2. Reactor Coolant Pressure Control"]
    sys --> f3["3. Inventory Control"]
    sys --> f4["4. Heat Removal Control"]
    sys --> f5["5. Containment Integrity"]

    f1 --> c1a["Rods - CVCS - Bp - RRS"]
    f1 --> c1b["SCRAM - Liquid Poison Backup"]
    f2 --> c2a["Pressurizer - Heaters - Spray"]
    f2 --> c2b["PORV - Code SVs"]
    f3 --> c3a["CVCS - Charging Pumps"]
    f3 --> c3b["HPIS - Accumulators"]
    f4 --> c4a["RCS Pumps - SG - AFWS"]
    f4 --> c4b["RHR - Containment Spray"]
    f5 --> c5a["Containment - Isolation Valves"]
    f5 --> c5b["Hydrogen Recombiners - Filtration"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `did1`, `tripA`, `comp`). Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting or pipe characters — ensuring clean rendering across all Mermaid-compatible viewers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

---

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set-A]

**Define the "Single Failure Criterion" as applied to safety system design in a nuclear power plant. State which class of systems it applies to.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The **Single Failure Criterion (SFC)** is a design requirement that a safety system must be capable of performing its intended safety function even with the loss of any *single active component* (e.g., a pump, valve, sensor, or power supply). It does NOT apply to passive components unless the active component test reveals an unrevealed failure.

SFC applies to **Class-1 (safety-grade) systems** such as the Reactor Protection System (RPS), Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), and Containment Isolation System. Compliance is achieved through **N+1 redundancy** with channel independence.

*Marking distribution:*
- Definition of SFC — 1.5 marks
- Class of systems — 0.5 mark
- Method of compliance (redundancy) — 1 mark

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Set-B]

**List the five levels of Defence-in-Depth. Mention one engineered safety feature corresponding to each level.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| DiD Level | Name | Example ESF |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conservative Design | Negative void coefficient (inherent safety) |
| 2 | Operational Control | Reactor Regulating System (RRS) |
| 3 | Engineered Safety Features | ECCS, RPS / SCRAM |
| 4 | Accident Management | Passive Autocatalytic Hydrogen Recombiner (PAR) |
| 5 | Emergency Response | Off-site evacuation plan, iodine prophylaxis distribution |

*Marking distribution:* 0.3 marks per level + 0.3 marks for one correct example = 3 marks.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer EITHER Choice-A OR Choice-B in full.

---

### CHOICE A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **five key engineering principles** that govern the design of reactor protection and control systems. Discuss how each contributes to overall plant safety. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A PWR has the following reactivity data:
- Maximum clean cold core excess reactivity: $\rho_{\text{ex}} = +0.19 \, \Delta k/k$
- Total control rod worth: $\rho_{\text{rods}} = 7,500$ pcm
- Highest worth single rod: $\rho_{\text{single}} = 1,100$ pcm
- Boron reactivity coefficient: $\alpha_{\text{B}} = -9.5$ pcm/ppm
- Required shutdown margin: $\Delta \rho_{\text{SM}} \geq 1,000$ pcm

Calculate the **minimum boron concentration** in ppm that must be maintained at **hot zero power** to ensure compliance with the AERB stuck-rod criterion. Comment on operational implications. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Choice A

#### (a) Five Key Engineering Principles (7 marks)

| # | Principle | Explanation (1.4 marks each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Redundancy** | Provision of **multiple identical components** (typically N+1) such that loss of any one does not defeat the safety function. In a PWR RPS, **4 independent trip channels** ensure single-failure tolerance. Reduces unavailability $Q$ by orders of magnitude. |
| 2 | **Diversity** | Use of **different physical principles or technologies** to perform the same safety function, defeating common-mode failure. Example: shutdown is achieved by both **control rod insertion** AND **borated water injection** — two fundamentally different mechanisms. |
| 3 | **Independence** | **Physical and electrical separation** between redundant trains — separate cable trays, separate I&C rooms, separate power supplies. Prevents a single event (fire, flood) from disabling multiple channels. |
| 4 | **Single Failure Criterion** | The system must perform its safety function despite the loss of **any single active component**. Tested through reliability block diagrams and PSA. |
| 5 | **Fail-Safe Design** | System defaults to a **safe state** on loss of power or signal. SCRAM works on **loss of current to rod-holding coils** — gravity does the rest. MSIVs fail closed on loss of air. |

> **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** These five principles, taken together, form the philosophical bedrock of nuclear safety culture and are non-negotiable for all safety-grade systems.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution — Minimum Boron Concentration (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the shutdown margin equation.

$$\Delta \rho_{\text{SM}} = (\rho_{\text{rods,total}} - \rho_{\text{rods,single}}) + \rho_{\text{boron}} - \rho_{\text{ex}} \geq 1,000 \text{ pcm}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the *effective* rod worth available when the highest-worth rod is stuck out.

\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{rods,effective}} &= \rho_{\text{rods,total}} - \rho_{\text{rods,single}} \\
&= 7{,}500 - 1{,}100 \\
&= 6{,}400 \text{ pcm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Rearrange to find the required boron reactivity worth.

\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{boron,required}} &= \rho_{\text{ex}} - \rho_{\text{rods,effective}} + \Delta \rho_{\text{SM}} \\
&= 19{,}000 - 6{,}400 + 1{,}000 \\
&= 13{,}600 \text{ pcm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Convert reactivity to boron concentration using $\alpha_{\text{B}}$.

\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{B,min}} &= \frac{\rho_{\text{boron,required}}}{\vert \alpha_{\text{B}} \vert} \\
&= \frac{13{,}600 \text{ pcm}}{9.5 \text{ pcm/ppm}} \\
&\approx 1{,}432 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Operational implications.

- Boron concentration must be **continuously monitored** by the CVCS chemistry program.
- An **interlock** prevents dilution below this value at HZP.
- During **boration/dilution operations** for load following, the operator must verify that the **Critical Boron Concentration (CBC)** curve is never violated.
- A **boron dilution event** (e.g., unborated water ingress) is a serious safety concern → it can lead to **uncontrolled criticality excursion**.

> **Final Answer:** $C_{\text{B,min}} \approx \mathbf{1,432 \text{ ppm}}$ at HZP.

---

### CHOICE B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q.B (a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **architecture of a 2-out-of-4 Reactor Protection System (RPS)**. Explain how it satisfies the **Single Failure Criterion** and the **fail-safe design** principle. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Discuss the **reactivity control mechanisms** in a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR). Compare them with those used in a Boiling Water Reactor (BWR) in terms of speed, control worth, and operational flexibility. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Choice B

#### (a) 2-out-of-4 RPS Architecture (7 marks)

**Schematic Description (3 marks):**

The PWR RPS consists of **four independent and electrically isolated trip channels** (A, B, C, D). Each channel contains:
- Sensors (excore neutron flux, $T_{\text{hot}}$, $T_{\text{cold}}$, pressure, flow, etc.)
- A **coincident logic unit** (Channel Bypass switch for surveillance)
- A **2-out-of-4 voter** that compares trip requests across all four channels

A reactor trip is initiated when **at least 2 out of 4 channels** generate a coincident trip signal. This trip signal **de-energizes** the undervoltage SCRAM relays, removing power from the rod-holding coils. **Gravity** then drops all control rods into the core.

**Single Failure Criterion (2 marks):**
With one channel failed (e.g., Channel-A in bypass), the logic degrades gracefully to a 2-out-of-3 coincidence. The system still retains trip capability → SFC is satisfied. **No single active failure can defeat the protection function**.

**Fail-Safe (2 marks):**
- Loss of power → relays de-energize → rods drop in → **reactor trips safely**
- Loss of sensor signal → channel trips → 2oo4 voting causes trip
- Loss of air to MSIV → MSIV closes → **steam line isolated safely**

> The RPS is **energize-to-actuate for normal operation** and **de-energize-to-trip for safety** — the textbook definition of fail-safe.

---

#### (b) Reactivity Control — PWR vs. BWR (7 marks)

| Aspect | PWR | BWR |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Control** | Control Rods (Ag-In-Cd) + Boric Acid (chemical shim) | Control Rods (B₄C) only |
| **Long-term control** | Boron concentration adjustment in coolant | Rod patterns, recirculation flow |
| **Speed** | Rods: fast (~2 s); Boron: slow (~100s of ppm/hr) | Rods: fast (~3 s) |
| **Total control worth** | ~5–8 % from rods, ~15–20 % from boron | ~15–25 % from rods (full core) |
| **Load following** | Boron + rod motion (RRS with ARI) | Recirculation flow control + rod pattern |
| **Diversity** | 2-fold (mechanical + chemical) | 1-fold (mechanical) |
| **Standby Liquid Control** | Not required (boron always present) | Required (Gd nitrate or Na pentaborate) for ATWS |
| **Operational flexibility** | Better long-term flexibility, slow transients | Faster load-following, simpler chemistry |
| **Limitation** | Boron dilution risk during transients | Higher exposure to operators (direct cycle) |

**Key Takeaway (0.5 mark):** PWR's use of borated water is itself a **form of diversity** — even if all control rods fail to insert, the inherent boron poisoning maintains subcriticality. BWRs, lacking chemical shim, must rely on the **Standby Liquid Control System (SLCS)** as a diverse backup.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Control Requirements" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Redundancy" with "Diversity"** — Redundancy = *same component, multiple times*; Diversity = *different principle, same function*. (–1 mark per error)
> 2. **Forgetting the Stuck-Rod Criterion** — When asked about shutdown margin, students often quote $k_{\text{eff}} < 0.99$ but forget to mention the **single highest-worth rod stuck out**. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing DiD levels** — Putting "ECCS" under DiD-2 instead of DiD-3. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Unit Errors in Boron Calculation** — ppm ≠ pcm. $1 \text{ ppm of boron} \approx 8\text{–}12 \text{ pcm}$ depending on spectrum. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Failing to mention Fail-Safe explicitly** — Just listing "gravity drop" is insufficient; write *"de-energize-to-trip"*. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Spelling/terminology errors** — "SCRUM" instead of "SCRAM" (Safety Control Rod Activation Mechanism). Examiners DO deduct for this. (–0.5 mark)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DiD has 5 levels** — Conservative Design → Operational → ESF → Accident Mgmt → Emergency Response
- ✅ **Five Engineering Principles** — Redundancy, Diversity, Independence, Single Failure Criterion, Fail-Safe
- ✅ **RPS typically uses 2-out-of-4 voting** with 4 independent trip channels
- ✅ **Shutdown margin:** $k_{\text{eff}} \leq 0.99$ with **highest-worth rod stuck out**
- ✅ **PWR reactivity control:** Rods + Boron (diverse mechanisms); **BWR:** Rods + Recirculation Flow
- ✅ **SCRAM is de-energize-to-trip** — fail-safe (gravity drop on power loss)
- ✅ **Boron reactivity coefficient $\alpha_{\text{B}}$** is **negative** (in pcm/ppm) → more boron = more negative reactivity
- ✅ **ECCS = HPIS + LPIS + Accumulators** (diverse, multiple trains)
- ✅ **Cooldown rate limit:** $dT/dt \leq 55 \text{ °C/hr}$ (prevents RPV thermal shock)
- ✅ **AERB unavailability target for Class-1 systems:** $Q \leq 10^{-3}$ per demand
- ✅ **Core Damage Frequency (CDF) goal:** $< 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year
- ✅ **Negative reactivity feedbacks** (Doppler, moderator temperature) are **inherent safety** features
- ✅ **AFWS (Auxiliary Feedwater)** is the **diverse backup** to MFW for PWR — turbine-driven, not dependent on plant power
- ✅ **MSIVs (Main Steam Isolation Valves)** — fail-closed, isolate steam line on isolation signal
- ✅ **ATWS (Anticipated Transient Without Scram)** is the **diversity test case** — BWRs need SLCS; PWRs benefit from inherent boron

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Means of Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Means of Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Means of Control** in Nuclear Safety refers to the comprehensive set of engineered, administrative, and inherent mechanisms designed to maintain nuclear reactor parameters (neutron flux, power, temperature, pressure, and reactivity) within prescribed operational envelopes, and to safely shut down the reactor during anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), design basis accidents (DBAs), and beyond design basis accidents (BDBAs).

The means of control are classified into **three hierarchical defense-in-depth categories** as per IAEA NS-R-1 and INSAG-12:

1. **Inherent (Intrinsic) Control** — Passive physical properties (e.g., Doppler coefficient, negative void coefficient, moderator temperature coefficient).
2. **Passive Engineered Control** — Systems that operate without active actuation (e.g., gravity-driven ECCS, accumulators, natural circulation cooling).
3. **Active Engineered Control** — Systems requiring external power/actuation (e.g., control rod drives, pumped ECCS, RPS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** "Means of Control" encompasses every engineered and natural mechanism that influences reactivity, heat removal, and radioactive material confinement during normal operation, transients, and accidents. The student must explicitly link each control to the relevant KTU Course Outcome (CO2–CO3) on safety philosophy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Triple-Braking System"

Imagine driving a heavy truck down a steep mountain road. To control it, you have:

- **Inherent Control** → The natural friction of the tires on the road (cannot be switched off, always present).
- **Passive Engineered Control** → The handbrake (mechanical, no battery needed, engages automatically if main system fails).
- **Active Engineered Control** → The hydraulic foot brake (requires you to push the pedal and the brake fluid pressure to work).

Similarly, in a nuclear reactor, you cannot rely on *just one* braking system. The nuclear fuel, once critical, has enormous energy potential. Therefore, **multiple overlapping means of control** are mandated by KTU/IAEA safety standards — each independent of the others.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics must be memorized for KTU exams:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactor period | Λ | ~10⁻⁵ | seconds |
| Mean neutron generation time | Λ | ~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ | seconds |
| Delayed neutron fraction (U-235) | β | 0.0065 | dimensionless |
| Effective delayed neutron fraction | β_eff | ~0.0064 | dimensionless |
| Inhour equation constant | ρ | – | Δk/k |
| Reactivity insertion rate | $d\rho/dt$ | ≤ 0.07 /sec | per second |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The delayed neutron fraction β is the *single most important* number in nuclear safety control. It is the reason humans have time to insert control rods — without delayed neutrons, the reactor period would be ~10⁻⁵ s, making control impossible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Principal Means of Control

The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus organizes "Means of Control" into **five principal categories**. Each is described below in hierarchical order from fastest to slowest, and from inherent to engineered.

### 2.1.1 Control Rods (Active, Fastest)

Control rods are neutron-absorbing materials (B₄C, Ag-In-Cd, Hf) inserted into the reactor core to regulate reactivity. Their **scram function** is the most rapid means of reactor shutdown.

- **Drive Mechanism:** Magnetic jack, rack-and-pinion, or hydraulic drive.
- **Scram Time:** Typically 1–2 seconds for full insertion.
- **Reactivity Worth:** ρ_rod ~ 0.10 to 0.15 Δk/k for full insertion in a PWR.

**Inhour Equation (Reactivity–Period Relation):**

$$\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = reactivity (Δk/k)
- $\Lambda$ = effective mean neutron generation time (s)
- $T$ = reactor period (s)
- $\beta_i$ = delayed neutron fraction for group *i*
- $\lambda_i$ = decay constant of precursor group *i* (s⁻¹)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board exams, the **six-group delayed neutron data for U-235** is often given in the question paper. The student is expected to compute reactivity for a given period, or vice versa.

### 2.1.2 Burnable Neutron Poisons (Passive, Long-term)

Burnable poisons (Gadolinium oxide Gd₂O₃, Erbium, IFBA-coated ZrB₂ in PWRs; Pyrex glass in BWRs) are incorporated into fuel assemblies to suppress excess reactivity in fresh fuel, and are progressively consumed (burned out) by neutron absorption as the fuel depletes.

- **Negative reactivity contribution:** ρ_BP ~ 0.02 to 0.05 Δk/k initially.
- **Burnout time:** Matches the fuel cycle length (typically 12–24 months).

### 2.1.3 Chemical Shim (Boric Acid Control, PWR-specific)

Soluble boric acid (H₃BO₃) dissolved in the primary coolant acts as a neutron absorber. Its concentration is varied to provide slow, coarse reactivity control.

- **Concentration range:** 0 to ~2500 ppm natural boron.
- **Reactivity coefficient:** ~ -0.01 Δk/k per 1000 ppm boron (typical).

### 2.1.4 Reactor Protection System (RPS) — Trip System

The RPS is the **automatic safety system** that initiates reactor scram when monitored parameters exceed safety limits. The **"1 out of 2 taken twice" (1oo2T2)** coincidence logic is the industry standard for KTU exam reference.

**Standard KTU Trips (must be memorized):**

- High neutron flux (power)
- High startup rate
- Low coolant pressure
- High coolant pressure
- Low coolant flow
- High coolant temperature
- High containment pressure
- Low steam generator level (PWR)
- High-high containment radiation

### 2.1.5 Engineered Safety Features (ESFs) — Active + Passive

ESFs activate on RPS signal to mitigate accident consequences. The **"4 + 1" ESF set** is the canonical KTU list:

1. **ECCS** (Emergency Core Cooling System) — PWR: High-Pressure Injection, Accumulators, Low-Pressure Injection. BWR: HPCS, LPCS, Core Spray.
2. **Containment Spray** — Reduces containment pressure and temperature post-LOCA.
3. **Containment Isolation** — Closes all penetrations to prevent radioactive release.
4. **Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) closure** — Prevents steam release.
5. **AID + AFW (Auxiliary Feedwater)** — Provides decay heat removal feedwater.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 L3 (Apply):** Students should be able to draw the ESF actuation signal flow from "Parameter Exceedance → Trip → ESF Actuation → Post-Accident Cooling" as a logic block diagram.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every equation** the KTU 2024 examiner can test for the topic "Means of Control":

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reactivity | $\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$ | Departure from criticality | dimensionless |
| Multiplication factor | $k_{eff} = \eta \varepsilon p f P_{NL}$ | Four-factor formula | dimensionless |
| Reactor period | $T = \frac{1}{\text{slope of } \ln n \text{ vs } t}$ | e-folding time of neutron population | seconds |
| Inhour equation (6-group) | $\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$ | Period-reactivity relation | Δk/k |
| Doppler reactivity | $\Delta \rho_D = -K_D \ln\left(\frac{T_f}{T_{f,0}}\right)$ | Inherent negative feedback | Δk/k |
| Void coefficient | $\alpha_v = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial V}$ | Reactivity change per unit void fraction | Δk/k per % |
| Boron worth | $W_B = -\frac{\Delta \rho}{\Delta C_B}$ | Reactivity change per ppm boron | Δk/k per ppm |
| Control rod worth | $\rho_{rod} = \int_0^{H} \phi^2(z) \Sigma_a^{rod}(z) dz$ | Integral rod worth | Δk/k |
| Shutdown margin | $\rho_{SDM} = \rho_{rod}^{HFP} - \rho_{excess}$ | Reactivity available for shutdown | Δk/k |
| Decay heat (Wigner-Way) | $P_d(t) = 0.0622 \, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - (t + T)^{-0.2} \right]$ | Post-shutdown heat | Watts |
| ANS Decay Heat (1971) | $P_d(t)/P_0 = 0.0622 t^{-0.2}$ | Long after shutdown approximation | dimensionless |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** Note the use of `\vert` is **not required** here since none of the formulas use absolute value. However, if you see $\vert x - x_0 \vert$ in tables, use `\vert x - x_0 \vert` to prevent markdown table breaking.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PWR Operational Practice:** Modern PWRs use **boron + control rods** combination (chemical shim + control rods). This is the *actual* means of control used in Indian PHWRs and LWRs at Kudankulam, Tarapur, and Kaiga.
- **BWR Control:** Uses only control rods and flow control (no soluble boron). This is the design basis for Tarapur BWRs.
- **PHWR Control:** Uses adjuster rods, control rods, and moderator level control (CANDU-type) — a unique 3-tier system that is **frequently asked** in KTU previous year papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CANDU-style three-tier control** (moderator poison, adjuster rods, control rods) is unique to PHWRs. KTU examiners expect students to differentiate between BWR, PWR, and PHWR control philosophies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inhour Equation from Point Kinetics

The **Inhour equation** is the single most important relationship for KTU nuclear safety exams. It connects reactivity insertion to the resulting reactor period. Below is the full six-group derivation from the point kinetics equations.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Point Kinetics Equations

The point kinetics equations for neutron population *n(t)* and precursor concentrations *Cᵢ(t)* for *i = 1, 2, ..., 6* groups are:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i$$

$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n - \lambda_i C_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6$$

where:
- $\rho$ = reactivity (Δk/k) — assumed constant in time for the derivation
- $\beta = \sum_{i=1}^{6} \beta_i$ = total delayed neutron fraction
- $\Lambda$ = effective prompt neutron generation time (s)
- $\lambda_i$ = decay constant of delayed neutron precursor group *i* (s⁻¹)
- $\beta_i$ = delayed neutron fraction of group *i*

### 3.1.2 Assume Exponential Solution

For a **constant reactivity insertion**, we assume the neutron population grows (or decays) exponentially, and the precursor concentrations reach quasi-equilibrium:

$$n(t) = n_0 \, e^{t/T} \quad \text{and} \quad C_i(t) = C_{i,0} \, e^{t/T}$$

where *T* is the **reactor period** — the time required for the neutron population to change by a factor of *e* (≈ 2.718).

### 3.1.3 Substitute into the Kinetics Equations

Substituting into the first kinetics equation:

$$\frac{n_0}{T} e^{t/T} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n_0 e^{t/T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_{i,0} e^{t/T}$$

Cancelling the common factor $n_0 \, e^{t/T}$:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\lambda_i C_{i,0}}{n_0}$$

### 3.1.4 Express Precursor Concentration in Terms of *n₀*

From the second kinetics equation at quasi-steady state:

$$\frac{C_{i,0}}{T} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n_0 - \lambda_i C_{i,0}$$

Rearranging for *Cᵢ,₀*:

$$\frac{C_{i,0}}{T} + \lambda_i C_{i,0} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n_0$$

$$C_{i,0} \left[ \frac{1}{T} + \lambda_i \right] = \frac{\beta_i n_0}{\Lambda}$$

$$C_{i,0} = \frac{\beta_i n_0}{\Lambda \left( \lambda_i + 1/T \right)} = \frac{\beta_i n_0}{\Lambda} \cdot \frac{T}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

### 3.1.5 Substitute Back into the Reactivity Equation

Replacing $C_{i,0}/n_0$ into the first equation:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i \cdot \frac{\beta_i T}{\Lambda (1 + \lambda_i T)}$$

Multiplying through by $\Lambda$:

$$\frac{\Lambda}{T} = \rho - \beta + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\lambda_i \beta_i T}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator inside the sum by *T*:

$$\frac{\Lambda}{T} = \rho - \beta + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \lambda_i T}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

Now rewrite $\lambda_i T / (1 + \lambda_i T)$ as $1 - 1/(1 + \lambda_i T)$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \lambda_i T}{1 + \lambda_i T} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} \beta_i \left[ 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \lambda_i T} \right] = \beta - \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{\Lambda}{T} = \rho - \beta + \beta - \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

The two β terms cancel:

$$\frac{\Lambda}{T} = \rho - \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

### 3.1.6 Final Inhour Equation

Rearranging to solve for reactivity:

$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The KTU examiner often asks: "If T = 100 s and Λ ≈ 10⁻⁵ s, what is the reactivity?" Since Λ/T is negligible (10⁻⁷), the equation simplifies to $\rho \approx \sum \beta_i / (1 + \lambda_i T)$. For large *T* (slow period), $\rho$ approaches *β*. This is the **definition of prompt criticality** boundary.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Pattern Question

**Problem:** A reactor operating with U-235 fuel has a period of 80 seconds. The delayed neutron data is given in the table. Compute the reactivity inserted. ($\Lambda = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ s, U-235 thermal fission.)

| Group *i* | βᵢ | λᵢ (s⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.000215 | 0.0124 |
| 2 | 0.001424 | 0.0305 |
| 3 | 0.001274 | 0.1110 |
| 4 | 0.002568 | 0.3010 |
| 5 | 0.000748 | 1.1400 |
| 6 | 0.000273 | 3.0100 |

**Step 1:** Compute the term $\Lambda/T$:
$$\Lambda/T = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} / 80 = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ (negligible)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term $\beta_i / (1 + \lambda_i T)$ for *T = 80* s:

| Group | $\lambda_i T$ | $1 + \lambda_i T$ | $\beta_i / (1 + \lambda_i T)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.992 | 1.992 | 0.0001079 |
| 2 | 2.440 | 3.440 | 0.0004140 |
| 3 | 8.880 | 9.880 | 0.0001290 |
| 4 | 24.080 | 25.080 | 0.0001024 |
| 5 | 91.200 | 92.200 | 0.0000081 |
| 6 | 240.800 | 241.800 | 0.0000011 |

**Step 3:** Sum the contributions:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T} = (0.1079 + 0.4140 + 0.1290 + 0.1024 + 0.0081 + 0.0011) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 0.7625 \times 10^{-3} = 7.625 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Δk/k}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Inhour equation:
$$\rho = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} + 7.625 \times 10^{-4} \approx 7.637 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Δk/k}$$

> [!TIP]
> In percentage terms, this is $\rho = 0.0764\%$ Δk/k, or **76.4 pcm** (per cent mille). This is a typical KTU expected answer format.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Means of Control Trade-offs

| Control Method | Speed | Reliability | Type | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control Rod Scram | Fastest (1–2 s) | Very High | Active | ★★★★★ |
| Boron Dilution | Slow (hours) | High | Active | ★★★ |
| Burnable Poison | Passive, slow | High | Passive | ★★ |
| Doppler Feedback | Inherent (ms) | Absolute | Inherent | ★★★★ |
| Moderator Temp | Inherent (s) | Absolute | Inherent | ★★★ |
| Void Coefficient | Inherent (ms) | Absolute | Inherent | ★★★ |
| ECCS (LPIS) | Active, post-trip | High | Active | ★★★★ |
| Accumulators | Passive, post-trip | Very High | Passive | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Defense-in-Depth Pyramid (KTU Canonical Diagram)

The following Mermaid block represents the KTU/IAEA defense-in-depth concept, where each "Means of Control" lives in its specific defense level.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1 - Sound Design and Quality Assurance"]:::def1
    L2["LEVEL 2 - Operational Means of Control"]:::def2
    L3["LEVEL 3 - Engineered Safety Features - ESF"]:::def3
    L4["LEVEL 4 - Accident Management - AM"]:::def4
    L5["LEVEL 5 - Emergency Response - Off-Site"]:::def5
    
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    
    M1["Control Rods Burnable Poisons Boric Acid"]:::mean1
    M2["RPS Reactor Trip ECCS Containment Spray"]:::mean2
    M3["HPCS LPCS Core Spray Accumulators"]:::mean3
    M4["Core Makeup Tank Firewater SAMG"]:::mean4
    
    L1 -.- M1
    L2 -.- M2
    L3 -.- M3
    L4 -.- M4
    
    classDef def1 fill:#1a472a,stroke:#0d2818,color:#ffffff
    classDef def2 fill:#2d5e3e,stroke:#1a472a,color:#ffffff
    classDef def3 fill:#3e7550,stroke:#2d5e3e,color:#ffffff
    classDef def4 fill:#4f8c62,stroke:#3e7550,color:#ffffff
    classDef def5 fill:#5fa374,stroke:#4f8c62,color:#ffffff
    classDef mean1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    classDef mean2 fill:#ffe680,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    classDef mean3 fill:#ffd633,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
    classDef mean4 fill:#ffc107,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed lines (`-.-`) link each defense level to the specific "Means of Control" that operate at that level. The colors progress from dark green (innermost safety) to lighter green (off-site emergency response), reflecting the increasing severity of the accident scenario.

## 4.2 Reactor Protection System (RPS) Trip Logic — 1oo2T2 Coincidence

The following diagram illustrates the **canonical 1-out-of-2-taken-twice coincidence logic** used in KTU Module 2 problems on Reactor Protection Systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PARAMETER\nNeutron Flux"]:::param
    P2["Pressure\nTransmitter A"]:::param
    P3["Pressure\nTransmitter B"]:::param
    P4["Temperature\nSensor"]:::param
    
    T1["Trip Channel 1"]:::trip
    T2["Trip Channel 2"]:::trip
    T3["Trip Channel 3"]:::trip
    T4["Trip Channel 4"]:::trip
    
    L1["1oo2 Logic A"]:::logic
    L2["1oo2 Logic B"]:::logic
    
    A1["AND Gate"]:::andgate
    A2["AND Gate"]:::andgate
    
    SCRAM["REACTOR SCRAM\nControl Rods Drop\nFuel Safe State"]:::scram
    
    ESF1["ESF ACTUATION\nECCS Starts\nContainment Isolates"]:::esf
    
    P1 --> T1
    P2 --> T1
    P2 --> T2
    P3 --> T2
    P3 --> T3
    P4 --> T3
    P4 --> T4
    P1 --> T4
    
    T1 --> L1
    T2 --> L1
    T3 --> L2
    T4 --> L2
    
    L1 --> A1
    L2 --> A1
    L1 --> A2
    L2 --> A2
    
    A1 -->|Both 1oo2 trip| SCRAM
    A2 -->|Both 1oo2 trip| ESF1
    
    SCRAM -->|Power Loss| ESF1
    
    classDef param fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000000
    classDef trip fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    classDef logic fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000000
    classDef andgate fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#ffffff
    classDef scram fill:#b71c1c,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff
    classDef esf fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#003300,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** The 1oo2T2 logic protects against a single instrument failure causing a spurious scram, while still tripping when *both* trip channels (A and B) register an out-of-limit condition. This is the **gold standard** for exam questions on RPS.

## 4.3 PHWR Three-Tier Control System (CANDU Architecture)

For PHWRs (Indian 220 MWe, 540 MWe, 700 MWe PHWRs at Kakrapar, Kaiga, Narora, etc.), the means of control is unique:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TIER1["TIER 1 - SLOW CONTROL\nModulator Poison\nBoron or Gadolinium in D2O Moderator"]:::t1
    TIER2["TIER 2 - MEDIUM CONTROL\nAdjuster Rods\nStainless Steel or Zr Absorber Rods"]:::t2
    TIER3["TIER 3 - FAST CONTROL\nShutoff Rods\nCadmium Absorber for Emergency Scram"]:::t3
    TIER4["TIER 4 - UNIQUE TO PHWR\nModerator Level Control\nD2O Dump Tank for Fast Shutdown"]:::t4
    
    NEUT["Reactor Neutron Flux and Power"]:::neut
    SHUT["SAFE SUBCRITICAL STATE"]:::safe
    
    NEUT -->|reactive zone| TIER1
    TIER1 -->|reactive zone| TIER2
    TIER2 -->|reactive zone| TIER3
    TIER3 -->|reactive zone| TIER4
    TIER4 -->|reactive zone| SHUT
    
    classDef t1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000
    classDef t2 fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000000
    classDef t3 fill:#42a5f5,stroke:#1976d2,color:#ffffff
    classDef t4 fill:#1e88e5,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef neut fill:#fff176,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000000
    classDef safe fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Special Topic:** Moderator dump in a PHWR is a **unique means of control** not present in PWRs or BWRs. When activated, the entire heavy water moderator is dumped into a dump tank by gravity, instantly making the core subcritical. This is the *last line of defense* in Indian PHWRs.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Means of Control" in the context of nuclear safety. List the three hierarchical categories with one example each.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Means of Control refers to the comprehensive set of engineered, administrative, and inherent mechanisms used to maintain reactor parameters within safe limits and to bring the reactor to a safe shutdown state during normal operation, anticipated transients, and accident conditions.

**Three Hierarchical Categories:**

1. **Inherent (Intrinsic) Safety Features** — Properties arising from the physics of the fuel-coolant-moderator combination. *Example:* Negative Doppler reactivity coefficient.
2. **Passive Engineered Safety Features** — Systems requiring no external power to operate. *Example:* Accumulators in PWR ECCS, or gravity-driven moderator dump in PHWRs.
3. **Active Engineered Safety Features** — Systems requiring external actuation and power. *Example:* Reactor Protection System (RPS) initiating scram of control rods.

---

### Q2. State the Inhour equation for a six-group delayed neutron model. Explain why the term $\Lambda/T$ can be neglected for *T > 1* second.

`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Inhour equation is:

$$\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

For a thermal reactor, the effective prompt neutron generation time is of the order $\Lambda \sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ s. For a period *T* greater than 1 second, the term $\Lambda/T$ evaluates to at most $10^{-4}$, which is two orders of magnitude smaller than the delayed neutron fraction $\beta \sim 6.5 \times 10^{-3}$. Therefore, the $\Lambda/T$ term is **negligible** and the Inhour equation reduces to:

$$\rho \approx \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

This is the working form used in nearly all KTU exam calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose 3–4 marks on the following: (1) Forgetting to multiply by *T* in the precursor term, (2) using wrong sign for Doppler coefficient, (3) confusing 1oo2 logic with 2oo3 logic, and (4) failing to list the **shutdown margin** explicitly. Always write down the **units** for every answer — KTU examiners award half-marks for correct unit notation alone.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Choice Option 1

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of the Reactor Protection System (RPS). Describe the 1oo2T2 coincidence logic with the help of a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal reactor operating on U-235 has the following delayed neutron parameters: β = 0.0065, Λ = 10⁻⁴ s. If a step reactivity of ρ = 0.001 Δk/k is inserted, calculate the resulting stable reactor period *T* using the **one-group delayed approximation** with $\lambda_{avg} = 0.08$ s⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The **Reactor Protection System (RPS)** is the principal active engineered means of control. Its purpose is to automatically initiate a reactor scram (rapid insertion of all control rods) when any monitored parameter exceeds a preset safety limit. The system comprises:

- **Sensors / Transmitters** (neutron flux, pressure, temperature, flow, level)
- **Trip Channels** (electronic comparators with setpoints)
- **Coincidence Logic** (1oo2T2 — 1-out-of-2-taken-twice)
- **Trip Breakers** (open to de-energize rod drive coils, releasing rods by gravity)

In **1oo2T2 logic**, the parameter is monitored by **two independent trip channels (A and B)**. Each channel can trip independently. However, a reactor scram is initiated only when **both channels (A and B) register a trip condition simultaneously** — hence "1-out-of-2 taken twice". This logic:

- Prevents spurious scrams from a single instrument failure.
- Ensures scram occurs when a genuine out-of-limit condition exists in both redundant channels.

*(Student should refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.2 for the schematic, and reproduce it freehand for full marks.)*

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks:**

For the one-group delayed approximation, the Inhour equation becomes:

$$\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \frac{\beta}{1 + \lambda_{avg} T}$$

Given:
- $\rho = 0.001$ Δk/k
- $\beta = 0.0065$
- $\Lambda = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ s
- $\lambda_{avg} = 0.08$ s⁻¹

For periods *T* of order ~10 s, $\Lambda/T = 10^{-4}/10 = 10^{-5}$, which is negligible compared to ρ = 10⁻³. Neglecting the $\Lambda/T$ term:

$$0.001 \approx \frac{0.0065}{1 + 0.08 T}$$

Solving for *T*:

$$1 + 0.08 T = \frac{0.0065}{0.001} = 6.5$$

$$0.08 T = 5.5$$

$$\boxed{T = 68.75 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** A positive reactivity insertion of $\rho = 0.001$ (which is *less* than the delayed neutron fraction β = 0.0065) results in a long, slowly increasing power transient. The reactor is *below prompt critical*, and control via the protection system is readily achievable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Choice Option 2

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** List and explain the **five principal means of control** in a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR). Compare them on the basis of speed of response and the type of control (inherent, passive, active). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PWR operates with a control rod worth of 1.5% Δk/k and a boron concentration of 1500 ppm. The boron worth is −1.0 × 10⁻⁵ Δk/k per ppm. If the boron concentration is suddenly reduced by 200 ppm due to a dilution event, calculate:
   (i) The change in reactivity.
   (ii) The new shutdown margin, given that the excess reactivity at HFP (Hot Full Power) is 0.005 Δk/k. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The five principal means of control in a PWR are:

1. **Control Rods** — *Active, fast* (~1–2 s scram). Cadmium-indium-silver or B₄C rods driven by magnetic jack mechanism. Provide fine, fast reactivity control and emergency shutdown.

2. **Boric Acid (Chemical Shim)** — *Active, slow* (hours to weeks). Soluble H₃BO₃ in primary coolant. Provides coarse, slow reactivity control to compensate fuel burnup. Reduced via dilution with demineralized water.

3. **Burnable Neutron Poisons** — *Passive, slow* (months). Gd₂O₃ or Er₂O₃ mixed with fuel. Self-burnout matches fuel depletion. Reduces required soluble boron concentration.

4. **Reactor Protection System (RPS)** — *Active, automatic* (~50 ms trip action). Initiates scram on detection of out-of-limit conditions. Independent, redundant, diverse trip parameters.

5. **Engineered Safety Features (ECCS, Containment Spray, etc.)** — *Active + Passive*. ECCS is auto-started on low-pressure or high-containment-pressure signal. Accumulators (passive) inject borated water at ~30 bar.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn by student):**

| Method | Speed | Type |
|---|---|---|
| Control Rods | Fast (~2 s) | Active |
| Boric Acid | Slow (hours) | Active |
| Burnable Poison | Passive, slow | Passive |
| RPS | Very fast (~50 ms) | Active |
| ESF (ECCS) | Post-trip (~min) | Mixed |

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Change in Reactivity:**

The boron worth is $W_B = -1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Δk/k per ppm.

A reduction in boron concentration by 200 ppm (i.e., $\Delta C_B = -200$ ppm) means less negative reactivity. The reactivity change is:

$$\Delta \rho = W_B \cdot \Delta C_B = (-1.0 \times 10^{-5}) \times (-200)$$

$$\Delta \rho = +2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Δk/k} = +0.002 \text{ Δk/k}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Diluting the boron by 200 ppm *adds* 0.2% reactivity to the core.

**(ii) New Shutdown Margin:**

The shutdown margin is defined as the **available negative reactivity from the most reactive control rod stuck out, with all other rods fully inserted**, minus the worst-case excess reactivity.

**Initial excess reactivity** at HFP: $\rho_{excess} = 0.005$ Δk/k.

**Total control rod worth** = 0.015 Δk/k.

**Worst case** (one rod stuck out, but this is more conservative — we use full rod worth for simple KTU calc): The single most-reactive rod stuck out is conservatively not subtracted for this KTU-level problem. So:

$$\rho_{SDM} = \rho_{rod} - (\rho_{excess} - \Delta \rho)$$

$$\rho_{SDM} = 0.015 - (0.005 - 0.002) = 0.015 - 0.003$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{SDM} = 0.012 \text{ Δk/k} = 1.2\% \Delta k/k}$$

**Interpretation:** A dilution event of 200 ppm reduces the shutdown margin from 1.0% to 1.2% (positive direction) — wait, careful. The dilution *adds* positive reactivity, which *decreases* the margin:

- **Before dilution:** SDM = 0.015 − 0.005 = 0.010 Δk/k
- **After dilution:** SDM = 0.015 − (0.005 − 0.002) = 0.012 Δk/k — but this is *incorrect* logic.

The correct formulation: After dilution, the excess reactivity becomes 0.005 − 0.002 = 0.003 Δk/k (wait, dilution *increases* reactivity). So the new excess reactivity is 0.005 + 0.002 = 0.007 Δk/k. Therefore:

$$\rho_{SDM, new} = 0.015 - 0.007 = 0.008 \text{ Δk/k}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Correction in Sign Convention:** Dilution means *less* neutron absorber → *more* positive reactivity. So the new excess reactivity is $\rho_{excess,new} = \rho_{excess} + \Delta \rho = 0.005 + 0.002 = 0.007$ Δk/k. The new shutdown margin is therefore $0.015 - 0.007 = 0.008$ Δk/k = **0.8%**. The dilution *reduced* the safety margin from 1.0% to 0.8%. This is why **boron dilution events** are taken extremely seriously in PWR safety analyses (e.g., the 1999-2002 worldwide boron dilution event studies).

**Final corrected answer:** $\rho_{SDM,new} = 0.008$ Δk/k.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist on the day before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Means of Control** = Inherent + Passive + Active. Always classify each control into one of these three categories.
- [ ] **Inhour equation** (6-group): $\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum \beta_i / (1 + \lambda_i T)$. For *T > 1* s, drop the $\Lambda/T$ term.
- [ ] **Delayed neutron fraction β for U-235** = 0.0065. **Threshold for prompt criticality** is exactly β.
- [ ] **1oo2T2 logic** = 1-out-of-2-taken-twice. Used in RPS to avoid spurious scrams.
- [ ] **Five ESF functions** = ECCS, Containment Spray, Containment Isolation, MSIV Closure, AFW/AID.
- [ ] **PWR control** = Boron + Control Rods. **BWR control** = Control Rods + Recirculation Flow. **PHWR control** = Moderator poison + Adjuster Rods + Shutoff Rods + Moderator Dump.
- [ ] **Boron dilution** *adds* positive reactivity. Always check sign convention in shutdown margin calculations.
- [ ] **Doppler coefficient** is always *negative* for UO₂ fuel — this is the *first* and *fastest* inherent safety feedback.
- [ ] **Decay heat formula (Wigner-Way):** $P_d(t) = 0.0622 \, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - (t + T)^{-0.2} \right]$. For *t >> T*, simplifies to $P_d(t) \approx 0.0622 \, P_0 \, t^{-0.2}$.
- [ ] **Shutdown margin** = (Control Rod Worth) − (Maximum Excess Reactivity). Regulatory minimum: ~1% Δk/k in PWRs.
- [ ] **Moderator dump in PHWRs** is a unique last-line passive means of control — must be mentioned if KTU question is on PHWRs.
- [ ] **Accumulators** in PWR ECCS are *passive* — they inject borated water at low pressure (~30 bar) without requiring AC power or operator action.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth** has 5 levels per IAEA NS-R-1. Each level has its own set of "means of control". Map your answer to the correct level for full marks.
- [ ] **Reactor period *T* = 80 s** → ρ ≈ 0.00076 Δk/k (typical KTU calculation). Always show the table of $\lambda_i T$ values.
- [ ] **RPS trip parameters** to memorize: high flux, low pressure, high pressure, low flow, high temperature, high containment pressure, low SG level (PWR).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
