topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control and Shutdown Rods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚛️ MODULE 2 — CONTROL AND SHUTDOWN RODS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Rods** are neutron-absorbing components, fabricated from materials possessing a high neutron-absorption cross-section, that are progressively inserted into or withdrawn from the reactor core to **fine-tune the effective multiplication factor (k_eff)**, compensate for reactivity changes arising from fuel burnup, fission-product poisoning (e.g., Xenon-135, Samarium-149), and temperature effects, thereby maintaining the desired steady-state reactor power level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shutdown Rods (Safety Rods / Scram Rods)** are specially designed, fast-acting neutron absorbers intended to introduce a large, **negative reactivity insertion (typically ≥ 10 % Δk/k)** within a strictly bounded time interval (usually **< 2 seconds** for a PWR) to bring the sub-critical reactor to a guaranteed safe-shutdown state under all postulated emergency transients and accident scenarios.

Together, both rod systems constitute the **primary reactivity control mechanism** of a nuclear reactor and form the **first line of defense-in-depth** in nuclear safety philosophy (IAEA SSR-2/1).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a giant campfire 🔥. To keep it burning at a steady, useful warmth, you cannot let it rage out of control, nor can you extinguish it completely — you need to **balance the heat**.

- **Control Rods** are like a precise **damper lever** on a wood stove. You slide it in tiny, measured increments. Slide it in a little → fewer neutrons survive → power drops. Slide it out a little → more neutrons multiply → power rises. The operator is *balancing* the fire continuously.
- **Shutdown Rods** are the **fire extinguisher** 🚨. You never use it for gentle trimming. When something goes catastrophically wrong (fuel leak, loss of coolant, earthquake), you slam the lever down — the rods drop by gravity into the core — and the chain reaction is killed almost instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> The same *physical hardware* can sometimes function as **both** a control and a shutdown rod (e.g., in BWRs, the same rods are used for control, scram, and shaping). The classification depends on **operational role, insertion speed, and reactivity worth** rather than material composition.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal neutron absorption cross-section of B-10 | σ_a(B-10) | **3837** | barns |
| Thermal absorption of natural B (in B₄C) | σ_a(B) | **750** | barns |
| Thermal absorption of Hafnium | σ_a(Hf) | **104** | barns |
| Thermal absorption of Cadmium-113 | σ_a(Cd-113) | **~20,600** | barns |
| PWR scram insertion time (regulatory) | t_scram | **< 2.0** | s |
| BWR scram insertion time | t_scram | **< 1.8** | s |
| Total negative reactivity inserted (scram) | Δρ_scram | **≥ 10 % Δk/k** | — |
| Reactivity unit (1 cent) | 1 cent | **0.01** | Δk/k |

### 1.4 Physical Locations in the Core

Control and shutdown rods are inserted into the core through dedicated guide tubes:

- **PWR (Pressurized Water Reactor):** Rod clusters (typically **20–24 absorber rods per cluster**, arranged as a "spider") move inside the fuel assembly's guide thimbles. The cluster is suspended from the **Control Rod Drive Mechanism (CRDM)** in the reactor pressure vessel head.
- **BWR (Boiling Water Reactor):** Cruciform (cross-shaped) rods inserted from the bottom of the vessel into the spaces between four adjacent fuel assemblies ("blades").
- **PHWR (Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor):** Rods drop vertically into horizontal reactor channels by gravity.
- **Fast Reactors:** Typically use B₄C absorber pins inside the fuel assembly, since fast neutrons need high cross-section absorbers (B-10 enriched).

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 expects students to clearly distinguish between the **material**, the **geometry**, the **drive mechanism**, and the **safety function** of rods — these are the four pillars of any exam answer on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Neutron Multiplication Context**
In a critical reactor, the four-factor formula holds:

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot \epsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

When control rods are inserted, they act as a **parasitic absorber**, increasing the absorption term in the neutron balance. The effective multiplication factor becomes:

$$k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} - \text{(Rod Absorption Loss)}$$

**Step 2 — Reactivity Definition**
Reactivity ρ is the normalised deviation of k_eff from unity:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$$

- ρ > 0 → Supercritical (power rising)
- ρ = 0 → Critical (steady)
- ρ < 0 → Subcritical (power falling)

**Step 3 — Differential Rod Worth (dρ/dx)**
The reactivity inserted per unit incremental rod insertion depth x:

$$\frac{d\rho}{dx} = -\frac{1}{k_{eff}} \int_{V_{rod}} \Sigma_a^{rod}(\vec{r}) \, \phi^2(\vec{r}) \, dV \cdot \text{weighting factor}$$

**Physical Meaning:** dρ/dx is **largest near the core centre** (where φ² is highest) and **smallest at the core top/bottom** (where flux is low). This is the *S-shaped* worth curve.

**Step 4 — Integral Rod Worth**
The total reactivity inserted when the rod moves from position 0 to position h:

$$\rho(h) = \int_{0}^{h} \frac{d\rho}{dx} \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Criticality Balance Equation**
For steady-state power, total reactivity must equal zero:

$$\rho_{rods} + \rho_{fuel} + \rho_{coolant} + \rho_{Xe} + \rho_{Sm} + \rho_{T} = 0$$

The control rods must continuously supply the **negative reactivity** to balance the positive reactivity from fresh fuel.

**Step 6 — Blackness of an Absorber**
A control rod is called **"black"** to neutrons of energy E if the probability of absorption in one traversal approaches 1:

$$P_{abs} = 1 - e^{-\Sigma_a^{rod} \cdot t_{rod}} \geq 0.9$$

For B₄C with thickness t = 1 cm and Σ_a ≈ 500 cm⁻¹:
P_abs = 1 − e^(−500) ≈ 1.0 → **completely black to thermal neutrons**

**Step 7 — Rod "Shadowing" Effect**
When multiple rods are present, a downstream rod receives a depressed flux due to upstream absorption. The **shadowing factor** S is:

$$S = \frac{\text{Actual worth of N rods}}{\text{N} \times \text{Worth of 1 isolated rod}} < 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Many students lose marks because they treat rods as "independent absorbers." In reality, in a typical PWR core with 50+ rod clusters, shadowing reduces total bank worth by **30–50 %** compared to the sum of individual worths.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Engineering Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$ | ρ | Reactivity (dimensionless, sometimes in cents or %Δk/k) |
| 2 | $\rho_{dollar} = \dfrac{\rho}{\beta_{eff}}$ | $ | Reactivity in "dollars" (1 $ = β_eff ≈ 0.0065 for U-235) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d\rho}{dx} = -\dfrac{1}{k_{eff}} \int \Sigma_a \phi^2 dV$ | dρ/dx | Differential worth (pcm/cm) |
| 4 | $\rho(h) = \int_0^h \dfrac{d\rho}{dx}\, dx$ | ρ(h) | Integral worth at insertion h |
| 5 | $P_{abs} = 1 - e^{-\Sigma_a t}$ | P_abs | Blackness probability |
| 6 | $\text{1 inhour} = 2.54 \times 10^{-5} \Delta k/k$ | inhour | Inhour equation inverse period |
| 7 | $t_{period} = \dfrac{\Lambda}{\rho - \beta_{eff}}$ for prompt critical | T | Reactor period (seconds) |
| 8 | $W_{scram} \geq \sum_{i} \rho_i^{excess}$ | W | Shutdown margin requirement |
| 9 | $\Sigma_a^{B-10} = N_{B10} \sigma_a^{B10}$ | Σ_a | Macroscopic cross-section |
| 10 | $\tau_{fall} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2H}{g}}$ | τ | Gravity drop time (H = drop height) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 dollar of reactivity = β_eff (effective delayed neutron fraction)**
> For U-235 thermal reactor: **β_eff ≈ 650 pcm ≈ 0.0065 Δk/k**
> For Pu-239: **β_eff ≈ 210 pcm**
> This is the threshold below which reactor response is **slower** (governed by delayed neutrons) and above which **prompt critical** is reached (uncontrollable power excursion).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Rod Systems

- **Operational Flexibility:** Daily load-following in grid-connected reactors
- **Burnup Compensation:** As U-235 depletes, rods withdraw gradually to maintain criticality over an 18–24 month fuel cycle
- **Xenon Override:** After power reduction, Xe-135 builds in; rods must withdraw to compensate
- **Cold Shutdown Margin:** At 50 °C, with all rods inserted except the **most reactive single rod stuck out**, k_eff must remain ≤ 0.95 (5 % subcritical) — **AERB/IAEA requirement**
- **Reactivity Control Budget:** Total available negative reactivity must exceed total positive reactivity from all anticipated transients

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite 14-mark question pattern:** "Discuss the design considerations for control and shutdown rods in a PWR. Compare the materials, drive mechanisms, and reactivity worth curves, and explain the safety philosophy of shutdown margin."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Differential Rod Worth from First Principles

We start with the **exact perturbation theory** expression for reactivity change due to an absorber perturbation in a critical reactor.

**Starting Equation (Perturbation Theory):**
For a small change in the absorption cross-section δΣ_a, the reactivity change is:

$$\delta\rho = -\frac{\int_V \delta\Sigma_a(\vec{r}) \, \phi^2(\vec{r}) \, dV}{\int_V \nu \Sigma_f(\vec{r}) \, \phi^2(\vec{r}) \, dV}$$

This is derived from the **first-order perturbation of the neutron transport equation** (exact treatment, not approximate).

**Step 1 — Identify the Perturbation**
The control rod of thickness t replaces moderator/fuel region of volume V_rod. The change in cross-section is:

$$\delta\Sigma_a = \Sigma_a^{rod} - \Sigma_a^{fuel}$$

Since Σ_a^rod ≫ Σ_a^fuel, we can approximate:

$$\delta\Sigma_a \approx \Sigma_a^{rod}$$

**Step 2 — Express Per Unit Length**
The rod is inserted by an incremental length dx at axial position z. The incremental absorption added is:

$$d(\delta\Sigma_a \, V) = \Sigma_a^{rod} \cdot A_{rod} \cdot dz$$

where A_rod is the rod's cross-sectional area projected perpendicular to the rod's axis.

**Step 3 — Form the Differential Rod Worth**
The reactivity change per unit insertion dz is:

$$\frac{d\rho}{dz} = -\frac{\Sigma_a^{rod} \cdot A_{rod} \cdot \phi^2(z)}{k_{eff} \cdot \int_V \nu\Sigma_f \phi^2 dV}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Importance Function (Adjoint Flux)**
To account for asymmetry in the multiplication process, multiply by the adjoint (importance) function φ*:

$$\frac{d\rho}{dz} = -\frac{\Sigma_a^{rod} \cdot A_{rod} \cdot \phi(z) \cdot \phi^*(z)}{k_{eff} \cdot \langle \nu\Sigma_f, \phi \phi^* \rangle}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise the S-Shaped Profile**
Since both φ and φ* are maximum at the core midplane and follow approximately a cos(πz/H) profile, the differential worth curve takes the form:

$$\frac{d\rho}{dz} \approx -C \cdot \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{H_{eff}}\right)$$

where C is a constant depending on rod material, geometry, and core size.

**Step 6 — Integrate for the Integral Worth Curve**
$$\rho(h) = -C \int_{H_{eff}/2 - h/2}^{H_{eff}/2 + h/2} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{H_{eff}}\right) dz$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\rho(h) = -\frac{C \cdot H_{eff}}{2} \left[ \frac{h}{H_{eff}} + \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{H_{eff}}\left(\frac{H_{eff}}{2} + \frac{h}{2}\right)\right) - \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{H_{eff}}\left(\frac{H_{eff}}{2} - \frac{h}{2}\right)\right)\right) \right]$$

**Step 7 — Final Simplified Form**
Using trigonometric identity $\sin(A+B) - \sin(A-B) = 2\cos A \sin B$:

$$\rho(h) = -\frac{C \cdot H_{eff}}{2} \left[ \frac{h}{H_{eff}} - \frac{1}{\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi h}{H_{eff}}\right)\cos(\pi) \right]$$

For small h compared to H_eff, this becomes approximately linear, then saturates as h → H_eff.

> [!NOTE]
> This S-shaped integral worth curve is the **hallmark signature** of any reactor's control rod calibration graph. KTU examiners love asking students to *sketch and label* this curve.

## 3.2 Derivation: Scram Drop Time (Free-Fall with Deceleration)

**Starting Equation — Free fall under gravity:**
$$v(t) = g \cdot t \quad \text{(velocity after time t)}$$

**Step 1 — Distance Travelled**
$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2} g t^2$$

**Step 2 — Total Drop Height**
For a PWR, drop height H ≈ 4 m. Pure free-fall time:
$$t_{free} = \sqrt{\frac{2H}{g}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4}{9.81}} = 0.903 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Account for Hydraulic Deceleration**
Real rods experience a hydraulic dashpot buffer at the end of travel. The deceleration a_d reduces final impact velocity. The effective scram time becomes:

$$t_{scram} = t_{free} + \Delta t_{dec} \approx 1.5 - 2.0 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative Reactivity Insertion Profile**
The reactivity inserted is a time-dependent function. For a uniformly falling rod, reactivity insertion rate is approximately constant during free-fall:

$$\frac{d\rho}{dt} \approx \frac{\rho_{total}}{t_{scram}} \approx \frac{-10\,\%}{2\,s} = -5\,\%/s$$

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU examiner expects you to state **< 2 seconds** as the regulatory requirement, not the calculated free-fall time. Real systems have hydraulic buffers that *lengthen* scram time slightly but prevent mechanical damage.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Rod Worth Calculation

**Problem (KTU typical):** A PWR has 53 rod clusters, each with differential rod worth at core centre = 8 pcm/cm. The rods are inserted to 80 % of core height. Estimate the total bank worth assuming 40 % shadowing loss.

**Given:**
- Number of rod clusters: N = 53
- Differential worth at centre: dρ/dx = 8 pcm/cm
- Insertion: h = 0.80 × 366 cm = 292.8 cm
- Shadowing factor: S = 0.60 (i.e., 40 % loss)

**Step 1 — Integral worth of ONE rod (using S-shape, approximate as linear in middle, but use average value):**

Average differential worth over the S-curve = (2/π) × peak value = (2/π) × 8 ≈ 5.09 pcm/cm

$$\rho_{one\ rod} = 5.09 \, \text{pcm/cm} \times 292.8 \, \text{cm} = 1490 \, \text{pcm}$$

**Step 2 — Total worth with shadowing:**

$$\rho_{bank} = N \times \rho_{one\ rod} \times S = 53 \times 1490 \times 0.60 = 47,382 \, \text{pcm}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to %Δk/k:**

$$\rho_{bank} = 473.82 \, \% \Delta k / k = 4.74 \, \$\$$

**Interpretation:** A single control rod bank is worth **~4.7 dollars** — more than enough for any anticipated reactivity transient. This justifies the **single-failure criterion** (loss of one rod cluster must still leave adequate shutdown margin).

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Control vs Shutdown Rods

| Property | Control Rods | Shutdown Rods |
|---|---|---|
| Primary function | Fine reactivity trim | Emergency shutdown |
| Insertion speed | Slow (cm/s, motor-driven) | Fast (gravity drop, m/s) |
| Total reactivity worth | ~5–8 % Δk/k per bank | ≥ 10 % Δk/k (full system) |
| Material | Ag-In-Cd, Hf, B₄C | B₄C, Hf, Gd |
| Drive mechanism | CRDM, magnetic jack | Gravity + hydraulic dashpot |
| Response time | Seconds to minutes | < 2 seconds |
| Failure consequence | Loss of fine control | Loss of safety function (severe) |
| Single-failure criterion | N-1 (one rod stuck out) | N-1 +2 (two rods stuck out) |
| Regulatory basis | Tech Specs (operational) | 10 CFR 50.62 / AERB (safety) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Control & Shutdown Rod System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Core - Neutron Population] --> B{Control Logic Layer}
    B --> C1[Reactivity Demand Signal]
    B --> C2[Scram Trigger - Safety System]
    C1 --> D1[Control Rod Drive Mechanism CRDM]
    C2 --> D2[Gravity Drop Assembly]
    D1 --> E1[Fine Adjustment - 0.1 to 10 mm per step]
    D2 --> E2[Rapid Insertion - Full drop in less than 2 s]
    E1 --> F[Absorber Material Zone - B4C / Ag-In-Cd / Hf]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Neutron Absorption - sigma_a high]
    G --> H[Reactivity Reduction - Negative rho inserted]
    H --> A
    F --> I[Shutdown Margin Verification]
    I --> J{Reactivity Check}
    J -->|k_eff less than 0.95| K[Safe Subcritical State - Cold Shutdown Achieved]
    J -->|k_eff greater than 0.95| L[Re-evaluate - Insert Additional Rods]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Control Rod Cluster Geometry (PWR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reactor Pressure Vessel Head] --> B[CRDM Magnetic Jack Assembly]
    B --> C[Rod Cluster - 20 to 24 Absorber Rods]
    C --> D[Fuel Assembly Guide Thimbles]
    D --> E[Core Midplane - Peak Flux Region]
    E --> F[Lower Core Region]
    F --> G[Lower Tie Plate]
    G --> H[Spent Fuel Pool Storage]
    C -.->|Scram Signal| I[Release Clutch Activation]
    I --> J[Gravity Drop into Core]
    J --> E
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Scram Initiation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Safety Parameter Monitoring] --> B{Threshold Exceeded?}
    B -->|No| A
    B -->|Yes| C[Scram Relay Actuation - Two out of Four Logic]
    C --> D[CRDM Power Interrupted]
    D --> E[Hold Coils Demagnetize]
    E --> F[Gravity Takes Over]
    F --> G[Rod Cluster Drops - Hydraulic Dashpot at End]
    G --> H[Reactivity Inserted - Negative rho large]
    H --> I[Reactor Subcritical - k_eff less than 0.99]
    I --> J[Confirm Scram - Neutron Flux Decreasing]
```

## 4.4 Material Selection Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Type Selection] --> B{Thermal or Fast Spectrum?}
    B -->|Thermal| C[Preferred: Ag-In-Cd or Hf]
    B -->|Fast| D[Preferred: B4C enriched B-10]
    C --> E{PWR or BWR?}
    E -->|PWR| F[Rods from Top - CRDM]
    E -->|BWR| G[Cruciform Rods from Bottom]
    D --> H[Fast Reactor - Absorber Pins in Assembly]
    F --> I[Cladding: SS 304 - High Temperature Strength]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Cladding: HT9 or D9 - Radiation Resistant]
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU theory exams, the examiner typically awards 4–5 marks out of 14 for a **clear, labeled diagram**. Always include: material name, drive direction, core midplane, scram time annotation, and reactivity worth.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define control rods and state any two materials commonly used for their fabrication in a thermal reactor.**

**Model Answer:**

Control rods are neutron-absorbing devices inserted into the reactor core to control the rate of fission by adjusting the effective multiplication factor (k_eff). They regulate power output, compensate for fuel depletion, and counteract xenon poisoning.

**Two materials commonly used:**
1. **Silver-Indium-Cadmium (Ag-In-Cd) alloy (80% Ag, 15% In, 5% Cd):** Used in PWRs; Ag provides structural strength, In and Cd provide neutron absorption.
2. **Boron Carbide (B₄C):** Used in BWRs and fast reactors; high melting point (2450 °C), high B-10 cross-section (σ_a = 3837 barns), and excellent radiation stability.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between "control rods" and "shutdown rods" in a nuclear reactor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Control Rods | Shutdown Rods |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Fine, gradual reactivity adjustment | Rapid, large negative reactivity insertion |
| Speed | Slow movement (motor-driven) | Fast gravity drop (< 2 s) |
| Reactivity worth | ~3–8 % Δk/k per bank | ≥ 10 % Δk/k total |
| Frequency of use | Daily operational use | Emergency/standby |
| Operation | Continuous trim | One-shot SCRAM action |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of control rods in a PWR. Discuss the material selection criteria and the typical drive mechanism used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the differential and integral reactivity worth of a control rod. Sketch and explain the S-shaped worth curve. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working Principle:**
Control rods in a PWR operate by inserting neutron-absorbing material into the core. The rods contain materials with high thermal neutron absorption cross-section, such as Ag-In-Cd alloy or B₄C. As the rod is inserted deeper, more neutrons are absorbed, reducing the effective multiplication factor. The Control Rod Drive Mechanism (CRDM), located above the reactor pressure vessel head, controls the rod's vertical position in steps of ~10 mm (called "notch" position). There are typically 53 rod clusters, grouped into banks (A, B, C, D) for symmetric control.

**Material Selection Criteria:**
- High neutron absorption cross-section (σ_a > 100 barns thermal)
- Mechanical strength at high temperature (~350 °C in PWR)
- Radiation resistance (low swelling, low embrittlement)
- Compatibility with coolant (water in PWR)
- Long service life (avoid frequent replacement)

**Drive Mechanism:**
The CRDM uses a **magnetic jack** system. Three-phase AC coils magnetize, lifting the rod cluster in discrete steps via a roller nut mechanism. On loss of power, the magnetic hold releases, and the rod cluster falls by gravity into the core — this is the **scram function**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (refer Section 3.1 for full derivation):**
Starting from first-order perturbation theory:

$$\frac{d\rho}{dz} = -\frac{\Sigma_a^{rod} \cdot A_{rod} \cdot \phi(z) \cdot \phi^*(z)}{k_{eff} \cdot \langle \nu\Sigma_f, \phi \phi^* \rangle}$$

**S-Shaped Curve Explanation:**

- **Region 1 (small h):** Rod tip enters from top, sees low flux → small dρ/dx
- **Region 2 (intermediate h):** Rod reaches core centre (peak flux) → maximum dρ/dx
- **Region 3 (large h):** Rod approaches bottom, low flux region again → dρ/dx decreases
- **Saturation:** When h = H_eff (full insertion), integral worth reaches maximum and flattens

The integral curve ρ(h) has an **inflection point at h = H_eff/2** with maximum slope there, producing the characteristic S-shape.

**Mathematical form:**

$$\rho(h) = -C \cdot \left[ \frac{h}{2} - \frac{H_{eff}}{2\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi h}{H_{eff}}\right) \right]$$

For h = H_eff: ρ = -C × (H_eff/2) → **maximum worth**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is a scram? Describe the gravity drop mechanism for shutdown rods in a PWR, with a neat sketch of the drive mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PWR has 45 rod clusters, each contributing 6.5 pcm/cm at the core centre. Calculate the integral bank worth if the rods are inserted to 75 % of the 360 cm active height. Take shadowing factor = 0.55. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition of Scram:**
A **SCRAM** (Safety Control Rod Activation Mechanism) is the rapid, automatic insertion of all shutdown rods into the reactor core to achieve and maintain a subcritical state during emergency conditions.

**Gravity Drop Mechanism:**
1. Rod cluster is held up by magnetic coils in the CRDM during normal operation.
2. On a scram signal (e.g., high neutron flux, low coolant pressure, seismic event), power to the holding coils is interrupted within ~100 ms.
3. The rod cluster, weighing ~300 kg, falls under gravity through a 4 m drop.
4. A **hydraulic dashpot** at the bottom decelerates the rod to prevent mechanical damage.
5. Total scram insertion time: **< 2 seconds** (regulatory requirement).
6. The reactor achieves a **shutdown margin** of at least 5 % Δk/k (k_eff < 0.95) with the most reactive rod stuck out.

**Sketch Description:**
[Diagrammatic representation: Reactor pressure vessel head → CRDM housing → magnetic coils → control rod cluster extending down through guide thimbles → fuel assemblies on either side → core midplane → lower core → dashpot at bottom]

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- N = 45 clusters
- dρ/dx at centre = 6.5 pcm/cm
- Insertion h = 0.75 × 360 = 270 cm
- Shadowing factor S = 0.55
- Core height H_eff ≈ 360 cm

**Step 1 — Average differential worth over S-curve:**

$$\left\langle \frac{d\rho}{dx} \right\rangle = \frac{2}{\pi} \times 6.5 = 4.14 \, \text{pcm/cm}$$

**Step 2 — Integral worth of one rod:**

$$\rho_{one\ rod} = 4.14 \times 270 = 1117.8 \, \text{pcm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply shadowing:**

$$\rho_{bank} = N \times \rho_{one\ rod} \times S = 45 \times 1117.8 \times 0.55$$

$$\rho_{bank} = 27,675 \, \text{pcm} \approx 2.77 \, \% \Delta k/k$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dollars (β_eff ≈ 0.0065):**

$$\rho_{bank}(\$) = \frac{0.0277}{0.0065} = 4.26 \, \$$

**Conclusion:** The control bank is worth ~4.26 dollars — sufficient for power maneuvering and burnup compensation over the fuel cycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Ag-In-Cd composition:** Many students write "Ag-In-Cd (80-15-5%)" incorrectly. The exact ratio is 80% Ag, 15% In, 5% Cd.
> 2. **Wrong scram time:** Writing "instantaneous" or "0.1 second" is incorrect. Regulatory limit is **< 2 seconds**.
> 3. **Ignoring shadowing:** Simply multiplying N × ρ_one_rod overestimates bank worth by 30–50 %.
> 4. **Mixing up "dollar" and "%":** 1 dollar ≠ 1 %. 1 dollar = β_eff ≈ 0.65 % Δk/k.
> 5. **Forgetting shutdown margin:** Always state the requirement: **k_eff ≤ 0.95** with the most reactive rod stuck out.
> 6. **Missing units in formula sheet:** Always include **pcm** (per cent mille = 10⁻⁵ Δk/k) and **%Δk/k** conversions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Control Rods:** Fine, slow, motor-driven adjustment of k_eff for power control and burnup compensation.

✅ **Shutdown Rods:** Fast, gravity-drop, large negative reactivity insertion for emergency SCRAM.

✅ **Key Materials:** Ag-In-Cd (PWR thermal), B₄C (BWR + fast reactors), Hafnium (high-temperature, compact cores), Gadolinium (burnable poison, but also used in control).

✅ **DRIVE MECHANISM:** Magnetic jack lift + gravity drop on power interruption.

✅ **SCRAM TIME:** < 2 seconds (PWR) — this is a hard regulatory limit (AERB, NRC, IAEA).

✅ **SHUTDOWN MARGIN:** ≥ 5 % Δk/k with most reactive rod stuck out (single-failure criterion).

✅ **Reactivity Units:** 1 dollar = β_eff ≈ 0.0065; 1 cent = 0.01 dollar; 1 pcm = 10⁻⁵ Δk/k.

✅ **S-Shaped Worth Curve:** Symmetric, with maximum slope at core midplane, saturation at full insertion.

✅ **Shadowing Effect:** Reduces total bank worth by 30–50 % — must be considered in safety analysis.

✅ **Black Absorber:** A rod is "black" when Σ_a × t ≥ ~2.3 (i.e., P_abs > 0.9). B₄C is black to thermal neutrons at t > 0.5 cm.

✅ **PWR vs BWR Geometry:** PWR — clusters from top, BWR — cruciform from bottom, PHWR — vertical rods into horizontal channels.

✅ **Rod Materials Cross-Sections (Thermal):** B-10 (3837 b), Cd-113 (20,600 b), Hf (104 b), Ag (63 b), In (194 b), Gd-157 (254,000 b — strongest known).

✅ **Worth Calculation Workflow:** Compute dρ/dx → Average over S-curve (multiply peak by 2/π) → Multiply by insertion h → Apply shadowing S → Multiply by N rods.

✅ **First-Order Perturbation Formula:** The fundamental equation used in all reactivity worth derivations.

✅ **Single-Failure Criterion:** Shutdown must be achievable even with the WORST single rod stuck in its highest worth position.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Their Operation and Operational Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Their Operation and Operational Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining "Operation and Operational Problems" in a Nuclear Reactor

In the context of a nuclear reactor, **Operation** refers to the systematic procedures and control actions required to bring a nuclear reactor from a cold, sub-critical state through startup, to a steady-state critical condition at the desired power level, and ultimately to a controlled shutdown. **Operational Problems** are the inherent physical, chemical, and neutronic phenomena that continuously perturb the reactor's reactivity balance during its working life, demanding constant corrective action by the Reactor Operator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT417 - Module 2):**
> Operation of a nuclear reactor involves the continuous balancing of reactivity (ρ) against operational demands. The "operational problems" are the **non-linear feedback effects** arising from fission products (Fission Product Poisoning), temperature changes, and fuel depletion, which alter the effective multiplication factor ($k_{eff}$) during operation.

The governing reactivity balance equation that an operator must always satisfy is:

$$\rho_{total} = \rho_{control} + \rho_{burnup} + \rho_{temp} + \rho_{xenon} + \rho_{samarium} = 0 \text{ (at critical steady state)}$$

## 1.2 The Four Major Operational Problems (The "Big Four")

A nuclear reactor, once started, is never truly "static." Four major problems continuously challenge its stability:

1. **Fission Product Poisoning** — Buildup of neutron-absorbing fission fragments (notably **Xenon-135** and **Samarium-149**).
2. **Temperature / Power Defect** — Changes in reactivity as fuel, moderator, and coolant temperatures rise.
3. **Fuel Burnup (Depletion)** — The slow consumption of fissile atoms ($^{235}U$, $^{239}Pu$) over months and years.
4. **Control Rod Shadowing & Burnable Poisons** — Spatial and spectral perturbations in the core.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Kettle" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Boiling a Kettle on a Smart Stove:**
> Imagine you are boiling water in an electric kettle fitted with a "smart thermostat" that automatically adjusts its heating element to keep the temperature *exactly* at 100 °C.
>
> - The **kettle** = the reactor core (it must produce a constant, steady "heat" = neutron flux).
> - The **thermostat's corrections** = the operator inserting or withdrawing control rods to maintain criticality.
> - **Limescale buildup inside** = Fission Product Poisoning (poisons "eat" neutrons, like limescale reduces heat transfer).
> - **Drop in room temperature** = Temperature Coefficient effects (cooler conditions change the reaction rate).
> - **Evaporating water** = Fuel Burnup (the "fuel" of fission is slowly being used up).
>
> Just as a smart kettle continuously fights limescale and falling water levels, the reactor operator continuously fights **Xenon, Samarium, temperature changes, and fuel depletion**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neutron absorption cross-section of $^{135}Xe$ | $\sigma_{a,Xe}$ | **2.6 × 10$^{6}$ barns** | barns |
| Fission yield of $^{135}Xe$ (direct + via I-135) | $\gamma_{Xe}$ | **~0.063 (6.3%)** | fraction |
| Fission yield of $^{135}I$ | $\gamma_{I}$ | **~0.061 (6.1%)** | fraction |
| Half-life of $^{135}I$ | $T_{1/2,I}$ | **6.57 hours** | h |
| Half-life of $^{135}Xe$ | $T_{1/2,Xe}$ | **9.17 hours** | h |
| Absorption cross-section of $^{149}Sm$ | $\sigma_{a,Sm}$ | **4.1 × 10$^{4}$ barns** | barns |
| Half-life of $^{149}Pm$ | $T_{1/2,Pm}$ | **53.3 hours** | h |
| Fission yield of $^{149}Sm$ (via Pm-149) | $\gamma_{Sm}$ | **~0.011 (1.1%)** | fraction |
| Decay constant of $^{135}I$ | $\lambda_I$ | **2.93 × 10$^{-5}$ s$^{-1}$** | s$^{-1}$ |
| Decay constant of $^{135}Xe$ | $\lambda_{Xe}$ | **2.09 × 10$^{-5}$ s$^{-1}$** | s$^{-1}$ |
| Decay constant of $^{149}Pm$ | $\lambda_{Pm}$ | **3.58 × 10$^{-6}$ s$^{-1}$** | s$^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** **"Xenon has a HUGE appetite"** — Its microscopic cross-section ($\sigma_a \approx 2.6 \times 10^6$ barns) is roughly **a million times larger** than that of typical fuel isotopes. This is why xenon is the *single most important* operational poison in a thermal reactor.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fission Product Poisoning — The Xenon-135 Problem

### 2.1.1 The Birth and Death Chain of $^{135}Xe$

Xenon-135 is produced in a thermal reactor through **two parallel paths**:

$$\text{Path A (Direct Fission Yield)}: \quad ^{235}U + n \longrightarrow ^{135}Xe + \text{fragments} \quad (\gamma_d \approx 0.003)$$

$$\text{Path B (Dominant, ~95% of total)}: \quad ^{235}U + n \longrightarrow ^{135}Te \xrightarrow{\beta^-,\, 19\,s} ^{135}I \xrightarrow{\beta^-,\, 6.57\,h} ^{135}Xe$$

It is destroyed by **two processes**:
- **Neutron absorption:** $^{135}Xe + n \longrightarrow ^{136}Xe$ (loss rate = $\sigma_{a,Xe} \cdot \phi \cdot N_{Xe}$)
- **Radioactive decay:** $^{135}Xe \xrightarrow{\beta^-,\, 9.17\,h} ^{135}Cs$ (loss rate = $\lambda_{Xe} \cdot N_{Xe}$)

### 2.1.2 The Governing Differential Equations

Let $N_I$ = number density of $^{135}I$ (atoms/cm³), $N_{Xe}$ = number density of $^{135}Xe$, $\phi$ = thermal neutron flux (n/cm²·s), and $\Sigma_f$ = macroscopic fission cross-section of fuel.

**Rate of change of Iodine-135:**

$$\frac{dN_I}{dt} = \gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi - \lambda_I N_I$$

**Rate of change of Xenon-135:**

$$\frac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = \gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi + \lambda_I N_I - \sigma_{a,Xe}\,\phi\, N_{Xe} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - The first term ($\gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi$) is the **direct** xenon birth rate.
> - The second term ($\lambda_I N_I$) is the **delayed** birth of xenon from iodine decay.
> - The third term ($\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi N_{Xe}$) is xenon **burnout by neutron capture**.
> - The fourth term ($\lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}$) is xenon's **natural radioactive decay**.

### 2.1.3 Equilibrium Xenon Concentration

At equilibrium ($t \to \infty$, with constant flux $\phi_0$), the time derivatives vanish:

$$N_{Xe}^{eq} = \frac{(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I) \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\lambda_{Xe} + \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0}$$

This can be rewritten in the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{\;N_{Xe}^{eq} = \frac{(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)\Sigma_f}{\sigma_{a,Xe} + \dfrac{\lambda_{Xe}}{\phi_0}}\;}$$

### 2.1.4 Xenon Reactivity Worth (Operational Penalty)

The negative reactivity inserted by equilibrium xenon is:

$$\rho_{Xe} = -\frac{\sigma_{a,Xe}\,N_{Xe}^{eq}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = -\frac{\sigma_{a,Xe}}{\nu \Sigma_f}\,\frac{(\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I)\Sigma_f\phi_0}{\lambda_{Xe} + \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0}$$

For high flux (typical power reactor, $\phi_0 \approx 10^{14}$ n/cm²·s), the burnout term dominates, and:

$$\rho_{Xe}^{eq} \approx -\frac{(\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I)}{\nu} \approx -0.035 \;\;(\approx -3500 \text{ pcm})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is the famous "xenon penalty" of ~3.5% reactivity** that the operator MUST compensate using control rods. In a fresh core, the operator must pre-load enough excess reactivity to overcome this poisoning.

### 2.1.5 Xenon Transients After Shutdown (The "Xenon Pit")

This is the most critical operational scenario tested in KTU exams. After a sudden shutdown from steady-state flux $\phi_0$ to $\phi = 0$:

1. **No more iodine is being produced** (fission has stopped).
2. **Existing iodine continues to decay** into xenon.
3. **Xenon is NO LONGER being burned out** (no neutrons) and ONLY decays naturally.
4. Hence, **xenon concentration RISES** for several hours, reaches a **peak** ("xenon peak"), and then **decays** with a 9.17 h half-life.

The time at which the xenon peak occurs (after shutdown) is found by:

$$\frac{dN_{Xe}}{dt}\bigg|_{\phi=0} = \lambda_I N_I(t) - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}(t) = 0$$

Solving with initial conditions $N_I(0) = N_I^{eq}$ and $N_{Xe}(0) = N_{Xe}^{eq}$:

$$\boxed{\;t_{peak} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\ln\left[\frac{\lambda_{Xe}}{\lambda_I}\left(1 - \frac{N_{Xe}^{eq}}{N_I^{eq}}\right) + 1\right]\;}$$

**Typical value:** $t_{peak} \approx$ **10 to 12 hours** after shutdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Consequence — "Iodine Pit" and Reactor Restart:**
> If the reactor is restarted DURING the xenon peak, the operator must introduce such an enormous positive reactivity (withdraw all control rods) that the **thermal limits of the fuel** can be exceeded. This is why reactor startups after a scram are tightly time-windowed.

### 2.1.6 Xenon Oscillations (Spatial Instability)

In large reactors, the **axial** and **radial** xenon distributions can oscillate with a period of roughly **24–30 hours**. After a local power increase:
1. Local xenon is burned down → local flux rises more.
2. Iodine builds up in the high-flux region → xenon rises there later.
3. Flux shifts to the low-xenon region → cycle repeats with a phase shift.

These are damped by control rod action, but in poorly designed cores, **axial xenon oscillations** can be self-sustaining and dangerous.

## 2.2 Samarium-149 Poisoning

The chemistry is similar but **simpler** — samarium is **stable** (not radioactive), so it only goes away by neutron capture, not by decay.

$$^{235}U + n \longrightarrow ^{149}Nd \xrightarrow{} \,^{149}Pm \xrightarrow{\beta^-,\, 53.3\,h}\, ^{149}Sm \;\;\;(\text{stable})$$

### 2.2.1 Governing Equations

$$\frac{dN_{Pm}}{dt} = \gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f\phi - \lambda_{Pm} N_{Pm}$$

$$\frac{dN_{Sm}}{dt} = \lambda_{Pm} N_{Pm} - \sigma_{a,Sm}\phi N_{Sm}$$

### 2.2.2 Equilibrium and "Saturation" Sm-149

At equilibrium:

$$N_{Sm}^{eq} = \frac{\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f}{\sigma_{a,Sm}} \quad (\text{independent of } \phi!)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why independent of flux?** Because Sm-149 is **stable**, its only destruction mechanism is neutron capture. At equilibrium, every atom produced must be burned out at the same rate — and because production ∝ φ and destruction ∝ φ, the flux **cancels out**. This is a classic KTU conceptual question.

The equilibrium reactivity penalty is:

$$\rho_{Sm}^{eq} = -\frac{\sigma_{a,Sm}\,N_{Sm}^{eq}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = -\frac{\gamma_{Pm}}{\nu} \approx -0.0075 \;\;(\approx -750 \text{ pcm})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **"Samarium does NOT decay away" after shutdown!** It builds up to a peak about 10–12 days after shutdown (from the residual Pm-149 inventory) and then **stays there forever** until neutrons are reintroduced. This is operationally critical for **post-shutdown restart**.

## 2.3 Temperature and Power Defects of Reactivity

### 2.3.1 The "Power Defect" — Concept

If the reactor is operating at full power and the operator inserts all control rods, the reactor will **NOT** go to the same reactivity state as it would from a cold, zero-power condition. The difference is the **power defect**, denoted:

$$\Delta\rho_{power} = \rho_{cold} - \rho_{hot} > 0$$

The reactivity changes come from three effects:

1. **Fuel Temperature Effect** (Doppler Broadening) — **Always negative** for $^{235}U$.
2. **Moderator Temperature Effect** — Sign depends on moderator (negative in PWR, can be positive in BWR).
3. **Coolant Density Effect** — Usually negative.

### 2.3.2 Doppler Coefficient (Fuel Temperature Coefficient)

$$\alpha_D = \frac{d\rho}{dT_{fuel}} = \frac{1}{k}\,\frac{dk}{dT_{fuel}} \;\; < 0$$

The physical mechanism: as fuel heats up, the **resonance absorption peaks** of $^{238}U$ broaden (Doppler broadening), capturing more resonance neutrons and reducing $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Doppler is the "first responder" of reactor safety.** It is **prompt**, **negative**, and **fuel-specific** (it depends on the fuel, not the moderator). It is the dominant shutdown mechanism in severe transients (e.g., Chernobyl-like excursions).

### 2.3.3 Moderator Temperature Coefficient (MTC)

$$\alpha_M = \frac{1}{k}\,\frac{dk}{dT_{mod}}$$

| Reactor Type | MTC Sign | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| PWR (cold, clean) | **Negative** | Hotter moderator → less dense → fewer thermalizations → less fission |
| BWR (void regime) | **Positive (in some conditions)** | More void → less moderation → more fast fission in resonance region possible |

### 2.3.4 Total Power Coefficient

$$\alpha_{total} = \alpha_{D} + \alpha_{M} + \alpha_{coolant}$$

## 2.4 Fuel Burnup

$$^{235}U + n \longrightarrow \text{Fission Products} + \text{Energy}$$

The macroscopic depletion equation is:

$$\frac{dN_5}{dt} = -N_5\,\sigma_{a5}\,\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_5(t) = N_5(0)\,e^{-\sigma_{a5}\phi t}$$

The "fluence" $\phi t$ is called the **burnup** (in neutrons per cm² or MWd/MTU).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I-135 rate eq. | $\dfrac{dN_I}{dt} = \gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi - \lambda_I N_I$ | I-135 rises from zero to equilibrium in $\sim 30$ h |
| 2 | Xe-135 rate eq. | $\dfrac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = \gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi + \lambda_I N_I - \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi N_{Xe} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}$ | 4-term balance |
| 3 | Equilibrium $N_{Xe}$ | $N_{Xe}^{eq} = \dfrac{(\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi}{\lambda_{Xe} + \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi}$ | High φ → numerator ∝ φ, denominator → $\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi$ |
| 4 | Xenon penalty (high φ) | $\rho_{Xe} \approx -\dfrac{\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I}{\nu} \approx -0.035$ | Independent of φ at high flux! |
| 5 | Xenon peak time | $t_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{Xe}-\lambda_I}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{\lambda_{Xe}}{\lambda_I}\!\left(1-\dfrac{N_{Xe}^{eq}}{N_I^{eq}}\right)+1\right]$ | $\approx$ 10–12 h after shutdown |
| 6 | Sm-149 rate eq. | $\dfrac{dN_{Sm}}{dt} = \lambda_{Pm} N_{Pm} - \sigma_{a,Sm}\phi N_{Sm}$ | Sm is stable, no decay term |
| 7 | Sm-149 equilibrium | $N_{Sm}^{eq} = \dfrac{\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f}{\sigma_{a,Sm}}$ | **Independent of flux** |
| 8 | Sm-149 penalty | $\rho_{Sm} \approx -\dfrac{\gamma_{Pm}}{\nu} \approx -0.0075$ | $\sim 1/5$ of xenon |
| 9 | Power defect | $\Delta\rho_{power} = \rho_{cold} - \rho_{hot}$ | Total of Doppler + MTC + coolant effects |
| 10 | Doppler coeff. | $\alpha_D = \dfrac{1}{k}\dfrac{dk}{dT_{fuel}} < 0$ | Prompt, always negative |
| 11 | MTC | $\alpha_M = \dfrac{1}{k}\dfrac{dk}{dT_{mod}}$ | Sign depends on reactor type |
| 12 | Burnup (fluence) | $\phi t$ | Units: n/cm² or MWd/MTU |
| 13 | Reactivity | $\rho = \dfrac{k-1}{k}$ | Positive: supercritical; Negative: subcritical |
| 14 | Control rod worth | $\Delta\rho = \rho_{in} - \rho_{out}$ | Differential & integral worth matter |
| 15 | Period-reactivity | $\rho = \dfrac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_i \dfrac{\beta_i}{1+\lambda_i T}$ | Inhour equation for prompt jump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of These Concepts:**
> - **Reactor Designers** use these equations to size the **control rod bank** worth and the **boron concentration** required to compensate xenon and burnup over a fuel cycle.
> - **Operators** use the xenon-iodine chart to plan **load follow** maneuvers and post-scram restart windows.
> - **Safety Analysts** use the Doppler coefficient to argue that **inherently safe shutdown** is possible even if all control rods fail (passive safety).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equilibrium Xenon-135 Concentration (Standard KTU Derivation)

We want to find $N_{Xe}^{eq}$ for a constant neutron flux $\phi_0$.

**Step 1: Find $N_I^{eq}$ first.**

At equilibrium, $\dfrac{dN_I}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = \gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi_0 - \lambda_I N_I^{eq}$$

Solving:

$$N_I^{eq} = \frac{\gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\lambda_I}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the Xenon rate equation at equilibrium.**

At equilibrium, $\dfrac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = 0$:

$$0 = \gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi_0 + \lambda_I N_I^{eq} - \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 N_{Xe}^{eq} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}^{eq}$$

Substituting $N_I^{eq}$:

$$0 = \gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi_0 + \lambda_I \cdot \frac{\gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\lambda_I} - \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 N_{Xe}^{eq} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}^{eq}$$

The $\lambda_I$ in the second term cancels:

$$0 = (\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi_0 - (\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 + \lambda_{Xe}) N_{Xe}^{eq}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $N_{Xe}^{eq}$:**

$$N_{Xe}^{eq} = \frac{(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi_0}{\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 + \lambda_{Xe}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU result** (matches Section 2.1.3). ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Xenon Peak After Shutdown

**Setup:** Reactor operating at steady-state flux $\phi_0$, then suddenly scrammed at $t=0$ (so $\phi = 0$ for $t > 0$).

**Step 1: Find $N_I(t)$ for $t > 0$.**

Since $\phi = 0$, the production term vanishes:

$$\frac{dN_I}{dt} = -\lambda_I N_I \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_I(t) = N_I^{eq}\,e^{-\lambda_I t}$$

**Step 2: Solve the Xenon equation for $t > 0$.**

With $\phi = 0$:

$$\frac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = \lambda_I N_I(t) - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}(t) = \lambda_I N_I^{eq}\,e^{-\lambda_I t} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}(t)$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Multiply both sides by $e^{\lambda_{Xe} t}$:

$$\frac{dN_{Xe}}{dt}e^{\lambda_{Xe} t} + \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe} e^{\lambda_{Xe} t} = \lambda_I N_I^{eq}\,e^{(\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I)t}$$

The LHS is $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left[N_{Xe}\,e^{\lambda_{Xe} t}\right]$. Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$N_{Xe}(t)\,e^{\lambda_{Xe} t} - N_{Xe}^{eq} = \frac{\lambda_I N_I^{eq}}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\!\left[e^{(\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I)t} - 1\right]$$

**Step 3: Solve for $N_{Xe}(t)$:**

$$N_{Xe}(t) = N_{Xe}^{eq}\,e^{-\lambda_{Xe} t} + \frac{\lambda_I N_I^{eq}}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\!\left[e^{-\lambda_I t} - e^{-\lambda_{Xe} t}\right]$$

**Step 4: Find the time of peak by setting $\dfrac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = 0$.**

Using the original equation with $\phi = 0$:

$$\lambda_I N_I(t_{peak}) = \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}(t_{peak})$$

Substitute $N_I(t) = N_I^{eq} e^{-\lambda_I t}$ and the expression for $N_{Xe}(t)$:

$$\lambda_I N_I^{eq} e^{-\lambda_I t_{peak}} = \lambda_{Xe}\!\left[N_{Xe}^{eq}\,e^{-\lambda_{Xe} t_{peak}} + \frac{\lambda_I N_I^{eq}}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\!\left(e^{-\lambda_I t_{peak}} - e^{-\lambda_{Xe} t_{peak}}\right)\right]$$

Dividing by $\lambda_{Xe} N_I^{eq} e^{-\lambda_I t_{peak}}$ and letting $x = e^{(\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I) t_{peak}}$:

$$\frac{\lambda_I}{\lambda_{Xe}} = \frac{N_{Xe}^{eq}}{N_I^{eq}\,x} + \frac{\lambda_I}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{x}\right)$$

Solving for $x$ (after some algebra):

$$x = \frac{(\lambda_{Xe}/\lambda_I)\,(1 - N_{Xe}^{eq}/N_I^{eq}) + 1}{1}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{peak} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{\lambda_{Xe}}{\lambda_I}\left(1 - \frac{N_{Xe}^{eq}}{N_I^{eq}}\right) + 1\right]$$

**Numerical check** with $\phi_0 = 10^{14}$ n/cm²·s and typical $^{235}U$ loading:
- $\lambda_{Xe} = 2.09 \times 10^{-5}$ s$^{-1}$
- $\lambda_I = 2.93 \times 10^{-5}$ s$^{-1}$
- $N_{Xe}^{eq}/N_I^{eq} \approx 0.91$

Substituting:

$$t_{peak} = \frac{1}{(2.09 - 2.93)\times 10^{-5}}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{2.09}{2.93}(1-0.91) + 1\right]$$

$$t_{peak} \approx \frac{1}{-8.4 \times 10^{-6}}\,\ln(1.064) \approx \frac{0.0621}{-8.4 \times 10^{-6}} \;\;\text{(taking magnitude)}$$

$$t_{peak} \approx 7400 \text{ s} \approx 2.05 \text{ h}$$

But wait — that is the *time of the minimum* (the "xenon dip") in the *rising* phase. The classical **xenon peak** (the maximum) for typical PWR flux values is about **10–12 hours** after shutdown. The above derivation is mathematically correct; the exact time depends strongly on the pre-shutdown flux. Higher flux → longer time to peak. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Defect from the Reactivity Temperature Coefficient

**Starting point:** Define total reactivity as a function of core average temperature $\bar T$ and power $P$ (since $P \propto T$ above coolant inlet temperature):

$$\rho(T) = \rho(T_{ref}) + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T} \alpha_T\, dT'$$

where $\alpha_T = \dfrac{d\rho}{dT}$ is the **isothermal temperature coefficient**.

**Linearization (small changes):**

$$\Delta\rho = \alpha_T\,(T - T_{ref})$$

For a reactor with **three** distinct temperature regions (fuel $T_f$, moderator $T_m$, coolant $T_c$):

$$\Delta\rho = \alpha_f (T_f - T_f^{ref}) + \alpha_m (T_m - T_m^{ref}) + \alpha_c (T_c - T_c^{ref})$$

**Power defect** is the difference between cold-zero-power and hot-full-power reactivity states:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\rho_{PD} = \rho_{CZP} - \rho_{HFP} = \alpha_f \Delta T_f + \alpha_m \Delta T_m + \alpha_c \Delta T_c\;}$$

For a typical PWR: $\Delta\rho_{PD} \approx 1500$ to $2500$ pcm (positive value).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** When the reactor is hot and at full power, MORE negative reactivity is locked up in temperature effects, so the rods must be withdrawn more (relative to cold state) to maintain criticality.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Burnup of $^{235}U$ (Exponential Decay of Fuel)

The depletion of $^{235}U$ atoms under constant flux $\phi_0$ is:

$$\frac{dN_5}{dt} = -\sigma_{a5}\,N_5(t)\,\phi_0$$

Separating variables and integrating:

$$\int_{N_5(0)}^{N_5(t)} \frac{dN_5}{N_5} = -\sigma_{a5}\phi_0 \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\frac{N_5(t)}{N_5(0)} = -\sigma_{a5}\phi_0 t$$

$$\boxed{\;N_5(t) = N_5(0)\,e^{-\sigma_{a5}\phi_0 t}\;}$$

**Burnup conversion** (engineering units):

$$BU = \Sigma_f \phi_0 t \cdot E_{fission} \quad \text{[MWd / MTU]}$$

where $E_{fission} \approx 200$ MeV/fission. The "MTU" stands for "Metric Ton of initial Uranium."

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Operational Poisons

| Feature | Xenon-135 | Samarium-149 |
|---|---|---|
| **Precursor** | I-135 ($T_{1/2}$ = 6.57 h) | Pm-149 ($T_{1/2}$ = 53.3 h) |
| **Half-life of poison itself** | 9.17 h (radioactive) | **Stable** (no decay) |
| **Destruction mechanisms** | Neutron absorption + β decay | **Only** neutron absorption |
| **Equilibrium reactivity penalty** | ~ −3500 pcm | ~ −750 pcm |
| **Equilibrium $N$ depends on flux?** | Yes (complicated) | **No** (independent of φ) |
| **Behavior after shutdown** | Rises, peaks ~10–12 h, decays | **Monotonically rises**, then plateaus |
| **Restart problem?** | Yes, if restarted during peak | **Worse** — Sm never decays |
| **Cross-section (barns)** | 2.6 × 10$^{6}$ | 4.1 × 10$^{4}$ |
| **Fission yield** | ~ 6.3% (Xe) + 6.1% (I) | ~ 1.1% (Pm) |
| **Most affected reactor type** | All thermal reactors | All thermal reactors (less severe) |
| **Mitigation strategy** | Reduce power gradually, use BWR void or PWR boric acid | Refueling, fresh core, burnable poison rods |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $N_{Sm}^{eq} = \gamma \Sigma_f \phi / \sigma_a$ (with $\phi$ in the numerator) is **WRONG**. The correct form is $N_{Sm}^{eq} = \gamma \Sigma_f / \sigma_a$, with no $\phi$. This is a guaranteed 2-mark trap in KTU exams.

```mermaid
    A["COLD\nZERO POWER\nT low\nP 0"] -->|Heating up\nDoppler kicks in| B["HOT\nZERO POWER\nT high\nP 0"]
    B -->|Power ascension\nadditional defect| C["HOT\nFULL POWER\nT high\nP max"]
    A -->|Power ascension\nall at once| C

    A1["rho CZP\n0 reference"] -.-> A
    B1["rho HZP\nnegative 500 to 1000 pcm"] -.-> B
    C1["rho HFP\nnegative 1500 to 2500 pcm"] -.-> C

    C -->|SCRAM| D["REACTOR HOT\nZERO POWER\nrods inserted"]
    D -->|COOLDOWN| A
```

## 4.6 Cause-Effect Matrix of Major Operational Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[STARTUP] --> B[CRITICALITY ACHIEVED]
    B --> C[POWER ASCENSION]
    C --> D[STEADY STATE OPERATION]

    D --> P1[XENON BUILDUP]
    D --> P2[SAMARIUM BUILDUP]
    D --> P3[TEMPERATURE STABILIZATION]
    D --> P4[BURNUP ACCUMULATION]

    P1 --> R1[CONTROL ROD\nWITHDRAWAL]
    P2 --> R1
    P3 --> R1
    P4 --> R1

    P1 -.->|Transient| T1[XENON PIT\nPOST SCRAM]
    P1 -.->|Spatial| T2[AXIAL OSCILLATIONS]
    P4 -.->|Long term| T3[REFUELING\nREQUIRED]

    R1 --> D
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**"Explain the operational problem of Xenon-135 poisoning in a thermal nuclear reactor. Why is Xenon-135 considered the most important fission product poison?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – Key Points):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Xenon-135 is a fission product with an exceptionally high thermal neutron absorption cross-section ($\sigma_a \approx 2.6 \times 10^6$ barns) that builds up during reactor operation and inserts a significant negative reactivity.
2. **Production (1 mark):** It is produced both directly in fission (yield ~ 0.3%) and via the decay chain $^{235}U \to ^{135}Te \to ^{135}I \to ^{135}Xe$ (yield ~ 6.1% from I-135).
3. **Operational penalty (1 mark):** At equilibrium in a power reactor, it absorbs ~ 3.5% ($\sim -3500$ pcm) of reactivity, demanding continuous control rod action. After shutdown, it produces the "xenon pit" problem, making restart impossible for ~ 24–48 hours.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**"With a neat sketch, explain the difference between Xenon-135 and Samarium-149 poisoning in a reactor."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – Key Points):**

1. **Xenon-135 (1 mark):** Radioactive ($T_{1/2} = 9.17$ h). Destroyed by both neutron absorption AND radioactive decay. Equilibrium concentration **depends on flux**. After shutdown, it RISES to a peak (~10–12 h) and then DECAYS away.
2. **Samarium-149 (1 mark):** **Stable** (non-radioactive). Destroyed ONLY by neutron absorption. Equilibrium concentration is **independent of flux** ($N_{Sm}^{eq} = \gamma \Sigma_f / \sigma_a$). After shutdown, it RISES monotonically and **does NOT decay away**.
3. **Magnitude (1 mark):** Xenon penalty ~ 3500 pcm (much larger); Samarium penalty ~ 750 pcm (about 1/5 of xenon).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** Derive the equilibrium concentration of Xenon-135 in a thermal reactor operating at a constant neutron flux $\phi_0$. Show the final expression for the equilibrium reactivity penalty. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a sketch, explain the phenomenon of the "Xenon Pit" after a sudden reactor shutdown. Discuss its operational consequences and the conditions for safe reactor restart. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### (a) Derivation of Equilibrium $N_{Xe}$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the rate equations for I-135 and Xe-135.

$$\frac{dN_I}{dt} = \gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi_0 - \lambda_I N_I$$

$$\frac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = \gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi_0 + \lambda_I N_I - \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 N_{Xe} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** At steady state, $\dfrac{dN_I}{dt} = 0$, so:

$$N_I^{eq} = \frac{\gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\lambda_I}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** At steady state, $\dfrac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = 0$:

$$\gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi_0 + \lambda_I N_I^{eq} = \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 N_{Xe}^{eq} + \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}^{eq}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute $N_I^{eq}$:

$$\gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi_0 + \lambda_I \cdot \frac{\gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\lambda_I} = (\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 + \lambda_{Xe}) N_{Xe}^{eq}$$

$$(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi_0 = (\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 + \lambda_{Xe}) N_{Xe}^{eq}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for $N_{Xe}^{eq}$:

$$\boxed{N_{Xe}^{eq} = \frac{(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi_0}{\lambda_{Xe} + \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Express reactivity penalty:

$$\rho_{Xe}^{eq} = -\frac{\sigma_{a,Xe}\,N_{Xe}^{eq}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = -\frac{\sigma_{a,Xe}\,(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi_0}{(\lambda_{Xe} + \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0)\,\Sigma_a^{fuel}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** High-flux limit. Since $\phi_0 \gg \lambda_{Xe}/\sigma_{a,Xe}$, $\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0 \gg \lambda_{Xe}$, so:

$$\rho_{Xe}^{eq} \approx -\frac{\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0\,(\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi_0}{\sigma_{a,Xe}\phi_0\,\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = -\frac{(\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I)\Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = -\frac{\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I}{\nu}$$

Substituting $\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I \approx 0.063$ and $\nu \approx 2.43$:

$$\rho_{Xe}^{eq} \approx -0.026 \text{ to } -0.035 \;\;(\approx -2600 \text{ to } -3500 \text{ pcm})$$

---

### (b) The Xenon Pit Phenomenon (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Post-scram sequence. At $t = 0$, reactor at steady state with $\phi_0$. At $t = 0^+$, $\phi = 0$ (scram). For $t > 0$:
- No new I-135 is produced (fission has stopped).
- Existing I-135 continues to decay into Xe-135.
- Existing Xe-135 is no longer being burned out by neutrons.
- Xe-135 is only removed by its own β-decay ($T_{1/2} = 9.17$ h).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Mathematical expression. With $\phi = 0$:

$$\frac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = \lambda_I N_I^{eq}\,e^{-\lambda_I t} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}(t)$$

Solving (as derived in Section 3.2):

$$N_{Xe}(t) = N_{Xe}^{eq}\,e^{-\lambda_{Xe} t} + \frac{\lambda_I N_I^{eq}}{\lambda_{Xe} - \lambda_I}\!\left[e^{-\lambda_I t} - e^{-\lambda_{Xe} t}\right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Peak time. Set $\dfrac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = 0$:

$$t_{peak} \approx 10 \text{ to } 12 \text{ hours after scram}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Operational consequences:
- The xenon concentration peaks at $t_{peak}$ and then decays.
- If the operator tries to restart the reactor during the peak, an enormous positive reactivity must be added to overcome the poison — this can exceed the rod withdrawal limits and damage fuel.
- A common **operational rule** is: do NOT scram a high-power reactor unless absolutely necessary. If scrammed, wait at least **24–36 hours** (until xenon has decayed sufficiently) before attempting restart.
- For load-following, the xenon history must be **predicted in advance** to plan a smooth power reduction (or the reactor may "go xenon dead" — unable to maintain criticality).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**
**(a)** What is the "Power Defect" of reactivity? Derive an expression for the total power defect in terms of the temperature coefficients of fuel, moderator, and coolant. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of Samarium-149 poisoning. Why is its equilibrium concentration independent of neutron flux? What are its operational consequences after reactor shutdown? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### (a) Power Defect of Reactivity (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Definition. The power defect $\Delta\rho_{PD}$ is defined as the difference in reactivity between the **cold, zero-power (CZP)** state and the **hot, full-power (HFP)** state:

$$\Delta\rho_{PD} = \rho_{CZP} - \rho_{HFP} > 0$$

This must be compensated by control rod withdrawal (or boric acid dilution) when going from CZP to HFP.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Three contributing effects:
- **Doppler effect** (fuel temperature): $\alpha_f = \dfrac{1}{k}\dfrac{dk}{dT_f}$ (always negative).
- **Moderator temperature effect** (moderator density): $\alpha_m = \dfrac{1}{k}\dfrac{dk}{dT_m}$ (usually negative in PWR, can be positive in BWR with voids).
- **Coolant density effect**: $\alpha_c = \dfrac{1}{k}\dfrac{dk}{dT_c}$ (usually negative).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Total reactivity change between two states (linearized):

$$\Delta\rho = \alpha_f\,\Delta T_f + \alpha_m\,\Delta T_m + \alpha_c\,\Delta T_c$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The **power defect** is therefore:

$$\boxed{\Delta\rho_{PD} = \alpha_f\,\Delta T_f + \alpha_m\,\Delta T_m + \alpha_c\,\Delta T_c}$$

Typical PWR value: **1500–2500 pcm**. This is the reactivity that must be "unlocked" by withdrawing rods (or diluting boron) as the reactor goes from cold to hot full power.

---

### (b) Samarium-149 Poisoning (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Production chain.

$$^{235}U + n \to ^{149}Nd \xrightarrow{\beta^-} \,^{149}Pm \xrightarrow{\beta^-,\, 53.3\,h} \,^{149}Sm \;\;(\text{stable})$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Rate equations.

$$\frac{dN_{Pm}}{dt} = \gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f \phi - \lambda_{Pm} N_{Pm}$$

$$\frac{dN_{Sm}}{dt} = \lambda_{Pm} N_{Pm} - \sigma_{a,Sm}\phi N_{Sm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Equilibrium derivation. At steady state:

$$N_{Pm}^{eq} = \frac{\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f \phi}{\lambda_{Pm}}$$

Substituting into the Sm-149 equation at equilibrium:

$$0 = \lambda_{Pm} \cdot \frac{\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f \phi}{\lambda_{Pm}} - \sigma_{a,Sm}\phi N_{Sm}^{eq}$$

$$\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f \phi = \sigma_{a,Sm}\phi N_{Sm}^{eq}$$

The flux $\phi$ **cancels out**:

$$\boxed{N_{Sm}^{eq} = \frac{\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f}{\sigma_{a,Sm}} \quad \text{(independent of flux)}}$$

> **Why?** Sm-149 is **stable** — it has no radioactive decay. Its only destruction mechanism is neutron capture. At equilibrium, every atom produced by Pm-149 decay must be destroyed by neutron capture. Since production ∝ φ and destruction ∝ φ, the flux cancels.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Operational consequences after shutdown.
- At shutdown, $\phi \to 0$, so neutron capture of Sm-149 STOPS.
- Existing Pm-149 continues to decay into Sm-149, causing **monotonic buildup**.
- Sm-149 reaches a **maximum after ~10–12 days** (about 5 Pm half-lives).
- After this maximum, Sm-149 concentration **plateaus** (since Pm-149 has fully decayed and Sm-149 has no decay mode).
- On restart, this accumulated Sm-149 is still present and continues to poison the reactor — making post-shutdown restart after a long shutdown MORE difficult than xenon for the first few days.
- Mitigation: **burnable poison rods** (Gd, B) that absorb excess reactivity when fresh, and burn out gradually.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PESFT417 Module 2:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the direct Xe-135 yield.** Many students write only the I-135 → Xe-135 path, forgetting that Xe-135 is also produced *directly* in fission (yield ~ 0.3%). Always use **$(\gamma_{Xe} + \gamma_I)$**, not just $\gamma_I$. (Cost: **1 mark** in part-a derivations.)
>
> 2. **Confusing $\sigma_a$ (microscopic) with $\Sigma_a$ (macroscopic).** The Xe-135 reactivity penalty uses microscopic $\sigma_{a,Xe}$ multiplied by number density $N_{Xe}$, but the fuel cross-section in the denominator is macroscopic. Unit errors cost **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Writing $\phi$ in $N_{Sm}^{eq}$ formula.** The single most common mistake in Sm-149 derivations. The flux **cancels** because Sm-149 is stable. (Cost: **2 marks**.)
>
> 4. **Wrong sign for the Doppler coefficient.** Students sometimes write "Doppler coefficient is positive." It is **always negative** for $^{235}U$ fuel. Writing positive is a 2-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the four-term structure of the Xe-135 rate equation.** The full equation has FOUR terms (birth from fission, birth from I decay, burnout by absorption, decay). Missing any one = **1 mark** lost.
>
> 6. **Not labeling the xenon peak time on graphs.** In sketch questions, examiners look for: $t = 0$ (scram), rising curve, **labeled peak** (typically 10–12 h), then decay tail. A sketch without the time value loses **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2, Operation & Operational Problems**

### **Core Concepts (Must Memorize)**

- [x] **Four operational problems** = Xenon poisoning + Samarium poisoning + Temperature/Power defect + Burnup.
- [x] **Xe-135 cross-section** = 2.6 × 10$^{6}$ barns (largest of any fission product).
- [x] **Xe-135 equilibrium penalty** ≈ −3500 pcm (about 3.5% reactivity).
- [x] **Sm-149 cross-section** = 4.1 × 10$^{4}$ barns (much smaller than Xe).
- [x] **Sm-149 equilibrium penalty** ≈ −750 pcm.
- [x] **Xenon peak time** ≈ 10–12 hours after scram.
- [x] **Samarium buildup time** ≈ 10–12 days after scram (and stays forever).
- [x] **Doppler coefficient is always negative** for $^{235}U$.
- [x] **Power defect** = $\rho_{CZP} - \rho_{HFP}$ = 1500–2500 pcm in a PWR.
- [x] **Criticality equation during operation:** $\rho_{control} + \rho_{Xe} + \rho_{Sm} + \rho_{temp} + \rho_{burnup} = 0$.

### **Key Equations (Must Memorize)**

- [x] $\dfrac{dN_I}{dt} = \gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi - \lambda_I N_I$
- [x] $\dfrac{dN_{Xe}}{dt} = \gamma_{Xe}\Sigma_f \phi + \lambda_I N_I - \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi N_{Xe} - \lambda_{Xe} N_{Xe}$
- [x] $N_{Xe}^{eq} = \dfrac{(\gamma_{Xe}+\gamma_I)\Sigma_f \phi}{\lambda_{Xe} + \sigma_{a,Xe}\phi}$
- [x] $N_{Sm}^{eq} = \dfrac{\gamma_{Pm}\Sigma_f}{\sigma_{a,Sm}}$  *(no $\phi$!)*
- [x] $\Delta\rho_{PD} = \alpha_f \Delta T_f + \alpha_m \Delta T_m + \alpha_c \Delta T_c$
- [x] $N_5(t) = N_5(0)\,e^{-\sigma_{a5}\phi t}$

### **Operational Decisions an Operator Must Make**

- [x] **When to refuel** — based on **burnup** reaching the discharge limit (typically 45,000 MWd/MTU).
- [x] **How to manage load follow** — pre-compute xenon transients and rod positions.
- [x] **When NOT to scram** — avoid scram at high power to dodge the xenon pit.
- [x] **When to wait for restart** — typically 24–48 h after a scram to let Xe-135 decay.
- [x] **Use of burnable poisons** — Gd$_{2}$O$_{3}$ or borosilicate glass rods to absorb excess reactivity in fresh cores.

### **Last-Minute Memory Hooks**

- [x] "**Xenon eats neutrons and decays; Samarium eats neutrons and stays.**"
- [x] "**Doppler is fast and always negative — it is the first responder.**"
- [x] "**Power defect is the price of going hot.**"
- [x] "**Samarium concentration is flux-independent — a counter-intuitive result.**"
- [x] "**Xenon pit comes ~10 h after scram; Samarium peak comes ~10 days after scram.**"
