Tolerates any single-channel failure. - **Diverse redundancy:** Use of *different* sensor types (e.g., RTD + thermocouple for the same temperature) defeats **Common-Cause Failures (CCF)**. - **Physical separation:** Per IEEE 384, redundant channels are routed in **separate conduit, separate rooms, separate power supplies** — protection against fire, flood, and seismic events. **2. Fault Tolerance (1.5 marks):** - **Self-diagnostics** continuously verify the integrity of every channel. - **Watchdog timers** force a known-safe state on processor hang-up. - **Fail-safe trip logic:** On loss of power to safety-grade I&C, the system defaults to a *safe* state (e.g., rods-in, SI actuated) — never fail-danger. **3. Cybersecurity — Why is it a First-Class Safety Concern? (3.5 marks):** - **Stuxnet (2010)** demonstrated that a cyber-intrusion can *physically destroy* centrifuges — proof that cyber-attack = physical hazard. - The **NRC's 10 CFR 73.54** and **AERB Computer Security Guidance** now require: - **Defence-in-depth network architecture** with **diode-based unidirectional gateways** (data diodes) between plant and corporate networks. - **Air-gapped** SPDS consoles with no internet connectivity. - **Role-based access control (RBAC)** and **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** for engineering workstations. - **Whitelisting** of all executable code on safety-grade systems. - **Continuous monitoring** for anomalous traffic (SIEM). - The **IEC 62643** standard formalises the **Cybersecurity Levels (SL1–SL4)** corresponding to the **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1–4)** of IEC 61508. - A **cyber-induced** sensor spoofing (e.g., fake-low neutron flux) could cause operators to withdraw control rods into a *supercritical* state — a direct threat to **CSF 1 (Subcriticality)**. Therefore, cybersecurity is no longer an *IT* problem; it is a **safety** problem. > [!WARNING] > **Most-Common Mark Loss Pitfalls (Recap):** > 1. Drawing the OCDPS block diagram *without* showing **separation between safety-grade and non-safety-grade** zones (-1 mark). > 2. Forgetting to mention the **15-minute ERDS latency** in Part B answers (-1 mark). > 3. Confusing **SPDS** (a display system) with the **entire OCDPS** (-1 mark). > 4. Writing "computer" instead of **"Digital Plant Processor"** or **"Safety Display Console"** (-0.5 mark per occurrence). > 5. Omitting the **2/3 TMR voter** equation $Y = X_1X_2 + X_2X_3 + X_1X_3$ in redundancy questions (-1 mark). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tick Before You Enter the Exam Hall!** - ✅ OCDPS = Field + Acquisition + Processing + Display + Emergency layers. - ✅ **SPDS** is the *safety-grade display*; it is *part of* the OCDPS, not the whole. - ✅ **Six CSFs** = Subcriticality, Core Cooling, Heat Sink, RCS Integrity, Containment, RCS Inventory. - ✅ **SPDS refresh ≤ 30 s**; **ERDS latency ≤ 15 min** (AERB SG/D-25 / 10 CFR 50.47 Appendix E). - ✅ Class 1E = safety-grade; IEEE 603, IEEE 384, IEEE 344 govern; **separation, redundancy, fail-safe**. - ✅ Redundancy voting: **2/3 TMR** — equation $Y = X_1X_2 + X_2X_3 + X_1X_3$ — eliminates single-point spurious trips. - ✅ ADC resolution: $\Delta V = V_{\text{FS}}/2^n$; for 4–20 mA, 250 Ω, 16-bit → $\Delta V_{\text{LSB}} \approx 76.3 \, \mu\text{V}$. - ✅ RTD linearisation: Callendar–Van Dusen; **Pt-100** with $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$. - ✅ Reactor period: $\tau = \phi / (d\phi/dt)$ — **negative** = subcritical. - ✅ Cybersecurity is a **first-class safety concern** (Stuxnet, 10 CFR 73.54, IEC 62643). - ✅ **Stuxnet (2010)** is the watershed event for cyber-safety in NPPs. - ✅ **ERDS** = "last mile" link; transmits ~60–80 SPDS parameters to the regulator. - ✅ Archival: **STS** (90 days, RAID) → **LTS** (LTO tapes, plant lifetime). - ✅ IEC 61226 classifies I&C into **Class A, B, C** based on safety function importance. - ✅ IEEE 7-4.3.2 governs **software** in Class 1E systems; IEC 60880 for **software** in safety systems. - ✅ Watchdog timer + fail-safe trip = mandatory for any safety-grade processor. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Online Central Data Processing System** > *Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!*

topics.title: Boiling Water Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiling Water Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Boiling Water Reactor (BWR)** is a type of light water nuclear reactor (LWR) in which the reactor coolant water is allowed to boil inside the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) itself, thereby generating steam that is **directly** routed to the turbine-generator system without any intermediate steam generator. The BWR operates on a **direct Rankine cycle**, where the same light water (H₂O) serves the dual role of (a) the neutron moderator and (b) the working fluid for the turbine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Boiling Water Reactors belong to **Generation II / Generation III** light water reactor family, characterized by a **negative void coefficient of reactivity** under most operating conditions, making it inherently self-stabilizing.

**Key Reference Parameters (GE BWR/6 — Generic Design):**
- **Thermal Power:** ~3,580 MW(th)
- **Electrical Output:** ~1,220 MW(e)
- **Coolant/Moderator:** Light water (H₂O)
- **Primary Pressure:** **~75 bar (7.5 MPa)**
- **Core Inlet Temperature:** ~215 °C
- **Core Outlet Temperature:** ~285 °C
- **Enrichment:** 2–5% ²³⁵U (Avg. ~2.5%)
- **Thermal Efficiency:** ~34%

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Series Containments** (Mark I, II, III) are the standard KTU-referenced pressure-suppression containment designs used in BWRs to manage Loss-Of-Coolant Accidents (LOCA).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker with a whistle** — but instead of cooking food, the heat comes from controlled nuclear fission. The water inside boils, and the steam travels straight out (after drying) to spin a giant fan (turbine) that produces electricity.

- **Cooker body** → Reactor Pressure Vessel
- **Heat source (stove)** → Nuclear fuel (UO₂ pellets in Zircaloy cladding)
- **Steam rising** → Two-phase flow (bubbly, slug, annular)
- **Whistle/dryer** → Steam separators and dryers in the RPV top
- **Fan driven by steam** → Turbine
- **Condensate returning** → Feedwater + Condensate system

The "cleverness" of a BWR is that it **eliminates the steam generator**, so the loop is simpler and cheaper. However, because the turbine sees slightly radioactive steam (due to activated nitrogen-16, ¹⁶N), the entire secondary side is treated as a **mildly radioactive zone**.

> [!TIP]
> A common student misconception: *BWR has no primary loop* — FALSE. It has a **primary loop**, but it is **not isolated** from the secondary (turbine) loop. There is **no steam generator boundary**.

---

### 1.3 Why BWR — Engineering Motivation

| Driver | BWR Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Economy** | No steam generators → ~15–20% lower capital cost per MWe |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | Higher exit temperature (≈285 °C) than PWR (~325 °C outlet, but no SG loss) |
| **Inherent Safety** | Strong negative void coefficient → self-shutdown on voiding |
| **Direct Cycle** | Simpler piping, fewer large components |

| Drawback | Mitigation |
|---|---|
| Turbine becomes mildly radioactive (¹⁶N, ²⁰⁷₂Pb) | Shielded turbine building, controlled access |
| Larger RPV required | Standardized Mk I/II/III designs |

---

### 1.4 BWR vs PWR (At a Glance)

| Parameter | **BWR** | **PWR** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Loop | **Direct** (steam to turbine) | **Indirect** (via SG) |
| Steam Generator | **None** | Yes (U-tube or Once-through) |
| Primary Pressure | **~75 bar** | **~155 bar** |
| Coolant Phase in Core | **Two-phase (≈14% void)** | Sub-cooled liquid |
| Moderator | Liquid water (same as coolant) | Liquid water (separate loop) |
| Containment | Pressure suppression (Mk I/II/III) | Large dry / ice condenser |
| Void Coefficient | **Strongly Negative** | Slightly negative (with B₁₀) |
| Fuel Enrichment | 2–5% | 3–5% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BWR Core Architecture — Layered Decomposition

**Layer 1 — Fuel Assembly:**
- Square **8×8** or **9×9** or **10×10** fuel array
- **UO₂ pellets** (sintered, ~95% TD) clad in **Zircaloy-2 or Zircaloy-4**
- Each fuel rod: ~10–12 mm OD, ~4 m active length
- **Control blades:** Cruciform B₄C / Hf absorber ("pumpkin" shape), inserted from below
- **Fuel channel:** Zircaloy-4 box surrounding the assembly (defines the coolant boundary)

**Layer 2 — Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV):**
- Forged low-alloy carbon steel, clad internally with stainless steel
- Diameter ~6.4 m, height ~22 m
- Houses: core, jet pumps, steam separators, steam dryers
- Single vessel (no separate pressurizer)

**Layer 3 — Internals Hierarchy (Bottom → Top):**
1. **Lower plenum** (jet pump suction)
2. **Core shroud & core plate**
3. **Active core** (fuel + control blades)
4. **Upper plenum** (steam-water mixture)
5. **Steam separators** (centrifugal + vane type)
6. **Steam dryers** (corrugated plates → moisture ≤ 0.1%)

### 2.2 The Direct Rankine Cycle — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1: Boiling in Core**
- Sub-cooled water at ~215 °C enters the core bottom.
- As it rises, it absorbs fission heat and begins to boil.
- Typical **exit quality (x₂):** 14–17% (i.e., 14–17% of mass is vapor).

**Step 2: Phase Separation**
- The two-phase mixture rises into the steam separators.
- Centrifugal forces throw water droplets to the wall → drain back to downcomer.
- Wet steam (still ~3–5% moisture) then enters the steam dryers.

**Step 3: Steam Drying**
- Corrugated vane dryers reduce moisture to **≤ 0.1%** (1100 ppm).
- Dry steam exits via four main steam lines.

**Step 4: Turbine & Condenser**
- Steam expands in HP → LP turbines.
- Spent steam is condensed in the **main condenser** (vacuum ≈ 50 mbar).
- ¹⁶N (t½ = 7.13 s) decays before reaching the condenser, but ¹⁹O (t½ = 27 s) is the dominant activity concern.

**Step 5: Recirculation**
- Feedwater is mixed with **recirculation water** from the jet pumps.
- **Reactivity control** is achieved by varying recirculation flow (not boron) — the cornerstone of BWR operation.

### 2.3 Reactivity Control Mechanisms in BWR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BWRs do **NOT** use soluble boron (Boric Acid) for reactivity control. They use a combination of:
> 1. **Control Blades** (B₄C / Hf) — coarse control, scram
> 2. **Recirculation Flow** — fine control
> 3. **Burnable Poison** (Gd₂O₃) — long-term compensation

**The Recirculation Trick:**
- Increasing core flow → more liquid water → more moderation → more thermal fission → **positive reactivity insertion**
- Range: ~30% → 100% of rated flow → ~25% Δk/k reactivity swing

### 2.4 Void Coefficient — The Heart of BWR Safety

$$\alpha_{\phi} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial V} \quad \text{[pcm/% void]}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = reactivity (pcm, per-cent mille = $10^{-5}$)
- $V$ = void fraction (%)

For a typical BWR at 14% void:
$$\alpha_{\phi} \approx -200 \text{ to } -300 \text{ pcm/\% void}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** If void fraction increases (e.g., loss of flow), the moderator density drops, fewer thermal neutrons are moderated, thermal fission rate drops, and **reactivity is removed automatically**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Negative Void Coefficient (NVC)** is the **single most important inherent safety feature** of a BWR. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{th} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Core thermal power (single-phase) | **3,580 MW** |
| 2 | $P_{th} = \dot{m} \cdot [h_2 - h_1]$ | Enthalpy-based (suitable for boiling) | MW |
| 3 | $x_2 = \frac{h_2 - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | Exit steam quality | **0.14 – 0.17** |
| 4 | $\alpha_\phi = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\Delta V}$ | Void coefficient of reactivity | **-200 to -300 pcm/%** |
| 5 | $CR = \frac{V_{H_2O}}{V_{fuel}}$ | Coolant-to-fuel volumetric ratio | ~2.5 |
| 6 | $k_\infty = \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon$ | Four-factor formula (infinite medium) | ≥ 1.03 |
| 7 | $P_{6} = \Sigma_a^{H_2O} \cdot \phi_{th}$ | Parasitic absorption in moderator | < 0.05 |
| 8 | $T_{eff} \approx 25^\circ C$ | Effective neutron temperature | — |
| 9 | $K_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL}$ | Effective multiplication factor | 1.000 (critical) |
| 10 | $P_{NL} = e^{-B^2 \tau}$ | Non-leakage probability | ~0.95 |
| 11 | $\rho = \frac{K_{eff} - 1}{K_{eff}}$ | Reactivity | pcm or Δk/k |
| 12 | $\text{SHM} = 25 \frac{P_{th}}{M_{HM}}$ | Specific power (Mass of Heavy Metal) | kW/kg |
| 13 | $BU = \frac{P_{th} \cdot t}{M_{HM}}$ | Burnup | MWd/tHM |
| 14 | $A_{void} = \frac{V_{void}}{V_{total}}$ | Void fraction | 0.14 |

> [!TIP]
> **Unit Conversions to Memorize:**
> - 1 pcm = $10^{-5}$ $\Delta$k/k
> - 1% $\Delta$k/k = 1000 pcm
> - 1 MWd/tHM = 86.4 GJ/tHM

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

BWR technology underpins commercial reactors worldwide — **~20% of global installed nuclear capacity** (e.g., Sweden, Japan, Switzerland, USA). Its design philosophy influences:
- **ABWR** (Advanced Boiling Water Reactor) — Generation III+ with passive safety
- **ESBWR** (Economic Simplified BWR) — natural circulation, 4,500 MW(th)
- **SMR-160 (Holtec)** — small modular BWR variant

The **negative void coefficient** and **direct cycle** architecture are key reference points for KTU Module 2 (Reactor Systems) and Module 3 (Reactivity Feedback).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Thermal Power from Two-Phase Flow

We wish to derive the **core thermal power** using enthalpy balance across the active core, accounting for the boiling that occurs.

**Given:**
- Inlet: Sub-cooled liquid at $T_1 = 215^\circ C$, $P = 7.5$ MPa
- Outlet: Two-phase mixture at quality $x_2 = 0.15$
- Core mass flux: $G = 2400 \text{ kg/m}^2\text{-s}$
- Active core cross-section: $A_c = 5.0 \text{ m}^2$
- Number of fuel assemblies: $N = 748$

**Step 1 — Enthalpy of inlet (sub-cooled water):**

$$h_1 = h_{f,215} \approx 905.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> *Logic: From steam tables at 7.5 MPa, the saturation temperature is ~290 °C, so 215 °C is sub-cooled. Reference: IAPWS-IF97.*

**Step 2 — Enthalpy of outlet (two-phase):**

At 7.5 MPa:
$$h_{f,290} \approx 1317 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{g,290} \approx 2773 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 1317 + 0.15 \cdot (2773 - 1317)$$

$$h_2 = 1317 + 0.15 \cdot 1456 = 1317 + 218.4 = 1535.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpy rise:**

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 1535.4 - 905.6 = 629.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Core mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = G \cdot A_c = 2400 \cdot 5.0 = 12{,}000 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Core thermal power:**

$$P_{th} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h = 12{,}000 \cdot 629.8 = 7.5576 \times 10^6 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{th} \approx 7{,}558 \text{ MW(th)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a realistic GE BWR/6, $P_{th} \approx 3{,}580$ MW, which corresponds to a smaller flow rate or quality. The student should focus on the **methodology**, not the absolute number.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Void Coefficient from First Principles

The **moderator temperature coefficient (MTC)** and **void coefficient** are related. We derive the void coefficient starting from the four-factor formula:

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon$$

For light water, the **thermal utilization factor** $f$ is most sensitive to voiding:

$$f = \frac{\Sigma_a^{fuel} \cdot V_{fuel}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel} \cdot V_{fuel} + \Sigma_a^{mod} \cdot V_{mod}}$$

**Step 1 — When water density drops (void increases), $V_{mod}$ effectively decreases:**

$$\Sigma_a^{mod} = N_{H_2O} \cdot \sigma_a^H \quad \text{where} \quad N_{H_2O} \propto \rho_{H_2O}$$

**Step 2 — As $\rho_{H_2O}$ drops (e.g., from 0.74 g/cm³ to 0.05 g/cm³ in dry steam):**

$$f \rightarrow 1, \quad \text{but} \quad p \rightarrow 0, \quad \text{and} \quad \eta \text{ drops (less moderation)}$$

**Step 3 — Net effect: the multiplication factor drops sharply:**

$$\frac{\partial k_\infty}{\partial \rho_{mod}} > 0 \implies \frac{\partial k_\infty}{\partial V_{void}} < 0$$

**Step 4 — Converting to reactivity:**

$$\alpha_\phi = \frac{1}{k_\infty} \cdot \frac{\partial k_\infty}{\partial V_{void}} \cdot \frac{10^5}{1} \text{ pcm/\%void}$$

**Step 5 — For a typical BWR lattice:**

$$\boxed{\alpha_\phi \approx -250 \text{ pcm/\% void}}$$

This means a 10% void increase removes 2500 pcm = **2.5% Δk/k** of reactivity — sufficient to shut down the reactor even with all control blades withdrawn (this is the basis of the **ATWS** (Anticipated Transient Without Scram) safety argument).

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Recirculation Flow Reactivity Feedback

**Recirculation Ratio (RR):**

$$RR = \frac{\dot{m}_{recirc}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} \quad \text{typically } \sim 5$$

**Core flow:**

$$\dot{m}_{core} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot (1 + RR)$$

**Void fraction correlation (typical BWR):**

$$V_{void}(\%) = 25 - 0.15 \cdot \dot{m}_{core} \quad \text{(simplified, for illustration)}$$

**Reactivity from voiding (linearized):**

$$\Delta \rho = \alpha_\phi \cdot \Delta V_{void} = -250 \cdot (-0.15 \cdot \Delta \dot{m}_{core})$$

$$\Delta \rho = 37.5 \cdot \Delta \dot{m}_{core} \text{ pcm}$$

**If $\dot{m}_{core}$ increases from 12,000 → 14,000 kg/s (+16.7%):**

$$\Delta \rho = 37.5 \cdot 2000 = 75{,}000 \text{ pcm}$$

> *This is unrealistically large because the model is linearized. In practice, the relation saturates. The real BWR swing is ~700–1000 pcm.*

**Reconciled with Reality:**
- Real BWR reactivity swing from recirculation: **~700 pcm** (0.7% Δk/k)
- Achieved by variable-speed **recirculation pumps** (modern: **Variable Frequency Drives — VFD**)
- Old BWRs used **jet pumps** driven by internal recirculation

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reactivity Feedback Summary

| Phenomenon | Physical Cause | Sign of $\Delta \rho$ | Magnitude (pcm) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increased recirculation flow | More moderation | + | ~700 |
| Increased void (e.g., LOCA) | Loss of moderator | – | ~-2000 |
| Fuel temperature rise (Doppler) | ²³⁸U resonance broadening | – | ~-200 per 100 °C |
| Moderator temperature rise | Lower density | – | ~-50 per °C |
| Xenon-135 build-up | Fission product poison | – | ~-2500 (peak) |
| Burnable poison (Gd) depletion | Loss of absorber | + | ~+1500 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the student must identify that **BWRs have a much stronger moderator feedback** than PWRs because the moderator and coolant are the **same fluid**, and its density (and hence moderation efficiency) varies directly with power.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BWR/6 Functional Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RPV["REACTOR PRESSURE VESSEL"]
    CORE["NUCLEAR CORE\nFUELED REGION"]
    JP["JET PUMPS\nINTERNAL RECIRC"]
    SEP["STEAM SEPARATORS\nCENTRIFUGAL"]
    DRY["STEAM DRYERS\nCORRUGATED VANE"]
    MSL["MAIN STEAM LINES\n4 OFF"]
    TURB["HIGH PRESSURE TURBINE\nLOW PRESSURE TURBINE"]
    GEN["GENERATOR\n1200 MWe"]
    COND["MAIN CONDENSER\nVACUUM 50 mbar"]
    FW["FEEDWATER HEATER\nAND CONDENSATE"]
    REC["RECIRCULATION\nPUMPS WITH VFD"]
    CNMT["MARK I CONTAINMENT\nPRESSURE SUPPRESSION\nTORUS AND DRYWELL"]
    ECCS["ECCS\nLPCI, HPCS, ADS"]
    ACC["ACCUMULATORS\nNITROGEN PRESSURIZED"]
    SCRAM["CONTROL BLADES\nB4C CRUCIFORM"]

    RPV --> CORE
    CORE --> SEP
    SEP --> DRY
    DRY --> MSL
    MSL --> TURB
    TURB --> GEN
    GEN --> COND
    COND --> FW
    FW --> REC
    REC --> JP
    JP --> RPV
    RPV --> CNMT
    CNMT --> ECCS
    ECCS --> RPV
    ACC --> RPV
    SCRAM --> CORE
```

---

### 4.2 Two-Phase Flow Pattern Map (BWR Channel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INLET\nSUB COOLED\nT = 215 C\nQUALITY = 0"] --> B["BOILING\nINITIATION\nONSET OF NUCLEATE BOILING"]
    B --> C["BUBBLY FLOW\nVOID 0 to 0.05\nQUALITY 0 to 0.02"]
    C --> D["SLUG FLOW\nVOID 0.05 to 0.15\nQUALITY 0.02 to 0.10"]
    D --> E["ANNULAR FLOW\nVOID 0.15 to 0.30\nQUALITY 0.10 to 0.18"]
    E --> F["DRYOUT\nCRITICAL QUALITY 0.17\nVOID 0.30 to 0.50"]
    F --> G["MIST FLOW\nPOST DRYOUT\nVOID > 0.50\nFILM BOILING"]
    G --> H["EXIT\nEXIT QUALITY 0.14 to 0.17\nVOID 0.40"]
```

---

### 4.3 BWR Control and Safety System Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["LEVEL 1\nPREVENTIVE\nCONTROL"]
    C2["LEVEL 2\nREACTIVITY\nCONTROL"]
    C3["LEVEL 3\nECCS\nEMERGENCY CORE COOLING"]
    C4["LEVEL 4\nCONTAINMENT\nPRESSURE SUPPRESSION"]
    C5["LEVEL 5\nSITE EMERGENCY\nRESPONSE"]

    C1 --> C1A["NORMAL FEEDWATER\nAND RECIRCULATION CONTROL"]
    C1 --> C1B["PRESSURE REGULATION\nBY RELIEF VALVES"]

    C2 --> C2A["CONTROL BLADES\nB4C CRUCIFORM"]
    C2 --> C2B["RECIRCULATION FLOW\nVFD"]
    C2 --> C2C["STANDBY LIQUID CONTROL\nSLC NA PENTABORATE"]

    C3 --> C3A["HPCS\nHIGH PRESSURE CORE SPRAY"]
    C3 --> C3B["LPCI\nLOW PRESSURE COOLANT INJECTION"]
    C3 --> C3C["ADS\nAUTOMATIC DEPRESSURIZATION"]
    C3 --> C3D["ACCUMULATORS\nN2 PRESSURIZED 4.8 MPa"]

    C4 --> C4A["MARK I TORUS\nWETWELL"]
    C4 --> C4B["DRYWELL\nPRIMARY CONTAINMENT"]
    C4 --> C4C["SUPPRESSION POOL\n5000 m3"]

    C5 --> C5A["EMERGENCY PLANNING\nZONE EPZ 10 km"]
    C5 --> C5B["SHIELDED BUILDING\nTGB"]
```

---

### 4.4 Direct Cycle (BWR) vs Indirect Cycle (PWR) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BWR["BWR DIRECT CYCLE"]
    B1["RPV CORE"] --> B2["STEAM DRIER"]
    B2 --> B3["TURBINE"]
    B3 --> B4["CONDENSER"]
    B4 --> B5["FEEDWATER"]
    B5 --> B1
    end

    subgraph PWR["PWR INDIRECT CYCLE"]
    P1["RPV CORE"] --> P2["STEAM GENERATOR\nPRIMARY SIDE"]
    P2 --> P3["TURBINE"]
    P3 --> P4["CONDENSER"]
    P4 --> P5["FEEDWATER"]
    P5 --> P2
    P2 --> P1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Takeaway:** In the BWR, the loop is **single-pass** with one fluid. In the PWR, the loop is **double-pass** with two isolated fluid streams. This is the single most tested concept in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain briefly the direct cycle concept in a Boiling Water Reactor (BWR). State two advantages and one disadvantage of this cycle compared to a PWR.*

**Model Answer (KTU Pattern):**
In a BWR, the same light water acts as **coolant, moderator, and working fluid**. Boiling takes place inside the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and the generated steam is routed **directly** to the turbine.

**Advantages:**
1. **No steam generator** → simpler plant, lower capital cost (~15–20% less).
2. **Higher thermal efficiency** at lower pressure (75 bar vs 155 bar).
3. **Strong negative void coefficient** → inherent safety.

**Disadvantage:**
1. The turbine and feedwater train become **mildly radioactive** due to ¹⁶N (t½ = 7.13 s) and ¹⁹O (t½ = 27 s), requiring additional shielding and controlled access. *(3 marks: 1 + 2 for sub-points)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*What is the void coefficient of reactivity? Why is it negative in a BWR?*

**Model Answer:**
The void coefficient of reactivity $\alpha_\phi$ is defined as the change in reactivity per unit change in the void fraction of the coolant:

$$\alpha_\phi = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial V_{void}} \text{ (pcm/\% void)}$$

In a BWR, an increase in void fraction (e.g., due to power excursion) reduces the **density of water** in the core. Since water is the moderator, fewer thermal neutrons are produced, reducing the thermal fission rate and hence the reactivity. The thermal utilization factor $f$ also falls. The net effect is a **negative** reactivity insertion, causing the reactor to **self-shut down**. Typical value: **-200 to -300 pcm/% void**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for mechanism)*

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. Writing "BWR has no primary loop" — **WRONG**. It has a primary loop; the **secondary** boundary is absent.
> 2. Confusing the **void coefficient** with the **moderator temperature coefficient** — they are related but not identical.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the role of **¹⁶N** activity in BWR turbine halls.
> 4. Misidentifying the **Mark I containment** as a "leak-tight" design — it is a **pressure-suppression** design.
> 5. Not specifying **units** in numerical problems (always write MW, pcm, %, °C).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **direct Rankine cycle** in a BWR and identify the major components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BWR core has the following data:
- Thermal power: $P_{th} = 3{,}580$ MW
- Inlet temperature: $T_1 = 215^\circ C$
- Pressure: 7.5 MPa
- Exit steam quality: $x_2 = 0.15$
- Specific enthalpy of sub-cooled water at inlet: $h_1 = 906$ kJ/kg

Compute:
1. The core mass flow rate
2. The exit enthalpy
3. Comment on whether the value of $x_2 = 0.15$ is safe with respect to **dryout** (use $h_{fg} = 1456$ kJ/kg at 7.5 MPa). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Direct Rankine Cycle — Block Diagram (7 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV)** | Houses core, jet pumps, separators, dryers |
| **Core** | Heat generation by controlled fission |
| **Steam Separators** | Centrifugal separation of water from steam |
| **Steam Dryers** | Reduce moisture to ≤ 0.1% (1100 ppm) |
| **Main Steam Lines (4 nos.)** | Carry dry steam to turbine |
| **Turbine (HP + LP)** | Converts thermal to mechanical energy |
| **Generator** | Mechanical to electrical |
| **Main Condenser** | Condenses exhaust steam under vacuum |
| **Feedwater System** | Returns condensate to RPV |
| **Recirculation System** | Controls core flow and reactivity |
| **Containment (Mark I/II/III)** | Pressure-suppression safety enclosure |

Block diagram (text-based reference):

```
[RPV] → [Separators] → [Dryers] → [Steam Lines] → [Turbine] → [Generator]
                                                                  ↓
                                                              [Condenser]
                                                                  ↓
                                                              [Feedwater]
                                                                  ↓
[Recirculation Pumps] ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ↓
   ↓
[Jet Pumps] → back to RPV
```

*(Marks breakdown: 1 for layout, 2 for components identification, 2 for working fluid path, 2 for safety/recirculation explanation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Exit enthalpy:**

$$h_2 = h_{f,290} + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 1317 + 0.15 \cdot 1456$$

$$h_2 = 1317 + 218.4 = 1535.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Enthalpy rise:**

$$\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 1535.4 - 906 = 629.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate:**

$$P_{th} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta h$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{P_{th}}{\Delta h} = \frac{3{,}580 \times 10^3 \text{ kW}}{629.4 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3{,}580{,}000}{629.4} = 5{,}688.6 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 5{,}689 \text{ kg/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Dryout Safety Check:**

The critical quality at which dryout occurs in a BWR fuel bundle is typically $x_{crit} \approx 0.17 - 0.20$.

Given $x_2 = 0.15 < x_{crit} \approx 0.17$, the operating condition is **SAFE** with respect to dryout, but the **safety margin is small (~12%)**. Any power excursion or flow reduction could trigger dryout, leading to **film boiling** and potential **fuel cladding failure**. *(2 marks for safe-with-caveat comment.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **reactivity control mechanisms** available in a BWR. Compare with those in a PWR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BWR has a void coefficient of $\alpha_\phi = -250$ pcm/% void. During a transient, the average void fraction in the core increases from 14% to 18%. If the reactor was initially critical, determine:
1. The reactivity insertion
2. The new value of $K_{eff}$
3. Will the reactor move toward subcritical or supercritical state? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Reactivity Control in BWR vs PWR (7 marks):**

| Mechanism | BWR | PWR |
|---|---|---|
| **Soluble Poison** | Not used (water is in the steam path) | Boric acid in primary coolant |
| **Control Rods** | Cruciform B₄C blades from below | Cluster rods from above |
| **Burnable Poison** | Gd₂O₃ in some fuel rods | Gd₂O₃ or Er₂O₃ |
| **Flow Control** | **Variable recirculation flow** (key!) | Not used |
| **Reflector** | Standard | Sometimes used |

**Key Point:** In BWR, the dominant short-term reactivity control is by **recirculation flow variation**, which changes the **void content** and hence the moderation. The moderator coefficient is strong because **moderator = coolant**.

*(Marks: 2 for identifying BWR mechanisms, 2 for PWR comparison, 2 for "why" the BWR uses flow control, 1 for neat table.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Change in void fraction:**

$$\Delta V_{void} = 18\% - 14\% = 4\%$$

**Step 2 — Reactivity insertion:**

$$\Delta \rho = \alpha_\phi \cdot \Delta V_{void} = (-250 \text{ pcm/\%}) \cdot (4\%)$$

$$\Delta \rho = -1000 \text{ pcm} = -0.01 \Delta k/k = -1.0\% \Delta k/k$$

**Step 3 — Initial condition:** $K_{eff,0} = 1.000$ (critical), so $\rho_0 = 0$.

**Step 4 — New reactivity:**

$$\rho_{new} = \rho_0 + \Delta \rho = 0 + (-0.01) = -0.01$$

**Step 5 — New $K_{eff}$:**

$$K_{eff,new} = \frac{1}{1 - \rho_{new}} = \frac{1}{1 - (-0.01)} = \frac{1}{1.01}$$

$$K_{eff,new} = 0.9901$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{K_{eff,new} \approx 0.9901 < 1 \quad \text{(SUBCRITICAL)}}$$

The reactor moves toward the **subcritical** state. This demonstrates the **inherent safety** of the BWR: an increase in voiding **automatically** drives the reactor to a safe, subcritical condition.

*(Marks: 1 each for steps 1, 2, 4, 5; 1 for step 6 interpretation; 1 for final boxed answer.)*

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- ✅ BWR operates on a **direct Rankine cycle** with **no steam generator**.
- ✅ Primary pressure is **~75 bar (7.5 MPa)** — much lower than PWR.
- ✅ Coolant and moderator are the **same light water**.
- ✅ **Void coefficient is strongly negative** (~-200 to -300 pcm/%) → inherent safety.
- ✅ **Reactivity control:** Control blades (B₄C), recirculation flow, **no soluble boron**.
- ✅ **¹⁶N (t½ = 7.13 s)** is the dominant activation product in the turbine hall.
- ✅ **Mark I, II, III** containments use **pressure suppression** (torus + wetwell).
- ✅ **Exit steam quality:** 14–17% (just below dryout).
- ✅ **ECCS systems:** HPCS, LPCI, ADS, accumulators.
- ✅ Direct cycle → **simpler, cheaper**, but **turbine is mildly radioactive**.
- ✅ For thermal power: $P_{th} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ where $h_2 = h_f + x_2 h_{fg}$.
- ✅ For reactivity: $\rho = (K_{eff} - 1)/K_{eff}$; void coefficient: $\alpha_\phi = \partial \rho / \partial V$.
- ✅ KTU-marked topics: **direct cycle, void coefficient, control methods, containment types, ECCS**.

> **🎯 Board Exam Tip:** KTU examiners **love** the void coefficient numerical. Practice at least 3 such problems before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radioactivity of Steam System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Radioactivity of Steam System # MODULE 2 — RADIOACTIVITY OF THE STEAM SYSTEM ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Radioactivity of the Steam System** refers to the inventory of radioactive isotopes (radionuclides) present in the working fluid (water/steam) of the secondary loop, the steam generator, the turbine, the condenser, and the associated piping of a nuclear power plant. It arises primarily from **(a) neutron activation of coolant and its impurities**, **(b) activation of corrosion products ("crud")**, and **(c) leakage of primary coolant into the secondary side (PWRs) or direct carryover (BWRs)**. In a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR), the primary and secondary loops are separated by the steam generator tubes. Under normal conditions, the steam is essentially non-radioactive, but several mechanisms introduce activity. In a Boiling Water Reactor (BWR), the steam is in **direct contact with the reactor core**, so the steam carries a much higher activity burden. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Commuter Train":** > Imagine the steam as a high-speed commuter train leaving a heavily contaminated "city" (the reactor core). Even though the train is supposed to be clean, it picks up "passengers" (radioactive atoms) along the way: > - **On board at departure:** Nitrogen-16 (N-16), formed *inside* the train by neutron activation of oxygen in the water. > - **Picked up at intermediate stations (steam generator tube leaks):** Strontium, Iodine, Cesium leaking from the primary side. > - **Stuck to the carriage walls (crud):** Cobalt-60, Mn-54, Fe-59 deposited on internal surfaces. > > The radiation field around the train (steam piping, turbine) depends on how many "passengers" are still aboard at each point — and the "turbine hall radiation" problem is fundamentally a problem of these radioactive passengers. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Highlighted) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Constants for This Topic:** > - **Half-life of N-16:** $T_{1/2} = 7.13$ **s** > - **Decay constant of N-16:** $\lambda_{N16} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{7.13} \approx 0.0972$ **s⁻¹** > - **Dominant N-16 gamma energies:** $E_\gamma = 6.13$ **MeV** and $E_\gamma = 7.11$ **MeV** > - **Typical primary-to-secondary leak rate (PWR):** ≤ **0.1 gallons/day** per steam generator (ASME limit ~75 gpd total) > - **Decontamination Factor (DF) of steam generator blowdown:** typically **10 – 100** > - **Primary coolant pressure:** ~**155 bar**; **Secondary steam pressure:** ~**68 bar** (typical 4-loop PWR) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Principal Sources of Steam Radioactivity 1. **Activation of Oxygen-16 (the N-16 problem):** - Reaction: $^{16}_{8}\text{O}(n,p)^{16}_{7}\text{N}$ - **Threshold reaction** — requires fast neutrons (E > ~10 MeV). - Only the fast-neutron flux in the core produces significant N-16. - N-16 is **chemically inert** (molecular N₂) → it does **not plate out** on surfaces. - It decays by high-energy gamma emission → major **shutdown and operational dose-rate** concern around the **main steam lines and turbine**. 2. **Activated Corrosion Products ("Crud"):** - Structural materials (Fe, Cr, Ni, Co, Mn) corrode slightly, get carried into the core, become neutron-activated, and re-deposit. - Important isotopes: - **Co-60** ($T_{1/2} = 5.27$ **y**) — long-term shutdown dose rate problem - **Mn-54** ($T_{1/2} = 312$ **d**) - **Fe-59** ($T_{1/2} = 44.5$ **d**) - **Cr-51** ($T_{1/2} = 27.7$ **d**) - Crud is the dominant cause of **outage dose rates** on piping. 3. **Primary-to-Secondary Leakage (PWRs) and Fission-Product Carryover (BWRs):** - PWR: defective SG tubes allow primary coolant (containing fission products Cs-137, I-131, Sr-90) to leak into the steam. - BWR: direct volatile fission product carryover (Xe, Kr, I) into the steam. ### 2.2 Why N-16 Dominates the PWR Steam Radiation Field > [!NOTE] > The activity at any point downstream of the steam generator depends on the **transit time** $t$ from the core exit (where N-16 is produced) to that point, because N-16 has a very short half-life. > > After **one half-life (7.13 s)**, activity drops to 50%. > After **three half-lives (~21 s)**, activity drops to **12.5%**. > This is why **shielding and isolation of the main steam line** is critical — the radiation field collapses rapidly once the reactor trips. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Decay of N-16 along steam line | $A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$ | Bq | Dose rate at any point downstream | | 2 | N-16 decay constant | $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$ | s⁻¹ | Time-decay of activity | | 3 | Saturation activity of N-16 | $A_{sat} = N_{O16}\,\sigma\,\phi\,V$ | Bq | Max possible N-16 inventory | | 4 | Activity in steam from SG leak (PWR) | $A_{steam} = A_{prim} \times (L/F_{steam}) \times \text{DF}^{-1}$ | Bq/kg | Estimate of secondary activity | | 5 | Decontamination Factor | $\text{DF} = C_{in}/C_{out}$ | dimensionless | Performance of cleanup systems | | 6 | Gamma dose rate from N-16 (point source) | $\dot{H} = \Gamma \, A / d^2$ | Sv/h | Shielding design | | 7 | Number density of O-16 in water | $N_{O16} = \rho N_A / 18 \approx 3.34 \times 10^{28}$ | atoms/m³ | Activation source term | | 8 | Half-life layers of shielding | $n = t_{transit}/T_{1/2}$ | dimensionless | Rule of thumb for N-16 decay | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on absolute value notation:** In all the formulas above, the absolute value of any variable $x$ must be written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never using the bare vertical bar `|x|` which corrupts markdown tables. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Outage planning:** Predicting shutdown dose rates allows utilities to plan ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) work windows. - **SG tube integrity programs:** Continuous monitoring of N-16 and I-131/I-134 in steam tells operators if a tube is leaking *in real time*. - **BWR stack emission control:** Radio-iodine and noble gas carryover directly affect public dose. - **Turbine shielding design:** Pre-shielding around the HP turbine based on expected N-16 transit times. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — N-16 Activity as a Function of Position in the Steam Line Let a slug of water leave the reactor core with N-16 saturation activity $A_{sat}$. As it travels through the primary coolant and then through the steam generator, its activity decays exponentially. **Step 1 — Define the production rate.** The production rate of N-16 per unit volume in the core is: $$R = N_{O16} \, \sigma_{O16 \to N16} \, \phi_{fast}$$ **Step 2 — At equilibrium (saturation) in the core, the activity per unit volume equals the production rate.** $$A_{sat} = R = N_{O16} \, \sigma \, \phi$$ where $N_{O16}$ is the number density of O-16 in water ($\approx 3.34 \times 10^{28}$ atoms/m³), $\sigma$ is the (n,p) cross-section (≈ 0.019 mb for fast neutrons, but use a spectrum-averaged value), and $\phi$ is the effective fast-neutron flux. **Step 3 — As the slug leaves the core, the activity decays by first-order kinetics.** $$\frac{dA}{dt} = -\lambda A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the initial condition.** At the moment of leaving the core, the activity equals the saturation value $A_{sat}$ (the water has resided in the core for a time much greater than $T_{1/2}$ of N-16, so it is at saturation). $$A_0 = A_{sat}$$ **Step 5 — Combine.** $$\boxed{\,A_{SG\,outlet}(t) = A_{sat}\, e^{-\lambda\, t_{transit}}\,}$$ **Step 6 — Express transit time.** If the steam velocity in the main steam line is $v$ (m/s) and the distance from SG outlet to the point of interest (e.g., turbine stop valve) is $L$: $$t_{transit} = \frac{L}{v}$$ Therefore: $$A_{turbine} = A_{sat}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} \cdot \frac{L}{v}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical illustration:** Take $L = 30$ m, $v = 40$ m/s, $T_{1/2} = 7.13$ s. Then $t = 0.75$ s and the activity reaches $e^{-0.0728} \approx 0.93\,A_{sat}$ — i.e., the activity is still 93% of saturation at the turbine. This is why N-16 dominates the radiation field around the turbine. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steam Activity from a Steam Generator Tube Leak (PWR) Let the primary coolant contain an activity concentration $C_p$ (Bq/kg). A fraction $f_L = L / F_s$ of the steam flow $F_s$ originates from primary leakage $L$ (kg/s). **Step 1 — Activity entering steam per unit time from the leak:** $$\dot{A}_{leak} = C_p \cdot L$$ **Step 2 — This activity is diluted in the total steam mass flow $F_s$:** $$C_{steam,\,leak} = \frac{\dot{A}_{leak}}{F_s} = C_p \cdot \frac{L}{F_s}$$ **Step 3 — Some isotopes are removed by blowdown / condenser cleanup. The decontamination factor DF is defined as:** $$\text{DF} = \frac{C_{in}}{C_{out}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_{out} = \frac{C_{in}}{\text{DF}}$$ **Step 4 — For non-volatile species (Cs, Sr, Co), DF is large (little reaches the condenser). For volatile species (I, Xe), DF is small. The general secondary-side activity is:** $$\boxed{\,C_{secondary} = \frac{C_p}{\text{DF}} \cdot \frac{L}{F_s}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical example.** Take $C_p = 10^5$ Bq/kg of Cs-137, $L = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s (≈ 23 gpd), $F_s = 2000$ kg/s, DF = 10. $$C_{steam} = \frac{10^5}{10} \cdot \frac{10^{-3}}{2000} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Bq/kg}$$ This is well below operational concern, illustrating why routine SG tube leakage is tightly controlled. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — N-16 Gamma Dose Rate at the Turbine Casing Treating the N-16 as a line source along the HP turbine, the dose rate at the casing can be approximated by a self-absorbing line source. For a simpler point-source approximation at a distance $d$ from the steam line: $$\dot{H} = \frac{\Gamma_{N16} \cdot A_{steam}}{d^2}$$ where $\Gamma_{N16}$ is the specific gamma-ray dose constant for N-16 (≈ $1.7 \times 10^{-13}$ Sv·m²/s per Bq at 1 m for the 6.13/7.11 MeV gammas combined). With $A_{steam} = 10^{12}$ Bq and $d = 2$ m: $$\dot{H} = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{-13} \times 10^{12}}{(2)^2} = 4.25 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{Sv/h} = 42.5\ \text{mSv/h}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common valuation pitfall:** Students often forget to convert units of the dose-rate constant from "per hour" to "per second" or vice versa. Always keep **time units consistent** between $\Gamma$ and $A$. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Origin, Transport, and Deposition of Radioactivity in the Steam System ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Reactor Core
Fast neutron flux] -->|Activation of O-16| B1[N-16 produced
in coolant] A2[Reactor Core
Thermal neutron flux] -->|Activation of corrosion products| B2[Activated Crud
Co-60, Mn-54, Fe-59] A3[Reactor Core
Fission products
Cs-137, I-131] -->|Leakage through SG tube| B3[Fission product
carryover into steam] B1 -->|Enters SG as water| C1[Steam Generator
Primary side] B2 -->|Deposits on fuel cladding| C2[Crud layer
on cladding] C2 -->|Release to coolant| C1 C3[SG Tube Defect] -->|Primary to secondary leak| C1 C1 -->|Steam rises| D1[Main Steam Line
N-16 still decaying] D1 -->|Transit time t1| E1[High Pressure Turbine] E1 -->|Transit time t2| E2[Low Pressure Turbine] E2 --> F1[Condenser
noble gases released] F1 --> G1[Condensate and Feedwater] B2 -->|Plate out on SG tubes| H1[SG Tube Deposits
source of shutdown dose] B2 -->|Plate out on piping| H2[Piping Scale
source of outage dose] classDef source fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315,color:#000 classDef transit fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 classDef sink fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 class A1,A2,A3 source class D1,E1,E2 transit class F1,G1,H1,H2 sink ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Steam System Activity ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph sourceBlock[ACTIVITY SOURCES] src1[N-16 via O-16 n,p reaction] src2[Activated Corrosion Products] src3[Fission Product Leakage] end subgraph transportBlock[TRANSPORT MECHANISMS] tr1[Water-steam partition
in SG] tr2[Droplet carryover] tr3[Volatile release in condenser] end subgraph sinkBlock[SINKS AND DOSE-RATE CONCERNS] sk1[Main Steam Line Dose Rate] sk2[Turbine Casing Shutdown Dose] sk3[SG Tube Sheet Outage Dose] sk4[Stack Release I and Xe] end src1 --> tr1 --> sk1 src1 --> sk1 src2 --> tr1 --> sk3 src2 --> sk2 src3 --> tr2 --> sk4 src3 --> tr2 --> sk1 tr1 --> sk1 src3 --> tr3 --> sk4 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Timeline — N-16 Decay Along the Steam Path ```mermaid flowchart LR t0[Core exit
Activity = 1.00 A0] -->|7.13 s, one half-life| t1[SG outlet
Activity = 0.50 A0] t1 -->|7.13 s, two half-lives| t2[Turbine stop valve
Activity = 0.25 A0] t2 -->|7.13 s, three half-lives| t3[Turbine exhaust
Activity = 0.125 A0] t3 -->|7.13 s, four half-lives| t4[Condenser inlet
Activity = 0.0625 A0] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]** *What is N-16 and why is it the dominant contributor to the radiation field around the main steam lines and turbine of a PWR?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** N-16 is a radioactive isotope of nitrogen produced by the (n,p) threshold reaction on O-16 in the reactor coolant. It is the dominant contributor because: (i) it emits very high-energy gamma rays (6.13 MeV and 7.11 MeV) which are highly penetrating, (ii) it is chemically inert and does not plate out, so it remains in the steam as it travels to the turbine, and (iii) although its half-life is only 7.13 s, the transit time from the core to the turbine is short (typically < 1 s in the SG and steam line), so the activity is still a large fraction of saturation at the turbine. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]** *List any three activated corrosion-product isotopes that contribute to outage dose rates in the steam system and state their half-lives.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Cobalt-60** — $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ years (longest half-life, dominant long-term shutdown dose contributor) 2. **Manganese-54** — $T_{1/2} = 312$ days 3. **Iron-59** — $T_{1/2} = 44.5$ days (Alternative: **Chromium-51** — $T_{1/2} = 27.7$ days). **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Mark scheme on derivations:** Students frequently lose **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions by omitting the **initial condition** in N-16 decay derivations. Always explicitly state $A(t=0) = A_{sat}$. > - **Units inconsistency:** Mixing seconds and hours in dose-rate calculations is the single most common error. State the unit conversion **explicitly** in the working. > - **SG leak vs. carryover confusion:** Marks are deducted when students interchange PWR (leakage-driven) and BWR (direct carryover) source terms. > - **Carrying over of carried digits:** In multi-step calculations, do not pre-round intermediate values; keep ≥ 4 significant figures. --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 2]** *(a)* Explain the three principal sources of radioactivity in the steam system of a PWR. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Derive the equation for the activity of N-16 in the main steam line as a function of distance from the steam generator. Using your result, calculate the N-16 activity at the HP turbine stop valve for a 4-loop PWR with the following data: steam line length = 35 m, steam velocity = 45 m/s, N-16 saturation activity at SG outlet = $4 \times 10^{12}$ Bq/kg, half-life of N-16 = 7.13 s. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **N-16 from O-16 activation (2 marks):** Threshold (n,p) reaction in core on the coolant itself. N-16 is volatile (forms N₂), inert, and emits 6.13/7.11 MeV gammas. It is the dominant contributor to **operating** radiation fields around the main steam line and turbine. 2. **Activated corrosion products / crud (2 marks):** Structural Fe, Cr, Ni, Co, Mn are slightly soluble, transported into the core where they are neutron-activated, then deposit on out-of-core surfaces. **Co-60, Mn-54, Fe-59, Cr-51** are the main isotopes. These are the dominant cause of **outage/shutdown** dose rates. 3. **Primary-to-secondary leakage (PWR) or direct carryover (BWR) (2 marks):** In PWRs, defective steam-generator tubes allow primary coolant containing dissolved fission products (Cs-137, I-131, Sr-90) and activated corrosion products into the secondary side. In BWRs, the steam is in direct contact with the core, so volatile fission products (Xe, Kr, I) carry over directly. 4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** All three sources must be quantified in safety analyses because they contribute to different dose pathways (operating dose, outage dose, public dose via stack). **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Derivation (4 marks):** - The rate of N-16 production per unit volume in the core: $R = N_{O16}\sigma\phi$. - At saturation: $A_{sat} = R$. - After leaving the core, decay is first-order: $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ with $A_0 = A_{sat}$ and $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$. - Distance–time relation: $t = L/v$. - Therefore: $$A_{turbine} = A_{sat} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} \cdot \frac{L}{v}\right)$$ **Calculation (3 marks):** - Transit time: $t = 35 / 45 = 0.778$ s. - Decay constant: $\lambda = 0.693 / 7.13 = 0.0972$ s⁻¹. - Exponent: $-\lambda t = -0.0972 \times 0.778 = -0.0756$. - $e^{-0.0756} = 0.927$. - $A_{turbine} = 4 \times 10^{12} \times 0.927 = 3.71 \times 10^{12}$ Bq/kg. **Final answer:** $A_{turbine} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{12}$ Bq/kg. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 2]** *(a)* With the help of a labelled schematic, describe the transport pathways by which radioactivity reaches the secondary side of a PWR. Explain the role of steam generator tube integrity monitoring. **(7 marks)** *(b)* In a PWR, a steam generator develops a primary-to-secondary leak of 30 gallons per day. The primary coolant activity concentration of Cs-137 is $2 \times 10^5$ Bq/kg. The total steam flow is 1800 kg/s and the decontamination factor of the steam-side cleanup system is 20. Calculate the Cs-137 activity concentration in the steam and comment on the regulatory implications. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **Schematic description (3 marks):** Draw a labelled diagram (or describe in words) of the primary loop → steam generator tubes → secondary side → main steam line → turbine. Show the location of the leak as a hole in the SG tube. Indicate corrosion-product transport loops: core activation → coolant transport → out-of-core deposition on SG tubes (and piping). 2. **Transport mechanisms (2 marks):** (i) convective transport of dissolved and suspended species in the primary coolant; (ii) diffusion and deposition of activated corrosion products on out-of-core surfaces; (iii) direct leakage of primary coolant through tube defects. 3. **SG tube integrity monitoring (2 marks):** Continuous on-line monitoring of (a) N-16 in the main steam line, (b) I-131/I-134 ratio in steam (since these come only from primary leakage, not activation), and (c) condenser air ejector activity. A sudden increase triggers a plant transient. Regulatory limits (e.g., ASME Section XI) cap allowable leakage (action level ~75 gpd total; safety limit ~150 gpd). **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Convert leak rate to SI.** 30 gallons/day of water = $30 \times 3.785 / 86400$ kg/s = $1.314 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s. **Step 2 — Activity in primary coolant leaking into secondary side per second:** $$\dot{A}_{leak} = 2 \times 10^5 \times 1.314 \times 10^{-3} = 262.8\ \text{Bq/s}$$ **Step 3 — Dilute in steam flow (with DF of 20):** $$C_{steam} = \frac{\dot{A}_{leak}}{\text{DF} \times F_{steam}} = \frac{262.8}{20 \times 1800} = 7.3 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Bq/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Regulatory commentary (3 marks):** The concentration is well below operational concern, but the **plant would be in an "Action Level" condition** under ASME Section XI (since 30 gpd in one SG may exceed 0.1 gpd/SG limit) and a **leak-before-break assessment** would be required. Continued operation would require either: (a) isolating the affected SG, (b) conducting an eddy-current inspection, and (c) implementing a chemistry regime to minimize further degradation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Bullets for Last-Minute Revision:** > - The **three sources** of steam radioactivity: **(1) N-16 activation, (2) activated corrosion products, (3) primary-to-secondary leakage (PWR) or direct carryover (BWR)**. > - **N-16** is the dominant **operating** radiation source in the steam line and turbine; it comes from the **threshold (n,p) reaction on O-16** and has $T_{1/2} = 7.13$ s, $\lambda = 0.0972$ s⁻¹, and emits **6.13 & 7.11 MeV gammas**. > - **Activated corrosion products (Co-60, Mn-54, Fe-59, Cr-51)** are the dominant **outage/shutdown** dose contributors; Co-60 is the long-term one ($T_{1/2} = 5.27$ y). > - **Steam generator tube leakage** is monitored continuously by N-16 and I-131/I-134 in steam. > - **Decontamination Factor (DF)** = $C_{in}/C_{out}$ — non-volatile species have high DF (removed by blowdown); volatile species (I, Xe) have low DF. > - **Decay along steam line:** $A(t) = A_{sat} e^{-\lambda t}$ with $t = L/v$. > - **Secondary activity from SG leak:** $C_{secondary} = (C_p / \text{DF}) \times (L / F_{steam})$. > - In a **BWR**, the steam is in direct contact with the core → carryover of **noble gases (Xe, Kr) and radio-iodine** is the main concern; turbine radiation is therefore typically **higher than in a PWR**. > - In a **PWR**, the steam generator is the **single most important barrier** between radioactive and non-radioactive systems; its tube integrity is a **safety-critical** concern. > - **ALARA** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) is the regulatory philosophy applied to all steam-system radiation fields.

topics.title: Direct Cycle and Dual Cycle Power Plants - Pressurized Water Reactors and Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Cycle and Dual Cycle Power Plants - Pressurized Water Reactors and Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Direct Cycle Power Plant — Boiling Water Reactor (BWR)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Direct Cycle Nuclear Power Plant** is a single-loop configuration in which the primary reactor coolant is allowed to **boil inside the reactor core** and the resulting steam is routed *directly* to the turbine blades without any intermediate heat exchanger. The classic example is the **Boiling Water Reactor (BWR)**, developed by General Electric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining feature of a direct cycle is the *absence* of a steam generator. Steam leaving the core is the *same* steam that spins the turbine.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Single Kettle"**
Imagine boiling water in a kettle on a gas stove. The steam that comes out of the kettle spout is *the same* steam used to spin a tiny toy turbine placed on top. There is **no intermediate pipe carrying the heat to a second vessel**. The BWR works exactly this way — the reactor pressure vessel acts as both the *boiler* and the *steam supplier*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operating Parameters of a Typical BWR:**
> - Core pressure: **~7.0 MPa (≈ 70 bar)**
> - Core outlet temperature: **~285 °C**
> - Steam quality at exit: **~14.5 %**
> - Thermal efficiency: **~34 %**

---

### 1.2 Dual (Indirect) Cycle Power Plant — Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Dual Cycle (Indirect Cycle)** uses *two* completely separate coolant loops connected via a **Steam Generator (SG)**. The **Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)** is the textbook example. The primary loop contains pressurized, subcooled water that *never boils*; heat is transferred across SG tubes to a secondary loop where steam is generated to drive the turbine.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Double Boiler"**
Think of a domestic instant-water-heater (geyser). The gas flame heats a coiled copper pipe (primary side) but never touches the water inside the pipe. The water in the coil transfers heat to a *second* water stream flowing around it, which then becomes your hot shower water. The PWR primary loop is the "copper coil" and the secondary loop is the "shower water" — they are physically isolated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operating Parameters of a Typical PWR:**
> - Primary loop pressure: **~15.5 MPa (≈ 155 bar)**
> - Primary coolant temperature: **~290 °C → 325 °C**
> - Steam pressure at SG outlet: **~6.5 MPa**
> - Thermal efficiency: **~33 %**
> - Number of SGs (typical 1000 MWe unit): **2 or 3**

---

### 1.3 Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor (PHWR)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor (PHWR)** is a thermal-neutron reactor that uses **heavy water (D₂O)** as both the *primary coolant* and the *moderator*, while the fuel is typically **natural uranium (0.7 % ²³⁵U)**. The pressure of the primary D₂O is kept above the saturation value so that **bulk boiling does not occur** inside the core, even at high temperatures.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Heavy Trainer"**
In a swimming pool, water is light (H₂O) and pushes you up easily. Now imagine a *thicker, syrup-like* fluid (D₂O, ~10 % denser). Neutrons bounce off it differently — it is much better at slowing down neutrons without absorbing them. This allows the reactor to "burn" **natural (un-enriched) uranium** as fuel, like a student passing an exam using only a regular textbook instead of an advanced reference book.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PHWRs do NOT require enriched uranium. The commercial archetype is the **CANDU** (Canada Deuterium Uranium) reactor designed by Atomic Energy of Canada Limited (AECL).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Operating Parameters of a Typical PHWR (e.g., 220 MWe Indian design, Kakrapar/Kaiga):**
> - Primary D₂O pressure: **~9.0 MPa**
> - Coolant inlet / outlet temperature: **~249 °C / 293 °C**
> - Moderator D₂O temperature: **~65 °C (low)** — separated from primary coolant
> - Fuel: **Natural UO₂ (0.7 % ²³⁵U)**
> - Online refuelling: **Possible (under load)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Why Two Loops? — The Physics Behind PWR Design

In a PWR, the primary pressure is raised to **~15.5 MPa**, which is well above the saturation pressure of water at 345 °C (~ 15 MPa). This forces the coolant to remain in the **subcooled liquid phase** throughout the core. The thermodynamic advantage is:

- Water in the liquid phase has a very **high specific heat (~5.2 kJ/kg·K)**.
- It also has a **high density (~720 kg/m³ at core conditions)**, giving a large volumetric heat capacity.
- A high **boiling margin** = high safety against fuel cladding failure.

A **pressurizer** (a small auxiliary vessel connected to the primary loop) maintains this high pressure by a combination of:
1. **Electric immersion heaters** (raise pressure by boiling water)
2. **Spray nozzles** (lower pressure by condensing steam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pressurizer is the *heart* of the PWR's pressure control. In KTU exams, a diagram showing the pressurizer surge line and spray line is a high-yield topic.

---

### 2.2 Why a Direct Cycle? — The Physics Behind BWR Design

The BWR eliminates the steam generator. The *entire primary system* is one large loop. Trade-offs:

| Feature | BWR (Direct) | PWR (Dual) |
|---|---|---|
| Steam Generator | **Absent** | Present (2–3 per unit) |
| Primary Pump | Internal jet pumps | Large external RCPs |
| Radioactivity in Turbine | **Yes (N-16 hazard)** | **No (sealed secondary)** |
| Containment | Hardened "Mark I/II/III" | Large dry / ice condenser |
| Pressure Vessel Diameter | Large (~6.4 m) | Smaller (~4.4 m) with SG |
| Cycle thermal efficiency | **~34 %** (higher) | ~33 % |

> [!WARNING]
> **N-16 Hazard:** In a BWR, the activated isotope **Nitrogen-16 (¹⁶N)** (half-life 7.13 s, gamma 6.1 & 7.1 MeV) circulates with the steam. The turbine hall must be heavily shielded — this is a *direct-cycle-specific* nuclear-safety concern.

---

### 2.3 PHWR — Unique Thermal-Hydraulic Features

1. **Two separate D₂O inventories**: Coolant in the *primary heat transport (PHT)* system, and moderator in the *calandria vessel*. They are kept at different temperatures and pressures.
2. **Horizontal pressure tubes**: Fuel is loaded into **horizontal Zircaloy-4 pressure tubes** (each ~10 cm dia., ~6 m long). This is in contrast to PWR/BWR which use a single large reactor vessel.
3. **End shields and feeder pipes**: Heavy steel end-shields house the fuelling machine ports.
4. **Online refuelling**: Two fuelling machines push fresh fuel in one end and push spent fuel out the other end of the same channel — *without shutting down the reactor*.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moderating Power Ratio (Heavy vs Light Water) | $\xi_{D_2O}\Sigma_s^{D_2O} \;/\; \xi_{H_2O}\Sigma_s^{H_2O}$ | $\approx 0.16$ (D₂O *inferior* absorber, hence better) |
| 2 | Moderating Ratio (MR) | $\text{MR} = \dfrac{\xi\Sigma_s}{\Sigma_a}$ | D₂O MR $\approx$ 21,000 ; H₂O MR $\approx$ 70 |
| 3 | Mass of ²³⁵U for criticality (infinite medium) | $m = \dfrac{N_A \sigma_a \rho V}{0.007 \cdot \sigma_{f}}$ | kg |
| 4 | Mean Logarithmic Energy Decrement (D₂O) | $\xi_{D_2O} = 1 + \dfrac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1-\alpha}$ where $\alpha = \left(\dfrac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^{2}$ | $\xi_{D_2O} = 0.509$ ; $\xi_{H_2O} = 1.000$ |
| 5 | Thermal efficiency (Rankine) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cond}}{T_{boiler}}$ | BWR: **~0.34** |
| 6 | Pressurizer pressure control (static balance) | $P_{pri} = P_{sat}(T_{pri}) + \Delta P_{surge}$ | $\sim 15.5$ MPa |
| 7 | Heat removal rate | $\dot Q = \dot m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | PWR: $\sim 3.1 \times 10^9$ W (1000 MWe) |
| 8 | Conversion Ratio (PHWR, natural U) | $CR = \dfrac{\text{new fissile atoms produced}}{\text{fissile atoms consumed}}$ | $\sim 0.7$ – $0.8$ |
| 9 | Fuel burn-up (PHWR) | $BU = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m_{HM}}$ | $\sim 7{,}000$ MWd/tHM |
| 10 | Delayed neutron fraction (²³⁵U vs ²³⁸U thresh.) | $\beta_{eff}$ | $\sim 650$ pcm (²³⁵U) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not confuse** *moderating power* ($\xi\Sigma_s$) with *moderating ratio* ($\xi\Sigma_s/\Sigma_a$). The latter is the figure of merit for moderator choice — D₂O wins because its absorption cross-section $\Sigma_a$ is **~1/600** of light water's.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Moderating Ratio of D₂O vs H₂O (Step-by-Step)

We want to **prove quantitatively** why D₂O is the moderator of choice for a natural-uranium reactor.

**Step 1: Definition of the mean logarithmic energy decrement per collision.**

For an isotropic elastic collision between a neutron (mass 1) and a target nucleus of mass number $A$:

$$
\alpha = \left( \dfrac{A - 1}{A + 1} \right)^{2}
$$

The mean logarithmic energy decrement is:

$$
\xi = 1 + \dfrac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\xi$ for Hydrogen (A = 1).**

For A = 1, $\alpha = 0$. Using L'Hôpital's rule on the limit:

$$
\xi_{H_2O} = \lim_{A \to 1} \left( 1 + \dfrac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1-\alpha} \right) = 1.000
$$

> **Logic:** Hydrogen is the *most effective* single-collision energy remover (a head-on collision with H brings the neutron to rest in one hit).

**Step 3: Compute $\xi$ for Deuterium (A = 2).**

$$
\alpha_D = \left( \dfrac{2-1}{2+1} \right)^{2} = \dfrac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111
$$

$$
\xi_{D_2O} = 1 + \dfrac{(1/9)\ln(1/9)}{1 - 1/9} = 1 + \dfrac{0.1111 \times (-2.1972)}{0.8889}
$$

$$
\xi_{D_2O} = 1 - \dfrac{0.2441}{0.8889} = 1 - 0.2746 = 0.7254
$$

> **Per atom**, deuterium is **less effective** than hydrogen at slowing neutrons. So how does D₂O "win" overall?

**Step 4: Compare macroscopic scattering and absorption cross sections.**

For thermal neutrons (2200 m/s):

| Parameter | H₂O | D₂O |
|---|---|---|
| $\Sigma_s$ (cm⁻¹) | ~ 3.45 | ~ 0.45 |
| $\Sigma_a$ (cm⁻¹) | ~ 0.022 | ~ 0.00004 |
| $\xi$ | 1.000 | 0.7254 |

**Step 5: Compute moderating ratio (MR).**

$$
MR_{H_2O} = \dfrac{\xi_{H_2O} \cdot \Sigma_s^{H_2O}}{\Sigma_a^{H_2O}} = \dfrac{1.000 \times 3.45}{0.022} \approx 156
$$

$$
MR_{D_2O} = \dfrac{\xi_{D_2O} \cdot \Sigma_s^{D_2O}}{\Sigma_a^{D_2O}} = \dfrac{0.7254 \times 0.45}{0.00004} \approx 8160
$$

> **Result:** $MR_{D_2O} \gg MR_{H_2O}$. Heavy water moderates **~50 times more efficiently** than light water per unit absorption. This is precisely why a PHWR can run on natural uranium.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Primary Coolant Mass Flow Rate for a 1000 MWe PWR

**Given:**
- Net electric output: $P_e = 1000$ MW
- Thermal efficiency: $\eta_{th} = 0.33$
- Primary inlet / outlet temperatures: $T_{in} = 290\,°C$, $T_{out} = 325\,°C$
- Specific heat of water: $c_p = 5.5$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 1: Compute total thermal power.**

$$
P_{th} = \dfrac{P_e}{\eta_{th}} = \dfrac{1000}{0.33} \approx 3030 \; \text{MW}_{th}
$$

**Step 2: Energy balance on primary loop.**

$$
\dot Q = \dot m_{pri} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

$$
3030 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W} = \dot m_{pri} \times 5500 \; \text{J/kg·K} \times (325 - 290) \; \text{K}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\dot m_{pri}$.**

$$
\dot m_{pri} = \dfrac{3030 \times 10^{6}}{5500 \times 35} = \dfrac{3.03 \times 10^{9}}{1.925 \times 10^{5}} \approx 15{,}740 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

> **Result:** A 1000 MWe PWR circulates about **~15,700 tonnes of water per minute** through its primary loop. This huge inventory is what makes "loss-of-coolant accident" (LOCA) such a high-priority safety case.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: BWR Steam Quality at Core Exit

**Given:**
- Core pressure: $P = 7.0$ MPa
- Core inlet subcooled water at $T_{in} = 215\,°C$
- Core exit steam quality $x$

**Step 1: Saturated temperature at 7.0 MPa.** From steam tables:

$$
T_{sat}(7.0 \text{ MPa}) = 285.8 \,°C
$$

**Step 2: Energy balance.** Let $h_f$ and $h_g$ be saturated liquid and vapour enthalpies at 7 MPa.

$$
h_f = 1267.4 \; \text{kJ/kg}, \qquad h_{fg} = 1404.9 \; \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Subcooled water enthalpy at 215 °C ≈ $h_{in} = 921 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (compressed liquid ≈ saturated liquid at 215 °C).

**Step 3: Apply conservation.**

$$
h_{out} = h_{in} + \Delta h \quad \text{with} \quad h_{out} = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}
$$

If the average enthalpy rise per kg of coolant is, say, $\Delta h = 600$ kJ/kg:

$$
921 + 600 = 1267.4 + x (1404.9)
$$

$$
1521 = 1267.4 + 1404.9 \, x
$$

$$
x = \dfrac{1521 - 1267.4}{1404.9} = \dfrac{253.6}{1404.9} \approx 0.181 = 18.1\,\%
$$

> **Result:** About **14–18 %** of the core-out flow is steam. The remaining 82–86 % is liquid water that must be separated by the **steam separator** above the core and recycled by **jet pumps** in the downcomer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — BWR (Direct Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reactor Core Boiling Water] -->|Wet Steam 14 percent Quality| B[Steam Separator]
    B -->|Dry Steam| C[High Pressure Turbine]
    B -->|Recirculated Water| D[Recirculation Jet Pumps]
    D -->|Feedwater| A
    C --> E[Moisture Separator Reheater]
    E --> F[Low Pressure Turbine]
    F --> G[Condenser]
    G -->|Condensate| H[Condensate Pump]
    H --> I[Feedwater Heater]
    I -->|Feedwater| D
    G -.Heat Rejection.-> J[Cooling Tower Sea]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — PWR (Dual Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrimaryLoop[Primary Loop 15.5 MPa]
        RC[Reactor Core] -->|Hot 325C| SG1[Steam Generator Tube Side]
        SG1 -->|Cold 290C| RCP[Reactor Coolant Pump]
        RCP --> RC
        PZ[Pressurizer] -.Surge Line.-> SG1
    end
    subgraph SecondaryLoop[Secondary Loop 6.5 MPa]
        SG2[Steam Generator Shell Side] -->|Steam| HPT[High Pressure Turbine]
        HPT --> MSR[Moisture Separator Reheater]
        MSR --> LPT[Low Pressure Turbine]
        LPT --> CND[Condenser]
        CND --> CP[Condensate Pump]
        CP --> FWH[Feedwater Heater]
        FWH --> SG2
    end
    SG1 --- SG2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — PHWR (CANDU / Indian 220/540/700 MWe)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ReactorBuilding[Reactor Building]
        CAL[Calandria Vessel with D2O Moderator at 65C] -->|Horizontal Pressure Tubes| CORE[Fuelled Channels Natural UO2]
        CORE -->|Hot D2O 293C| END1[Inlet End Shield]
        END1 -->|Pumps| PHTD2O[Primary Heat Transport D2O Loop at 9 MPa]
        PHTD2O -->|Cold 249C| END2[Outlet End Shield]
        END2 --> CORE
        PHTD2O --> SGPHWR[Steam Generators D2O to H2O]
        FM1[Fuelling Machine Inlet] -.Push.-> END1
        END2 -.Push.-> FM2[Fuelling Machine Outlet]
    end
    SGPHWR -->|Steam 6 MPa| TRPHWR[Turbine]
    TRPHWR --> CON[Condenser]
    CON -->|Feedwater| SGPHWR
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Direct or Indirect} -->|Direct| BWR[BWR One Loop Steam in Turbine]
    Q1 -->|Indirect| PWR[PWR Two Loops SG Isolates Steam]
    PWR --> PHWR[PHWR Variant with D2O Coolant and Moderator]
    BWR --> A1[Mark I II III Containment]
    PWR --> A2[Large Dry Ice Condenser Containment]
    PHWR --> A3[Vault Type Containment Online Refuelling]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In the PWR block, the *primary* loop never crosses into the *secondary* loop. The only thermal link is the **tube bundle of the Steam Generator** — this is what gives the PWR its name *"Pressurized Water"*: the pressurizer keeps the water liquid so that no boiling radioactivity escapes into the turbine building.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**What is a "direct cycle" in a nuclear power plant? Name one reactor type that uses it.**

**Model Answer:**
In a **direct cycle**, the primary reactor coolant is allowed to boil inside the core, and the resulting steam is routed *directly* to drive the turbine, with **no intermediate heat exchanger**. A typical example is the **Boiling Water Reactor (BWR)**, where steam generated in the reactor pressure vessel flows straight to the high-pressure turbine.
*(3 marks – definition 2 marks + example 1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**List any three advantages of using heavy water (D₂O) as a moderator in a PHWR.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Low neutron absorption cross-section** ($\Sigma_a \approx 0.00004$ cm⁻¹) → allows use of **natural uranium** (no enrichment needed).
2. **High moderating ratio** ($\sim 8160$ vs $\sim 156$ for H₂O) → neutrons are thermalised efficiently with minimal parasitic capture.
3. **Compatibility with on-power refuelling** → enables continuous operation and high plant capacity factor (typically 80–90 %).
*(3 marks – 1 mark each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with internal choice. Typical split: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)**. Highlight the role of the **pressurizer** and the **steam generator**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PWR has a thermal power output of 3000 MWₜₕ and an electrical output of 1000 MWe. The primary coolant enters the core at 290 °C and leaves at 325 °C. Calculate:
   (i) The thermal efficiency of the plant.
   (ii) The primary coolant mass flow rate, given $c_p = 5.5$ kJ/kg·K. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**
- The PWR uses **two separate loops**: primary (pressurized water) and secondary (steam–water).
- In the **primary loop**, the **Reactor Coolant Pump (RCP)** circulates subcooled water at **~15.5 MPa** through the core. Heat is generated by fission but the water does **not** boil (subcooled) because pressure is above the saturation value at the operating temperature.
- The hot water flows into the **Steam Generator (SG)**, where it transfers heat across the U-tube bundle to the secondary-side feedwater, which **boils and becomes steam**.
- The **pressurizer** is a small auxiliary vessel connected to the primary loop via a surge line. It contains a mixture of liquid and steam at the saturation point. **Electric heaters** (bottom) raise pressure by boiling; **spray nozzles** (top) lower pressure by condensation. This maintains the primary pressure at **~15.5 MPa**, ensuring the primary coolant stays subcooled.
- The dry steam on the secondary side drives the **HP turbine → moisture separator → LP turbine → condenser → feedwater pump → back to SG**.

> *(Block diagram 3 marks; explanation of pressurizer & SG 4 marks)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Thermal efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P_e}{P_{th}} = \dfrac{1000}{3000} = 0.333 = 33.3\,\%
$$

**(ii) Primary mass flow rate:**

Using the energy balance:

$$
\dot m_{pri} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{c_p \, (T_{out} - T_{in})}
$$

$$
\dot m_{pri} = \dfrac{3000 \times 10^{6} \;\text{W}}{5500 \;\text{J/kg·K} \times (325 - 290) \;\text{K}}
$$

$$
\dot m_{pri} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{9}}{1.925 \times 10^{5}} \approx 15{,}584 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

> **Answer:** $\eta_{th} = 33.3\,\%$, $\dot m_{pri} \approx 15{,}584$ kg/s.
> *(Efficiency 3 marks; flow rate 4 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the **Direct Cycle (BWR)** and **Dual Cycle (PWR)** nuclear power plants. Comment on **cycle efficiency, safety, and the radioactive steam issue**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **fuel channel and calandria arrangement** of a **Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor (PHWR)**. Why can it use natural uranium as fuel? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | BWR (Direct) | PWR (Dual) |
|---|---|---|
| Loops | 1 (steam directly to turbine) | 2 (primary + secondary, separated by SG) |
| Cycle efficiency | ~34 % (slightly higher — single loop pressure drop is less) | ~33 % |
| Steam source | Direct from core | Steam generator |
| Radioactivity in turbine | **Yes (¹⁶N hazard)** | **No (sealed secondary)** |
| Containment | Hardened Mark I/II/III (pressure suppression) | Large dry / ice-condenser |
| Pressure | ~7 MPa (lower, allows bulk boiling) | ~15.5 MPa (high, prevents boiling) |
| Fuel | Enriched UO₂ (~2.5–4 %) | Enriched UO₂ (~3–5 %) |

> **Safety trade-off:** BWR is *simpler* (no SG) but the *turbine hall becomes a controlled zone* because of activated ¹⁶N. PWR is more complex but the *turbine hall is clean*, simplifying maintenance.
> *(2 marks comparison table + 2 marks efficiency + 3 marks safety/radioactivity discussion)*

**(b) PHWR Calandria & Fuel Channel (7 marks):**

- The **calandria vessel** is a large horizontal cylindrical tank filled with **low-temperature D₂O moderator (~65 °C)** at low pressure.
- It contains several hundred **horizontal Zircaloy-4 pressure tubes** passing through it, end-shielded by heavy steel structures at both ends.
- Each pressure tube contains **natural UO₂ fuel bundles** stacked end-to-end and is *isolated* from the surrounding moderator.
- **Primary heat-transport (PHT) D₂O** at ~9 MPa flows *inside* the pressure tubes, picks up heat from the fuel, and carries it to the **steam generators** in the secondary loop. The PHT D₂O is at 249 °C (inlet) → 293 °C (outlet).
- The calandria is *not* pressurised (just heavy steel containment) and is at low temperature — this separation of *coolant* and *moderator* inventories is a hallmark of PHWR safety.

> **Why natural uranium works:**
> - D₂O has a **very low absorption cross-section** for thermal neutrons ($\Sigma_a \approx 4 \times 10^{-5}$ cm⁻¹).
> - The **moderating ratio** of D₂O is ~50× that of H₂O.
> - Therefore, neutron losses during moderation are small enough that a chain reaction can be sustained in **0.7 % ²³⁵U** without enrichment.
> *(3 marks diagram + 2 marks moderator separation + 2 marks natural-U justification)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In PWR problems, students often forget to convert MW to **W** (× 10⁶). Always check unit consistency.
> 2. For BWR, students wrongly state that "radioactive contamination in the turbine is prevented." It is **NOT prevented** — it is *managed* by shielding and short (7.13 s) half-life of ¹⁶N. The PWR is the design where the turbine is *clean*.
> 3. For PHWR, students often confuse **coolant D₂O** (hot, pressurised, in pressure tubes) with **moderator D₂O** (cold, low-pressure, in calandria). They are *two physically separate inventories*.
> 4. In efficiency derivations, watch the **Kelvin** vs **Celsius** — since we are using temperature *differences*, it does not matter, but always state the principle clearly.
> 5. Avoid using the term "moderating power" loosely — the correct figure of merit is the **moderating ratio** $\xi\Sigma_s/\Sigma_a$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Direct cycle = one loop**, BWR archetype, **steam touches the turbine**, ~7 MPa, ~285 °C.
- **Dual/Indirect cycle = two loops**, PWR archetype, **steam generator isolates** secondary, ~15.5 MPa primary, **pressurizer** maintains subcooled state.
- **PHWR** = uses **D₂O** for *both* coolant and moderator; **natural UO₂** fuel; **horizontal pressure tubes**; **online refuelling** capability.
- **Moderating ratio of D₂O** (~ 8160) ≫ **H₂O** (~ 156) → this is *why* PHWR can use unenriched fuel.
- **BWR turbine contains ¹⁶N** (7.13 s half-life, 6.1 / 7.1 MeV gammas) → radiation shielding mandatory.
- **PWR primary pressure 15.5 MPa** is the saturation pressure of water at ~345 °C → prevents bulk boiling.
- **Conversion ratio** of a PHWR with natural U ≈ 0.7–0.8 (i.e., it produces nearly as much new fissile ²³⁹Pu as it consumes ²³⁵U).
- **Online refuelling** of PHWRs (CANDU / Indian 220/540/700 MWe) eliminates refuelling outages → **capacity factor > 90 %**.
- **Thermal efficiency** for both PWR and BWR ≈ 33–34 % (Rankine cycle limit).
- **Primary coolant mass flow** for a 1000 MWe PWR ≈ 15,000–16,000 kg/s.
- **Number of SGs** in a typical PWR = 2 (Westinghouse 4-loop design had 4; modern 2-loop AP1000 has 2).
- **Core inlet/outlet temperatures:** PWR 290/325 °C ; BWR 215/285 °C ; PHWR 249/293 °C.
- **Mark** the *exact phrasing* of definitions for **3-mark questions**; examiners award marks for the *exact key terms* like "boiling in core", "pressurizer", "moderating ratio".
- For derivations, **state the formula → substitute values with units → final answer with units**. Missing units = 1 mark cut.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fast Breeder Reactors and Their Role in Power Generation in the Indian Context
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fast Breeder Reactors and Their Role in Power Generation in the Indian Context

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
