```mermaid
    C1 --> C5[EDG]
    C1 --> C6[Containment Isolation]
    
    D1[DID Level 4 Severe Accident Mgmt] --> D2[SAMG Procedures]
    D1 --> D3[Core Catcher VVER EPR]
    D1 --> D4[Filtered Venting]
    
    E1[DID Level 5 Emergency Response] --> E2[Offsite Emergency Plan]
    E1 --> E3[Evacuation Sheltering Thyroid Blocking]
```

## 4.5 Redundancy and Single-Failure Compliance

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Train A Active ESF] --> A2[Pump A]
    A1 --> A3[Valve A]
    A1 --> A4[Sensor A]
    
    B1[Train B Active ESF] --> B2[Pump B]
    B1 --> B3[Valve B]
    B1 --> B4[Sensor B]
    
    C1[Train C Active ESF] --> C2[Pump C]
    C1 --> C3[Valve C]
    C1 --> C4[Sensor C]
    
    A1 --> Z1[Any Single Failure of Train A Still Allows B and C to Function]
    B1 --> Z1
    C1 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Y1[Compliance with Single Failure Criterion 10 CFR 50 Appendix A]
```

## 4.6 Active vs Passive ESF Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Engineered Safety Features] --> B1[Active ESF]
    A1 --> C1[Passive ESF]
    
    B1 --> B2[Needs AC Power]
    B1 --> B3[Needs Operator Action or Auto Signal]
    B1 --> B4[Examples HPIS LPIS AFW Pump EDG]
    
    C1 --> C2[Uses Stored Energy]
    C1 --> C3[Gravity Driven]
    C1 --> C4[Compressed Gas Driven]
    C1 --> C5[Natural Circulation]
    C1 --> C6[Examples Accumulators Gravity Injection AP1000 Passive Cooling]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern** with proper mark distribution, Bloom's taxonomy tagging, and complete model solutions.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO3, Bloom: Remember/Understand)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q1.** Define **Engineered Safety Features (ESF)**. List any four major ESF systems used in a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR).

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Engineered Safety Features (ESF) are the diverse, redundant, and physically engineered active and passive safety systems that are installed in a nuclear power plant to prevent fuel damage, limit radioactive releases, and bring the reactor to a controlled, safe shutdown state during and after postulated initiating events (PIEs).

**Four Major ESF in a PWR:**

1. **Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS)** — High-Pressure Injection, Low-Pressure Injection, Accumulators.
2. **Residual Heat Removal System (RHRS)** — Long-term decay heat removal.
3. **Containment Spray System (CSS)** — Reduces containment pressure and scrubs radioiodine.
4. **Auxiliary Feedwater System (AFWS)** — Secondary-side cooling when main feedwater fails.

*(Any four — full 3 marks awarded.)*

---

### Question 2 (CO3, Bloom: Understand)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q2.** Explain the **"Single Failure Criterion"** as applied to the design of Engineered Safety Features in nuclear power plants.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The single failure criterion (per IEEE 379 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix A) states that a safety system must be capable of performing its intended safety function even when **any single active component** within the system has failed (e.g., a pump, valve, sensor, or circuit).
>
> **Implications for ESF design:**
> 1. **Redundancy:** At least two independent trains (typically N+1 or 2N) of safety equipment.
> 2. **Independence:** Physical and electrical separation of trains.
> 3. **Testability:** Each train must be testable during power operation.
> 4. **Diversity:** Different operating principles to defeat common-mode failures.
>
> **Example:** ECCS has separate HPIS trains (Train A, Train B) so that if Pump A fails, Pump B can still inject water into the core.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) (CO3, CO4, Bloom: Apply/Analyze)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected]** *(14 Marks)*

**Q (A).** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS)** of a PWR. Explain the role of high-pressure injection, low-pressure injection, and accumulators. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3000 MW(th) reactor trips and shuts down. Using the **ANS-1971 decay heat standard**, calculate the decay heat power after (i) 10 seconds, (ii) 1 hour, and (iii) 1 day. Comment on the design implications for the Residual Heat Removal System. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) ECCS Detailed Discussion

The **Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS)** is the most critical ESF in a PWR. Its purpose is to ensure that the reactor core remains covered with coolant and cooled during a Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA) so that fuel cladding temperature remains below the embrittlement limit of **1477 K (2200 °F)**.

**Components of ECCS:**

1. **High-Pressure Injection System (HPIS):**
   - Functions at full primary pressure (~15.5 MPa).
   - Uses centrifugal pumps taking suction from the **Refuelling Water Storage Tank (RWST)**.
   - Delivers borated water at high pressure to maintain core inventory and prevent core uncovery during the early phase of LOCA.
   - Activated automatically on low pressurizer pressure or low coolant inventory signal.

2. **Low-Pressure Injection System (LPIS):**
   - Activates after the primary system has depressurized below ~2 MPa.
   - Delivers large volumes of borated water from the RWST to the reactor vessel.
   - Functions in the **refill and long-term cooling** phases of LOCA.

3. **Accumulators (Passive):**
   - Large, nitrogen-pressurized (~4-5 MPa) tanks containing borated water.
   - Discharge passively on low primary pressure (no AC power required).
   - Provide immediate core cooling during the **blowdown phase** of LOCA (first ~20 seconds).
   - Each accumulator typically holds 30–50 m³ of borated water.

**Phase-wise Operation of ECCS:**

- **Phase 1 — Blowdown (0–20 s):** Accumulators discharge. HPIS active.
- **Phase 2 — Refill (20–60 s):** Core refills with borated water.
- **Phase 3 — Reflood (60 s–10 min):** Core quenched, water level rises.
- **Phase 4 — Long-term Cooling (10 min onward):** RHRS takes over.

**Acceptance Criteria (per 10 CFR 50.46):**
- Peak Cladding Temperature (PCT) ≤ 1477 K.
- Local cladding oxidation ≤ 17%.
- Core-wide hydrogen generation ≤ 1% of total potential.

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Decay Heat Calculation

**Given:** $P_{th} = 3000$ MW, $P_{decay}(t) = 0.0622 \cdot P_{th} \cdot t^{-0.2}$

$$
P_{decay}(t) = 0.0622 \times 3000 \times t^{-0.2} = 186.6 \times t^{-0.2} \text{ MW}
$$

**Case (i) — t = 10 s:**

$$
P_{decay}(10) = 186.6 \times 10^{-0.2}
$$

$$
10^{-0.2} = 1/10^{0.2} = 1/1.5849 = 0.6310
$$

$$
P_{decay}(10\,s) = 186.6 \times 0.6310 = 117.7 \text{ MW}
$$

**Case (ii) — t = 1 hour = 3600 s:**

$$
P_{decay}(3600) = 186.6 \times 3600^{-0.2} = 186.6 \times 0.1944 = 36.27 \text{ MW}
$$

**Case (iii) — t = 1 day = 86,400 s:**

$$
P_{decay}(86400) = 186.6 \times 86400^{-0.2} = 186.6 \times 0.1029 = 19.20 \text{ MW}
$$

**Design Implications for RHRS:**

- Even 1 day after shutdown, **~19.2 MW** of decay heat must be removed.
- The RHRS must have **continuous, reliable** heat removal capacity for days/weeks.
- **Two independent trains** with redundancy are required.
- Heat sink must be the **ultimate heat sink** (river, sea, cooling tower, or atmospheric cooling tower).
- For Gen III+ reactors, **passive residual heat removal (PRHR)** systems are added for additional defense.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) (CO3, CO4, Bloom: Apply/Analyze/Evaluate)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Expected Alternative]** *(14 Marks)*

**Q (B).** **(a)** Explain the **Containment Spray System (CSS)** in a PWR. With the help of a neat sketch, describe its components and its role in pressure suppression and iodine scrubbing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a postulated LOCA in a PWR containment (free volume 50,000 m³), the energy release rate into the containment is 70 MW. If the gas mixture has $\gamma = 1.4$, calculate the rate of pressure rise (in Pa/s and bar/min). What design measures would you recommend to ensure containment integrity? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a) Containment Spray System

The **Containment Spray System (CSS)** is an ESF designed to:
1. Reduce **containment pressure and temperature** after a LOCA.
2. **Scrub radioactive iodine** (especially I-131, which is volatile) from the containment atmosphere.
3. Maintain containment integrity below design pressure.

**Components:**

1. **Spray Headers** — Distribution piping with nozzles mounted in the upper dome of containment.
2. **Spray Pumps (Redundant A & B)** — Centrifugal pumps drawing from the RWST (initially) and later from the containment sump.
3. **NaOH Additive Tank** — Adds NaOH (sodium hydroxide) to the spray water to maintain alkaline pH (>7), which **enhances iodine removal efficiency** because I₂ reacts with NaOH to form soluble iodide ions.
4. **Manual and Automatic Actuation Valves** — Auto-activation on high containment pressure signal.
5. **Test and Isolation Valves** — For periodic testing.

**Working Principle:**

- On a high-containment-pressure signal, spray pumps start automatically.
- Borated, alkaline water is sprayed into the upper containment atmosphere.
- Heat is removed by evaporation of spray water droplets → pressure drops.
- Iodine in the atmosphere **partitions into the alkaline water droplets** (scrubbing efficiency > 99% for elemental iodine, ~85% for organic iodides).
- After RWST is depleted, the pumps re-align to take suction from the **containment sump** (recirculation mode).

**Design Pressure:** Typically 3–5 bar (gauge).

---

#### Model Solution — (b) Containment Pressure Rise Calculation

**Given:**
- $V_{con} = 50{,}000$ m³
- $\dot{Q}_{total} = 70$ MW = $70 \times 10^6$ W
- $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 1 — Apply pressure rise equation:**

$$
\frac{dP_{con}}{dt} = \frac{(\gamma - 1)}{V_{con}} \dot{Q}_{total}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\frac{dP_{con}}{dt} = \frac{(1.4 - 1)}{50{,}000} \times 70 \times 10^6
$$

$$
= \frac{0.4 \times 70 \times 10^6}{50{,}000} = \frac{28 \times 10^6}{50{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{dP_{con}}{dt} = 560 \text{ Pa/s}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bar/min:**

$$
560 \text{ Pa/s} = 560 \times 10^{-5} \text{ bar/s} = 5.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ bar/s}
$$

$$
5.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 = 0.336 \text{ bar/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{dP_{con}}{dt} = 0.336 \text{ bar/min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Pressure rise over 10 minutes (no spray):**

$$
\Delta P = 0.336 \times 10 = 3.36 \text{ bar}
$$

This would **exceed** the typical design pressure of 3 bar (gauge) within 10 minutes.

**Step 5 — Design Measures to Ensure Integrity:**

1. **Containment Spray System (CSS)** — automatic on high pressure.
2. **Passive Autocatalytic Recombiners (PARs)** for hydrogen control.
3. **Containment Isolation** within 5 seconds to prevent leakage.
4. **Filtered Venting** as a last-resort pressure relief.
5. **Suppression Pool (BWR)** or **Ice Condenser (PWR-Westinghouse)** for pressure suppression.
6. **Pre-stressed concrete containment** with steel liner to withstand overpressure.
7. **Leak-tightness testing** at 1.0 × design pressure integrated leak rate test (ILRT).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Not writing units** in the final answer → 0.5–1 mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing passive and active ESFs** — students often mix up accumulator (passive) and HPIS (active). Examiner's deduction: 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong ANS-1971 formula** — use only $0.0622 \cdot P_{th} \cdot t^{-0.2}$ where $t$ is in **seconds**. Wrong unit conversion (e.g., using hours directly) loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping ECCS acceptance criteria (PCT ≤ 1477 K)** — examiner allocates 1 mark for the regulatory citation.
> 5. **Not mentioning redundancy (N+1, 2N)** in long-answer questions — at least 1 mark reserved for redundancy.
> 6. **Confusing $k_{eff}$ and reactivity $\rho$** — write formulas clearly with their definitions.
> 7. **Not labeling diagrams** in part (a) — 0.5 mark deduction per unlabeled component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — KTU 2024 Module 3, ESF**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **ESF:** Engineered, active or passive safety systems preventing fuel damage and radioactive release.
- **Reactor Trip (SCRAM):** Rapid insertion of negative reactivity with $k_{eff} \le 0.99$.
- **Single Failure Criterion:** System must work even if any single active component fails.
- **Redundancy (N+1 or 2N):** At least 2 independent trains of safety equipment.
- **Defense-in-Depth:** 5 levels of protection; ESF = Level 3.
- **Decay Heat (ANS-1971):** $P_{decay}(t) = 0.0622 \cdot P_{th} \cdot t^{-0.2}$ (t in seconds).

### 🔧 Major ESF Components
1. **ECCS** — HPIS, LPIS, Accumulators.
2. **RHRS** — Long-term heat removal.
3. **Containment Spray** — Pressure & iodine control.
4. **Containment Isolation** — Closure within 5 s.
5. **AFW** — Secondary-side cooling.
6. **EDG** — Emergency AC power.
7. **MSIV** — Steam line isolation.
8. **H₂ Recombiners** — Hydrogen explosion prevention.

### 📊 Key Numerical Benchmarks
- **PCT Limit:** 1477 K (2200 °F).
- **Decay heat @ 1 s:** ~6.22% of $P_{th}$.
- **Decay heat @ 1 day:** ~0.6% of $P_{th}$.
- **Typical ECCS injection rate:** 50–200 kg/s per train.
- **Containment design pressure:** 3–5 bar (gauge).
- **Safety system unavailability target:** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$/demand.

### 📐 Equations to Memorize
- Shutdown Reactivity: $\rho_{sd} = -(1 - 1/k_{eff})$
- Choked break flow: $\dot{m} = C_d A P_0 \sqrt{k/(RT_0) \cdot (2/(k+1))^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}$
- Containment pressure rise: $dP/dt = (\gamma - 1)/V \cdot \dot{Q}$
- Heat removal: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$
- LMTD heat exchanger: $\dot{Q} = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$

### 🏛️ Regulatory Standards
- **IAEA SSR-2/1** (Design)
- **10 CFR 50.46** (ECCS)
- **10 CFR 50 Appendix A** (Single Failure)
- **IEEE 323, 379, 603** (Equipment qualification, redundancy, separation)
- **AERB Safety Codes** (Indian NPPs)

### 🎯 Active vs Passive ESF
- **Active:** Needs AC power + actuation signal (HPIS, AFW pumps, EDG).
- **Passive:** Uses stored energy, no AC power needed (Accumulators, gravity-driven injection, AP1000 passive cooling).

### 🧠 Exam Strategy Tips
1. Always define the system first, then list components.
2. State acceptance criteria with regulatory citation (e.g., 10 CFR 50.46).
3. Show every algebraic step in numerical problems.
4. Mention redundancy and single-failure compliance explicitly.
5. Draw block diagrams with proper labels.
6. Compare active and passive ESF in any "discuss" question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Site Related Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Site Related Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Site Related Factors** in nuclear safety refer to the comprehensive set of natural and human-induced characteristics of a proposed location that must be rigorously evaluated to ensure the safe siting, design, operation, and long-term decommissioning of a nuclear power plant (NPP). These factors form the first tier of the **Defence-in-Depth (DID)** strategy and are governed by the **As Low As Reasonably Achievable (ALARA)** principle.

According to the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) of India** and the **International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) Safety Standards Series (SSR-1, NS-R-3)**, site evaluation must consider:

- **External initiating events** (natural and human-induced)
- **Site characteristics** affecting the transfer of radioactive material to humans
- **Population density and distribution** in the vicinity
- **Emergency planning feasibility**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Site Related Factors form the foundation of the **"Siting and Design"** stage of nuclear safety. The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes understanding why a site is evaluated BEFORE plant design begins — the principle is "**Site dictates Safety Envelope, Safety Envelope dictates Design**."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are choosing a location to build a **hospital that handles infectious diseases**. You wouldn't build it in:
- A flood-prone riverbank (it would be destroyed)
- A densely populated slum (contagion risk)
- An earthquake fault zone (structural collapse)
- An area with no road access (emergency evacuation impossible)

A **Nuclear Power Plant is a 100-times more critical facility** than a hospital. The "patient" is a million curies of radioactive inventory. So before laying a single brick, geologists, seismologists, meteorologists, and demographers must certify the land.

### The "Three-Tier Cake" Analogy

| Tier | Analogy | Real-World Equivalent |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **Tier 1** (Base) | The Ground | Site Related Factors (geology, hydrology, seismology) |
| **Tier 2** (Middle) | The Building | Plant Design (containment, redundancy) |
| **Tier 3** (Top) | The Doctors | Operational Safety (procedures, training) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A perfectly engineered reactor on a seismically active fault or in a floodplain is a **disaster waiting to happen**, regardless of design quality. The 2011 Fukushima Daiichi accident in Japan was a site-related factor failure (tsunami exceeding design basis), NOT a design failure.

---

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards

- **Exclusion Zone Radius:** **1.6 km** (AERB standard) around the reactor
- **Sterilized Zone Radius:** **5 km** (population growth restriction)
- **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ):** **16 km** radius
- **Distance from major airport:** > **8–10 km** (aviation crash hazard)
- **Distance from large water body:** sufficient to prevent flooding
- **Design Basis Earthquake (DBE):** **0.1g to 0.3g PGA** (Peak Ground Acceleration, where **g = 9.81 m/s²**)
- **Tsunami design height:** site-specific, evaluated for 10,000-year return period

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often confuse *Exclusion Zone* with *EPZ*. The exclusion zone is **evacuated**; the EPZ is **planned for emergency response**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Site Evaluation

The AERB and IAEA classify site-related factors into **six fundamental pillars**. Each must be evaluated during the **Site Evaluation Stage (Pre-Construction)** and re-evaluated periodically (every 10 years as **Periodic Safety Review - PSR**).

### Pillar 1: **Geological & Geotechnical Factors**

- **Soil bearing capacity** for foundation design
- **Liquefaction potential** (soil turning to liquid under earthquake shaking)
- **Slope stability** (landslide risk)
- **Subsidence potential** (Karst topography - limestone caves)
- **Rock characteristics** for excavation and tunneling

**Why it matters:** If the foundation fails, the entire containment fails. The 1971 San Fernando and 2011 Tohoku events proved that site geology can be a silent killer.

### Pillar 2: **Seismotectonic Factors**

- **Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA)** at the site
- **Seismic hazard curve** (return period vs. PGA)
- **Capable fault identification** (fault capable of displacement at/near surface)
- **Design Basis Earthquake (DBE)** — annual exceedance probability **~10⁻⁴**
- **Beyond Design Basis Earthquake (BDBE)** — typically **1.5 × to 2 × DBE**

The **Safe Shutdown Earthquake (SSE)** is the maximum ground motion for which the plant is designed to safely shut down.

### Pillar 3: **Meteorological Factors**

- **Wind rose diagram** (frequency vs. direction)
- **Atmospheric dispersion factors** (χ/Q values)
- **Tornado and hurricane frequency**
- **Ambient temperature extremes** (affects cooling efficiency)
- **Precipitation and snow load**

**Atmospheric Dispersion Equation (Gaussian Plume Model):**

$$\chi(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Where:
- $\chi$ = concentration at point (x, y, z) [Bq/m³]
- $Q$ = release rate [Bq/s]
- $u$ = wind speed [m/s]
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients [m]

### Pillar 4: **Hydrological Factors**

- **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** — derived from Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP)
- **Probable Maximum Surge (PMS)** for coastal sites
- **Cooling water availability** (once-through vs. cooling tower)
- **Groundwater flow direction** and aquifer protection
- **Tsunami run-up height** for coastal plants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Favorite:** The Fukushima Daiichi accident (March 11, 2011) was caused by a **tsunami of ~14 meters** exceeding the design basis of **5.7 meters**. This is a guaranteed 14-mark question topic.

### Pillar 5: **Population Distribution & Demography**

- **Exclusion Zone (1.6 km):** No permanent habitation; controlled access
- **Sterilized Zone (5 km):** Restricted population growth
- **Emergency Planning Zone (16 km):** Evacuation plans must be operational
- **Population center distance** (min **10 km** for major cities > 25,000)
- **Low Population Zone (LPZ):** 5-mile radius (US NRC standard) for control of severe accidents

### Pillar 6: **External Human-Induced Events**

- **Aircraft crash probability** (airport proximity, flight paths)
- **Chemical plant explosion** (toxic gas release risk)
- **Military installations** (storage of explosives)
- **Transportation routes** (road, rail, pipeline carrying hazardous materials)
- **Missile impact** (relevant for some international jurisdictions)

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Variables** | **Typical Value** | **Application** |
|--------------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Gaussian Plume Model** | $\chi = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}-\dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2})$ | $\chi, Q, u, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ | $\chi/Q$ in s/m³ | Atmospheric dispersion |
| **Wind Sector χ/Q** | $\chi/Q = \dfrac{1}{u \cdot \sigma_z \cdot 2\pi \cdot \sigma_y} \cdot f_{sector}$ | $f_{sector}$ = sector frequency | 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ s/m³ | Dose calculation at boundary |
| **Pasquill-Gifford Stability Class** | A (most unstable) to F (most stable) | Atmospheric conditions | A, B, C, D, E, F | $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ selection |
| **Design Basis Earthquake (DBE)** | Annual exceedance = 10⁻⁴ | Return period ≈ 10,000 yrs | PGA = 0.1g–0.3g | Plant design |
| **Beyond DBE (BDBE)** | BDBE = 1.5 × to 2 × DBE | Scaling factor | PGA = 0.15g–0.6g | Severe accident analysis |
| **Liquefaction Factor of Safety** | $FS = \dfrac{\text{CRR}}{\text{CSR}}$ | CRR = Cyclic Resistance Ratio, CSR = Cyclic Stress Ratio | FS > 1.5 | Geotechnical design |
| **PMF Derivation** | PMF = PMP × Basin Coefficient | PMP from Hershfield equation | Site-specific | Flood design |
| **Dose Rate from Plume** | $D = \chi \cdot B \cdot DCF$ | B = breathing rate, DCF = dose conversion factor | Sieverts/year | Off-site dose |
| **Exclusion Zone Radius** | AERB standard | Fixed regulatory limit | **1.6 km** | Population control |
| **EPZ Radius** | AERB standard | Fixed regulatory limit | **16 km** | Emergency planning |
| **Cooling Water Requirement** | $\sim 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s per 1000 MW}_{\text{th}}$ | Flow rate vs. plant capacity | ~150 m³/s for typical plant | Hydrology design |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Tarapur Atomic Power Station (TAPS), India:** Built on coastal basaltic rock with stable geology. Uses Arabian Sea cooling water.
2. **Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant (KKNPP):** Site selected considering seismic, tsunami, and cyclonic factors; tsunami design height of **~10 m** post-Fukushima.
3. **Jaitapur Nuclear Power Project (Proposed):** Site evaluation in progress considering the Western Ghats seismotectonics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The cost of site evaluation (typically 2–5% of total project cost) is trivial compared to the **cost of a single accident** — Chernobyl economic loss estimated **> $700 billion**, Fukushima **> $200 billion**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Gaussian Plume Concentration Formula

The **Gaussian Plume Model** is the cornerstone of atmospheric dispersion calculations for nuclear safety. The following is the complete derivation.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Fick's Law of Diffusion

For a passive pollutant released continuously from an elevated point source, the 3D diffusion equation in atmospheric coordinates is:

$$\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial x} = K_x \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial x^2} + K_y \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial z^2}$$

Where:
- $\chi$ = pollutant concentration [kg/m³]
- $u$ = mean wind speed in x-direction [m/s]
- $K_x, K_y, K_z$ = eddy diffusivities in x, y, z directions

### Step 2: Apply Steady-State Assumption

For a continuous release, $\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial t} = 0$. Also, in the **downwind direction (x)**, turbulent diffusion is negligible compared to advection, so $K_x \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial x^2} \approx 0$.

This reduces the equation to:

$$u\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial z^2}$$

### Step 3: Introduce Dispersion Coefficients

In practice, eddy diffusivity is difficult to measure directly. We replace it with empirically determined **dispersion coefficients** $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ (functions of downwind distance x and stability class):

$$K_y = \frac{u \sigma_y^2}{2x}, \quad K_z = \frac{u \sigma_z^2}{2x}$$

The general steady-state solution is then:

$$\chi(x,y,z) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{Q}{\sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{(x-ut)^2}{2\sigma_x^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Time Integration (Continuous Release)

For a **continuous release**, integrate over all time $t$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$. Using the identity:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-ut)^2}{2\sigma_x^2}\right) dt = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi} \sigma_x}{u}$$

This yields the **canonical Gaussian Plume Equation** for ground-level reflection (with H = effective release height):

$$\chi(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

### Step 5: Ground-Level Concentration (z = 0)

Setting $z = 0$ and using the fact that $\exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2) = \exp(-H^2/2\sigma_z^2)$ (both terms become equal):

$$\chi(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

### Step 6: Centerline Maximum Ground-Level Concentration (y = 0)

At the plume centerline ($y = 0$) and ground level ($z = 0$):

$$\chi_{max}(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

This is the **maximum concentration at downwind distance x** along the plume centerline.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Off-Site Dose Calculation

**Problem:** A nuclear plant releases Iodine-131 at a rate $Q = 1 \times 10^{9}$ Bq/s through a 100 m stack. Wind speed $u = 5$ m/s, neutral stability (Class D), downwind distance $x = 1$ km.

**Given:** For Class D, stability at $x = 1000$ m: $\sigma_y = 35$ m, $\sigma_z = 18$ m.

**Step 1: Compute Ground-Level Centerline Concentration**

Using the Gaussian plume formula with $H = 100$ m, $y = 0$, $z = 0$:

$$\chi = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

$$\chi = \frac{1 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 5 \times 35 \times 18} \exp\left(-\frac{100^2}{2 \times 18^2}\right)$$

$$\chi = \frac{1 \times 10^9}{9895.7} \exp\left(-\frac{10000}{648}\right)$$

$$\chi = 1.01 \times 10^5 \times \exp(-15.43)$$

$$\chi = 1.01 \times 10^5 \times 1.99 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{\chi \approx 0.0201 \text{ Bq/m}^3 = 2.01 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Bq/m}^3}$$

**Step 2: Compute Inhalation Dose**

Inhalation dose rate:

$$\dot{D} = \chi \cdot B \cdot DCF$$

For I-131 (thyroid), $DCF = 2.4 \times 10^{-7}$ Sv/Bq (adult), breathing rate $B = 2.66 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s:

$$\dot{D} = 0.0201 \times 2.66 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.4 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\dot{D} = 1.28 \times 10^{-12} \text{ Sv/s}$$

**Step 3: Annual Dose (continuous exposure)**

$$D_{annual} = 1.28 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s/year}$$

$$D_{annual} = 4.04 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Sv/year} = 0.0404 \text{ mSv/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory Limit:** AERB annual dose limit for public = **1 mSv/year**. Our result is well within limits, demonstrating the importance of stack height and dispersion.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Site Factors vs. Plant Design Parameters

| **Site Factor** | **Affects Plant Design Parameter** | **Quantitative Criterion** |
|-----------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Peak Ground Acceleration | Seismic Category of building | PGA > 0.1g → Category I |
| Wind Rose (prevailing direction) | Stack orientation and height | ≥ 5 km downwind sparse population |
| PMF height | Plant grade elevation | Plant grade > PMF + 1 m freeboard |
| Tornado frequency | Tornado missile protection | Designed for F4/F5 tornado missile spectrum |
| Cooling water source | Once-through vs. cooling tower | $\Delta T_{max} = 8°C$ to $10°C$ at outfall |
| Soil bearing capacity | Foundation type (raft vs. pile) | Bearing > 100 kPa preferred |
| Population within 5 km | Emergency planning requirements | < 10,000 preferred |
| Aircraft crash | Wall thickness and orientation | Probability < 10⁻⁷/year |

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Site Evaluation Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Site Identification] --> B[Initial Site Survey]
    B --> C[Detailed Site Characterization]
    C --> D[Site Acceptance Review]
    D --> E[Construction Permit]
    E --> F[Periodic Safety Review]
    
    C1[Geological Survey] --> C
    C2[Seismotectonic Study] --> C
    C3[Meteorological Study] --> C
    C4[Hydrological Study] --> C
    C5[Demographic Study] --> C
    C6[External Hazard Analysis] --> C
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
1. **Site Identification:** Regional screening (state-level).
2. **Initial Survey:** Aerial and preliminary geological reconnaissance.
3. **Detailed Characterization:** All six pillars evaluated with 3–5 years of data.
4. **Site Acceptance Review:** AERB independent review.
5. **Construction Permit:** Final approval for reactor construction.
6. **Periodic Safety Review:** Re-evaluation every 10 years.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define the Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ) around a nuclear power plant. What is its typical radius as per AERB guidelines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ)** is the area around a nuclear power plant within which pre-planned emergency response measures, including evacuation plans, sheltering, distribution of potassium iodate (KI) tablets, and radiological monitoring, are maintained to protect the public from potential radiation exposure during a nuclear accident.

As per **AERB safety guidelines**, the EPZ has a typical radius of **16 km** from the reactor. Within this zone:
- Detailed evacuation routes are pre-identified
- Siren systems and public address systems are installed
- Hospitals and emergency shelters are designated
- Population is periodically educated on emergency response

It must be distinguished from the **Exclusion Zone (1.6 km)**, which is permanently evacuated, and the **Sterilized Zone (5 km)**, which has restricted population growth.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark radius, 1 mark distinguishing features)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**List any six site-related factors that must be evaluated before the construction of a nuclear power plant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six major site-related factors for a nuclear power plant are:

1. **Geological factors** – soil stability, bearing capacity, liquefaction potential
2. **Seismotectonic factors** – Peak Ground Acceleration, capable faults
3. **Meteorological factors** – wind speed, direction, atmospheric stability, tornadoes
4. **Hydrological factors** – flooding, tsunami, cooling water availability
5. **Population distribution** – exclusion zone, EPZ, low population zone
6. **External human-induced events** – aircraft crash, chemical plant explosion, military installations

*(3 marks: 0.5 × 6 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **seismotectonic factors** that must be considered during the site evaluation of a nuclear power plant. Include the concepts of **Design Basis Earthquake (DBE)** and **Safe Shutdown Earthquake (SSE)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A nuclear power plant releases $Q = 5 \times 10^9$ Bq/s of particulate radionuclide through a 60 m stack. The wind speed is $u = 4$ m/s in neutral (Class D) stability. At a downwind distance of $x = 800$ m, the dispersion coefficients are $\sigma_y = 28$ m and $\sigma_z = 14$ m. Calculate the **maximum ground-level centerline concentration**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Seismotectonic factors** form the most critical geological input to nuclear plant safety. The evaluation process involves:

**1. Seismic Hazard Assessment (2 marks)**

- Identification of **seismic sources** within a 300 km radius (faults, subduction zones, capable faults)
- Construction of a **seismic hazard curve** showing annual exceedance probability vs. PGA
- Use of **deterministic seismic hazard analysis (DSHA)** and **probabilistic seismic hazard analysis (PSHA)**

**2. Design Basis Earthquake (DBE) — 2 marks**

- The DBE is the maximum earthquake for which the plant is designed to operate safely
- Annual exceedance probability ≈ **10⁻⁴** (return period ~10,000 years)
- PGA typically **0.1g to 0.3g** depending on regional seismicity
- Used for **Category I** structures (containment, fuel building, control room)

**3. Safe Shutdown Earthquake (SSE) — 2 marks**

- The SSE is the maximum ground motion for which safety-critical systems (reactor trip, emergency core cooling, containment isolation) must remain functional
- The plant must achieve and maintain **safe shutdown** following an SSE
- Beyond SSE is the **BDBE (Beyond Design Basis Earthquake)**, typically 1.5× to 2× SSE

**4. Capable Faults (1 mark)**

A fault is considered "capable" if there is evidence of:
- Movement at or near the surface within the last **35,000 years** (or 500,000 years for major displacement)
- Macro-seismicity associated with the fault
- Structural relationship to other capable faults

NPPs in India must maintain a **minimum 5 km** buffer from any capable fault.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $Q = 5 \times 10^9$ Bq/s
- $H = 60$ m, $u = 4$ m/s
- $x = 800$ m, $\sigma_y = 28$ m, $\sigma_z = 14$ m
- $y = 0$ (centerline), $z = 0$ (ground level)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Apply the Gaussian Plume Ground-Level Centerline Formula**

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Substitute Numerical Values**

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{\pi \times 4 \times 28 \times 14} \exp\left(-\frac{60^2}{2 \times 14^2}\right)$$

$$\chi_{max} = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{4926.4} \exp\left(-\frac{3600}{392}\right)$$

$$\chi_{max} = 1.015 \times 10^6 \times \exp(-9.184)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Compute the Exponential Term**

$$\exp(-9.184) = 1.026 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\chi_{max} = 1.015 \times 10^6 \times 1.026 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Final Answer**

$$\boxed{\chi_{max} \approx 104.1 \text{ Bq/m}^3}$$

This is the maximum ground-level centerline concentration at 800 m downwind.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **atmospheric dispersion concept** using the **Gaussian Plume Model**. Define **Pasquill-Gifford stability classes** and explain how they affect dispersion coefficients. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **hydrological factors** that must be evaluated during nuclear power plant site selection. Explain the significance of **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** and **Probable Maximum Surge (PMS)** with reference to the **Fukushima Daiichi accident**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**1. Atmospheric Dispersion Concept (2 marks)**

Atmospheric dispersion describes the transport and dilution of radioactive effluents released to the atmosphere. The fundamental principle is:

- A pollutant released into a moving air mass is transported **downwind by advection**
- It is **diluted by turbulent diffusion** in the crosswind (y) and vertical (z) directions
- The **Gaussian Plume Model** assumes:
  - Steady-state release
  - Constant wind speed and direction
  - Normally distributed concentrations in the crosswind and vertical directions

**2. Gaussian Plume Equation (2 marks)**

$$\chi(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**3. Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes (2 marks)**

The dispersion coefficients $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ depend on atmospheric stability, classified into six classes (A–F):

| Class | Stability | Description | $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ |
|-------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| A | Very Unstable | Strong solar radiation, low wind | Largest (poor dispersion aloft, good horizontal) |
| B | Unstable | Moderate solar heating | Large |
| C | Slightly Unstable | Overcast, windy | Moderate |
| D | Neutral | Strong overcast, high wind | Moderate (default) |
| E | Stable | Clear night, light wind | Small |
| F | Very Stable | Clear night, calm | Smallest (worst dispersion — trapping) |

**4. Effect on Dispersion (1 mark)**

Under **stable (E/F)** conditions, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are small → high concentration at ground level (worst-case scenario). Under **unstable (A/B)** conditions, larger $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ → better dispersion, lower concentration. **F class is the design basis for accident analysis** (worst case).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**1. Hydrological Factors in Site Evaluation (2 marks)**

- **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** — Derived from Probable Maximum Precipitation (PMP) and basin characteristics
- **Probable Maximum Surge (PMS)** — For coastal sites, max storm surge + tide + wave setup
- **Tsunami run-up** — For coastal sites, particularly Pacific Ring of Fire locations
- **Cooling water source** — Adequate quantity, quality, and year-round availability
- **Groundwater protection** — Aquifer mapping to prevent radioactive contamination of drinking water

**2. PMF Definition and Derivation (1 mark)**

- **PMF** is the theoretically largest flood that could occur at a site
- Derived from **PMP (Probable Maximum Precipitation)** — the greatest depth of precipitation meteorologically possible
- Hershfield equation: $PMP = \bar{P} + K \cdot \sigma_P$, where $K$ is a frequency factor (15 for short durations)

**3. Fukushima Daiichi Case Study (3 marks)**

- **Location:** Coastal site in Fukushima Prefecture, Japan
- **Design basis tsunami height:** **5.7 m** (estimated for 100-year return period)
- **Actual tsunami height (March 11, 2011):** **~14 m** (1 in 10,000-year event)
- **Cause:** Magnitude 9.0 Tohoku earthquake triggered a mega-tsunami
- **Consequence:** Loss of off-site power + loss of emergency diesel generators → station blackout → core meltdown in Units 1, 2, and 3
- **Lessons learned:**
  1. Under-estimation of tsunami design basis
  2. Insufficient redundancy of safety systems against flooding
  3. Need for **hardened vents** and **core catchers** for beyond-design-basis events
  4. All coastal NPPs worldwide re-evaluated their tsunami design basis post-2011

**4. Implications for Indian Sites (1 mark)**

- Indian coastal NPPs (Kudankulam, Jaitapur, Tarapur) have undergone **post-Fukushima safety reassessment**
- Kudankulam's tsunami design height was enhanced to **~10 m** with additional **seawall protection**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points (Examiner Insights):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DBE and SSE** (–2 marks): DBE is the design earthquake; SSE is the safe shutdown earthquake. They are often the same value, but the *concepts* differ.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Gaussian Plume** (–2 marks): Students often write $\frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}$ for elevated sources (which is correct for ground-level only) and lose marks for stack releases.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up Exclusion Zone (1.6 km), Sterilized Zone (5 km), and EPZ (16 km)** (–2 marks): This is a guaranteed 2-mark trap. Memorize: 1.6 / 5 / 16.
> 
> 4. **Not mentioning AERB specifically** in the Indian context (–1 mark): Always cite **AERB** and **IAEA** regulatory frameworks in answers.
> 
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** (–1 mark): Always write units (Bq/m³, mSv/year, etc.). AERB evaluates unit correctness strictly.
> 
> 6. **Not drawing/labeling diagrams** in 7-mark questions (–1 to 2 marks): A clear **wind rose**, **exclusion zone diagram**, or **Gaussian plume sketch** can fetch 1–2 easy marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Site Related Factors**

### Core Concepts
- Site evaluation is the **first tier of Defence-in-Depth**
- Six pillars: **Geology, Seismotectonics, Meteorology, Hydrology, Demography, External Hazards**
- AERB (India) + IAEA (international) are governing bodies

### Numerical Values to Memorize
- **Exclusion Zone:** 1.6 km
- **Sterilized Zone:** 5 km
- **EPZ:** 16 km
- **DBE return period:** ~10,000 years (10⁻⁴ annual exceedance)
- **BDBE factor:** 1.5× to 2× DBE
- **Dose limit (public):** 1 mSv/year
- **Cooling water:** ~50 m³/s per 1000 MWth

### Key Equations
- **Gaussian Plume:** $\chi = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2})$
- **Ground-level centerline:** $\chi = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2})$
- **Dose rate:** $\dot{D} = \chi \cdot B \cdot DCF$

### Case Studies
- **Fukushima (2011):** Tsunami 14 m vs. design 5.7 m → core meltdown
- **Chernobyl (1986):** Site factor = poor safety culture + RBMK design flaws (not site, but operating)
- **Three Mile Island (1979):** Design/operator error (not site)

### Common Confusions Cleared
- **Exclusion Zone ≠ EPZ** (1.6 km vs. 16 km)
- **DBE ≠ SSE** (design vs. shutdown)
- **PMF ≠ 100-year flood** (theoretical max vs. statistical)
- **Pasquill F (stable) = worst dispersion** (don't confuse with Class A "unstable = better")

### AERB Documents to Reference
- **AERB/NF/SC/S** (Site Evaluation Code)
- **AERB Safety Manual on Site Evaluation**
- **IAEA SSR-1** (Site Evaluation for Nuclear Installations)

### One-Line Exam Punchlines
- "**Site dictates Safety Envelope, Safety Envelope dictates Design**"
- "**Fukushima: Design basis exceeded, not design basis adequate**"
- "**Defence-in-Depth starts with the right plot of land**"

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Safety Related Systems — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

A **Safety-Related System (SRS)** in a nuclear power plant is a system whose malfunction or failure could lead to undue radiation exposure of plant personnel or members of the public. According to **IAEA Safety Glossary (2018)** and **AERB Safety Code AERB/NPP-1/2008**, safety-related systems comprise all those structures, systems, and components (SSCs) that perform functions necessary to:

1. **Prevent** the development of an accident (i.e., controlling process variables during normal operation and anticipated operational occurrences).
2. **Limit** the consequences of an accident once it has occurred (i.e., engineered safety features actuation).
3. **Mitigate** the release of radioactive materials following an accident.
4. **Ensure** the integrity of the three physical barriers to radioactive release (fuel matrix, fuel cladding, and reactor coolant pressure boundary plus containment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Distinction:** The syllabus makes a precise distinction between **"Safety Systems"** (e.g., the Reactor Trip System, Emergency Core Cooling System, Containment Isolation System) and **"Safety-Related Systems"** (auxiliary/supporting systems such as Class-1E power, safety-grade HVAC, safety-related I&C, fire protection, and radiation monitoring). Both are governed by **Redundant, Diverse, and Independent** design philosophy.

## Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a nuclear power plant as an **airport's security architecture**:

| Airport Security | Nuclear Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Security cameras (passive monitoring) | Safety-related I&C and Radiation Monitoring |
| Primary metal detectors (first barrier) | Reactor Protection System (RPS) |
| Backup hand-held wands (redundancy) | Diverse trip parameters (redundancy) |
| Independent fire crew from police (diversity) | Diverse ECCS trains (low-head & high-head) |
| Airport fire service (post-event mitigation) | Containment Spray & Isolation Systems |
| Emergency exits (passive) | Containment structure (3rd barrier) |

Just as an airport does not depend on a **single** metal detector, a nuclear plant does not depend on a **single** sensor or **single** pump. If one fails, the others must work — this is the **Single Failure Criterion (SFC)**.

## Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reliability Targets for Safety-Related Systems (per IEEE 323 / IEC 61513):**
> - **Reactor Protection System (RPS) Unavailability:** $\leq 10^{-4}$ per demand (i.e., probability of failure on demand, PFD $\leq 10^{-4}$)
> - **Engineered Safety Features (ESF) PFD:** $\leq 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ per demand
> - **Core Damage Frequency (CDF) Target:** $< 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year
> - **Large Early Release Frequency (LERF):** $< 10^{-6}$ per reactor-year
> - **Single Failure Criterion (10 CFR 50 Appendix A / AERB):** Loss of **any single active component** must not result in loss of safety function.
> - **Diesel Generator Reliability:** Availability $\geq 0.95$ per demand.

## Hierarchy of Safety Classification

```
SAFETY-IMPORTANT SSCs
       |
       |-- SAFETY SYSTEMS (Class A — IAEA / IEEE Class 1E)
       |       |-- Reactor Trip System (RTS)
       |       |-- Engineered Safety Features (ESF)
       |       |       |-- Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS)
       |       |       |-- Containment Isolation System (CIS)
       |       |       |-- Containment Spray System (CSS)
       |       |       |-- Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) System
       |       |       |-- Auxiliary Feedwater System (AFWS)
       |       |
       |-- SAFETY-RELATED SYSTEMS (Class B/C)
       |       |-- Class 1E Electrical Power System
       |       |       |-- EDGs (Emergency Diesel Generators)
       |       |       |-- Class 1E Batteries & Inverters
       |       |       |-- Class 1E Switchgear & MCCs
       |       |
       |       |-- Safety-Related I&C
       |       |       |-- Neutron Flux Monitoring
       |       |       |-- Process Variable Sensors
       |       |       |-- Radiation Monitoring
       |       |
       |       |-- Safety-Related HVAC
       |       |       |-- Control Room Habitability
       |       |       |-- Containment Purge Isolation
       |       |
       |       |-- Fire Protection System
       |       |       |-- Detection, Suppression, Barriers
       |       |
       |-- NON-SAFETY (Balance of Plant)
              |-- Turbogenerator, Condensate, etc.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Questions frequently ask to **classify** a given system (e.g., "Classify HVAC of the control room — is it safety or safety-related?"). The correct answer is **Safety-Related** because its failure can defeat the operator's ability to mitigate an accident, even though it does not directly actuate safety functions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Foundational Design Principles of Safety-Related Systems

### 1. **Redundancy (N-out-of-M Configuration)**

Redundancy is the provision of **multiple identical (or diverse) channels, divisions, or trains** so that the safety function can still be performed if one or more components fail.

The standard configurations are:

- **1oo1** (One-out-of-One) — No redundancy, $Q_{sys} = \lambda \cdot T_{M}$
- **1oo2** (One-out-of-Two) — Both must work; *less reliable* than 1oo1 but used in fail-safe
- **2oo2** — Both must work; used where spurious trip must be avoided
- **2oo3** (Two-out-of-Three voted) — **Standard RPS architecture**, balances safety and availability
- **2oo4** — Standard 4-train ESF
- **3oo4** — High availability for systems that should not spuriously trip

### 2. **Diversity**

Diversity is the provision of **two or more redundant systems or components** to perform the same function using **different physical principles, manufacturers, software platforms, or signal processing methods**.

- **Equipment diversity:** Pumps from two different vendors.
- **Functional diversity:** Pressure, flow, and temperature trips (different parameters).
- **Signal diversity:** Analog and digital implementations of the same trip.
- **Software diversity:** Different programming teams, languages, and operating systems (DAS — Diverse Actuation System).

### 3. **Independence (Physical & Electrical)**

Independence ensures that a single event (fire, flood, earthquake, electrical fault, or operator error) cannot simultaneously disable multiple redundant divisions. Implemented through:

- **Physical separation** (3-hour fire barriers, 6 m spacing in some standards)
- **Electrical isolation** (separate power supplies, separate raceways)
- **Communication independence** (no shared data buses between divisions)
- **Functional independence** (no shared sensors, no shared final actuators)

### 4. **Separation & Segregation**

A refined form of independence following **IEEE 384** and **IEC 60780** — applies specifically to **Class 1E circuits** to ensure that a fault in one division does not propagate to another.

### 5. **Single Failure Criterion (SFC)**

Per **AERB/NPP-1 (Clause 7.3)** and **10 CFR 50 Appendix A (Criterion 21)**: A safety-related system must be capable of performing its safety function in the presence of:

- A single failure of any active component
- A single failure in the supporting systems (power, cooling, instrumentation)
- Removal of any single component from service for maintenance

**Exemptions** are allowed only for:
- Passive components with verified reliability
- Systems whose failure is **demonstrably incredible**
- Failures occurring in combination only after non-coincident events

### 6. **Defense in Depth (DiD)**

Five levels (per **IAEA INSAG-10** and **INSAG-12**):
1. **Conservative Design** (sound design, safety margins, QA)
2. **Operational Control** (limits, surveillance, training)
3. **Engineered Safety Features** (ESF, RPS)
4. **Accident Management** (severe accident guidelines, EOPs, SAMGs)
5. **Emergency Response** (off-site plans, public protection)

### 7. **Fail-Safe Design**

On loss of power, signal, or pneumatic supply, the system transitions to a **safe state** (e.g., control rods drop by gravity; ECCS valves open by depressurization of air; MSIVs close by spring force).

### 8. **Qualification**

Components must be **qualified** for the harshest expected environment (seismic, radiation, temperature, humidity) at the time of their intended safety function — per **IEEE 323** (equipment qualification) and **IEEE 344** (seismic qualification).

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Quantity | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unreliability of 1oo1 (constant hazard) | $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$ | $Q$ is probability of failure in interval $t$ |
| 2 | Unreliability of 1oo2 (both required) | $Q_{1oo2} \approx 2\lambda t$ | **Worse** than 1oo1 due to more parts |
| 3 | Unreliability of 2oo2 (both required) | $Q_{2oo2} \approx 2\lambda t$ | Same as 1oo2 by symmetry |
| 4 | Unreliability of 2oo3 (voted) | $Q_{2oo3} \approx 3(\lambda t)^{2}$ | **Dramatically lower** — voting benefits |
| 5 | Unreliability of 1oo3 | $Q_{1oo3} \approx (\lambda t)^{2}$ | Same order as 2oo3 but more sensitive |
| 6 | Mean Unavailability (1oo1, exponential) | $\bar{U}_{1oo1} = \lambda \cdot T_{surv}/2$ | $T_{surv}$ is surveillance interval |
| 7 | Beta-Factor (CCF) | $\beta = Q_{CCF} / Q_{total}$ | Typical $\beta = 0.05$ to $0.15$ |
| 8 | PFD with CCF (1oo2) | $PFD_{1oo2} = 2 \lambda_{D} t_{CE} \cdot \beta$ | $\lambda_{D}$ is dangerous failure rate |
| 9 | PFD (1oo2 perfect, no CCF) | $PFD_{1oo2} = \lambda_{D}^{2} t_{CE}^{2}$ | Quadratic — usually dominated by CCF term |
| 10 | Core Damage Frequency (CDF) | $CDF = \sum_{i} f_{i} \cdot PFD_{i}$ | Sum of accident frequencies $\times$ PFD |
| 11 | SIL (per IEC 61508) | PFD range for SIL | SIL 1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$; SIL 2: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$; SIL 3: $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$; SIL 4: $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| 12 | Availability | $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ | $A \to 1$ is best |
| 13 | Reactor Trip System Unavailability | $U_{RPS} \le 10^{-4}$ per demand | KTU mandatory benchmark |
| 14 | ECCS PFD | $PFD_{ECCS} \le 10^{-3}$ per demand | Single-train; system PFD tighter |
| 15 | Seismic Design Basis | $0.3g$ PGA (NPP typical, AERB) | Operating Basis Earthquake (OBE) |
| 16 | Safe Shutdown Earthquake | $0.6g$ PGA (typical) | SSE — structures must remain functional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the four standard KTU redundancy equations (1-4) and the Beta Factor model (7-8).** Past KTU papers consistently ask for derivations and numerical estimates of system unavailability.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Safety-related system design philosophy directly governs:

1. **Reactor core protection** during loss-of-coolant, station blackout, and anticipated transients.
2. **Public dose minimization** following severe accidents (Fukushima lessons: multi-unit, multi-hazard).
3. **Regulatory licensing** (AERB in India, USNRC in the USA, ASN in France) — without demonstrated compliance with SFC, no operating licence is granted.
4. **Post-Fukushima safety upgrades** — Diverse and Flexible Coping Strategies (FLEX), Severe Accident Management Guidelines (SAMGs), hardened vent for Mark I containments.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Unavailability of a Single Channel (1oo1) Under Surveillance Test

Consider a single component that is checked every $T_{surv}$ hours. Between two tests, the component is in service for the full interval. We assume a constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda$ and that failures are not self-announcing.

**Step 1 — Probability of failure in an infinitesimal interval $\Delta t$:**

For an exponential distribution, the failure probability density is $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 2 — Average unavailability over the test interval $T$:**

For a component failing uniformly within the test interval, the average unavailability (fraction of time the component is failed) is obtained by integrating from 0 to $T$:

$$
\bar{U}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \lambda \tau \, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\bar{U}_{1oo1} = \frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{\lambda T^{2}}{2} = \frac{\lambda T}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $T \equiv T_{surv}$:**

$$
\boxed{\bar{U}_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda_{D} \cdot T_{surv}}{2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **average fraction of time** the single component is failed but undetected. If the component is tested more frequently (smaller $T_{surv}$), unavailability drops linearly.

## Derivation 2 — Unavailability of a 1oo2 System (Both Channels Required, with Common-Cause Failure)

For a 1oo2 redundant pair (both must work to satisfy safety function), we have three failure modes:
- Channel A fails
- Channel B fails
- Both fail simultaneously due to **Common-Cause Failure (CCF)**

**Step 1 — Probability of A failing alone (independent):**

By the 1oo1 result: $U_{A} = \lambda T / 2$

**Step 2 — Probability of B failing alone (independent):**

By symmetry: $U_{B} = \lambda T / 2$

**Step 3 — Add the independent failure modes:**

$$
U_{indep} = U_{A} + U_{B} = \lambda T
$$

(The probability both fail independently is $(\lambda T)^{2}/4$, a higher-order term we ignore.)

**Step 4 — Introduce the Beta Factor for Common-Cause Failure:**

The CCF component is modeled as a fraction $\beta$ of the single-component unavailability:

$$
U_{CCF} = \beta \cdot U_{1oo1} = \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda T}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Total 1oo2 unavailability:**

$$
\boxed{\bar{U}_{1oo2} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \lambda T + \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda T}{2} \approx (1 - 0.5\beta) \lambda T}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** If $\beta = 0.1$ (typical for well-separated redundant trains), then $\bar{U}_{1oo2} \approx 0.95 \lambda T$ — **only marginally better than 1oo1.** This is why **independence** (physical, electrical, and functional) is non-negotiable: it directly reduces $\beta$.

## Derivation 3 — Unavailability of a 2oo3 Voted System (with CCF)

A 2oo3 system fails the safety function if **two or more** of the three channels are in failed state. Failure occurs if:
- Two channels fail (any pair) independently, OR
- All three fail due to CCF

**Step 1 — Number of ways to pick 2 failed out of 3 channels:**

There are $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ ways.

**Step 2 — Probability of any specific pair failing independently:**

Each pair fails with probability $(\lambda T / 2)^{2}/...$, simplifying via the mean unavailability:

$$
P(\text{pair fails}) = \left(\frac{\lambda T}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Total independent unavailability:**

$$
U_{indep, 2oo3} = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda T}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{3 (\lambda T)^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the CCF contribution:**

$$
U_{CCF, 2oo3} = \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda T}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Total 2oo3 unavailability:**

$$
\boxed{\bar{U}_{2oo3} = \frac{3 (\lambda T)^{2}}{4} + \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda T}{2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** For small $\lambda T$ (e.g., $\lambda T = 10^{-2}$), the $(\lambda T)^{2}$ term is $10^{-4}$, while the CCF term is $\sim 5 \times 10^{-4}$. **CCF dominates** — hence 2oo3 gains are limited by $\beta$, motivating diverse trip parameters.

## Derivation 4 — Core Damage Frequency (CDF) Contribution from a Single Accident Sequence

Per **NUREG-1855** and **ASME/ANS RA-Sa-2009**:

**Step 1 — Define initiating event frequency:**

Let $f_{IE}$ be the frequency of the initiating event (e.g., Large LOCA, Medium LOCA, Small LOCA, Steam Line Break, etc.), in units of events/reactor-year.

**Step 2 — Define conditional probability of safety-system failure:**

Let $PFD$ be the probability of failure on demand of the mitigating safety system.

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$
CDF_{sequence} = f_{IE} \times PFD
$$

**Step 4 — Sum over all accident sequences:**

$$
\boxed{CDF_{plant} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_{i} \cdot PFD_{i}}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Typical):**

Suppose:
- Large LOCA: $f_{1} = 10^{-6}$ /rx-yr; ECCS PFD = $10^{-3}$
- Medium LOCA: $f_{2} = 10^{-5}$ /rx-yr; ECCS PFD = $5 \times 10^{-4}$
- Small LOCA: $f_{3} = 10^{-3}$ /rx-yr; AFW PFD = $10^{-2}$

$$
CDF = (10^{-6})(10^{-3}) + (10^{-5})(5 \times 10^{-4}) + (10^{-3})(10^{-2}) = 10^{-9} + 5 \times 10^{-9} + 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\boxed{CDF \approx 1.0006 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per reactor-year}}
$$

This satisfies the KTU benchmark of CDF $< 10^{-5}$ per rx-yr, but the small-LOCA sequence dominates.

## Derivation 5 — Beta Factor Model Origin (Brief)

For a redundant pair, the total failure rate $\lambda$ is split into:

$$
\lambda = \lambda_{indep} + \lambda_{CCF}
$$

where the CCF portion represents correlated failures. The Beta factor is defined as:

$$
\boxed{\beta = \frac{\lambda_{CCF}}{\lambda_{total}}}
$$

Empirically, $\beta \in [0.01, 0.30]$; AERB/IEEE 500 recommend using $\beta = 0.1$ as a screening value when plant-specific data is unavailable.

## Analytical Comparison Matrix — Redundancy Architectures

| Configuration | Unavailability $\bar{U}$ | Spurious Trip Rate $\bar{S}$ | Use Case (Nuclear) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $\lambda T/2$ | $\lambda T/2$ | Non-safety, e.g., feedwater regulation |
| 1oo2 (both must work) | $\approx (1 - 0.5\beta)\lambda T$ | $\lambda T$ | ECCS train actuation (fail-safe) |
| 2oo2 (both must work) | $\approx (1 - 0.5\beta)\lambda T$ | $\lambda T$ | Where spurious trip is acceptable |
| 2oo3 (voted) | $\frac{3(\lambda T)^{2}}{4} + \beta \lambda T / 2$ | $3 \lambda T$ | **Reactor Trip System** (Gold standard) |
| 2oo4 (voted) | $\frac{6(\lambda T)^{2}}{4} + 2 \beta \lambda T / 2$ | $4 \lambda T$ | **ESF trains (Low-head ECCS)** |
| 3oo4 (voted) | $(\lambda T)^{2}$ | $4 \lambda T$ | High availability — control rod drives |

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Define the term **"Safety-Related System"** as used in nuclear power plant design. How is it distinguished from a **"Safety System"**? Give **two** examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **Safety-Related System (SRS)** is a system whose malfunction or failure could lead to undue radiation exposure of plant personnel or the public. It includes all structures, systems, and components (SSCs) that are needed for the prevention, control, or mitigation of accidents and for ensuring the integrity of barriers to radioactive release, but which are not part of the primary safety systems.

**Distinction:**

| Aspect | Safety System | Safety-Related System |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Directly performs a safety action (e.g., SCRAM, ECCS injection) | Supports safety systems (e.g., provides power, cooling, monitoring) |
| Examples | Reactor Trip System, ECCS, Containment Isolation | Class 1E Electrical Power, Safety-Related HVAC, Fire Protection, Radiation Monitoring |

**Examples:**

- **Safety Systems:** (i) Reactor Trip System (RTS) with 2oo3 logic, (ii) Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) with 4-train redundancy.
- **Safety-Related Systems:** (i) Emergency Diesel Generators (Class 1E), (ii) Safety-Grade Control Room HVAC.

**[3 Marks: 1.5 for definition + 1 for distinction + 0.5 for examples]**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
State and explain the **Single Failure Criterion (SFC)** as applied to safety-related systems in a nuclear power plant. Mention **two** exemptions permitted under this criterion.

**Model Answer:**

The **Single Failure Criterion (SFC)** states that a safety-related system must be capable of performing its intended safety function in the presence of:
1. A single failure of any **active** component in the system.
2. A single failure of any **active** component in the supporting auxiliary systems.
3. The **removal** of any single component from service for maintenance or testing.

**Exemptions** (per AERB/NPP-1 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix A, Criterion 21):
1. **Passive components** with demonstrated reliability (e.g., piping, check valves, pressure vessels) that fail only by gross rupture.
2. Components whose failure modes have been shown to be **incredible** (e.g., the reactor vessel itself).
3. Failure combinations that require **non-coincident** independent events.

**[3 Marks: 2 for criterion + 1 for exemptions]**

---

## Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **design principles** of safety-related systems in a nuclear power plant. Discuss **Redundancy, Diversity, and Independence** in detail, with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A safety-related system consists of a **2-out-of-3 voted** logic. Each channel has a dangerous failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The surveillance test interval is $T_{surv} = 2000$ hours. The beta factor for common-cause failure is $\beta = 0.1$. Compute:
   (i) The independent unavailability of the 2oo3 system.
   (ii) The CCF contribution to unavailability.
   (iii) The total system unavailability. Comment on whether the system meets the KTU benchmark of $U \le 10^{-4}$ per demand. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Design Principles [7 Marks]

The design of safety-related systems in a nuclear power plant is governed by the **defense-in-depth** philosophy and the requirement to maintain **three physical barriers** (fuel matrix, cladding, reactor coolant boundary, and containment). The key principles are:

**1. Redundancy** [2 Marks]

Redundancy means providing **multiple, independent, and identical (or functionally equivalent) components** such that the safety function continues even if one fails. Common configurations include:
- **1oo2 (one-out-of-two):** Both channels must work; used where spurious actuation is acceptable but component failure cannot be tolerated (e.g., ECCS pump trains).
- **2oo3 (two-out-of-three voted):** Two of three channels must vote for trip; this is the **standard RPS architecture** because it balances high reliability against spurious trips.

For example, in a 2oo3 RPS, if any one channel fails, the system still has two functioning channels, satisfying the Single Failure Criterion.

**2. Diversity** [2 Marks]

Diversity is the use of **different physical principles, vendors, software, or signal processing methods** to defeat common-mode failures. Types include:
- **Equipment diversity** (different manufacturers)
- **Functional diversity** (different measured parameters — e.g., pressure AND flow)
- **Software diversity** (different programming teams, languages)

Example: The post-Fukushima **Diverse Actuation System (DAS)** uses independent sensors, hard-wired logic, and FPGA-based voting to provide a backup to the digital RPS.

**3. Independence (and Separation)** [2 Marks]

Independence ensures that a single event (fire, flood, electrical fault, or operator error) cannot disable more than one redundant division. Implemented through:
- **Physical separation** (3-hour fire-rated barriers per IEEE 384)
- **Electrical isolation** (separate power supplies, separate raceways, separate I&C cabinets)
- **Communication independence** (no shared data bus between divisions)

Example: In a 4-train ECCS, each train has a **dedicated** pump, motor, power supply from a **separate** Class 1E bus, and a **dedicated** diesel generator.

**4. Fail-Safe Design** [1 Mark]

On loss of power, signal, or pneumatic supply, the system transitions to the safe state. Control rods insert by gravity; ECCS valves open by spring force; MSIVs close by spring force.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour
- $T_{surv} = 2000$ hours
- $\beta = 0.1$
- Configuration: 2oo3 voted

**Step 1 — Compute the single-channel unavailability** (1oo1 result):

$$
\bar{U}_{1oo1} = \frac{\lambda T_{surv}}{2} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(2000)}{2} = \frac{10^{-2}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute independent 2oo3 unavailability (equation from Derivation 3):**

$$
U_{indep, 2oo3} = \frac{3 (\lambda T)^{2}}{4} = \frac{3 \times (10^{-2})^{2}}{4} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute CCF contribution:**

$$
U_{CCF, 2oo3} = \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda T}{2} = 0.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Total 2oo3 unavailability:**

$$
\bar{U}_{2oo3} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-4} = 5.75 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to KTU benchmark:**

$$
\bar{U}_{2oo3} = 5.75 \times 10^{-4} > 10^{-4} \text{ benchmark}
$$

**Conclusion:** The system **does NOT meet** the KTU benchmark of $U \le 10^{-4}$ per demand. The CCF component is the dominant contributor. To reduce $U$, the plant should either:
- Reduce $T_{surv}$ (e.g., from 2000 to 500 hours — reduces $\lambda T$ by 4x)
- Increase physical separation to reduce $\beta$ (e.g., $\beta = 0.02$)
- Use a **diverse** backup system (DAS)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Engineered Safety Features (ESF)** of a Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR). Describe the function of the **Containment Spray System (CSS)** and the **Containment Isolation System (CIS)** with their actuation logic. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A nuclear power plant has the following initiating event frequencies and associated safety system unreliabilities:

| Initiating Event | Frequency $f_i$ (per rx-yr) | PFD of Safety System |
|---|---|---|
| Large LOCA | $5 \times 10^{-7}$ | $10^{-3}$ |
| Medium LOCA | $10^{-5}$ | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Small LOCA | $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-2}$ |
| Transient with AFW | $10^{-1}$ | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ |

Compute the **Core Damage Frequency (CDF)**. Comment on whether the plant meets the AERB safety goal of CDF $< 10^{-5}$ per reactor-year. If the dominant sequence is identified, suggest **two** engineering improvements. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Engineered Safety Features [7 Marks]

**Engineered Safety Features (ESF)** are those systems specifically designed to **limit the consequences of accidents** (mainly LOCAs and main-steam-line breaks) once the initiating event has occurred. They are actuated automatically by the ESF Actuation System upon a coincident signal from the RPS-grade sensors.

**Major ESF in a PWR** [2 Marks]:

1. Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) — 4 trains (2 LPCI + 1 HPCI + 1 accumulator)
2. Containment Isolation System (CIS) — closes all containment penetrations
3. Containment Spray System (CSS) — sprays borated water for pressure suppression and iodine scrubbing
4. Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) System — closes MSIVs on steam-line break
5. Auxiliary Feedwater System (AFWS) — provides secondary-side cooling on loss of MFW

**Containment Spray System (CSS)** [2.5 Marks]:

- **Function:** (i) Reduce **containment pressure** following a LOCA by condensing steam; (ii) **Scrub** radioactive iodine and particulates from containment atmosphere.
- **Components:** 2 to 4 independent spray trains with dedicated pumps, headers, nozzles in the upper containment region, and a NaOH additive tank for iodine retention.
- **Actuation:** Automatic on **High-High Containment Pressure** (typically 0.2 bar above setpoint) AND **Low Pressurizer Pressure** coincidence, or manual from the control room.

**Containment Isolation System (CIS)** [2.5 Marks]:

- **Function:** Close all fluid and electrical lines penetrating the containment boundary within a few seconds of a LOCA, to prevent release of fission products.
- **Components:** 2oo4 logic, isolation valves on every penetration (HVAC, sampling, instrument lines, purge).
- **Actuation:** Automatic on **Low Pressurizer Pressure** OR **High Containment Pressure** OR **High-High Containment Radiation** (ESF-coincident logic).

#### Part (b) — Core Damage Frequency [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute CDF per sequence using $CDF_{i} = f_{i} \times PFD_{i}$:**

**Sequence 1 — Large LOCA:**

$$
CDF_{1} = (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (10^{-3}) = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ /rx-yr}
$$

**Sequence 2 — Medium LOCA:**

$$
CDF_{2} = (10^{-5}) \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ /rx-yr}
$$

**Sequence 3 — Small LOCA:**

$$
CDF_{3} = (10^{-3}) \times (10^{-2}) = 10^{-5} \text{ /rx-yr}
$$

**Sequence 4 — Transient with AFW:**

$$
CDF_{4} = (10^{-1}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /rx-yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Total CDF:**

$$
CDF_{plant} = 5 \times 10^{-10} + 5 \times 10^{-9} + 10^{-5} + 5 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
CDF_{plant} \approx 1.5005 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /rx-yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to AERB Safety Goal:**

$$
CDF = 1.5005 \times 10^{-5} > 10^{-5} \text{ AERB goal}
$$

**The plant DOES NOT meet the AERB safety goal.**

**Step 4 — Dominant Sequence Identification:**

The **Small LOCA sequence** dominates with $CDF_{3} = 10^{-5}$, contributing ~67% of total CDF.

**Step 5 — Engineering Improvements** [2 Marks]:

1. **Reduce the initiating event frequency of Small LOCA** through enhanced pre-service and in-service inspection (ISI) of small-bore piping, application of leak-before-break (LBB) criteria, and use of high-reliability bellows seals.
2. **Reduce AFW PFD** by adding a **passive** secondary-side makeup path (e.g., isolation condensers or passive residual heat removal), reducing human error dependency and adding diversity to the AFW trains.

Other possible improvements: improve operator training, install Diverse Actuation System (DAS), reduce common-cause failure beta factor via additional physical separation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Safety" vs "Safety-Related":** Students frequently misclassify the Control Room HVAC as a "safety system" — it is **safety-related** because its failure impairs operator mitigation. Each misclassification costs ~0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the Beta Factor:** In any unavailability derivation, omitting the CCF term $\beta \lambda T/2$ is treated as a **conceptual error** in 13-mark questions. Marks are reserved for explicitly identifying and quantifying CCF.
> 3. **Numerical Unit Errors:** Always state the result with **correct units** — e.g., unavailability is **dimensionless** (per demand), CDF is **per reactor-year**, $\lambda$ is **per hour**. Mixing units of time (hours vs years) without conversion is a frequent valuation penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting the SFC Maintenance Case:** The criterion includes the **removal from service** condition in addition to active failure. Examiners specifically test this nuance for 1 to 2 marks.
> 5. **In the 2oo3 derivation, students often forget that the CCF term can DOMINATE the result** for small $\lambda T$. Always compute both terms separately before summing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3):**

- **Safety-Related System Definition:** Any SSC whose failure could lead to undue radiation exposure — INCLUDES safety systems AND their support systems.
- **Four Pillars of SRS Design:** **Redundancy, Diversity, Independence, Separation** (with **Fail-Safe** as the fifth essential principle).
- **Single Failure Criterion (SFC):** System must survive loss of any SINGLE active component OR removal of any single component from service. Exemptions: passive components, incredible failures, non-coincident events.
- **Defense in Depth (DiD):** 5 levels — Conservative Design, Operational Control, Engineered Safety Features, Accident Management, Emergency Response.
- **Redundancy Voting Configurations:** **2oo3 for RPS**, **2oo4 for ECCS trains**, **1oo2 for fail-safe ECCS actuation**, **3oo4 for high-availability systems**.
- **Unavailability Formula 1oo1:** $U = \lambda T_{surv}/2$
- **Unavailability Formula 2oo3:** $U = 3(\lambda T)^{2}/4 + \beta \lambda T/2$
- **Beta Factor Model:** $\beta$ quantifies CCF; typical $\beta = 0.1$ (screening value).
- **CDF Computation:** $CDF = \sum f_i \cdot PFD_i$; target $< 10^{-5}$ /rx-yr.
- **LERF Target:** $< 10^{-6}$ /rx-yr.
- **ESF Components (PWR):** ECCS, Containment Isolation, Containment Spray, MSIV, AFWS.
- **Class 1E Power:** Includes EDGs, Class 1E batteries, switchgear — these are SAFETY-RELATED (not safety systems).
- **Fail-Safe Examples:** SCRAM (gravity drop), ECCS valves (spring open on air failure), MSIVs (spring close on air failure).
- **Equipment Qualification Standards:** IEEE 323 (EQ), IEEE 344 (seismic), IEEE 384 (separation), IEC 61513 (overall I&C), IEC 60780 (qualification).
- **Post-Fukushima Lessons:** Hardened vents, FLEX strategies, SAMGs, severe accident-resistant containments, multi-unit event consideration.
- **AERB Safety Goal:** CDF $< 10^{-5}$/rx-yr, LERF $< 10^{-6}$/rx-yr (AERB/NPP-1).
- **Three Physical Barriers:** Fuel matrix, fuel cladding, reactor coolant pressure boundary — and the **containment** as the 4th (engineered) barrier.
- **Quality Assurance (QA):** Per AERB/SS-1 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix B — mandatory for ALL safety-related SSCs, from design through decommissioning.

> **Master these 16 points and the formulas in SECTION 2 — they form the backbone of 90% of KTU Module 3 questions.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transport Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Transport Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Heat Transport System (HTS)** in a nuclear power plant is the engineered network of components, piping, pumps, valves, and heat exchangers responsible for the controlled transfer of thermal energy generated in the reactor core to the steam cycle (or process heat user) while maintaining strict radiological, thermal-hydraulic, and pressure-boundary safety conditions. According to KTU 2024 PESFT417 syllabus, HTS encompasses the **primary coolant system**, the **secondary (and tertiary, where applicable) coolant system**, and the **intermediate cooling loops** that decouple the radioactive primary fluid from the external environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The HTS is the *physical barrier* between the radioactive core inventory and the public. Its integrity directly satisfies the **defence-in-depth principle (Level 3)** mandated by AERB safety codes and forms the engineering backbone of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB).

## 1.2 Classification of Heat Transport Loops

| Loop Type | Description | Example Reactor |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Cycle** | Primary coolant boils in the core; steam directly drives turbine | BWR (Boiling Water Reactor) |
| **Indirect Cycle** | Primary loop transfers heat through SG to a separate secondary fluid | PWR, PHWR |
| **Dual / Triple Cycle** | Intermediate loop(s) inserted for additional isolation | HTGR, MSR, some naval reactors |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Pressure-Cooker" Analogy

Think of the HTS as a **giant, multi-loop pressure cooker network**:

- The **reactor core** is the *stove burner* producing intense heat (≈ **300 °C** to **325 °C** in PWRs).
- The **primary coolant** is the *water sealed inside the cooker* — it never leaves the pot, it just circulates, picks up heat, and returns.
- The **steam generator (SG)** is the *smaller inner vessel* where the primary water's heat boils a *separate, clean* batch of water.
- The **secondary loop** is the *steam rising out* that goes to spin the turbine, then condenses back to water.
