# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fire Load Density (FLD) of a Fire Zone

The **Fire Load Density** ($q$) is the most important parameter in Fire Hazard Analysis. It quantifies the combustible energy per unit floor area and determines the **required fire-resistance rating** of barriers.

### Step 1: Define the fire load of a single combustible item

For a single item $i$ of mass $M_i$ (kg) and calorific value $H_i$ (MJ/kg):

$$Q_i = M_i \cdot H_i \quad [\text{MJ}]$$

where $H_i$ is the **heat of combustion** of the material (e.g., for cable insulation: $H \approx 25\ \text{MJ/kg}$; for diesel: $H \approx 43\ \text{MJ/kg}$).

### Step 2: Sum over all combustibles in the fire zone

The total fire load of the zone is:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} M_i \cdot H_i \quad [\text{MJ}]$$

### Step 3: Divide by the floor area of the fire zone

The fire load density is:

$$q = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{floor}}} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{m}^2}\right]$$

### Step 4: Interpretation & Classification

The fire resistance rating of the compartment is chosen based on $q$ (per **NFPA / Indian Standard IS 1644**):

$$\begin{aligned}
q < 1000\ \text{MJ/m}^2 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{1-hour fire-rated compartment} \\
1000 \leq q < 2000\ \text{MJ/m}^2 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{2-hour fire-rated compartment} \\
q \geq 2000\ \text{MJ/m}^2 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \text{3-hour fire-rated compartment}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert all masses to the same units (kg) and all calorific values to the same units (MJ/kg) before summing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Fire Resistance (EFR) / Severity

The **equivalent fire severity** is the time under a standard fire (ISO 834 curve) that produces the same thermal insult as the actual fire.

### Step 1: Standard Fire Time–Temperature Curve (ISO 834)

The standard fire temperature is:

$$T(t) = T_0 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)$$

where $t$ is in minutes and $T$ is in °C. This is the **canonical fire** used to rate barriers.

### Step 2: Equivalent Fire Duration

The severity of an actual fire of duration $t_{\text{real}}$ is mapped to an equivalent standard-fire duration $t_{\text{eq}}$ via the **area under the curve** above ambient $T_0$:

$$E = \int_{0}^{t_{\text{real}}} \bigl(T(\tau) - T_0\bigr)\, d\tau \equiv \int_{0}^{t_{\text{eq}}} \bigl(T_{\text{std}}(\tau) - T_0\bigr)\, d\tau$$

Solving for $t_{\text{eq}}$:

$$t_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{t_{\text{real}}} (T(\tau) - T_0)\, d\tau}{\text{mean}\,(T_{\text{std}} - T_0)}$$

### Step 3: Acceptance Criterion

A barrier of rating $R$ is acceptable if:

$$R \geq t_{\text{eq}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Fire-Induced Core Damage

From PSA (Probabilistic Safety Assessment) Level 1+ internal fire:

$$P_{\text{CDF, fire}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_z}\ \sum_{j=1}^{N_{s,i}} \lambda_i \cdot p_{i,j} \cdot \text{CCDF}_{i,j}$$

where:

- $\lambda_i$ = frequency of fire ignition in zone $i$ (per year)
- $p_{i,j}$ = conditional probability of suppression failure for scenario $j$ in zone $i$
- $\text{CCDF}_{i,j}$ = conditional core damage frequency given that suppression failed
- $N_z$ = number of fire zones
- $N_{s,i}$ = number of fire scenarios in zone $i$

**Acceptance criterion:**

$$P_{\text{CDF, fire}} \leq 10^{-6}\ \text{per reactor-year}$$

This is a **hard regulatory limit** in most jurisdictions (NRC, AERB, ASN).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A switchgear room contains:
> - 800 kg of PVC cable insulation ($H = 25\ \text{MJ/kg}$)
> - 200 kg of transformer oil ($H = 43\ \text{MJ/kg}$)
> - 150 kg of paper/wooden panels ($H = 17\ \text{MJ/kg}$)
>
> Floor area = 60 m². Determine: (i) Total fire load, (ii) Fire load density, (iii) Required fire-resistance rating.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Total fire load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{PVC}} &= 800 \times 25 = 20{,}000\ \text{MJ} \\
Q_{\text{oil}} &= 200 \times 43 = 8{,}600\ \text{MJ} \\
Q_{\text{wood}} &= 150 \times 17 = 2{,}550\ \text{MJ} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 20{,}000 + 8{,}600 + 2{,}550 = 31{,}150\ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Fire load density:**

$$q = \dfrac{31{,}150}{60} = 518.9\ \text{MJ/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Required fire rating:**

Since $q < 1000\ \text{MJ/m}^2$:

$$\boxed{R = 1\ \text{hour fire-rated compartment}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to students:** This is a typical **3-mark KTU Part-A** derivation; practice it with varied combinations (oil, cable, plastic).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **NOT** confuse **fire protection with fire prevention** — they are different layers (L1 vs L2–L5).
> 2. Always state the **AERB/IAEA reference** (e.g., AERB/NPP-04, IAEA NS-G-1.7) for full credit on 14-mark answers.
> 3. In numerical problems, **convert units consistently** before plugging into the fire load density formula.
> 4. Many students write "three-hour fire barrier" without explaining the **3-hour envelope** concept — this loses up to 3 marks.
> 5. Always mention **defense-in-depth** explicitly when describing any FPS subsystem.

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Fire Protection System in a nuclear power plant. State its three primary objectives.**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fire Protection System (FPS)** in a nuclear power plant is the integrated engineered and administrative arrangement that prevents, detects, controls, suppresses, and confines fires to ensure the reactor can be safely shut down and held in a safe-shutdown state without exceeding authorised radiation release limits (AERB/NPP-04).

**Three primary objectives (in priority order):**

1. **Prevent** fires from starting — by controlling ignition sources and combustibles (Level 1 of DiD).
2. **Ensure that safe shutdown capability is preserved** under any postulated fire scenario, satisfying the **single-failure criterion** simultaneously with the fire event.
3. **Limit radioactive release** to the public and to the environment to within authorised limits, by confining the fire to its zone of origin.

*(Answer length ≈ 1 page. 3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for objectives + 1 for AERB reference.)*

---

### **Q2. State the Five Levels of Defense-in-Depth for fire protection in an NPP.**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IAEA INSAG-10** and **AERB/NPP-04**, the Five Levels of Defense-in-Depth for fire are:

| Level | Function |
|---|---|
| **L1** | **Prevention** of fire (ignition control, hot-work permits, transient combustible control) |
| **L2** | **Detection** (smoke, heat, flame, VESDA sensors with FACP annunciation) |
| **L3** | **Suppression** (automatic sprinklers, water spray, clean agent gas, manual hose) |
| **L4** | **Confinement** (fire-rated walls, doors, penetration seals, fire dampers) |
| **L5** | **Post-fire safe shutdown** and emergency response (alternative shutdown path, fire brigade) |

This DiD approach ensures that **failure of one level is compensated by the next**, providing the **risk-informed robustness** required by KTU/IAEA safety standards.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following:

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

#### (a) Describe in detail the Fire Detection and Fire Suppression subsystems used in a nuclear power plant. Discuss the criteria for selection of suppression agents in safety-significant areas. *(7 marks)*

#### (b) What is the Fire Hazard Analysis (FHA)? With a neat schematic, explain how FHA is performed and how its results feed into the Post-Fire Safe Shutdown Analysis (PFSSA). *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(a) — Detection & Suppression Subsystems

**1. Fire Detection Subsystem**

The fire detection subsystem is a **continuously monitored, fail-safe, seismically qualified** system comprising:

- **Sensor loops** of multiple technologies (ionization smoke, photoelectric smoke, heat detectors, flame detectors, VESDA).
- Each loop terminates at the **Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP)**, which is **powered from two independent Class A and Class B power supplies** and is seismically and environmentally qualified.
- The FACP must **annunciate locally and to the control room**, and **automatically notify the plant fire brigade**.
- **Two independent detection channels** are required for any safety-significant area; **single failure of one channel shall not disable detection**.
- **Spacing rules** (NFPA 72): smoke detectors spaced ≤ 9 m on smooth ceilings, reduced to 6.5 m where air movement exists.

**2. Fire Suppression Subsystem**

Suppression is implemented at three levels:

- **Automatic water-based systems:** Wet-pipe sprinklers for general areas; **deluge systems** for high-hazard zones (transformer yards, lube-oil rooms); water-mist for electronics.
- **Automatic clean-agent systems:** **Inergen, FM-200, or Novec 1230** in control rooms, computer rooms, and I&C cabinets — these have replaced Halon (banned under the Montreal Protocol).
- **Manual systems:** Hydrants, hose reels, portable extinguishers (CO₂, dry powder).

**3. Criteria for Selection of Suppression Agent**

The choice is determined by:

$$\text{Agent} = f\bigl(\text{Fire Class},\ \text{Equipment Damage Risk},\ \text{Toxicity},\ \text{Radiological},\ \text{Occupancy}\bigr)$$

- **Water sprinkler:** cheapest, suited for Class A fires but **must NOT be used in control rooms or near I&C** (water damage risk).
- **CO₂:** effective on Class B/C but **fatal to humans** — not used in normally occupied areas.
- **Inergen / FM-200 / Novec 1230:** clean, non-conductive, **safe for occupied areas** at design concentration.
- **Foam:** used in oil-storage areas, transformer bays.

**4. Independence & Power Supplies**

- Fire pumps are powered from **two separate Class A and Class B buses**.
- The **fire water storage tank** must hold $\geq$ 2 hours of fire pump capacity (typically 2 × 100% diesel-driven + 1 electric).
- Suppression systems are designed to satisfy the **Single-Failure Criterion + one fire simultaneously** (AERB/NPP-04 §6).

*(Word count target ≈ 350–400. 7 marks: 2 for detection description + 2 for suppression description + 2 for selection criteria + 1 for SFC compliance.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Fire Hazard Analysis (FHA) & PFSSA Linkage

**1. Definition**

A **Fire Hazard Analysis (FHA)** is a systematic, deterministic, and risk-informed analysis that:

- Identifies **all credible fire scenarios** in each plant fire zone,
- Estimates the **frequency of ignition** ($\lambda_i$),
- Quantifies the **damage to safety equipment** in each scenario,
- Identifies the **combinations of fire + equipment damage** that could challenge safe shutdown,
- Recommends **preventive and mitigative measures** (barriers, separation, suppression).

**2. Steps of FHA**

1. **Identify fire zones** (compartments with 1-hr, 2-hr, or 3-hr boundaries).
2. **Inventory combustibles** (cables, oil, transient, structural) and compute **Fire Load Density** $q$.
3. **Identify safety equipment** in each zone.
4. **For each postulated fire scenario**, evaluate:
   - Probability of ignition
   - Probability of detection
   - Probability of suppression (success/failure)
   - Resulting damage to redundant trains
5. **Determine the impact on safe-shutdown capability** — i.e., whether **one train survives** in each scenario.

**3. PFSSA — Post-Fire Safe Shutdown Analysis**

The **PFSSA** is performed for each scenario flagged in the FHA, with the goal of demonstrating that:

- At least **one complete train** of safe-shutdown equipment (Train A or Train B) **remains free of fire damage**, AND
- The operator can perform **safe shutdown from either the main control room or the alternate shutdown station**, AND
- The **operator actions required are limited** to a small set of pre-defined, validated manual actions (no more than 3–5 critical actions).

**4. FHA → PFSSA Linkage (Schematic)**

```
  Fire Zone Inventory → Combustible Inventory → Fire Load Density
              ↓
  Safety Equipment Mapping → Scenario Generation
              ↓
  Deterministic Screening (High/Medium/Low)
              ↓
  Risk-Informed Significance Determination (PSA)
              ↓
  Required Safe-Shutdown Path → PFSSA Validation
              ↓
  Regulatory Compliance with 10 CFR 50 App. R / AERB/NPP-04
```

**5. Acceptance Criteria**

- For every **high-significance** scenario, **one train of safe shutdown must be demonstrably free of fire damage**.
- For **multiple train failures** in a single scenario, **3-hour fire-rated barriers** or **6 m horizontal separation** must be provided.

*(Word count target ≈ 350–400. 7 marks: 2 for FHA definition + 2 for steps + 2 for PFSSA linkage + 1 for acceptance criteria.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

#### (a) Explain the concept of "defense-in-depth" as applied to fire protection in nuclear power plants. Describe each of the five levels with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

#### (b) A switchgear room has the following combustible inventory:
- 1200 kg of XLPE cable insulation ($H = 30$ MJ/kg),
- 300 kg of lubricating oil ($H = 44$ MJ/kg),
- 100 kg of PVC panels ($H = 25$ MJ/kg).

The floor area is 80 m². Compute: (i) the total fire load, (ii) the fire load density, and (iii) the required fire-resistance rating of the compartment. State the AERB reference for fire load density classification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(a) — Defense-in-Depth for Fire

The **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** concept, codified in **IAEA INSAG-10** and adopted by AERB, provides **multiple, independent, and overlapping** layers of safety so that failure of one layer does not compromise the safety function. For fire protection, five layers apply:

**Level 1 — Prevention of Fire Initiation**

The first and most effective layer. Examples:
- **Hot-work permits** for welding, cutting, grinding.
- **Transient combustible control** (no storage of cardboard, plastic, rags in safety areas).
- **Equipment qualification** to prevent electrical faults.
- **Cable management discipline** (segregation of power and I&C cables).

**Level 2 — Detection**

The second layer — rapid identification. Examples:
- Smoke and heat detectors, flame sensors, **VESDA** aspirating systems.
- Detection must be **fast, reliable, and seismically qualified**.
- **Two independent detection channels** in each safety-significant zone.

**Level 3 — Suppression**

If the fire is not prevented or detected early, suppression must extinguish it. Examples:
- **Automatic sprinklers, deluge, water-mist** systems.
- **Clean-agent gas systems** (Inergen, Novec 1230) in control rooms.
- **Manual fire-fighting** by the in-house fire brigade.

**Level 4 — Confinement**

If suppression fails, the fire must be confined to prevent spread. Examples:
- **Fire-rated walls, floors, ceilings** (1 hr, 2 hr, 3 hr rating).
- **Fire doors, penetration seals, fire dampers, fire stops**.
- **Three-hour fire barrier** (3-hr envelope) between redundant safety trains.

**Level 5 — Post-Fire Safe Shutdown**

The final layer — ensuring safety even if all the above fail partially. Examples:
- **Independent redundant trains** of safe-shutdown equipment (Train A & Train B).
- **Alternate shutdown station** outside the main control room.
- **Emergency operating procedures** for fire-induced scenarios.

$$\text{DiD}_{\text{Fire}} = L_1 \cup L_2 \cup L_3 \cup L_4 \cup L_5 \quad \text{(independent and overlapping)}$$

*(7 marks: 1 for concept + 1 each for 5 levels + 1 for overlapping nature.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Numerical Problem on Fire Load

**Given data:**

| Item | Mass $M_i$ (kg) | Calorific value $H_i$ (MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| XLPE cable insulation | 1200 | 30 |
| Lubricating oil | 300 | 44 |
| PVC panels | 100 | 25 |
| Floor area $A$ | — | 80 m² |

**Step 1 — Total fire load $Q_{\text{total}}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{XLPE}} &= 1200 \times 30 = 36{,}000\ \text{MJ} \\
Q_{\text{oil}} &= 300 \times 44 = 13{,}200\ \text{MJ} \\
Q_{\text{PVC}} &= 100 \times 25 = 2{,}500\ \text{MJ} \\
Q_{\text{total}} &= 36{,}000 + 13{,}200 + 2{,}500 = 51{,}700\ \text{MJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Fire load density $q$:**

$$q = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{floor}}} = \dfrac{51{,}700}{80} = 646.25\ \text{MJ/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Required fire-resistance rating:**

Since $q = 646.25\ \text{MJ/m}^2 < 1000\ \text{MJ/m}^2$:

$$\boxed{R = 1\ \text{hour fire-rated compartment}}$$

**AERB Reference:**

- **AERB/NPP-04 (Safety Classification and Fire Protection of Nuclear Power Plants)** classifies compartments as:
  - **Low fire load** ($q < 1000\ \text{MJ/m}^2$) → 1-hr rating
  - **Moderate** ($1000 \leq q < 2000\ \text{MJ/m}^2$) → 2-hr rating
  - **High** ($q \geq 2000\ \text{MJ/m}^2$) → 3-hr rating
- Also referenced: **IAEA NS-G-1.7** and **IS 1644** (Indian Standard on fire safety in industrial buildings).

*(7 marks: 2 for $Q_{\text{total}}$ + 2 for $q$ + 2 for $R$ + 1 for AERB reference.)*

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Fire Protection System (FPS)** in an NPP ensures safe shutdown can be maintained under any single fire, even with a single random equipment failure — this is the **SFC + 1 fire** test.
- ✅ Five Levels of **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)**: **P-D-S-C-S** → **P**revention, **D**etection, **S**uppression, **C**onfinement, post-fire **S**afe shutdown.
- ✅ **Fire Load Density** $q = \dfrac{\sum M_i H_i}{A}$ in **MJ/m²** is the key design parameter.
  - $q < 1000$ → **1-hr** barrier
  - $1000 \leq q < 2000$ → **2-hr** barrier
  - $q \geq 2000$ → **3-hr** barrier
- ✅ **Three-Hour Envelope (3-hr fire barrier)** OR **6 m horizontal separation** must exist between redundant safety trains (IEEE 384 / 10 CFR 50 App. R).
- ✅ **Fire Detection** uses multi-technology sensors (smoke, heat, flame, VESDA) with **two independent channels** per safety area.
- ✅ **Clean agents** (Inergen, Novec 1230, FM-200) have replaced **Halon** in NPP control rooms (Montreal Protocol).
- ✅ **Fire Hazard Analysis (FHA)** + **Post-Fire Safe Shutdown Analysis (PFSSA)** are **mandatory regulatory deliverables** for licensing.
- ✅ **Risk acceptance:** $P_{\text{CDF, fire}} \leq 10^{-6}$ per reactor-year.
- ✅ Key references: **AERB/NPP-04**, **IAEA NS-G-1.7**, **IAEA INSAG-10**, **IEEE 308, 384, NFPA 13, 72**, **10 CFR 50 App. R**, **IS 1644**.
- ✅ **Single-failure criterion** + fire: redundancy must survive a fire concurrent with a random failure in another redundant component.
- ✅ **Cable failure temperature:** PVC $\approx 150$°C; XLPE $\approx 330$°C — used in fire modeling for safe-shutdown proof.
- ✅ **Fire water storage tank** must hold $\geq$ 2 hours of pump capacity, with **2 × 100% diesel + 1 × electric** fire pumps as standard.
- ✅ **Detection time** target: $<$ 60 s smoke, $<$ 30 s flame; **suppression activation** $<$ 10 min automatic.
- ✅ The **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** risk is mitigated by **physical and electrical separation** of redundant trains.
- ✅ **Fire dampers** are mandatory at all HVAC penetrations through fire barriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word of Advice (KTU Exam Day):** For any FPS-related 14-mark question, **always begin with a one-line definition** (1 mark), **state the AERB/IAEA reference** (1 mark), **draw a clean block/schematic diagram** (1–2 marks), and **end with the regulatory acceptance criterion** (1 mark). This structured approach alone can lift your score by 4–5 marks in a 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Assurance in Plant Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Quality Assurance in Plant Components # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Quality Assurance (QA) in Plant Components** is a planned and systematic pattern of all actions necessary to provide adequate confidence that a structure, system, or component will perform satisfactorily in service. In the nuclear context, QA is governed by regulatory documents such as **10 CFR 50 Appendix B** (U.S. NRC), **IAEA Safety Standards Series 50-C/SG-Q**, **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (Section III and Section XI)**, and in India, the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) Safety Code on Quality Assurance (AERB/SC/QA)**. A formal statement (per IAEA NS-G-1.1 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix B) reads: > *"Quality Assurance is the function of a management system that provides confidence that the requirements for nuclear safety and quality will be met, by ensuring that processes and activities are planned, controlled, verified, and documented throughout the lifecycle of the plant."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** QA in nuclear plants is **regulatory-mandated**, not voluntary. Every safety-related component must be designed, manufactured, installed, and tested under an audited QA program. This is fundamentally different from commercial QA (ISO 9001). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Passport Control" of Nuclear Components Imagine an international airport where every traveller (component) entering a country (the nuclear plant) must pass through **multiple security checkpoints** before being allowed to board a plane (operate in safety systems). - **Checkpoint 1** — *Design Review*: Is the traveller authorised? (Design Control) - **Checkpoint 2** — *Document Verification*: Are papers genuine? (Document Control) - **Checkpoint 3** — *Material Screening*: Any prohibited items? (Material Control & Traceability) - **Checkpoint 4** — *Inspection*: Body and luggage scan. (Inspection & Testing) - **Checkpoint 5** — *Records & Audit*: A complete log is kept forever. (Records & Independent Audit) If any checkpoint fails, the traveller is **rejected, the defect is documented, and the root cause is investigated** so that similar travellers are prevented from entering. This is exactly how QA works for nuclear plant components — only the consequences of failure are far more severe than a missed flight. ## 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | **Safety-Related Component (SSC)** | A Structure, System, or Component whose malfunction could cause undue risk to public health and safety. | | **QA Program** | The overall documented plan describing how QA will be implemented for a facility. | | **Quality Control (QC)** | The operational techniques and activities used to fulfil QA requirements (QA is the *plan*, QC is the *execution*). | | **Graded Approach** | Application of QA measures commensurate with the safety significance of the item. | | **NDE/NDT** | Non-Destructive Examination / Testing (Ultrasonic, Radiographic, Eddy Current). | | **Hold Point** | A mandatory witness/inspection point beyond which work cannot proceed without QA acceptance. | | **10 CFR 50 Appendix B** | U.S. regulation listing **18 mandatory QA criteria** for nuclear plants. | > [!NOTE] > **In a Nutshell:** QA is a *systematic, documented, auditable* system of controls. In nuclear plants, it extends from the designer's office, through the foundry, fabrication shop, construction site, commissioning, and into the operational phase for the entire **40–60 year plant life**. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 18 Criteria of 10 CFR 50 Appendix B (The Backbone of Nuclear QA) The U.S. NRC codified **18 mandatory QA Criteria** that every nuclear plant must follow for safety-related SSCs. These have been adopted, with national variations, worldwide (AERB in India, CNSC in Canada, etc.). 1. **Organization** — Independence of QA function from production. 2. **QA Program** — Written, approved, implemented. 3. **Design Control** — Verification of design (reviews, alternate calculations, prototype testing). 4. **Procurement Document Control** — Suppliers must comply with applicable codes & standards. 5. **Instructions, Procedures, and Drawings** — Written procedures for all quality-affecting activities. 6. **Document Control** — Issuance, distribution, and revision control of documents. 7. **Control of Purchased Material, Equipment, and Services** — Source evaluation, receiving inspection. 8. **Identification and Control of Materials** — Traceability from melt to installation. 9. **Control of Special Processes** — Welding, heat treatment, NDE — qualified per codes. 10. **Inspection** — Planned and documented; performed by qualified personnel. 11. **Test Control** — Test procedures; evaluation of test results. 12. **Control of Measuring and Test Equipment (M&TE)** — Calibration at traceable standards. 13. **Handling, Storage, and Shipping** — Preservation of material integrity. 14. **Inspection, Test, and Operating Status** — Visible indicators (tags, stickers). 15. **Control of Nonconforming Materials, Parts, or Components** — Segregation, disposition (Rework / Use-As-Is / Repair / Reject). 16. **Corrective Action** — Root-cause analysis (e.g., Why-Staircase, 5-Why) to prevent recurrence. 17. **Quality Assurance Records** — Lifetime retention (often the life of the plant + 10 years). 18. **Audits** — Internal and external, performed by qualified auditors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **18 × 18 = 324 elements** in the typical detailed QA matrix. KTU questions on this topic often ask students to list, categorise, or explain any **3–5** of these criteria. ## 2.2 The Graded Approach (Safety Classification Drives QA Rigor) Not all components are created equal. A hydraulic jack in the turbine hall does **not** require the same QA rigor as a reactor pressure vessel nozzle weld. The **graded approach** matches QA effort to safety significance. | Safety Class | Examples | QA Level | Documentation | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Class 1 (Safety Critical)** | Reactor Pressure Vessel, Primary Loop, Containment | ASME III, NQA-1, 18 criteria fully | 100% NDE, full traceability | | **Class 2** | Steam Generators, Pressuriser | ASME III (limited) | Substantial | | **Class 3** | Turbine, Condenser, Feedwater | ASME III (code cases) | Moderate | | **Non-Safety (Augmented)** | HVAC, Cranes | Commercial grade | Minimal | ## 2.3 The QA Hierarchy — A Layered System QA operates at **four layers**, each verifying the one below: - **Layer 1 (Bottom)** — *Self-Check by Worker* (first-party). - **Layer 2** — *QC Inspection* (second-party, in-house QA). - **Layer 3** — *Independent QA Verification* (third-party, in-house QA). - **Layer 4 (Top)** — *Regulatory Audit & Surveillance* (NRC / AERB + external ISO/ASME auditors). This is the **"Defence-in-Depth" of Quality**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Table of Key Quality & Reliability Metrics for Plant Components** | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = constant failure rate (per hour), $t$ = mission time (h) | Probability component performs without failure up to time $t$. | | **Unreliability / Failure Probability** | $F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | — | Cumulative failure probability. | | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | For exponential distribution | Average operating time between failures. | | **Quality Index (AQL-based)** | $\text{LTPD} = \dfrac{d}{\,N+1\,} \approx d/N$ | $d$ = max acceptable defectives, $N$ = sample size | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective — consumer's risk metric. | | **Single-Lot Acceptance Probability (Binomial)** | $P_a = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{c}\binom{N}{k} p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k}$ | $p$ = true defect fraction, $c$ = acceptance number | Probability a lot with true fraction $p$ is accepted under sampling plan $(N,c)$. | | **Operating Characteristic Slope** | Slope of $P_a$ vs $p$ at $p_1$ (AQL) | — | Measures discriminatory power of the plan. | | **QA Multiplier on $\lambda$** | $\lambda_{\text{achieved}} = \lambda_{\text{nominal}} \cdot k_{\text{QA}}$ | $k_{\text{QA}}$ = quality factor (0.05–1.0) | Higher QA rigor → smaller $k_{\text{QA}}$ → lower $\lambda$. | | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $\text{PFD} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2}$ | $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate, $T_I$ = proof test interval | For safety-instrumented functions (IEC 61508). | | **Redundancy Reliability** | $R_n = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) \cdots (1 - R_n)$ | $R_i$ = individual reliabilities, identical for $n$ identical components | $n$-out-of-$n$ parallel redundancy. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Do not confuse **Quality Assurance (the system)** with **Quality Control (the activity)**. QA designs the rules; QC executes them. The exam loves this distinction. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Regulatory Compliance**: Without a certified QA program, AERB/NRC will not license a plant. - **Defect Prevention**: The 18-criteria approach catches defects at the *paper-stage* (design review) rather than the *operational-stage* (after a leak). - **Traceability**: If a pipe weld cracks 20 years into operation, the QA records allow engineers to identify the heat of steel, the welder, the procedure, and the NDE results. - **Public Confidence**: QA is the primary reason nuclear power has a public safety record vastly better than most industrial sectors. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Problem Setup **Context:** A safety-related pump in a nuclear plant is to be procured. The QA department must recommend a sampling acceptance plan $(N, c)$ to verify incoming quality. We need to derive: - (a) The probability of accepting a lot with true defect fraction $p$. - (b) The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve slope** at the Acceptable Quality Level (AQL). - (c) The **achieved failure rate** $\lambda$ after applying a QA multiplier. The derivation below uses the **binomial** distribution for sampling inspection and the **exponential** model for component reliability — both standard assumptions in nuclear QA per **NUREG-1855** and **IEC 61508**. --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation ### Step 1 — Define the Sampling Plan and Acceptance Probability A lot of $N$ components is sampled. Let $p$ be the true (unknown) fraction of defectives in the lot. The number of defectives found in the sample of $N$, denoted $X$, follows: $$ X \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p) $$ The lot is **accepted** if $X \le c$, where $c$ is the acceptance number. Therefore the probability of acceptance $P_a$ is: $$ P_a(p) \;=\; P(X \le c) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{N}{k} \, p^{k} \, (1 - p)^{N - k} $$ **Logic:** The $\binom{N}{k}$ term counts the number of ways to pick exactly $k$ defectives; $p^k$ is the probability of those $k$ being defective; $(1-p)^{N-k}$ is the probability of the remaining $N-k$ being good. --- ### Step 2 — Evaluate $P_a$ at the AQL (Acceptable Quality Level $p_1$) By design, at $p = p_1$ (AQL), we set $P_a(p_1) \approx 0.95$ (5% producer's risk). For the special case of a **rectifying inspection plan** with $c = 0$ (zero-acceptance plan): $$ P_a(p) \;=\; (1 - p)^{N} $$ Setting $P_a(p_1) = 0.95$: $$ (1 - p_1)^{N} \;=\; 0.95 $$ Taking the natural logarithm on both sides: $$ N \cdot \ln(1 - p_1) \;=\; \ln(0.95) $$ For small $p_1$ (typical AQL values are 0.01 to 0.10), use the expansion $\ln(1 - p_1) \approx -p_1$: $$ N \cdot (-p_1) \;\approx\; \ln(0.95) $$ Solving for the **required sample size**: $$ N \;\approx\; -\dfrac{\ln(0.95)}{p_1} \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.0513}{p_1} $$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $p_1 = 0.05$ (AQL = 5%): $$ N \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.0513}{0.05} \;\approx\; 1.03 \;\Rightarrow\; N = 2 \text{ (rounded up)} $$ So we must sample at least 2 components with $c = 0$ to achieve a 95% acceptance probability at the AQL of 5%. --- ### Step 3 — Slope of the OC Curve at the AQL (Discriminatory Power) The slope of the OC curve at $p = p_1$ measures how sharply the plan rejects lots worse than AQL. Differentiating $P_a$ with respect to $p$: $$ \dfrac{dP_a}{dp} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{c} \binom{N}{k} \left[\,k \, p^{k-1} (1-p)^{N-k} - (N-k)\,p^{k}(1-p)^{N-k-1}\right] $$ For the $c = 0$ case, $P_a = (1-p)^{N}$: $$ \dfrac{dP_a}{dp} \;=\; -N\,(1-p)^{N-1} $$ At $p = p_1$: $$ \dfrac{dP_a}{dp}\bigg|_{p_1} \;=\; -N\,(1-p_1)^{N-1} \;\approx\; -N\,(1-p_1)^{N} $$ For the example $p_1 = 0.05$, $N = 2$: $$ \dfrac{dP_a}{dp}\bigg|_{0.05} \;\approx\; -2 \cdot (0.95)^{1} \;\approx\; -1.90 $$ A steeper negative slope = better discrimination between good and bad lots. A more rigorous QA plan (larger $N$, smaller $c$) increases this magnitude. --- ### Step 4 — Achieved Failure Rate with QA Multiplier The nominal manufacturer-quoted failure rate is $\lambda_{\text{nominal}}$. After applying graded QA, the *achieved* in-service failure rate is: $$ \lambda_{\text{achieved}} \;=\; k_{\text{QA}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{nominal}} $$ where $k_{\text{QA}}$ is a dimensionless quality multiplier (typical values: $1.0$ for commercial grade, $0.1$ for ASME III Section 1, $0.05$ for augmented QA with full NDE). **Deriving Reliability for a 1-Year Mission ($t = 8760$ h):** Using the exponential reliability function: $$ R(t) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_{\text{achieved}} \cdot t} \;=\; e^{-k_{\text{QA}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{nominal}} \cdot t} $$ **Numerical Example:** Suppose $\lambda_{\text{nominal}} = 10^{-4}$ per hour, mission $t = 8760$ h. - **With $k_{\text{QA}} = 1.0$ (no extra QA):** $$ R(1\,\text{yr}) \;=\; e^{-10^{-4} \cdot 8760} \;=\; e^{-0.876} \;\approx\; 0.4168 $$ So the component has only **41.68%** chance of surviving 1 year. - **With $k_{\text{QA}} = 0.05$ (full ASME III QA):** $$ R(1\,\text{yr}) \;=\; e^{-0.05 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 8760} \;=\; e^{-0.0438} \;\approx\; 0.9572 $$ So the same component has a **95.72%** chance of surviving 1 year — a **massive improvement** demonstrating the value of nuclear-grade QA. --- ### Step 5 — Final Consolidated Analytical Matrix | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Numerical Value (Example) | Unit | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | Sample Size for $c=0$, AQL=5% | $N$ | $-\ln(0.95)/p_1$ | $2$ | components | | OC slope at AQL | $dP_a/dp$ | $-N(1-p_1)^{N-1}$ | $-1.90$ | per unit $p$ | | QA Multiplier (Class 1) | $k_{\text{QA}}$ | Defined by graded approach | $0.05$ | dimensionless | | Achieved failure rate | $\lambda_{\text{ach}}$ | $k_{\text{QA}}\,\lambda_{\text{nom}}$ | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ | per hour | | 1-year reliability | $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda_{\text{ach}} t}$ | $0.9572$ | dimensionless | # 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — QA Program Flow: From Regulation to Field Implementation The following Mermaid block depicts how regulatory requirements cascade down to actual field inspection activities in a nuclear plant component procurement and installation cycle. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[International Standards
IAEA NS-G-1.1] --> B[National Regulator
AERB Safety Code QA] B --> C[Utility Owner
NPCIL QA Manual] C --> D[Architect Engineer
Design QA Program] C --> E[Manufacturer
Fabrication QA Plan] C --> F[Construction Contractor
Site QA Plan] D --> G[Design Review
Independent Verification] E --> H[Material Traceability
Melt to Installation] E --> I[Special Process Qualification
Welding, NDE, Heat Treatment] E --> J[In-Process Inspections
Hold Points Witnessed by QC] F --> K[Erection and Installation
Field Inspection Test Plans] F --> L[Commissioning Tests
Pre-Operational] H --> M[Document Control
Lifetime Records Retention] I --> M J --> M G --> M K --> M L --> M M --> N[Internal Audits
Every 12 months] M --> O[External Audits
NRC / AERB / ISO 9001] N --> P[Corrective Action
Root Cause Analysis] O --> P P --> Q[Continuous Improvement
Lessons Learnt Database] Q --> D Q --> E Q --> F ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — 18 QA Criteria of 10 CFR 50 Appendix B (Hierarchical View) ```mermaid flowchart TB root[10 CFR 50 Appendix B
18 QA Criteria] root --> c1[C1: Organization] root --> c2[C2: QA Program] root --> c3[C3: Design Control] root --> c4[C4: Procurement Control] root --> c5[C5: Procedures and Drawings] root --> c6[C6: Document Control] root --> c7[C7: Purchased Items Control] root --> c8[C8: Material Identification] root --> c9[C9: Special Processes] root --> c10[C10: Inspection] root --> c11[C11: Test Control] root --> c12[C12: M&TE Calibration] root --> c13[C13: Handling Storage Shipping] root --> c14[C14: Inspection Test Status] root --> c15[C15: Nonconforming Items] root --> c16[C16: Corrective Action] root --> c17[C17: QA Records] root --> c18[C18: Audits] c1 -. Independence of QA .-> root c3 -. Verification .-> c5 c9 -. Welder / Procedure Qualification .-> c11 c15 -. Disposition: Rework / Repair / Reject .-> c16 c16 -. Why-Staircase / 5-Why .-> c18 c17 -. Lifetime Retention .-> c18 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Component QA Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR In[Input
Component Drawing + Spec] --> A1[Document Review] A1 --> A2[Hold Point Definition] A2 --> A3[Material Receipt Inspection] A3 --> A4[In-Process QC Inspection] A4 --> A5[Special Process Verification
Welding, NDE] A5 --> A6[Final Inspection and Testing] A6 --> D1{Conformance Achieved?} D1 -- Yes --> A7[QA Release Tag] A7 --> A8[Records Archival] A8 --> Out[Output
Released Component] D1 -- No --> A9[Nonconformance Report NCR] A9 --> A10[Engineering Disposition] A10 --> A11[Rework / Repair / Reject / Use-As-Is] A11 --> A4 A9 --> A12[Corrective Action
Root Cause Analysis] A12 --> A13[Update Procedures] A13 --> A1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid and not physical drawings?** Mermaid renders perfectly on every markdown viewer (GitHub, Obsidian, VS Code, KTU e-learning portal), whereas embedded images often fail to render on university LMS systems. # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reminder:** > - Part A (3 marks) → ~1–2 sentence conceptual answer. > - Part B (14 marks) → 2 sub-parts × 7 marks each; **internal choice** mandatory (Q-A or Q-B). > - Bloom's Levels tested: L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse). --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) in the context of nuclear plant components.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Control (QC) | |:---|:---|:---| | **Scope** | All planned and systematic actions (the *system*). | Operational techniques used to meet QA requirements (the *activity*). | | **Nature** | Proactive, preventive, management-oriented. | Reactive, detective, execution-oriented. | | **Question answered** | *"Are we doing the right things, the right way?"* | *"Did we produce a conforming product?"* | | **Examples** | Writing the QA Manual, Auditing, Training. | Welding inspection, dimensional checks, NDE. | | **Independence** | QA function is independent of production. | QC is part of production supervision. | **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition each + 1 mark for distinction example.)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **State any six (6) of the 18 QA criteria listed in 10 CFR 50 Appendix B.** **Model Answer (any 6 of the following — full marks for any 6):** 1. Organization (C1) 2. QA Program (C2) 3. Design Control (C3) 4. Procurement Document Control (C4) 5. Instructions, Procedures, Drawings (C5) 6. Document Control (C6) 7. Control of Purchased Material (C7) 8. Identification & Control of Materials (C8) 9. Control of Special Processes (C9) 10. Inspection (C10) 11. Test Control (C11) 12. Control of M&TE (C12) 13. Handling, Storage, Shipping (C13) 14. Inspection, Test & Operating Status (C14) 15. Control of Nonconforming Items (C15) 16. Corrective Action (C16) 17. QA Records (C17) 18. Audits (C18) **(3 marks: 0.5 mark per correctly stated criterion.)** --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Choice Format:** Attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B**. --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **18 QA criteria of 10 CFR 50 Appendix B** in detail. Discuss the significance of the criterion *"Control of Nonconforming Materials"* and the various **dispositions** available. **(b) [7 Marks]** A nuclear-grade pump has a nominal failure rate $\lambda_{\text{nominal}} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour. The plant uses a graded QA approach with $k_{\text{QA}} = 0.08$ for safety-related components. Calculate the **1-year reliability** of this pump. Comment on whether the achieved reliability meets the typical safety target of $R \ge 0.95$ over 1 year. --- #### 📖 Model Solution — Question A (a) The 18 criteria of 10 CFR 50 Appendix B form the **regulatory baseline** for QA at every U.S. nuclear plant, and have been adopted in modified form worldwide (e.g., AERB/SC/QA in India). They are: **(Group 1 — Programmatic, Criteria 1–6):** These establish the management infrastructure. - **C1 Organization:** The QA organisation must have sufficient authority, independence, and organisational freedom to identify quality problems, recommend solutions, and verify implementation. QA cannot report to production management. - **C2 QA Program:** A written, approved QA program must be established at the earliest practical time consistent with the schedule for accomplishment of activities. - **C3 Design Control:** Design must be verified by one or more methods: design reviews, alternate calculations, qualification testing. Changes to approved design must be subject to the same controls as the original. - **C4 Procurement Document Control:** Procurement documents must include design-basis requirements, regulatory commitments, QA program requirements, and right-of-access for the buyer. - **C5 Instructions, Procedures, Drawings:** All quality-affecting activities must be prescribed by documented, approved procedures. - **C6 Document Control:** Documents must be reviewed, approved, distributed, and controlled. Obsolete documents must be prevented from inadvertent use. **(Group 2 — Implementation, Criteria 7–12):** These translate the program into practice. - **C7 Control of Purchased Items:** Source evaluation, source surveillance, receiving inspection. - **C8 Material Identification:** Traceable from the original material to the installed location — using heat numbers, serial numbers, and the ASME Code stamp. - **C9 Special Processes:** Welding, brazing, heat treatment, NDE — performed by qualified personnel using qualified procedures per ASME IX. - **C10 Inspection:** Performed by qualified personnel using approved procedures, with results documented. - **C11 Test Control:** Test procedures specify prerequisites, acceptance criteria, and methods of evaluating results. - **C12 M&TE Calibration:** All measuring and test equipment calibrated against standards traceable to national/international standards (e.g., NABL in India, NIST in U.S.). **(Group 3 — Assurance & Improvement, Criteria 13–18):** These provide feedback and assurance. - **C13 Handling, Storage, Shipping:** Preservation of material integrity (e.g., nitrogen blanketing of reactor components, climate-controlled storage). - **C14 Inspection/Test Status:** Visible indication of acceptance status (tags, stamps, colour codes). - **C15 Nonconforming Materials:** Segregation, identification, and disposition. - **C16 Corrective Action:** Root-cause analysis; prevention of recurrence. - **C17 QA Records:** Lifetime retention. - **C18 Audits:** Independent verification of program effectiveness. **Significance of Criterion 15 — Control of Nonconforming Materials:** A nonconformance is any departure from the approved design, code, or specification. Because nuclear components have decades-long service lives, a nonconformance, if not properly controlled, could propagate into the plant, causing safety problems decades later. The criterion therefore mandates: 1. **Segregation** of nonconforming items to prevent inadvertent use. 2. **Identification** by tag, label, or distinctive colour. 3. **Disposition** by Engineering via one of four options: - **Rework** — Restore to original specification. - **Repair** — Restore to an acceptable condition, *not necessarily the original* (requires design evaluation). - **Use-As-Is** — Accept the deviation without correction (requires design basis justification). - **Reject / Scrap** — Discard. Each disposition must be **documented** and **approved by the responsible engineer** at a level commensurate with the safety significance. **(Award: 1 mark per criterion group overview, 2 marks for nonconformance detail, 1 mark for dispositions.)** --- #### 📖 Model Solution — Question A (b) **Given:** - Nominal failure rate $\lambda_{\text{nominal}} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ per hour - QA multiplier $k_{\text{QA}} = 0.08$ - Mission time $t = 1$ year $= 8760$ hours - Target $R(1\text{ yr}) \ge 0.95$ **Step 1 — Compute the Achieved Failure Rate:** $$ \lambda_{\text{achieved}} \;=\; k_{\text{QA}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{nominal}} \;=\; 0.08 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Exponential Reliability Model:** $$ R(1\,\text{yr}) \;=\; e^{-\lambda_{\text{achieved}} \cdot t} \;=\; e^{-4 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760} $$ Compute the exponent: $$ \lambda_{\text{achieved}} \cdot t \;=\; 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760 \;=\; 0.3504 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate:** $$ R(1\,\text{yr}) \;=\; e^{-0.3504} \;\approx\; 0.7045 $$ **Step 4 — Comparison with Safety Target:** $$ R_{\text{achieved}} \;\approx\; 70.45\% \;\;<\;\; 95.0\% \text{ (target)} $$ **Conclusion & Engineering Recommendation:** The achieved reliability of **70.45%** is **significantly below** the 95% safety target for safety-related components. Engineering actions recommended: 1. **Reduce $k_{\text{QA}}$ further** — by augmenting inspection (e.g., 100% volumetric NDE). - Required: $e^{-k_{\text{QA,new}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{nom}} \cdot t} \ge 0.95$ $$ k_{\text{QA,new}} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 8760 \;\le\; -\ln(0.95) \;=\; 0.0513 $$ $$ k_{\text{QA,new}} \;\le\; \dfrac{0.0513}{4.38} \;\approx\; 0.0117 $$ So the QA multiplier must be reduced from 0.08 to **≤ 0.0117** (i.e., much more rigorous QA), or 2. **Reduce $\lambda_{\text{nominal}}$** by upgrading to a higher-grade pump. 3. **Add redundancy** — for a 1-out-of-2 redundant pair with $R_{\text{each}} = 0.7045$: $$ R_{\text{pair}} \;=\; 1 - (1 - 0.7045)^2 \;=\; 1 - (0.2955)^2 \;=\; 1 - 0.0873 \;=\; 0.9127 $$ A 2-out-of-3 voting system (typical for safety-grade pumps) provides $R \approx 0.964$ — meeting the target. **(Award: 2 marks for achieved $\lambda$, 2 marks for R computation, 2 marks for target comparison & discussion, 1 mark for engineering recommendation.)** --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **Graded Approach** in nuclear QA? Explain how component safety classification determines the **QA level** and the corresponding documentation, NDE, and traceability requirements. Use a suitable table. **(b) [7 Marks]** A lot of $N = 50$ safety-related valves is offered for acceptance inspection. A single sampling plan with acceptance number $c = 1$ is used. The true fraction defective in the lot is $p = 0.04$. Calculate the **probability of acceptance** $P_a$. Comment on the **producer's risk** if AQL = 1%. --- #### 📖 Model Solution — Question B (a) **Definition:** The **Graded Approach** is the principle that the *depth, scope, and rigor* of QA measures applied to a structure, system, or component (SSC) should be **commensurate with its safety significance and importance to the plant mission**. Higher safety importance → more rigorous QA, more documentation, more NDE, more traceability. This avoids the impractical (and uneconomical) alternative of applying maximum QA to every nut and bolt, while still ensuring that the most safety-critical items receive the most scrutiny. **Safety Classification and Corresponding QA Levels:** | Safety Class | Component Examples | Applicable Code | NDE Coverage | Material Traceability | Documentation | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Class 1 (Safety Critical)** | Reactor Pressure Vessel, Primary Piping, Core Barrel | ASME III Div 1 (NB, NC) + NQA-1 | 100% volumetric NDE on all welds | Heat-by-heat from steel mill to installation; full ASME stamp traceability | Full lifetime records | | **Class 2 (Safety Related)** | Steam Generators, Pressuriser, ECCS Piping | ASME III (limited sections) | Substantial; risk-informed NDE | Heat-level traceability; partial ASME stamps | Substantial | | **Class 3 (Augmented Quality)** | Turbine, Main Condenser, Feedwater Heaters | ASME III code cases | Reduced — typically surface NDE on critical welds | Lot-level traceability | Reduced | | **Non-Safety (Commercial Grade)** | HVAC, Lighting, Cranes, Office Furniture | Commercial codes (e.g., AISC, ASTM) | As per commercial practice | Not required | Minimal — commercial certs only | **Justification for the Graded Approach:** 1. **Defence-in-Depth Philosophy:** Class 1 components form the primary pressure boundary; their failure could cause a large early release. 2. **Risk-Informed Reasoning:** Probability of failure × consequence of failure = risk. Components with low failure probability AND low consequence need not be over-engineered. 3. **Economic Practicality:** Applying 100% NDE to *all* welds in a plant would multiply cost by 10× with negligible safety gain. **Practical Application:** The safety classification is decided during **Design Basis work** (e.g., per AERB/NRC safety classification guidelines) and locked into the **Equipment Qualification List (EQ-list)** for the entire plant life. **(Award: 2 marks definition, 3 marks table, 2 marks justification.)** --- #### 📖 Model Solution — Question B (b) **Given:** - Lot size: not directly used (we work with sample) — sampling plan $(N_s, c)$ with $N_s = 50$, $c = 1$. - True defect fraction: $p = 0.04$. - AQL (acceptable quality level): $p_1 = 0.01$ (1%). **Step 1 — Apply the Binomial Acceptance Probability Formula:** $$ P_a \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{N_s}{k} p^k (1 - p)^{N_s - k} \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{1} \binom{50}{k} (0.04)^k (0.96)^{50 - k} $$ **Step 2 — Expand the Sum (k = 0 and k = 1):** **Term k = 0:** $$ T_0 \;=\; \binom{50}{0} (0.04)^0 (0.96)^{50} \;=\; 1 \cdot 1 \cdot (0.96)^{50} $$ Compute $(0.96)^{50}$: $$ \ln(0.96)^{50} \;=\; 50 \cdot \ln(0.96) \;=\; 50 \times (-0.04082) \;=\; -2.041 $$ $$ (0.96)^{50} \;=\; e^{-2.041} \;\approx\; 0.1299 $$ So $T_0 \approx 0.1299$. **Term k = 1:** $$ T_1 \;=\; \binom{50}{1} (0.04)^1 (0.96)^{49} \;=\; 50 \cdot 0.04 \cdot (0.96)^{49} $$ $$ (0.96)^{49} \;=\; \dfrac{(0.96)^{50}}{0.96} \;\approx\; \dfrac{0.1299}{0.96} \;\approx\; 0.1353 $$ $$ T_1 \;=\; 50 \times 0.04 \times 0.1353 \;=\; 0.2706 $$ **Step 3 — Sum:** $$ P_a \;=\; T_0 + T_1 \;\approx\; 0.1299 + 0.2706 \;\approx\; 0.4005 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Producer's Risk $\alpha$ at AQL = 1%:** We need $P_a$ at $p = p_1 = 0.01$, $c = 1$, $N_s = 50$: $$ P_a(p_1 = 0.01) \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{1} \binom{50}{k} (0.01)^k (0.99)^{50-k} $$ **Term k = 0:** $$ T_0' \;=\; (0.99)^{50} \;\approx\; e^{50 \cdot \ln(0.99)} \;\approx\; e^{50 \times (-0.01005)} \;\approx\; e^{-0.5025} \;\approx\; 0.6050 $$ **Term k = 1:** $$ T_1' \;=\; 50 \cdot 0.01 \cdot (0.99)^{49} \;\approx\; 0.50 \times 0.6112 \;\approx\; 0.3056 $$ (where $(0.99)^{49} = 0.6050/0.99 \approx 0.6112$) **Sum:** $$ P_a(p_1) \;\approx\; 0.6050 + 0.3056 \;\approx\; 0.9106 $$ Producer's risk: $$ \alpha \;=\; 1 - P_a(\text{AQL}) \;\approx\; 1 - 0.9106 \;\approx\; 0.0894 \;\approx\; 8.94\% $$ **Interpretation:** - The producer's risk $\alpha \approx 8.94\%$ is **higher than the typical target of 5%** for nuclear-grade sampling plans. - The producer's risk is the probability of rejecting a *good* lot. An 8.94% rejection rate at AQL means about 9 good lots in 100 will be wrongly rejected — commercially painful for the manufacturer. - **Engineering Recommendation:** Tighten the plan (e.g., increase $N_s$ to 95, keep $c=1$) to bring $\alpha$ below 5%. **(Award: 2 marks binomial setup, 2 marks numerical evaluation, 2 marks producer's risk calc, 1 mark engineering interpretation.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing QA and QC** (costs ~1 mark in every related question). > 2. **Forgetting the exponential reliability formula** $R = e^{-\lambda t}$ — students often write $R = 1 - \lambda t$ (Taylor's 1st-order), which is only valid for very small $\lambda t$. If $\lambda t > 0.1$, full marks will NOT be awarded. > 3. **Mixing the 18 criteria with ISO 9001 clauses** — they overlap but are not identical. Stick to the Appendix B wording in 10 CFR 50. > 4. **Ignoring units** — $\lambda$ must be in *per hour* and $t$ in *hours* (or consistent units). Examiners deduct up to 0.5 mark per wrong unit. > 5. **Not stating the assumption** of constant failure rate (exponential distribution) before using $R = e^{-\lambda t}$. > 6. **Skipping the binomial expansion step** — always write out $T_0$, $T_1$, ... individually for partial credit. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **QA ≠ QC:** QA is the *system / plan*; QC is the *activity*. Both are mandatory for safety-related SSCs. - ⭐ **18 Criteria of 10 CFR 50 Appendix B** form the regulatory backbone. Memorise at least 10 by name. - ⭐ **Graded Approach** = QA effort ∝ Safety Significance. Class 1 → ASME III + NQA-1; Class 4 → commercial grade. - ⭐ **Four Layers of QA Defence:** Worker → QC → Independent QA → Regulatory Audit. - ⭐ **Reliability Formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$; for $n$ redundant identical components: $R_n = 1 - (1-R)^n$. - ⭐ **Failure Probability:** $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$. - ⭐ **MTBF = $1/\lambda$** for constant failure rate. - ⭐ **QA Multiplier** $k_{\text{QA}}$ reduces achieved failure rate: $\lambda_{\text{ach}} = k_{\text{QA}}\,\lambda_{\text{nom}}$. Typical Class 1 $k_{\text{QA}} \approx 0.05$. - ⭐ **Sampling plan $(N, c)$** acceptance probability uses the **binomial** distribution; for $c=0$ it simplifies to $(1-p)^N$. - ⭐ **Producer's Risk $\alpha$** is evaluated at AQL; **Consumer's Risk $\beta$** is evaluated at LTPD. Standard targets: $\alpha \le 5\%$, $\beta \le 10\%$. - ⭐ **Nonconformance Dispositions:** Rework / Repair / Use-As-Is / Reject — *each requires documented engineering approval.* - ⭐ **Corrective Action** uses tools like **5-Why**, **Fishbone (Ishikawa)**, **Why-Staircase**, **FMEA**. - ⭐ **QA Records** retained for the **life of the plant + 10 years** (often longer). - ⭐ **Audits** must be performed at least **every 12 months** for safety-related activities. - ⭐ **Indian context:** AERB Safety Code on QA (AERB/SC/QA) and AERB Safety Guide on QA for Nuclear Power Plants (AERB/SG/QA-1) are the primary regulatory documents. - ⭐ **International context:** IAEA NS-G-1.1, ISO 9001, ASME NQA-1, ASME III (for design/material/ fabrication), ASME XI (for in-service inspection). - ⭐ **Special Processes** (welding, NDE, heat treatment) require **qualified procedures + qualified personnel**, as per ASME Section IX. - ⭐ **Material Traceability:** Heat number → forging → machining → installation site, documented at every step.

topics.title: Operational Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operational Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Safety (IAEA SSR-2/2 Definition):**
> *Operational Safety* of a nuclear power plant (NPP) is defined as the set of **planned and systematic measures, procedures, controls, and human actions** undertaken during the routine and anticipated operational states of the plant to ensure that **(a)** the plant is operated within its established Operational Limits and Conditions (OLCs), **(b)** Safety Functions (Reactivity Control, Heat Removal, Confinement) are preserved, **(c)** Radiation doses to workers, public, and environment remain **As Low As Reasonably Achievable (ALARA)**, and **(d)** the integrity of all three **Barriers to Radioactive Release** (Fuel Cladding, Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary, Containment) is maintained.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** outcome-based framework, Operational Safety is mapped to **Course Outcome CO3** of PESFT417 and is evaluated under the cognitive levels of **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** in the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Hospital ICU for a Reactor" Analogy:**
> Imagine a Nuclear Reactor is like a **critically ill patient in an ICU**. Operational Safety is essentially the **"Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) manual"** that the doctors (Control Room Operators) and nurses (Shift Engineers) follow minute-by-minute.

| ICU Element | Nuclear Equivalent |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Patient's vital sign limits (BP, HR) | **Operational Limits & Conditions (OLCs)** |
| Doctor's written prescription | **Technical Specifications (Tech Specs)** |
| Periodic blood tests & checkups | **Surveillance & In-Service Inspection (ISI)** |
| Code Blue emergency protocol | **Emergency Operating Procedures (EOPs)** |
| Nurses' shift handover logbook | **Shift Turnover Records** |
| Hospital incident report | **Licensee Event Report (LER)** |

Just as a hospital cannot let a doctor "wing it," a nuclear plant cannot let an operator "guess." **Every action is pre-written, pre-approved, and post-verified.**

---

## 1.3 Key Performance & Standard Metrics

The following **physical constants and regulatory thresholds** form the cornerstone of KTU 2024 numerical problems on this topic:

- **Annual Occupational Dose Limit (Whole Body)** = **20 mSv/year** (averaged over 5 years)
- **Annual Public Dose Limit** = **1 mSv/year**
- **Core Damage Frequency (CDF) target** = **< 10⁻⁵ /reactor-year**
- **Large Early Release Frequency (LERF) target** = **< 10⁻⁶ /reactor-year**
- **Defence-in-Depth (DiD) Levels** = **5 levels** (per IAEA INSAG-10)
- **Single Failure Criterion (SFC) redundancy** = **N+2 or 2×100%** for safety systems
- **Safety Culture Triangle (per INSAG-4)** = **Policy + Mechanism + Individuals**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** The 5 Levels of Defence-in-Depth (DiD) and the hierarchy of **Normal → Abnormal → Emergency → Severe Accident** procedures are the most frequently asked 3-mark and 14-mark questions in Module 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 Pillars of Operational Safety (Decomposition)

Operational Safety is not a single activity — it is a **layered, integrated system** of five mutually reinforcing pillars. Each pillar must function for the system to deliver safety.

### Pillar 1 — Operational Limits & Conditions (OLCs)
These are the **quantitative boundaries** within which the plant *must* be operated. They are derived from the **Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR)** and are part of the licence.

- **Safety Limits (SLs):** Hard barriers; violation = immediate shutdown (e.g., DNBR > 1.30, Fuel Centre-line Temp < incipient melting point).
- **Limiting Safety System Settings (LSSS):** The *automatic* trip setpoints (e.g., reactor trip on high neutron flux).
- **Limiting Conditions for Operation (LCOs):** The minimum operable equipment configurations (e.g., 2 of 4 loops operable for PWR).
- **Surveillance Requirements (SRs):** Periodic testing (e.g., calibration of radiation monitors every 3 months).

### Pillar 2 — Operating Procedures (The "How" of Operation)
Hierarchy of procedures, in increasing severity:

1. **Normal Operating Procedures (NOPs)** — startup, power operation, shutdown.
2. **Abnormal Operating Procedures (AOPs)** — for anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs).
3. **Alarm Response Procedures (ARPs)** — short checklist for each alarm tile.
4. **Emergency Operating Procedures (EOPs)** — for Design Basis Accidents (DBAs).
5. **Severe Accident Management Guidelines (SAMGs)** — for Beyond Design Basis Accidents (BDBAs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Symptom-Based vs. Event-Based EOPs:**
> Modern EOPs are **symptom-based** (e.g., "High Pressuriser Level + Low Subcooling" — regardless of cause), not event-based. This prevents operators from misdiagnosing the initiator.

### Pillar 3 — Operating Organization (Human Factor)
- **Shift Composition:** Shift Supervisor, Reactor Operator (RO), Shift Technical Advisor (STA), Turbine Operator, Radiation Protection (RP) Technician, Fire Brigade.
- **Independent Review:** Plant Operations Review Committee (PORC) and Plant Health Committee (PHC) must independently review changes.
- **Fitness-for-Duty:** Drug & alcohol testing, work-hour controls (typically ≤ 12 hrs/shift, ≤ 72 hrs/week per 10 CFR 26 for US, similar in India under AERB).

### Pillar 4 — Surveillance, Testing & Maintenance
- **Surveillance Testing:** Pre-defined frequency for every safety system (pumps, valves, diesels, batteries).
- **Corrective Action Program (CAP):** Every anomaly is logged into a Corrective Action system (e.g., Condition Report).
- **Maintenance Rule (10 CFR 50.65 / AERB D-20):** Functions must be maintained such that (a)(1) goals are met — i.e., the system achieves ≥ 95% reliability and availability.

### Pillar 5 — Operating Experience & Feedback (OPEX)
- **Internal OPEX:** Own plant's near-misses, LERs, and human performance events.
- **External OPEX:** **INPO** (Institute of Nuclear Power Operations) and **IAEA IRS** (Incident Reporting System) bulletins.
- **Effectiveness:** Lessons learned must feed into procedure revisions and training.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar — KTU Examination Lens

| Pillar | Engineering "Why" | Common Exam Question Type |
|-------|------------------|---------------------------|
| OLCs | Prevents operation in un-analysed state | "What action is mandated on SL violation?" |
| Procedures | Reduces human error under stress | "Compare NOP vs EOP" |
| Organization | Defence against single-person error | "Role of PORC" |
| Surveillance | Catches latent equipment failures | "Frequency of diesel generator test" |
| OPEX | Prevents recurrence of past events | "Purpose of INPO/IAEA-IRS" |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Operational Safety is primarily a **qualitative + management** topic, but the following quantitative formulas appear in Part B (14-mark) numerical/analytical sub-parts:

| # | Quantity / Metric | Formula / Expression | Standard Threshold | Unit |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | Annual Occupational Dose Limit (5-yr avg) | $H_{occ} = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} H_i$ | $\leq 20$ | mSv/yr |
| 2 | Public Dose Limit (single source) | $H_{pub}$ | $\leq 1$ | mSv/yr |
| 3 | Collective Effective Dose (SED) | $S = \sum_{j} \overline{H_j} \cdot N_j$ | Minimize (ALARA) | man-Sv |
| 4 | Cumulative Core Damage Frequency | $CDF = \sum_{i} f_i \cdot P_i$ | $< 10^{-5}$ | /reactor-yr |
| 5 | Large Early Release Frequency | $LERF = \sum_{k} f_k \cdot P_k^{LER}$ | $< 10^{-6}$ | /reactor-yr |
| 6 | System Unavailability (3-train) | $Q = \prod_{i=1}^{3} q_i$ | $\leq 10^{-3}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Plant Availability Factor | $AF = \dfrac{T_{oper}}{T_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | $\geq 90$ | % |
| 8 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy}}{\text{Max Possible Energy}} \times 100$ | $\geq 85$ | % |
| 9 | Safety System Failure Probability | $P_f = 1 - R$ where $R$ is reliability | $\leq 10^{-2}$ /demand | dimensionless |
| 10 | Maintenance Rule (a)(1) Goal | $MR = \dfrac{T_{unavail,uncorr}}{T_{scheduled}} \times 100$ | $\leq 5$ | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Table Formatting Safeguard Applied:** All absolute/conditional separators use `\vert` and `\leq` notation — never the raw pipe symbol `|` — to prevent Markdown table breakage during PDF export.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Operational Safety is the **daily practice** of all the theory covered in Modules 1 & 2 (reactor physics, barriers, DiD). It is the discipline that translates design safety into **lived safety**.

- **Nuclear Industry:** Every AERB / IAEA / NRC operating licence is contingent upon demonstrating a fully functional Operational Safety program.
- **Aerospace (Analogy):** Like pre-flight checklists + crew resource management (CRM) in commercial aviation.
- **Process Industries (Refineries, Chemical):** Same hierarchy of procedures (SOP, EOP, emergency shutdown) scaled down.
- **Medical (Radiotherapy):** Operational safety protocols (e.g., source inventory, dosimetry) follow identical principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Procedure Selection Logic

A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"For a given plant state, which procedure applies?"* The following decision matrix is exhaustively derived below.

> **Given:** A PWR is operating at 100% power. The following transient occurs:
> A small Loss-of-Coolant Accident (LOCA) of 2-inch break occurs in the cold leg. RCS pressure drops, containment pressure rises, and high containment radiation alarm trips.
>
> **Required:** Identify the correct procedural pathway and justify with the Defence-in-Depth (DiD) framework.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Deduction

**Step 1 — Classify the Initiating Event**

We classify the 2-inch cold-leg break against the FSAR Chapter 15 (Accident Analysis) envelope.

$$
\text{Event Size} = 2 \text{ inch} \ll 2 \times \text{Largest Pipe Diameter} = \text{DBA LOCA envelope}
$$

Hence, this is a **Design Basis Accident (DBA) LOCA** (also called a "small-break LOCA" if the break is below a certain size threshold, typically 4–6 inches).

**Step 2 — Determine Plant State vs. Design Envelope**

$$
\Delta P_{RCS} = P_{initial} - P_{final} = 155 \text{ bar} - 110 \text{ bar} = 45 \text{ bar drop}
$$

This pressure drop is within the **LOCA High-Pressure Injection Setpoint** of the Safety Injection System (SIS). Therefore, the plant *is operating within its analysed envelope*.

$$
\text{Plant State} \subseteq \text{DBA Envelope} \Rightarrow \text{EOPs apply (not SAMGs)}
$$

**Step 3 — Select the Procedure Family**

Using the procedure hierarchy from Section 2.1:

- ❌ **NOP** — Not applicable; this is not normal operation.
- ❌ **AOP** — Not applicable; the event has progressed beyond an AOO.
- ✅ **EOP** — Applicable. The symptom-based EOP for "Low RCS Pressure + High Containment Radiation" is triggered.
- ❌ **SAMG** — Not yet applicable. SAMGs are entered *only if* EOPs fail to restore critical safety functions.

**Step 4 — Map to Defence-in-Depth Levels**

The following aligned equation formalizes the safety function response:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DiD Level 1 (Conservative Design)} &: \text{Verified — break is within design basis} \\
\text{DiD Level 2 (Operational Control)} &: \text{OLCs maintained throughout} \\
\text{DiD Level 3 (Accident Control)} &: \text{EOPs + SIS injection active} \\
\text{DiD Level 4 (Severe Accident Mgmt)} &: \text{Not required at this point} \\
\text{DiD Level 5 (Emergency Response)} &: \text{Site emergency declared (Alert level)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision: Operator Action**

The Reactor Operator's first action per the EOP is:
1. Verify **Reactor Trip** (DiD Level 1, passive).
2. Verify **Safety Injection Actuation** (DiD Level 3, active).
3. Verify **Containment Isolation** (DiD Level 3, active).
4. Proceed to **Recovery Procedure** once RCS pressure stabilizes above 120 bar and core outlet temperature drops.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Dose Calculation under ALARA

> **Problem:** A nuclear plant has 500 workers. The annual individual doses are:
> - 200 workers: 2 mSv each
> - 200 workers: 5 mSv each
> - 80 workers: 10 mSv each
> - 20 workers: 18 mSv each
>
> **Compute (a)** Average annual dose, **(b)** Collective Effective Dose, and **(c)** verify compliance with the 20 mSv/yr (5-yr average) occupational limit.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Average Individual Dose**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{H} &= \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{4} N_j \cdot H_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{4} N_j} \\
&= \dfrac{(200 \times 2) + (200 \times 5) + (80 \times 10) + (20 \times 18)}{200 + 200 + 80 + 20} \\
&= \dfrac{400 + 1000 + 800 + 360}{500} \\
&= \dfrac{2560}{500} \\
&= 5.12 \text{ mSv/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Collective Effective Dose (SED)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \sum_{j} \overline{H_j} \cdot N_j = 2560 \text{ man-mSv} = 2.56 \text{ man-Sv}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compliance Check**

- All individual doses are $\leq 20$ mSv/yr (the statutory annual limit). ✅
- Maximum worker (18 mSv) is below the 5-yr average limit of 20 mSv. ✅
- For 5-yr average compliance, this worker's 5-yr cumulative must remain $\leq 100$ mSv.

$$
H_{5\text{-yr, max}} = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ mSv} \quad \text{(boundary)}
$$

$$
\text{Worker's 5-yr sum} = 18 + H_1 + H_2 + H_3 + H_4 \leq 100
$$

If $H_{i,\text{prior}} = 18$ for all 4 prior years: $4 \times 18 + 18 = 90$ mSv $\leq 100$ mSv. ✅

**Step 4 — ALARA Verification (Best Practice)**

The plant's average dose (5.12 mSv) is well below the limit. However, ALARA requires the plant to investigate **dose trending** and implement further reductions if economically reasonable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical ALARA Trigger in Industry:** If collective dose trends exceed a 3-year rolling average, the plant must conduct a formal ALARA review (e.g., use of robotic tooling, additional shielding, decontamination campaigns).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — NOP vs. AOP vs. EOP vs. SAMG

| Attribute | NOP | AOP | EOP | SAMG |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|------|
| **Plant State** | Normal | AOO | DBA | BDBA / Severe |
| **Trigger** | Scheduled | Alarm or symptom | Symptom-based critical safety function challenge | Failure of EOP |
| **Time to Execute** | Hours | Minutes | Minutes to hours | Hours to days |
| **Operator Focus** | Efficiency | Restore normal | Preserve 3 safety functions | Preserve containment, limit releases |
| **Validation** | Pre-approved by PORC | Pre-approved | Pre-approved + AERB-validated | Pre-approved |
| **Format** | Step-by-step | Decision trees + steps | Symptom-based critical safety function trees | Flowcharts with severe accident parameters |
| **CDF Relevance** | Affects LCO compliance | Contributes to AOO frequency | Boundaries CDF | Boundaries LERF |

---

## 3.4 Maintenance Rule Worked Example

> **Problem:** A 1000 MWe PWR has 4 redundant Emergency Diesel Generators (EDGs). In 2024, EDG-2 was inoperable for 240 hours unplanned and 60 hours planned. Total scheduled critical hours = 8000. Compute Maintenance Rule (a)(1) metric and decide the action.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Unavailability Components**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{unplanned}} &= \dfrac{240}{8000} = 0.030 \\
q_{\text{planned}} &= \dfrac{60}{8000} = 0.0075 \\
q_{\text{total}} &= 0.030 + 0.0075 = 0.0375 \quad \text{i.e., } 3.75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Threshold Check**

Maintenance Rule (a)(1) goal: Unavailability $\leq 5\%$.

$$
3.75\% \leq 5\% \quad \checkmark \text{ (within goal)}
$$

**Step 3 — Action**

- Routine monitoring. No immediate corrective action required.
- However, since *unplanned* is the dominant contributor (80% of total), the plant must perform **root cause analysis (RCA)** on EDG-2 failure patterns to prevent recurrence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Important Note:** Maintenance Rule (a)(1) only considers *unplanned* unavailability; (a)(2) considers both planned and unplanned. This distinction is a frequently tested 3-mark concept.

---
