# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* Define the term "Effective Dose" as used in radiation protection regulation. State the ICRP 103 annual dose limit for occupational exposure and for members of the public.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Effective Dose (E):** The tissue-weighted sum of the equivalent doses in all specified tissues and organs of the body, given by the equation:

$$E = \sum_{T} w_T \cdot H_T = \sum_{T} w_T \sum_{R} w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$$

where $w_T$ is the tissue weighting factor (sum of $w_T$ = 1), $H_T$ is the equivalent dose to tissue $T$, $w_R$ is the radiation weighting factor, and $D_{T,R}$ is the absorbed dose to tissue $T$ from radiation type $R$. The unit is Sievert (Sv).

**ICRP 103 annual limits:**
- **Occupational (radiation workers):** **20 mSv per year**, averaged over defined periods of 5 years, with the additional restriction that the dose in any single year shall not exceed **50 mSv**.
- **Members of the public:** **1 mSv per year**, averaged over 5 years (special circumstances up to 5 mSv in a single year if the 5-year average is maintained).

*(Total: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for limits.)*

---

> **Question 2.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* List the **five stages of licensing** in the Indian nuclear regulatory process, and name the statutory body that issues the operating licence for a nuclear power plant.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five stages of licensing in the Indian nuclear regulatory process (administered by the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board — AERB**) are:

1. **Siting Consent** — site evaluation for suitability (seismology, hydrology, population, exclusion zone, EPZ).
2. **Construction Consent** — approval of design, PSAR, construction procedures.
3. **Commissioning Consent (OLAC — Operating Licence at Commissioning)** — authorisation to load fuel and operate the plant.
4. **Operating Consent / Renewal** — issued after periodic safety reviews (every 10 years).
5. **Decommissioning Consent** — issued after permanent shutdown and post-operational clean-out plan approval.

**Statutory authority:** The **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)**, established under the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962** *(2 marks for stages, 1 mark for AERB identification).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*
> **(a)** Explain in detail the role and functions of the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) in the Indian nuclear safety regulatory framework. Discuss its organisational structure and its relationship with the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE). (7 marks)
> **(b)** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the **licensing process for a nuclear power plant in India** from siting to decommissioning. Highlight the role of the Preliminary and Final Safety Analysis Reports (PSAR/FSAR). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role and Functions of AERB (7 marks):**

The **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** was constituted on **15 November 1983** by the Government of India, *vide* a Resolution under the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962**, to carry out the regulatory and safety functions of the Department of Atomic Energy (DAE).

**Statutory Backing:** Although AERB derives its authority from the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962**, it is an *attached office* of DAE. The Chairman of AERB is typically an eminent scientist, and the Board has members from BARC, IGCAR, NPCIL, Ministry of Health, and external experts (academia and civil society).

**Core Functions (6 bullet points → ~6 marks):**
1. **Framing Safety Policies and Standards** — AERB issues Safety Codes (mandatory), Safety Guides (recommendatory), and Safety Manuals (procedural) for siting, design, construction, commissioning, operation, and decommissioning of nuclear and radiation facilities.
2. **Issuing Consents (Licences)** — Multi-stage licensing: Siting, Construction, Commissioning (OLAC), Operation, Modifications, Decommissioning.
3. **Safety Review and Assessment** — Review of PSAR, FSAR, in-service inspection reports, periodic safety review reports.
4. **Regulatory Inspections** — Routine, announced, and unannounced inspections; enforcement of stipulations.
5. **Radiation Surveillance and Emergency Preparedness** — National Dose Registry, environmental monitoring, coordination of national-level nuclear and radiological emergency plans.
6. **Enforcement** — Power to issue directives, suspend or revoke consents, and recommend prosecution under Sections 17, 18, and 24 of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962.

**Relationship with DAE:**
- AERB is *organisationally* under DAE for administrative purposes but is *functionally independent* in regulatory decisions.
- This functional independence was reaffirmed by a 2014 Supreme Court observation and is consistent with **IAEA GSR Part 1, Requirement 2** (independence of the regulatory body).
- Critics argue that *true* independence requires AERB to report to Parliament (like the NRC in the USA reports to Congress), not to DAE.

**(b) Licensing Process with Block Diagram (7 marks):**

**Step-by-step description:**

1. **Stage 1 — Siting Consent:**
   - NPCIL (or the operator) submits a **Site Evaluation Report** to AERB.
   - AERB's Siting Committee evaluates seismic, geotechnical, hydrological, meteorological, and demographic data.
   - An **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** is conducted under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).
   - **Output:** Siting Consent (with stipulations on exclusion zone, EPZ radius, environmental clearance).

2. **Stage 2 — Construction Consent:**
   - The operator submits the **Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR)**, which includes design basis, safety design, accident analysis, and preliminary PSA Level-1 results.
   - Multiple AERB directorates (Nuclear Safety, Radiological Safety, Industrial-Fire Safety, Civil-Structural Safety) review the PSAR in parallel.
   - **Output:** Construction Consent (with a list of regulatory hold-points).

3. **Stage 3 — Commissioning Consent (OLAC):**
   - After construction, the operator submits the **Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR)**, which incorporates as-built data, commissioning test results, and updated PSA Level-1, 2, 3.
   - AERB conducts pre-commissioning inspections and audits.
   - **Output:** **Operating Licence at Commissioning (OLAC)** → Authorisation to load fuel, achieve first criticality, and start power generation.

4. **Stage 4 — Operating Consent (Renewal):**
   - Issued after **Periodic Safety Review (PSR)** every 10 years (IAEA NS-G-2.10).
   - PSR includes deterministic safety reassessment, PSA update, ageing management review, and emergency planning update.

5. **Stage 5 — Decommissioning Consent:**
   - Issued after permanent shutdown.
   - Operator submits a **Decommissioning Plan** (immediate dismantling, deferred dismantling, or entombment strategies).
   - **Output:** Consent to commence decommissioning, with the formation of a **decommissioning fund**.

**Block Diagram (within answer script):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stage 1: Siting] -->|Site Report| B[Stage 2: Construction]
    B -->|PSAR| C[Stage 3: Commissioning OLAC]
    C -->|FSAR + Tests| D[Stage 4: Operation + Periodic Safety Review]
    D -->|Every 10 years| D
    D -->|After 60-100 years| E[Stage 5: Decommissioning]
    B -->|Parallel: EIA| F[MoEFCC]
    C -->|Pre-criticality tests| G[AERB Hold-Points]
    D -->|Annual Dose Records| H[National Dose Registry]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often skip the **PSAR vs. FSAR** distinction. **PSAR is submitted *before* construction** (preliminary design) and forms the basis of the construction consent. **FSAR is submitted *before* commissioning** (as-built final design) and forms the basis of the OLAC. Confusing the two will cost you **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

> ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* (Alternative choice)
> **(a)** Discuss the principles of **Defense-in-Depth (DiD)** and explain how each of the **five levels** is implemented in a modern Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR). (7 marks)
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **nuclear safety regulatory frameworks of India and the USA** with respect to statutory basis, organisational independence, licensing stages, and enforcement powers. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Defense-in-Depth — Five Levels (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Defense-in-Depth (DiD) is the IAEA-recommended central safety philosophy (INSAG-10) that mandates multiple, *redundant*, *independent*, and *diverse* layers of protection, so that failure of one level does not lead to failure of subsequent levels.

**The Five Levels of DiD (with PWR implementation):**

**Level 1 — Conservative Design and High-Quality Construction**
- Application of **conservative design margins**: e.g., Design Basis Accident (DBA) envelope, ASME Section III code for primary coolant piping.
- Quality assurance during fabrication (e.g., welding procedures, NDT inspections).

**Level 2 — Operational Limits, Surveillance, and Control**
- **Technical Specifications (Tech-Specs)** define Limiting Conditions for Operation (LCOs).
- Reactor protection system (RPS) trips on neutronic, thermal-hydraulic, and process parameter deviations.
- 1E-class Instrumentation & Control (I&C) with redundancy and diversity.

**Level 3 — Engineered Safety Features (ESFs)**
- **Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS)**: high-pressure injection (HPSI), low-pressure injection (LPSI), accumulators.
- **Containment Spray System** for heat and fission-product removal.
- **Containment Isolation System** (closed on High Containment Pressure signal).
- These are *passive* and *active* systems — e.g., AP1000 has passive ECCS relying on gravity and natural circulation.

**Level 4 — Severe Accident Management**
- **Core Catcher** (e.g., EPR has a core catcher designed to contain molten corium).
- **Filtered Containment Venting System (FCVS)** to control overpressure in severe accidents.
- Hydrogen recombiners (e.g., PARs — Passive Autocatalytic Recombiners).
- Severe Accident Management Guidelines (SAMGs).

**Level 5 — Emergency Preparedness and Response**
- **On-site Emergency Plan** (within 1.6 km exclusion zone).
- **Off-site Emergency Plan** (within 16 km EPZ in USA, as per NUREG-0654).
- Evacuation plans, KI pill distribution, public alerting systems.
- In India: AERB Safety Code **AERB/SC/E-3** (Emergency Preparedness) governs this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the 5 levels:** "**D-O-E-S-E**" — *Design, Operate, Engineered features, Severe accident, Emergency response.*

**(b) India vs. USA — Comparative Framework (7 marks):**

| Dimension | India (AERB) | USA (NRC) |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Statutory Basis** | Atomic Energy Act, 1962 (Sections 6, 17, 18, 22, 23, 24, 25 govern regulatory functions) | Atomic Energy Act, 1954 (as amended by Energy Reorganization Act 1974) |
| **Organisational Independence** | AERB is *functionally* independent but *administratively* under DAE (which also promotes nuclear power) | NRC is fully independent — 5 Commissioners, appointed by the President, confirmed by the Senate, reports directly to Congress |
| **Licensing Stages** | 4–5 stages (Siting, Construction, OLAC, Operation, Decommissioning) | 5 stages (Limited Work Authorisation, Construction Permit, Operating Licence, Decommissioning, plus License Renewal every 20–40 years) |
| **Dose Limits (Worker)** | 20 mSv/yr (avg 5 yr) — ICRP aligned | 50 mSv/yr — codified in 10 CFR 20 |
| **Enforcement Power** | Powers under Sections 17, 18, 24, 25 of AE Act; can suspend/revoke consent; criminal prosecution possible | Civil penalties (10 CFR 2.205), orders (10 CFR 2.202), licence suspension, revocation; "Backfit Rule" (10 CFR 50.109) |
| **Public Participation** | Limited (no public hearing in licence issuance) | Extensive — mandatory public hearings (10 CFR 50, Part 2) before licence issuance and renewal |
| **Inspection Force** | AERB-designated inspectors; integrated with DAE units | Resident Inspectors at every operating site + Regional Inspectors in 4 regions |
| **International Treaty Status** | Signatory to CNS, CSC, CPPNM amendments | Founding member of CNS and CSC |
| **Strengths of Each** | Centralised, technically deep, AERB codes are detailed | Publicly transparent, judicially reviewed, strong enforcement track record (e.g., post-TMI, post-9/11) |
| **Weaknesses of Each** | "Self-regulation" criticism; lack of public hearings | Perceived regulatory capture, length of licence renewal (e.g., Vogtle 3&4) |

**Synthesis (closing 1–2 lines):** Both frameworks aspire to IAEA GSR Part 1 compliance. India's framework is evolving toward greater transparency, and the 2014 *AERB Restructuring Committee Report* and the 2020 *Nuclear Safety Regulatory Authority (NSRA) Bill* (pending in Parliament) propose to make AERB a *fully independent statutory body* on the NRC pattern.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing AERB with AEC.** AEC is *policy body*; AERB is *regulatory body*. Many students mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the 5th level of DiD** (Emergency Preparedness). Students often list only 4 levels.
> 3. **Stating the dose limit as 50 mSv** for India. India's ICRP-aligned limit is **20 mSv/yr** averaged; **50 mSv** is the USA-specific limit. Wrong attribution costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing PSAR with FSAR.** (See warning in Question A above.)
> 5. **Not mentioning the international treaties** (CNS, CPPNM, CSC). The KTU 2024 syllabus *specifically* requires this in Module 3.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The **Safety Regulation Process** has **four pillars**: Statutory Foundation, Independent Regulator, Multi-Stage Licensing, and Inspection/Enforcement.
- [ ] In India, the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB)** is the regulator; it was constituted in **1983** under the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962**.
- [ ] The **five stages of licensing** in India are: **Siting → Construction → Commissioning (OLAC) → Operation (with PSR every 10 years) → Decommissioning**.
- [ ] **PSAR** is submitted *before* construction; **FSAR** is submitted *before* commissioning. Do not confuse them.
- [ ] **Effective Dose (E)** is the sum $E = \sum_T w_T H_T$. ICRP 103 worker limit: **20 mSv/yr averaged over 5 years** (max 50 mSv in any one year). Public limit: **1 mSv/yr**.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth has 5 levels**: Design, Operational Control, Engineered Safety Features, Severe Accident Management, Emergency Preparedness (mnemonic: *D-O-E-S-E*).
- [ ] **Quantitative safety goals** (from IAEA INSAG-12 / NRC): **CDF < 10⁻⁵/reactor-yr**; **LERF < 10⁻⁶/reactor-yr**.
- [ ] **International framework** is built on IAEA Safety Standards (SF, GSR, GSG, SSG), which are *not* legally binding on member states unless voluntarily adopted.
- [ ] India is a signatory to the **Convention on Nuclear Safety (CNS, 1994)**, the **Joint Convention on Spent Fuel and Waste (1997)**, and the **Convention on Supplementary Compensation (CSC)**.
- [ ] **AERB Safety Codes are mandatory** ("shall"); **Safety Guides are recommendatory** ("should").
- [ ] **Indian nuclear liability** under the **Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act, 2010** is capped at **₹1500 crore** (≈ SDR 300 million).
- [ ] **AEC** = policy body under PMO; **AERB** = regulatory body under DAE; **NPCIL** = operator; **BARC** = research. Memorize this hierarchy.
- [ ] Dose-rate attenuation through a shield: $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$. Useful for solving regulatory shielding problems.
- [ ] **IAEA GSR Part 1 Requirement 2** mandates *effective independence* of the regulatory body from promotional activities.
- [ ] KTU 2024 examiners specifically test: the **5 levels of DiD**, the **dose limits (20/1 mSv)**, and the **distinction between Codes and Guides** of AERB. Be ready with all three.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Awareness and Emergency Preparedness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Public Awareness and Emergency Preparedness** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) **Public Awareness and Emergency Preparedness** in the nuclear safety context refers to the integrated set of policies, plans, procedures, and proactive communication measures that ensure the public, local authorities, and emergency responders are informed, trained, and ready to act in the event of a nuclear or radiological emergency. It is a mandatory pre-licensing and operational requirement governed by the **International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) General Safety Requirements (GSR) Part 7** and the **International Commission on Radiological Protection (ICRP) Publication 109 / 146** framework. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > Emergency preparedness is the capability to take actions that will effectively mitigate the consequences of a nuclear or radiological emergency for human health and safety, quality of life, property, and the environment. It encompasses pre-emergency planning, training, public information, and post-event recovery. The system is structured around **three pillars of emergency preparedness**: 1. **Pre-Emergency Phase:** Planning, public education, drills, and infrastructure. 2. **Emergency Phase:** Activation of response, protective actions, communication. 3. **Post-Emergency Phase:** Recovery, medical follow-up, and lessons learned. ### 1.2 Key Terminology (Glossary) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **EPZ (Emergency Planning Zone)** | A geographical area around a nuclear facility for which pre-planning for emergency response is mandated. | | **PAG (Protective Action Guide)** | A projected radiological dose level at which a specific protective action is recommended. | | **EAL (Emergency Action Level)** | A pre-defined, observable threshold that triggers a specific emergency classification. | | **ICRP** | International Commission on Radiological Protection. | | **IAEA** | International Atomic Energy Agency. | | **Thyroid Blocking** | Administration of stable iodine (KI) to prevent uptake of radioiodine. | | **Sheltering** | Staying indoors to reduce inhalation and external exposure from a passing plume. | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Fire Drill Concept** > Imagine your college campus. Every semester, a fire alarm test is conducted. You are trained to (a) recognize the alarm, (b) know your nearest exit, (c) gather at the assembly point, and (d) wait for instructions. You do not panic because you have been **trained, informed, and rehearsed**. > > **Nuclear Emergency Preparedness is the same principle, scaled up to a city level.** > > Just as a fire drill involves the campus, the fire department, and the administration, nuclear emergency preparedness involves the **plant operator, the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB), district administration, hospitals, police, media, and every household** within the EPZ. ### 1.4 Why Public Awareness is Critical The **Three Mile Island (1979)**, **Chernobyl (1986)**, and **Fukushima Daiichi (2011)** accidents all exposed one common weakness: **the failure to communicate effectively with the public during a crisis.** Pregnant women in Poland drank contaminated milk after Chernobyl because iodine prophylaxis was not communicated. In Fukushima, the 30 km exclusion zone caused mass confusion due to delayed and unclear instructions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The AERB Safety Code **AERB/SC/N-1** and the National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) guidelines mandate annual public awareness programs within a **16 km radius** of every Indian nuclear power plant (e.g., Kakrapar, Kudankulam, Tarapur, Kaiga, Kalpakkam). ### 1.5 International Framework & Standards - **IAEA GSR Part 7 (2015):** *Preparedness and Response for a Nuclear or Radiological Emergency.* Replaces the older GS-R-2 (2002). - **ICRP Publication 109 (2008):** Application of the Commission's Recommendations to the protection of people living in long-term contaminated areas. - **WHO (World Health Organization):** Coordinates public health response. - **Convention on Early Notification of a Nuclear Accident (1986):** Triggered after Chernobyl. - **Convention on Assistance in the Case of a Nuclear Accident (1986):** Provides framework for international aid. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Emergency Classification Levels (IAEA NE 1.16 / AERB) The IAEA standardizes nuclear emergencies into a graded response framework. Each level demands progressively larger public protective actions. | Class | Trigger Condition | Public Action | |---|---|---| | **Notification of Unusual Event (NOUE)** | Minor abnormality, no release | Enhanced monitoring | | **Alert** | Plant safety system degraded, possible degradation | Standby by emergency teams | | **Site Area Emergency (SAE)** | Major degradation, possible release | Activate off-site response | | **General Emergency (GE)** | Imminent/actual significant release | **PUBLIC PROTECTIVE ACTIONS EXECUTED** | | **Beyond Design Basis Accident** | Severe accident with core damage | Extended evacuation, KI distribution | > [!NOTE] > **Indian Context (NPCIL/AERB):** India uses a **4-tier classification** aligned with the IAEA scale: NOUE, Alert, Site Emergency, and **Plant Emergency** (the highest, triggering evacuation of the 5 km zone and sheltering up to 16 km). ### 2.2 Emergency Planning Zones (EPZ) — The "Concentric Ring" Concept The EPZ is a **geographically predefined zone** around a nuclear plant where pre-planning is performed. The size is chosen based on **worst-case accident analysis** and **meteorological studies**. | Zone | Radius (Typical) | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Plume Exposure Pathway EPZ (PAZ)** | **0 – 16 km** | Acute protective actions (sheltering, evacuation, KI) | | **Ingestion Pathway EPZ (IPZ)** | **16 – 80 km** | Food/water control, agriculture bans | | **Extended Planning Distance (EPD)** | Up to **300 km** | Long-term food chain surveillance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 16 km?** The 16 km PAZ is derived from Gaussian plume dispersion modeling for a **Medium Break Loss of Coolant Accident (MBLOCA)** with a stack height of 100 m and a 1-hour average wind speed of 1 m/s. Beyond 16 km, projected doses fall below the PAG for sheltering. ### 2.3 Protective Action Guides (PAGs) — The "Decision Triggers" PAGs are **dose thresholds** above which a specific protective action is justified. Below the PAG, the action is not warranted. | Protective Action | PAG Threshold (Effective Dose) | Source | |---|---|---| | **Sheltering** | $\geq$ **5 mSv** projected | ICRP / IAEA | | **Evacuation** | $\geq$ **50 mSv** projected (acute) or 100 mSv (longer term) | ICRP 109 | | **Stable Iodine (KI)** | $\geq$ **50 mGy** projected committed thyroid dose | IAEA GSR Part 7 | | **Relocation** | $\geq$ **30 mSv** first-year dose | ICRP 109 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Averted Dose Principle:** The PAG is based on the **averted dose** (the dose avoided by taking the action), not the projected dose. This justifies the small inconvenience of evacuation against the long-term cancer risk. ### 2.4 Public Awareness Strategies The public within the EPZ must be trained through: 1. **Door-to-door awareness drives** (NPCIL annual programs). 2. **Information brochures, pamphlets, and local radio broadcasts.** 3. **Public address systems and SMS alert networks.** 4. **School and college education modules.** 5. **Mock evacuation drills (annual / biennial).** 6. **Designation of Assembly Points** (marked on Google Maps and public boards). ### 2.5 The Emergency Response Organization (ERO) Every Indian nuclear plant maintains a **three-tier emergency response structure**: - **Tier 1 (Plant Level):** Main Control Room (MCR), Technical Support Center (TSC), Operational Support Center (OSC). - **Tier 2 (Site Level):** Emergency Operations Facility (EOF), Site Emergency Director. - **Tier 3 (Off-Site Level):** District Magistrate, State Disaster Authority, AERB Crisis Centre (Mumbai). ### 2.6 The Thyroid Blocking Mechanism (Detailed) Radioactive iodine isotopes ($^{131}$I, $^{133}$I) are released in significant quantities during reactor accidents. The thyroid gland concentrates iodine, leading to thyroid cancer (especially in children). **Mechanism:** Saturating the thyroid with **stable iodine (KI — Potassium Iodide)** blocks the gland's uptake of radioactive iodine via the **Wolff-Chaikoff effect** (a feedback inhibition by excess iodine on the sodium-iodide symporter in thyroid cells). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Pediatric Dose:** 50 mg KI for adults; **25 mg** for children aged 3–12; **12.5 mg** for infants (per WHO 2017 guidelines). ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Effective Dose** | $E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$ | mSv | | **Committed Dose** | $H_T(\tau) = \int_0^\tau \dot{H}_T(t)\, dt$ | mSv | | **Plume Concentration (Gaussian)** | $\chi(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Bq/m$^3$ | | **Inhalation Dose Rate** | $\dot{H}_{inh} = BR \cdot \sum_i (C_i \cdot DCF_i)$ | Sv/h | | **Thyroid Committed Dose** | $D_{thyroid} = A_{inh} \cdot DCF_{thyroid}(age)$ | mGy | | **Sheltering Dose Reduction Factor (DRF)** | $DRF = \dfrac{D_{\text{outdoor}}}{D_{\text{indoor}}}$ | Dimensionless, **typically 2 – 5** | | **Averted Dose** | $D_{averted} = D_{projected} - D_{with\ action}$ | mSv | | **PAG: Sheltering** | $D_{projected} \geq 5$ mSv | Threshold | | **PAG: Evacuation** | $D_{projected} \geq 50$ mSv | Acute | | **PAG: KI Administration** | $D_{thyroid} \geq 50$ mGy | Committed | | **Evacuation Time Formula** | $T_{evac} = \dfrac{N_{pop}}{R_{vehicles} \times C_{veh}}$ | hours | | **Plume Arrival Time** | $t_{arrival} = \dfrac{x}{u_{mean}}$ | hours | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constants to Memorize:** > - Average human breathing rate $BR$ $\approx$ **0.83 m³/h** (resting adult, ICRP 71). > - Thyroid dose conversion factor (adult inhalation of $^{131}$I) $\approx$ **1.5 × 10⁻⁷ Sv/Bq**. > - Stack release height factor: ground level concentration is maximal for $H_s / \sigma_z \approx 2$. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Plume Arrival Time and Dose Projection **Problem:** A nuclear accident releases a plume of $^{131}$I. The plant stack is at **100 m**. The release rate is **$Q = 1 \times 10^{12}$ Bq/s** for **2 hours**. The mean wind speed at stack height is **$u = 5$ m/s**. A village is located **5 km downwind**. Using Pasquill-Gifford stability class D (neutral), calculate the **time of plume arrival** and the **peak ground concentration** at the village. (Use $\sigma_y = 80$ m and $\sigma_z = 40$ m at 5 km for class D.) **Step 1: Plume Arrival Time** The plume travels with the mean wind speed $u$. $$ t_{arrival} = \frac{x}{u} $$ $$ t_{arrival} = \frac{5000 \text{ m}}{5 \text{ m/s}} = 1000 \text{ s} $$ Converting to hours: $$ t_{arrival} = \frac{1000}{3600} \approx 0.278 \text{ hours} \approx 16.7 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 2: Peak Ground-Level Concentration (Plume Centerline, y = 0, z = 0)** Using the Gaussian Plume model: $$ \chi(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H_s^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ The exponential term accounts for the **ground reflection** plus stack-height plume rise effect. The simplest form (ignoring reflection at the surface, $z = 0$): $$ \chi = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H_s^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ (Note: Factor of 2 difference for ground-level ground reflection, depending on convention.) **Step 3: Plug in values** $$ \chi = \frac{(1 \times 10^{12})}{\pi \times 5 \times 80 \times 40} \exp\left(-\frac{(100)^2}{2 \times (40)^2}\right) $$ Calculate the denominator: $$ \pi \times 5 \times 80 \times 40 = \pi \times 16000 = 50265.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$ Calculate the exponent: $$ -\frac{10000}{2 \times 1600} = -\frac{10000}{3200} = -3.125 $$ $$ \exp(-3.125) = 0.0439 $$ Therefore: $$ \chi = \frac{1 \times 10^{12}}{50265.5} \times 0.0439 $$ $$ \chi = 1.989 \times 10^{7} \times 0.0439 $$ $$ \chi \approx 8.73 \times 10^{5} \text{ Bq/m}^3 $$ **Step 4: Inhalation Dose Rate for an Adult** The dose rate from inhalation is: $$ \dot{H}_{inh} = BR \times \chi \times DCF $$ Where: - $BR$ = **0.83 m³/h** (resting adult) - $DCF$ for $^{131}$I adult inhalation $\approx$ **1.5 × 10⁻⁷ Sv/Bq** (ICRP 71) $$ \dot{H}_{inh} = 0.83 \times 8.73 \times 10^5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7} $$ $$ \dot{H}_{inh} = 0.83 \times 1.31 \times 10^{-1} $$ $$ \dot{H}_{inh} \approx 0.1087 \text{ Sv/h} = 108.7 \text{ mSv/h} $$ **Step 5: Total Inhalation Dose (2-hour exposure)** $$ H_{total} = \dot{H}_{inh} \times t = 108.7 \times 2 = 217.4 \text{ mSv} $$ **Step 6: Compare with PAG** | PAG Type | Threshold | Our Calculation | Decision | |---|---|---|---| | Sheltering | $\geq$ 5 mSv | 217.4 mSv | **EXCEEDED** — Shelter advised | | Evacuation | $\geq$ 50 mSv | 217.4 mSv | **EXCEEDED** — Evacuate | | KI (thyroid) | $\geq$ 50 mGy | Approx. 217.4 mGy* | **EXCEEDED** — Distribute KI | *(Thyroid dose assumed roughly equal to effective dose for $^{131}$I in worst case — actual ICRP 71 value used in exam would be more specific.)* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Based on the projected dose of **217.4 mSv**, all three protective actions must be initiated. This is the kind of calculation KTU examiners love to test in a 14-mark question. ### 3.2 Derivation: Sheltering Dose Reduction Factor (DRF) **Problem:** A person is sheltering inside a concrete house. Outdoor gamma dose rate is **$\dot{D}_{out} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ Sv/h**. The DRF for a typical Indian concrete house is **3.5**. Calculate the indoor dose rate and the dose avoided after **6 hours** of sheltering. **Step 1: Indoor Dose Rate** $$ \dot{D}_{in} = \frac{\dot{D}_{out}}{DRF} $$ $$ \dot{D}_{in} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5} = 2.857 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Sv/h} $$ **Step 2: Dose Over 6 Hours (Indoor)** $$ D_{in} = \dot{D}_{in} \times t = 2.857 \times 10^{-5} \times 6 = 1.714 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Sv} = 0.1714 \text{ mSv} $$ **Step 3: Dose if No Sheltering (Outdoor)** $$ D_{out} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Sv} = 0.6 \text{ mSv} $$ **Step 4: Averted Dose** $$ D_{averted} = D_{out} - D_{in} = 0.6 - 0.1714 = 0.4286 \text{ mSv} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Sheltering reduced the dose by **~71%** in this scenario. This validates sheltering as a cost-effective protective action when evacuation is delayed. ### 3.3 Evacuation Time Estimation **Problem:** A village of **20,000 people** is to be evacuated. **100 buses** are available, each carrying **50 people**. One round trip takes **2 hours**. Calculate total evacuation time. **Step 1: Total Capacity per Trip** $$ C_{trip} = N_{bus} \times C_{bus} = 100 \times 50 = 5000 \text{ people/trip} $$ **Step 2: Number of Trips Required** $$ N_{trips} = \frac{P_{total}}{C_{trip}} = \frac{20000}{5000} = 4 \text{ trips} $$ **Step 3: Total Evacuation Time** $$ T_{evac} = N_{trips} \times T_{trip} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ hours} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** Plume arrival time was **~17 minutes** in Section 3.1. An 8-hour evacuation is too slow — **sheltering must be the immediate first action** while evacuation is being organized. This is the **"Phase Strategy"** of emergency response. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Logic for Protective Actions | Projected Dose | Sheltering? | Evacuation? | KI? | Food Ban? | |---|---|---|---|---| | < 5 mSv | No | No | No | No | | 5 – 50 mSv | **Yes** | No | No | No | | 50 – 100 mSv | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | No | | > 100 mSv | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Emergency Response Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Accident Initiating Event] --> node2{Detect and Classify} node2 -->|NOUE| node3A[Enhanced Monitoring] node2 -->|Alert| node3B[Activate Onsite Teams] node2 -->|Site Emergency| node3C[Notify Offsite Authorities] node2 -->|General Emergency| node3D[Execute Public Protective Actions] node3A --> node4[Emergency Operations Facility] node3B --> node4 node3C --> node4 node3D --> node4 node4 --> node5[Technical Support Center] node4 --> node6[Operational Support Center] node4 --> node7[Main Control Room] node4 --> node8[District Magistrate] node8 --> node9[Police and Traffic Control] node8 --> node10[Public Hospitals] node8 --> node11[Public Alert System] node11 --> node12[SMS and Sirens] node11 --> node13[All India Radio] node11 --> node14[Doordarshan] node12 --> node15[Public within 16 km EPZ] node13 --> node15 node14 --> node15 node15 --> node16[Shelter in Place] node15 --> node17[Evacuation to Assembly Point] node15 --> node18[KI Tablet Distribution] ``` ### 4.2 Emergency Planning Zone Schematic (Concentric Rings) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph zone1["INNER ZONE 0 to 5 km"] z1[Immediate Evacuation Zone
Pre-emptive Evacuation
All Hazards Level] end subgraph zone2["MIDDLE ZONE 5 to 16 km"] z2[Plume Exposure EPZ
Sheltering and KI
Evacuation if needed] end subgraph zone3["OUTER ZONE 16 to 80 km"] z3[Ingestion Pathway EPZ
Food and Water Control
Agriculture Surveillance] end subgraph zone4["EXTENDED ZONE 80 to 300 km"] z4[Extended Planning Distance
Long-term Monitoring
Trade and Tourism Control] end zone1 --> zone2 --> zone3 --> zone4 npp[REACTOR BUILDING] -.-> zone1 ``` ### 4.3 Public Awareness Communication Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD apex[Apex Body AERB and Crisis Group] apex --> state[State Disaster Authority] apex --> central[Central Government NDMA] state --> district[District Magistrate] district --> block[Block Development Officer] block --> panchayat[Gram Panchayat] panchayat --> family[Household Level] central --> media[Media and Telecom] media --> family family --> training[Annual Mock Drills] family --> school[School Awareness Programs] family --> siren[SMS Alert Network] ``` ### 4.4 Protective Action Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Accident Declared] --> monitor[Monitor Dose Rates] monitor --> calc[Project Doses to Public] calc --> q1{Dose less than 5 mSv?} q1 -->|Yes| none[No Action Required] q1 -->|No| q2{Dose 5 to 50 mSv?} q2 -->|Yes| shelter[Shelter in Place] q2 -->|No| q3{Dose greater than 50 mSv?} q3 -->|Yes| evac[Evacuate to Assembly Point] q3 -->|No| none shelter --> q4{Thyroid dose above 50 mGy?} evac --> q4 q4 -->|Yes| ki[Distribute KI Tablets] q4 -->|No| monitor2[Continue Monitoring] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs follow the **alpha-prefixed alphanumeric rule** (e.g., `node1`, `nppl`, `zone1`). All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric without markdown formatting inside the quotes. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Define Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ). List the two main types of EPZ and their typical radii for an Indian nuclear power plant.** **Model Answer:** An Emergency Planning Zone (EPZ) is a designated geographical area around a nuclear facility for which advance planning and preparation of protective actions for the public are undertaken. The two main types are: 1. **Plume Exposure Pathway EPZ (PAZ):** Radius of **0 to 16 km** around the plant. Used for acute protective actions such as sheltering, evacuation, and KI administration. 2. **Ingestion Exposure Pathway EPZ (IPZ):** Radius of **16 to 80 km**. Used for control of contaminated food, water, and agricultural produce. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for the two types with radii)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **What is a Protective Action Guide (PAG)? State the PAG for sheltering and for stable iodine (KI) administration.** **Model Answer:** A Protective Action Guide (PAG) is a projected dose level at which a specific protective action is recommended to reduce or avert public exposure during a nuclear emergency. - **PAG for Sheltering:** $\geq$ **5 mSv** projected effective dose. - **PAG for KI Administration:** $\geq$ **50 mGy** projected committed thyroid dose (especially in children). **(3 marks: 1 for PAG definition + 1 for each threshold value)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **four levels of emergency classification** prescribed by the AERB for an Indian nuclear power plant. For each level, describe the corresponding public awareness and protective action strategy. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A nuclear accident results in the release of $^{131}$I at a rate of **$Q = 5 \times 10^{11}$ Bq/s** for **3 hours** at stack height **$H_s$ = 100 m**. Wind speed $u$ = **4 m/s**. A village is located **8 km downwind** (plume centerline). Pasquill-Gifford class D dispersion coefficients at 8 km are $\sigma_y$ = **120 m** and $\sigma_z$ = **50 m**. The inhalation DCF for $^{131}$I is **1.5 × 10⁻⁷ Sv/Bq** and breathing rate is **0.83 m³/h**. Calculate: - (i) The peak ground-level air concentration. - (ii) The 3-hour inhalation dose to an unsheltered adult. - (iii) The dose if the person shelters indoors with **DRF = 3** for the full duration. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** See the table below for the four-level emergency classification. | Level | Trigger | Public Awareness / Action | |---|---|---| | **NOUE** | Minor abnormality, no release | Public kept informed via media; no action | | **Alert** | Safety function degraded | Standby alert in nearby villages | | **Site Emergency** | Major safety degradation, possible release | District authority activates; sirens tested | | **Plant Emergency** | Significant release imminent/ongoing | **Full public protective actions: shelter, evacuation, KI** | Public awareness strategy: - Door-to-door information, brochure distribution, local language media, school drills, mock evacuation, and SMS alerts. - All residents within **16 km** are pre-registered with household data. - Assembly points are pre-designated and GPS-mapped. - Hospitals are pre-equipped with **Prussian Blue** and **KI stocks**. - Police and traffic control plans are pre-coordinated with the District Magistrate. **(7 marks: 3 for classification table + 2 for action strategies + 2 for public awareness programs)** --- **Part (b):** Detailed step-by-step solution: **Step 1: Peak ground concentration at 8 km** $$ \chi = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H_s^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ Substituting values: $$ \chi = \frac{5 \times 10^{11}}{\pi \times 4 \times 120 \times 50} \exp\left(-\frac{10000}{2 \times 2500}\right) $$ Denominator: $$ \pi \times 4 \times 120 \times 50 = \pi \times 24000 = 75398.2 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$ Exponent: $$ -\frac{10000}{5000} = -2 $$ $$ \exp(-2) = 0.1353 $$ Therefore: $$ \chi = \frac{5 \times 10^{11}}{75398.2} \times 0.1353 $$ $$ \chi = 6.632 \times 10^6 \times 0.1353 $$ $$ \chi \approx 8.97 \times 10^5 \text{ Bq/m}^3 $$ **(2 marks for part i)** **Step 2: 3-hour inhalation dose (unsheltered)** $$ \dot{H}_{inh} = BR \times \chi \times DCF $$ $$ \dot{H}_{inh} = 0.83 \times 8.97 \times 10^5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7} $$ $$ \dot{H}_{inh} = 0.83 \times 0.1346 $$ $$ \dot{H}_{inh} \approx 0.1117 \text{ Sv/h} = 111.7 \text{ mSv/h} $$ Total dose over 3 hours: $$ H_{unsheltered} = 111.7 \times 3 = 335.1 \text{ mSv} $$ **(2 marks for part ii)** **Step 3: Indoor dose with DRF = 3** $$ H_{sheltered} = \frac{H_{unsheltered}}{DRF} = \frac{335.1}{3} \approx 111.7 \text{ mSv} $$ **(1 mark for part iii)** **Step 4: Decision and Recommendation** - Unsheltered dose: **335.1 mSv** — exceeds PAG for **evacuation (50 mSv)** and **KI (50 mGy)**. - Sheltered dose: **111.7 mSv** — still exceeds evacuation PAG, so **evacuation must follow sheltering**. **Recommendation:** Immediate **sheltering**, followed by **evacuation** of the 16 km zone, with **KI tablet distribution** to all residents, prioritizing children and pregnant women. **(2 marks for decision and recommendations)** --- **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **(a)** Discuss the role of the **public awareness program** as a pre-emergency preparedness measure. Include in your answer: - (i) Objectives of public awareness. - (ii) Communication channels used. - (iii) Stakeholders involved. - (iv) Frequency and content of mock drills. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **Gaussian Plume Model** for atmospheric dispersion of radioactive effluents. State the assumptions, write the equation, and discuss the role of **Pasquill stability classes**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a): Public Awareness Program** **(i) Objectives:** (2 marks) - Reduce panic and confusion during emergencies. - Ensure public knows what to do upon hearing sirens. - Build long-term trust between operator and community. - Enable effective self-protection (sheltering, KI ingestion, controlled evacuation). - Minimize psychosocial impact (e.g., Chernobyl-induced "radiophobia"). **(ii) Communication Channels:** (2 marks) - **Mass media:** All India Radio, Doordarshan, local FM. - **Print media:** Local newspapers, brochures in regional languages. - **Digital:** SMS alerts, dedicated mobile apps, social media (official NPCIL handles). - **Physical:** Wall paintings, hoardings, pamphlets, school textbooks. - **Community:** Village meetings, women's self-help groups, religious gatherings. **(iii) Stakeholders:** (2 marks) - **NPCIL** (Nuclear Power Corporation of India Ltd.) — operator and primary communicator. - **AERB** (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board) — independent oversight. - **District Magistrate / State Disaster Authority** — on-site coordination. - **NDMA** (National Disaster Management Authority) — national-level policy. - **Public health department** — medical response. - **Schools and colleges** — sustained education. - **Local Panchayats** — last-mile reach. - **Media** — information multiplier. **(iv) Frequency and Content of Mock Drills:** (1 mark) - **Onsite drill:** Twice a year. - **Offsite drill:** Annually with full district coordination. - **Integrated exercise:** Every 3 years with state-level participation. - **Content:** Siren activation, sheltering, evacuation routes, KI distribution, hospital drill, decontamination setup, media briefing simulation. --- **Part (b): Gaussian Plume Model** [See SECTION 4.1 for the architectural flow diagram of plume dispersion and emergency response. The full derivation and class discussion follow below.] **Assumptions:** (2 marks) 1. Steady-state release (constant $Q$). 2. Wind speed $u$ is constant and uniform in space and time. 3. The plume disperses **Gaussianly** in the crosswind ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions. 4. **No chemical reactions** or radioactive decay during transport (decay can be added via an effective decay constant). 5. Total reflection at the ground (no deposition initially). 6. Flat terrain (no topography). 7. Pasquill-Gifford stability class describes the turbulence. **Equation (Standard form):** (2 marks) $$ \chi(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \exp\left(-\frac{(z - H_s)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ For **ground-level centerline** ($y = 0$, $z = 0$): $$ \chi(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{H_s^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) $$ **Role of Pasquill Stability Classes:** (2 marks) Pasquill defined 6 classes (A–F) based on **solar insolation** and **wind speed**: | Class | Stability | $\sigma_y$ growth | $\sigma_z$ growth | Use case | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | Very Unstable | Fast | Very Fast | Strong daytime sun, low wind | | B | Unstable | Moderate-fast | Fast | Daytime, moderate wind | | C | Slightly Unstable | Moderate | Moderate | Daytime, stronger wind | | D | **Neutral** | Moderate | Moderate | Overcast / nighttime / high wind | | E | Stable | Slow | Slow | Clear night, low wind | | F | Very Stable | Very slow | Very slow | Clear night, calm | - Class **A–C** produce **wide plumes** (high turbulence) — the ground concentration spreads over a large area but is lower in peak. - Class **D** is the **default** for emergency planning and conservatively chosen for dose calculations. - Class **E–F** produce **narrow, concentrated plumes** (low turbulence) — high peak ground concentration close to the stack. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why D is the design basis:** It represents the most common meteorological condition and provides a **realistic, conservative** estimate for routine releases. For accidents, classes E and F are often used to assess worst-case (downwind residents). **Conclusion:** The Gaussian Plume Model is the foundation of all nuclear emergency dose assessments. It links the release rate $Q$ to the ground concentration $\chi$, which then drives the projected dose and the consequent protective action decisions. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by breathing rate $BR$** in the dose calculation — examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. > 2. **Mixing up inhalation dose and thyroid dose** — these use different DCFs. Inhalation dose is in **Sv**, thyroid committed dose in **Gy** for $^{131}$I. > 3. **Wrong units:** $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are in **meters**, $u$ in **m/s**, $Q$ in **Bq/s** — final $\chi$ is in **Bq/m³**. > 4. **Not converting time units** (seconds vs hours) — leads to factor-of-3600 errors. > 5. **Forgetting the DRF** when asked about sheltering vs unsheltered dose. > 6. **In Part A questions,** students often write only the definition and skip the numerical threshold — examiners expect the **number**, not just the concept. > 7. **Confusing EPZ and EPD** — EPZ is for **public protective actions**, EPD is for **long-term food chain surveillance**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** > > - **EPZ:** 0–16 km PAZ, 16–80 km IPZ, 80–300 km EPD. **Memorize the radii.** > - **PAGs:** Sheltering 5 mSv, Evacuation 50 mSv, KI 50 mGy (thyroid), Relocation 30 mSv (first year). > - **Emergency Levels (4):** NOUE → Alert → Site Emergency → Plant Emergency (Indian classification). > - **IAEA Levels (5):** NOUE → Alert → SAE → GE → Beyond Design Basis. > - **Breathing rate (adult resting):** **0.83 m³/h.** > - **DCF for $^{131}$I (adult inhalation):** **1.5 × 10⁻⁷ Sv/Bq.** > - **Wolff-Chaikoff effect:** KI blocks thyroid uptake of radioactive iodine. > - **Sheltering DRF:** 2 – 5 (typical concrete house ~ 3.5). > - **Prussian Blue** is used for **Cs-137** decorporation; **KI** is for **I-131**. > - **Stakeholders:** NPCIL, AERB, NDMA, District Magistrate, Public. > - **International:** IAEA GSR Part 7 (2015) replaced GS-R-2; ICRP 109 guides. > - **Indian radius:** 16 km PAZ, 80 km IPZ, **16 km** KI pre-distribution zone for children. > - **Convention on Early Notification (1986):** Post-Chernobyl treaty. > - **Three Mile Island (1979), Chernobyl (1986), Fukushima (2011):** All exposed the criticality of public communication. > - **Gaussian Plume equation:** $\chi = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\left(-\dfrac{H_s^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ — memorize the form. > - **Evacuation priority:** Children, pregnant women, and the elderly first. > - **Dosimeter types:** TLD (Thermoluminescent Dosimeter) for environmental monitoring. > - **Pre-distribution of KI:** Done within **5 km** in India, in advance, in pre-packed kits. ---

topics.title: Accident Case Studies - Three Mile Island Accident and Chernobyl Accident
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accident Case Studies - Three Mile Island Accident and Chernobyl Accident

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Three Mile Island (TMI) Accident — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (TMI, 1979):**
> The Three Mile Island accident was a partial nuclear meltdown of Unit 2 of the Three Mile Island Nuclear Generating Station near Harrisburg, Pennsylvania, USA, occurring on **March 28, 1979**. It is classified as **INES Level 5 (Accident with Wider Consequences)** and represents the most significant commercial nuclear power plant accident in U.S. history, primarily driven by a combination of **mechanical failure** (stuck-open pressure relief valve), **instrumentation ambiguity**, and **operator cognitive error** under stress.

### 1.2 The Chernobyl Accident — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Chernobyl, 1986):**
> The Chernobyl disaster was a **catastrophic nuclear accident** that occurred on **April 26, 1986**, at Reactor No. 4 of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in the Ukrainian SSR (then Soviet Union). It is the only commercial nuclear incident classified as **INES Level 7 (Major Accident)** in history, caused by a flawed RBMK reactor design coupled with serious violations of operating procedures during a safety experiment. The event released massive quantities of radioactive material across the Northern Hemisphere.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — TMI: The Clogged Sink and the Wrong Valve**
> Imagine a high-pressure water system in a factory. A small blockage (failing pump) causes pressure to build up. A pressure-relief valve *opens* to dump excess water, but then *fails to close* — it gets stuck open. Now the system is draining water (coolant) out faster than the pump can supply it. The operators look at the **dashboard** and see a *misleading* gauge that says "tank is full of water" (because the sensor was downstream of the break). Panicked, they *turn off the emergency water injection* — the exact opposite of what was needed. Within **2 hours**, half the core melted. **TMI is the classic "right diagnosis, wrong action" disaster.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Chernobyl: The Car in Neutral on a Hill**
> Picture an old car parked on a steep hill. To test the parking brake, someone releases the brake while in **neutral** and disengages the clutch. The car begins to roll backwards (power excursion). The driver presses the brake pedal — but the brakes were *deliberately disabled* for the test. The car accelerates uncontrollably down the hill, jumps the curb, and crashes (steam explosion). A second, chemical explosion follows (graphite fire). **Chernobyl is the "removed every safety net before performing the stunt" disaster.**

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Key Numerical Facts (Bold for Recall)

| Parameter | TMI (1979) | Chernobyl (1986) |
|-----------|------------|------------------|
| **Reactor Type** | **PWR (Pressurized Water Reactor)** | **RBMK-1000 (Graphite-moderated, Boiling Water)** |
| **Thermal Power** | **2772 MWt** | **3200 MWt** |
| **INES Level** | **5** | **7** |
| **Core Damage** | **~50% partial melt** | **~100% core destruction** |
| **Direct Deaths** | **0** | **31 (2 immediate + 29 within months)** |
| **Long-term Deaths (estimates)** | **~0 to ~1 (controversial)** | **Up to 4,000–93,000 (debated)** |
| **Evacuation Radius** | **~16 km (5 miles)** | **~30 km (later 10 km exclusion zone)** |
| **Radioactive Release** | **~2.5 × 10¹⁷ Bq (Xenon)** | **~5.2 × 10¹⁸ Bq total (10–100× TMI)** |
| **Public Cost** | **~$1 billion cleanup** | **~$18 billion (USSR cost)** |

### 1.5 Significance for KTU Nuclear Safety (NEP 2020 Alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises this topic:**
> These two accidents are the **founding pillars of modern nuclear safety culture**. Every international regulation (IAEA, NRC, AERB India) traces its philosophy back to lessons learned from TMI and Chernobyl. Understanding their **root causes, timeline, and corrective actions** forms the basis of *Course Outcome CO3* of PESFT417 (Analyze nuclear accident case studies and derive safety lessons).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three Mile Island — Event Timeline and Causal Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Ladder:** Walk through the incident in the exact order a Board examiner expects.

**Phase 1 — Initiating Event (00:00:00 — 00:00:02)**
- A minor malfunction in the **feedwater system** causes the **main feedwater pumps** to trip.
- The **turbine** automatically trips; residual heat must now be removed by the **auxiliary feedwater system (AFWS)**.
- The AFWS valves are found **closed** (maintenance error — left closed after testing) → **no emergency cooling water reaches the core**.

**Phase 2 — Mechanical Failure (00:00:02 — 00:02:00)**
- Core temperature and pressure rise. The **Pressurizer Pressure Relief Valve (PORV)** opens automatically at **~2250 psia** to dump excess pressure into the **drain tank**.
- The PORV **fails to reseat** — it remains stuck open. **Coolant is now leaking continuously out of the primary loop.**
- Unknown to operators, the **reactor coolant is bleeding into the containment sump**.

**Phase 3 — Instrumentation Mislead (00:02:00 — 00:10:00)**
- The control panel shows that the PORV indicator light is **OFF** (suggesting the valve is closed — this was a design flaw: the light shows *electrical signal*, not *actual valve position*).
- Operators **misinterpret** the situation. They believe the pressurizer is full of water → they **throttle the high-pressure injection pump (HPI)**.
- **Reality:** The core is uncovered; coolant is boiling away. The pressurizer reads "full" because of steam binding, not water level.

**Phase 4 — Core Uncovery and Damage (00:10:00 — 02:30:00)**
- The core begins to uncover within **8 minutes**; cladding temperature exceeds **2200 °F (1204 °C)**.
- **Zircaloy cladding reacts with steam** in a violent exothermic reaction:
   - $\text{Zr} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{ZrO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2 + \text{Heat} \;(\Delta H \approx -584 \text{ kJ/mol})$
- Released **hydrogen gas** accumulates in the containment building — a small explosion occurs in the **hydrogen bubble** (at 02:01:50), but containment holds.
- Approximately **50% of the core melts** (about **62 tonnes of fuel**).

**Phase 5 — Recovery (02:30:00 — Day 13)**
- Operators eventually recognise the problem; HPI is restored.
- The **hydrogen bubble** is the major post-accident concern. A 10-foot bubble forms at the top of the reactor vessel — a feared "explosive" if disturbed. President Carter visits the site.
- The bubble is gradually **vented and dissolved** over the following days. Core is permanently cooled.

**Phase 6 — Aftermath and Lessons**
- **No casualties** to the public; small committed dose (~0.01 mSv average to nearby population).
- $1 billion spent on cleanup. Unit 2 never operated again; Unit 1 operated until 2019.
- Led to creation of the **Institute of Nuclear Power Operations (INPO)** and NRC reforms.

### 2.2 Chernobyl — Event Timeline and Causal Chain

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept Ladder:** The Chernobyl accident has *three* root causes — *design defects, procedural violations, and absence of safety culture.*

**Phase 1 — Pre-conditions (00:00 — 13:00 Local Time)**
- Reactor 4 was scheduled for a **safety test**: to verify that **turbine rundown** could provide power for cooling pumps long enough for the diesel generators to start.
- The test required the **reactor to operate at low power (~200 MWt, 7%)**. Due to a power-grid delay, operators continued the test much later than planned, through a vulnerable power range.

**Phase 2 — Reactivity Insertion (01:23:04 — 01:23:40)**
- Operators **disabled multiple safety systems** to perform the test:
   - **Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS)** — disconnected
   - **Reactor Protection System (trip signals) — bypassed**
   - **Local automatic control rods — withdrawn**
- Power had dropped to **~30 MWt** due to **xenon poisoning** ($^{135}$Xe buildup: thermal neutron absorption cross-section $\sigma_a \approx 2.6 \times 10^6$ barns).
- Operators pulled out **most control rods** to raise power → **extremely unstable, low reactivity margin state**.

**Phase 3 — Positive Void Coefficient and Power Excursion (01:23:40 — 01:23:43)**
- Cooling water flow was restricted → coolant began to **boil** in the channels.
- **RBMK flaw:** The reactor has a **positive void coefficient of reactivity**:
   $\alpha_{\phi} = \frac{\partial k}{\partial V_{\text{void}}} > 0$
- Water (good neutron absorber) turned to steam (poor absorber) → fewer neutrons absorbed → **reactivity increases** → power surges exponentially.
- The **prompt critical excursion** occurred in **~4 seconds**. Power spiked from 200 MWt to over **30,000 MWt (30 GWt)** — *ten times the rated thermal output*.

**Phase 4 — Steam Explosion (01:23:43)**
- The instantaneous vaporization of coolant water generated a **steam explosion** equivalent to several hundred kg of TNT.
- The 1000-tonne **upper biological shield (lid)** was blown off, penetrating the roof of the reactor hall.

**Phase 5 — Second Explosion — Graphite Fire (01:23:46 — 01:23:52)**
- The exposed **graphite moderator blocks** ignited in open air (graphite temperature > 700 °C).
- The fire lifted **fission products** and **graphite particles** into a **~1 km high** plume.
- The graphite burned for **10 days**, dispersing $^{131}$I, $^{137}$Cs, $^{90}$Sr, and **plutonium isotopes** across Europe.

**Phase 6 — Heroic Mitigation**
- **5,000 tonnes of sand, boron, dolomite, and lead** were dropped by helicopter onto the burning core (~$1 billion in materials).
- On **May 6, 1986**, the fire was extinguished.
- The **sarcophagus** (steel-concrete containment) was built by **400,000 workers** under extreme radiation exposure, completed in **206 days**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Equations for Both Accidents

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Physical Meaning | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zircaloy-Steam Reaction** | $\text{Zr} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{ZrO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\uparrow$ | Generates H₂ and heat → explosive in TMI | $\Delta H \approx -584$ kJ/mol |
| **Reactivity** | $\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}}$ | Measure of deviation from criticality | dimensionless (pcm or $\Delta k/k$) |
| **Void Coefficient (RBMK)** | $\alpha_\phi = \frac{\partial k}{\partial V_{\text{void}}} > 0$ | **Positive** in RBMK = unstable under boiling | $\Delta k / k$ per % void |
| **Xenon-135 Poisoning** | $\frac{dI}{dt} = \gamma_I \Sigma_f \phi - \lambda_I I$ | I-135 from fission decays to Xe-135 | $T_{1/2}(\text{Xe-135}) = 9.17$ h |
| **Reactor Period (τ)** | $P(t) = P_0 \, e^{t/\tau}$ | Time for power to change by factor $e$ | **τ = 0.3 s in Chernobyl** |
| **Power Excursion** | $P_{\text{peak}} = P_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}$ | 4 s × 1/0.3 = 13.3 e-folds → ×6.6×10⁵ | Critical for KTU numericals |
| **Decay Heat (Wigner-Way)** | $P_d(t) = 0.0622 \cdot P_0 \cdot t^{-0.2}$ (W) | Residual heat after shutdown, $t$ in seconds | $P_0$ in W |
| **Dose Commitment (ICRP)** | $E = \sum_T w_T H_T$ | Effective dose equivalent | Sievert (Sv) |
| **Chernobyl Release** | $\sim 5.2 \times 10^{18}$ Bq (≈26 MCi) | Total release, 200× Hiroshima | Becquerels (Bq) |
| **TMI Release** | $\sim 2.5 \times 10^{17}$ Bq (Xe/Kr gases) | Mostly noble gases | Becquerels (Bq) |
| **Source Term Fractions** | $^{131}$I, $^{137}$Cs, $^{134}$Cs, $^{90}$Sr, Pu-239/240 | Key isotopes for dose | Half-lives matter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip — Remember these three numbers cold:**
> 1. **TMI: 0 deaths, INES 5, PWR, stuck valve, 50% core melt.**
> 2. **Chernobyl: 31 immediate deaths, INES 7, RBMK, positive void coeff., graphite fire, sarcophagus.**
> 3. **Chernobyl release was ~10–100× the TMI release.**

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice (Real-World Utility)

- **Reactor Design Standards:** After TMI, **containment buildings** became mandatory in all new reactors; after Chernobyl, **positive void coefficient reactors** were redesigned globally.
- **Safety Culture:** Both accidents birthed the **INSAG-1 (Chernobyl) and INSAG-7 (post-TMI reforms)** documents — the basis of **IAEA Safety Standards** today.
- **Human Factors Engineering:** TMI led to **NUREG-0700 (Human-System Interface Design Guidelines)** — used in every control room worldwide.
- **Indian Context:** **AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board)** mandates *post-accident sampling, hydrogen recombiners (PARs), and core-catchers* in Indian PWRs (Kudankulam, Kakrapar) — direct TMI and Chernobyl learnings.
- **Career Relevance:** Nuclear Safety engineers use these case studies in **Probabilistic Safety Assessments (PSA)** as initiating-event benchmarks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 KTU Analytical Matrix — Root Cause Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Board examiners love this comparison table. Memorise it.**

| Analysis Dimension | Three Mile Island (TMI) | Chernobyl |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactor** | PWR (Westinghouse, Babcock & Wilcox) | RBMK-1000 (Soviet design) |
| **Operational Mode** | Routine full-power operation (97%) | Low-power test (7% then ~5%) |
| **Primary Failure** | Mechanical: PORV stuck open | Procedural: safety systems disabled |
| **Latent Failure** | Maintenance: AFWS valves left closed | Design: positive void coefficient |
| **Operator Error Type** | Mis-diagnosis due to bad indicator | Knowing violation of procedures |
| **Safety Systems** | Tripped manually by operators | Deliberately disabled before test |
| **Reactivity Feedback** | **Negative** (PWR safe) | **Positive** (RBMK unstable at low power) |
| **Time to Damage** | ~2 hours | **~4 seconds** (prompt critical) |
| **Containment Integrity** | Held fully (designed-in safety) | RBMK had **partial/no containment** |
| **Public Health Outcome** | Negligible dose (< 0.01 mSv avg) | Evacuation, long-term cancer risk |
| **INES Level** | 5 | 7 |
| **Key Lesson** | Human factors + control room design | Reactor design + safety culture |

### 3.2 Derivation — Chernobyl Power Excursion (KTU Numerical Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a frequently asked 14-mark KTU Board question. Learn to derive this in class.**

**Given:**
- Initial power at excursion start: $P_0 = 200$ MWt
- Final reactor period: $\tau = 0.3$ s
- Time to fuel failure: $t = 4$ s
- Total energy release: must not exceed fuel failure limit

**Step 1 — Power Excursion Formula (Point Kinetics, no delayed neutrons)**

For a super-prompt-critical excursion (delayed neutron fraction $\beta_{\text{eff}}$ overwhelmed), the point kinetics equation simplifies to:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{\rho(t)}{\Lambda} P(t)$$

where $\Lambda$ is the **mean neutron generation time** ($\Lambda \approx 10^{-5}$ s for thermal reactors). For a step insertion of reactivity, $\rho$ is constant, giving exponential growth.

**Step 2 — Define Reactor Period**

The reactor period $\tau$ is defined as the time for power to grow by a factor of $e$:

$$P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}$$

where the period is related to reactivity by:

$$\tau = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho - \beta_{\text{eff}}}$$

For the RBMK during the excursion, effective $\rho - \beta_{\text{eff}} \approx \$3$–\$5 (about 3–5 dollars above prompt critical). With $\Lambda \approx 10^{-5}$ s:

$$\tau \approx \frac{10^{-5}}{3 \times 0.0065} \approx 0.0005 \text{ s (theoretical) to } 0.3 \text{ s (observed)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Peak Power**

Substituting $P_0 = 200$ MWt, $t = 4$ s, $\tau = 0.3$ s:

$$P_{\text{peak}} = 200 \cdot e^{4/0.3} = 200 \cdot e^{13.33}$$

Evaluating the exponential:

$$e^{13.33} \approx 6.6 \times 10^{5}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\text{peak}} \approx 200 \times 6.6 \times 10^{5} = 1.32 \times 10^{8} \text{ MWt} = 132 \text{ GWt}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Released in 4 Seconds**

Total energy released before fuel disassembly:

$$E = \int_0^{4} P(t) \, dt = P_0 \tau \left(e^{t/\tau} - 1\right)$$

$$E = 200 \text{ MWt} \times 0.3 \text{ s} \times (6.6 \times 10^5 - 1)$$

$$E \approx 60 \times 6.6 \times 10^5 \text{ MJ} \approx 3.96 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ} \approx 4 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to TNT Equivalent**

1 tonne of TNT $\equiv 4.184 \times 10^9$ J. So:

$$E_{\text{TNT}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{13}}{4.184 \times 10^{9}} \approx 9560 \text{ tonnes of TNT} \approx 10 \text{ kilotonnes}$$

This energy is comparable to the **Hiroshima bomb (~15 kt)** — a chilling comparison often quoted in KTU Board questions.

**Step 6 — Steam Explosion Mechanism**

The instantaneous vaporization of ~200 m³ of coolant water produces:

$$E_{\text{steam}} = m_{\text{water}} \cdot L_v$$

where latent heat $L_v = 2.26 \times 10^6$ J/kg. This expansion in a closed channel produces a **shock wave** that ruptures pressure tubes → primary cause of the first explosion.

### 3.3 Derivation — TMI Cladding Heat-Up (Thermal-Hydraulic Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Often asked as a 7-mark sub-question.**

**Given:**
- Cladding material: Zircaloy-4, melting point **~1850 °C (3370 °F)**
- Initial cladding temperature: $T_0 = 320$ °C
- Time at peak heat flux: $t = 8$ minutes (uncovery began at this time)
- Volumetric heat generation in fuel: $q''' \approx 250$ W/cm³

**Step 1 — Heat Balance on Cladding**

The cladding receives heat from the fuel by conduction; loses heat by convection to steam (and by Zr-steam reaction once $T > 900$ °C):

$$\rho_{\text{Zr}} c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = q_{\text{cond}} - q_{\text{conv}} - q_{\text{rxn}}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Convective Loss (boiling crisis)**

When coolant is lost, convective term goes to zero (film boiling). Cladding temperature rises nearly adiabatically in 2–3 minutes.

**Step 3 — Zr-Steam Reaction Onset (above 900 °C)**

Once cladding reaches ~900 °C, the exothermic reaction releases **additional heat** that *accelerates* the temperature rise:

$$q_{\text{rxn}} = \Delta H \cdot \frac{d\xi}{dt} = -584 \text{ kJ/mol} \times \text{reaction rate}$$

**Step 4 — Final Cladding State at TMI**

Within ~10 minutes, cladding temperature exceeded **2200 °C** (above melting). This produced:
- Molten fuel-Zr- stainless steel mixture ("corium")
- ~30% of core mass relocated to lower plenum
- 50% of core mass damaged (oxidised + molten)

### 3.4 KTU Frequently-Missed Analytical Steps (Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Board Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **prompt critical** ($\rho > \$$1) with **delayed critical** ($\rho \approx \beta_{\text{eff}}$). Chernobyl was **prompt critical** — delayed neutrons were bypassed.
> 2. Forgetting that **RBMK void coefficient flips sign**: It is *negative* at high power (safe) but *positive* at low power with low coolant inventory (Chernobyl's trap).
> 3. Saying "Chernobyl had no containment" — it had a *partial* reactor building, but no Western-style full-pressure containment dome. This is the key design difference.
> 4. Mis-stating the deaths: **31 confirmed** (2 in explosion, 29 from acute radiation syndrome within weeks), plus the long-term estimate is **highly debated**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TMI Event Sequence — Causal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedwater Pump Trip] --> B[Turbine Trips - Loss of Heat Sink]
    B --> C[AFWS Valves Found CLOSED - Maintenance Error]
    C --> D[Primary Pressure Surges Above 2250 psia]
    D --> E[PORV Opens to Relieve Pressure]
    E --> F[PORV STUCK OPEN - Mechanical Failure]
    F --> G[Continuous Coolant Leakage to Drain Tank]
    G --> H[Control Panel Shows PORV Light OFF - Misleading]
    H --> I[Operators MISCALIBRATE Pressurizer - Thinks Core is FULL]
    I --> J[HPI Throttled - Emergency Cooling Withheld]
    J --> K[Core Uncovery Starts at 8 minutes]
    K --> L[Zircaloy-Steam Reaction Begins at 900 C]
    L --> M[Cladding Temp Exceeds 2200 C]
    M --> N[50 Percent Core Melt - 62 tonnes]
    N --> O[Hydrogen Bubble Forms in Vessel Head]
    O --> P[Bubble Vented and Dissolved - Day 13]
    P --> Q[No Public Fatality - INES Level 5]
```

### 4.2 Chernobyl Event Sequence — Causal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Safety Test Planned on Reactor 4] --> B[Power Reduced to 200 MWt then 30 MWt]
    B --> C[Xenon-135 Poisoning - Power Drops Further]
    C --> D[Operators Disable Safety Systems for Test]
    D --> E[ECCS Disconnected - Protection System Bypassed]
    E --> F[Control Rods Withdrawn to Combat Xenon]
    F --> G[RBMK Enters UNSTABLE Low-Power State]
    G --> H[Coolant Flow Restricted - Water Begins to Boil]
    H --> I[Positive Void Coefficient Activates]
    I --> J[Reactivity Rises - Power Surges]
    J --> K[Prompt Critical Excursion in 4 Seconds]
    K --> L[Power Spikes to 30 GWt - 150x Rated]
    L --> M[Fuel Disassembly - Steam Explosion]
    M --> N[1000-tonne Lid Blown Off Reactor]
    N --> O[Second Explosion - Graphite Ignites]
    O --> P[10-Day Graphite Fire - Massive Release]
    P --> Q[Sarcophagus Built in 206 Days - INES 7]
```

### 4.3 Reactor Type Comparison — PWR vs RBMK

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["PWR Three Mile Island Design"]
        A1[Pressurized Primary Loop] --> A2[Steam Generator Secondary Loop]
        A2 --> A3[Turbine Generator]
        A3 --> A4[Condenser Cooling Tower]
        A1 -.-> A5[Containment Dome Full Pressure 4 bar]
    end
    subgraph B["RBMK Chernobyl Design"]
        B1[Graphite-Moderated Core] --> B2[Boiling Water in Pressure Tubes]
        B2 --> B3[Steam Separator Drums]
        B3 --> B4[Turbine Generator]
        B4 --> B5[Condenser Cooling]
        B1 -.-> B6[Partial Reactor Hall - No Full Containment]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Major Nuclear Accident Occurs] --> B{Type of Failure?}
    B -->|Mechanical| C[Improved Equipment Reliability]
    B -->|Human Error| D[Operator Training and HFE Redesign]
    B -->|Design Flaw| E[Reactor Design Modification]
    C --> F[NRC IAEA AERB Reforms]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Safety Culture: Defense-in-Depth]
    F --> H[Probabilistic Safety Assessment]
    F --> I[Emergency Preparedness Zone 30 km]
    G --> J[Modern Reactor Gen III+ Standards]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.5 Dose Consequence Comparison (Population Impact)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Population Doses] --> B[TMI 1979]
    A --> C[Chernobyl 1986]
    B --> B1[Avg Dose to 20 km: 0.01 mSv]

    B --> B3[No Evacuation - Advisory]
    C --> C1[Avg Dose to Evacuees: 30 mSv]
    C --> C2[Thyroid Dose Children: 0.5 Gy Region]
    C --> C3[30 km Exclusion Zone Permanent]
    B3 --> D[INES Level 5]
    C3 --> E[INES Level 7]
```
