# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the term "ALARA" and list any two engineering controls used to implement it in a nuclear facility.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable)** is a radiation protection principle (from ICRP Publication 103) requiring that occupational and public doses be kept as low as is reasonably achievable, taking into account economic and social factors.

**Two engineering controls (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Source Reduction**: Use of low-activity sources, removal of disused sources, dilution, or decontamination of components.
2. **Shielding**: Installation of lead, borated polyethylene, or concrete barriers between worker and source.
3. **Containment**: Glove boxes, hot cells, fume hoods, and ventilation systems with HEPA filtration.
4. **Automation / Remote Handling**: Master-slave manipulators and robotics to remove the human from the radiation field.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Differentiate between stochastic and deterministic effects of ionizing radiation with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Stochastic Effects | Deterministic Effects |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold | **No threshold** (LNT model) | **Threshold dose exists** |
| Severity | Independent of dose | Increases with dose |
| Probability | Increases with dose | Appears after threshold is exceeded |
| Mechanism | DNA damage → cancer/leukaemia | Cell killing / functional damage |
| Onset time | Years (latency) | Days to weeks |
| Example | Radiation-induced leukaemia | Skin erythema, cataract, radiation burns, sterility |

(2 marks for differentiation table + 1 mark for examples)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the various types of personnel dosimeters used in nuclear facilities, with their working principles, range, and limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A radiation worker receives the following organ doses in one year: Lung $H_{Lung} = 15$ mSv, Thyroid $H_{Thyroid} = 30$ mSv, Red Bone Marrow $H_{RBM} = 8$ mSv, Gonads $H_{Gonads} = 12$ mSv. Calculate the **Effective Dose** using ICRP 103 tissue weighting factors and comment on the regulatory compliance. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — (a) Types of Personnel Dosimeters (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> Personnel dosimeters are devices worn on the body to measure cumulative external radiation dose. They are divided into **passive** and **active** types.

**1. Film Badge (1.5 marks)**
- **Principle**: Photographic film darkening proportional to radiation exposure.
- **Range**: 0.1 mSv to 10 Sv.
- **Limitations**: Latent image fading, single-use (cannot be re-read), energy dependence.

**2. TLD — Thermoluminescent Dosimeter (1.5 marks)**
- **Principle**: LiF:Mg,Ti crystal traps electrons; on heating, light emitted proportional to dose.
- **Range**: 0.01 mSv to 10 Sv.
- **Limitations**: Reading destroys stored information; requires annealing for reuse; batch calibration needed.

**3. OSL Dosimeter (1 mark)**
- **Principle**: Al₂O₃:C optically stimulated → blue light proportional to dose.
- **Range**: 0.01 mSv to 10 Sv.
- **Advantages over TLD**: Re-readable, less fading, faster readout.

**4. Electronic Personal Dosimeter — EPD (1.5 marks)**
- **Principle**: Si-diode detector with MOSFET readout; gives real-time cumulative dose and dose rate.
- **Range**: 0.01 µSv to 10 Sv.
- **Limitations**: Battery dependent, fragile, used in addition to passive dosimeter (not a replacement).

**5. Neutron Dosimeter (1.5 marks)**
- **CR-39 Track Etch**: Charged particles create tracks in polymer; etched tracks counted.
- **Albedo TLD**: Reflects thermal neutrons back to LiF for measurement.
- Used at reactors, criticality areas.

#### **Model Solution — (b) Effective Dose Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Recall ICRP 103 tissue weighting factors** ($W_T$):

| Tissue | $W_T$ |
|---|---|
| Red Bone Marrow | 0.12 |
| Lungs | 0.12 |
| Thyroid | 0.04 |
| Gonads | 0.08 |
| (Others summed) | 0.64 |

**Step 2 — Apply effective dose formula:**

$$E = \sum_T W_T \cdot H_T$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$E = (0.12 \times 15) + (0.04 \times 30) + (0.12 \times 8) + (0.08 \times 12) \text{ mSv}$$

$$E = 1.80 + 1.20 + 0.96 + 0.96 \text{ mSv}$$

$$E = 4.92 \text{ mSv}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with occupational dose limit:**

$$\text{Fraction of limit} = \frac{4.92 \text{ mSv}}{20 \text{ mSv}} = 24.6\%$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The effective dose is **4.92 mSv**, which is **24.6% of the annual limit**. This is **within regulatory compliance**, but since it exceeds 10% of the limit, the worker falls under **Category A** (higher exposure category) and must be placed under enhanced medical surveillance per AERB guidelines.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **components of an Internal Dosimetry Programme** for a fuel fabrication facility handling uranium compounds. (7 marks)

**(b)** A worker inhales **5.0 kBq of UO₂ aerosol** (AMAD = 5 µm, Type S). Using ICRP dose coefficient $e(50) = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}$ Sv/Bq, calculate the **committed effective dose** and the **ALI**, then determine whether the intake is acceptable under AERB dose limit of 20 mSv/year. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution — (a) Internal Dosimetry Programme (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> An internal dosimetry programme for a U-fuel facility must address **intake routes** (inhalation, ingestion, wounds), **Uranium compounds** (UO₂, UF₄, U₃O₈), and **long-term retention** in lungs/bones.

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------------------+
|  INTERNAL DOSIMETRY PROGRAMME              |
+--------------------------------------------+
|                                            |
|  1. Workplace Air Sampling (CAM)  ---------|---> Real-time DAC alarm
|  2. Surface Contamination Surveys  --------|---> α, β smear surveys
|  3. Baseline Bioassay (Pre-employment)  ---|---> Urine uranium, fecal
|  4. Routine Bioassay (Annual)  ------------|---> Lung counting (WBC)
|  5. Special Bioassay (Post-incident)  -----|---> 24-hr urine, 72-hr fecal
|  6. Dose Calculation  ---------------------|---> ICRP systemic model
|  7. Medical Surveillance  -----------------|---> Kidney function tests
|  8. Record Keeping & AERB Reporting  ------|---> Lifetime dose file
|                                            |
+--------------------------------------------+
```

**Description of each component (3 marks):**
1. **CAMs** (Continuous Air Monitors): Real-time α-spectroscopic detection of airborne U.
2. **Surface Surveys**: Smear tests for removable α contamination; direct frisking with ZnS scintillator.
3. **Baseline Bioassay**: Pre-employment urine U analysis and lung count to establish worker-specific reference.
4. **Routine Bioassay**: Annual urine uranium by ICP-MS or kinetic phosphorescence analysis (KPA).
5. **Special Bioassay**: Triggered when air sample > DAC, contamination > limit, or incident.
6. **Dose Calculation**: Use ICRP 119 systemic model for U; $f_1$ values (Type F: 0.02, Type M: 0.02, Type S: 0.002).
7. **Medical Surveillance**: BUN, creatinine, β₂-microglobulin for renal function (U is nephrotoxic).
8. **Records & AERB Compliance**: AERB-formatted dose record, reported annually.

#### **Model Solution — (b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Intake $I = 5.0$ kBq $= 5.0 \times 10^3$ Bq
- Dose coefficient $e(50) = 6.0 \times 10^{-6}$ Sv/Bq
- Dose Limit $DL = 20$ mSv/yr $= 0.020$ Sv/yr

**Step 1 — Calculate Committed Effective Dose:**

$$E(50) = I \cdot e(50)$$

$$E(50) = (5.0 \times 10^3 \text{ Bq}) \times (6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Sv/Bq})$$

$$E(50) = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Sv} = 30 \text{ mSv}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with annual limit:**

$$E(50) = 30 \text{ mSv} > 20 \text{ mSv} \quad (\text{Annual Limit})$$

The committed effective dose **exceeds** the annual limit by 50%.

**Step 3 — Calculate ALI for UO₂ (Type S):**

$$ALI = \frac{DL}{e(50)} = \frac{0.020 \text{ Sv}}{6.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ Sv/Bq}}$$

$$ALI = 3333 \text{ Bq} \approx 3.33 \text{ kBq}$$

**Step 4 — Compare intake with ALI:**

$$\frac{I}{ALI} = \frac{5.0 \text{ kBq}}{3.33 \text{ kBq}} = 1.50$$

This is **1.5 ALI** — meaning the intake is **50% above the annual limit**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion and Action:**

> [!WARNING]
> **The intake is NOT acceptable under AERB limits.** The worker must be:
> 1. Removed from further uranium work pending investigation.
> 2. Subject to immediate medical evaluation (kidney function tests).
> 3. **Incident report filed with AERB** within 24 hours (as per AERB/SC/G-3).
> 4. Chelation therapy considered if soluble form (Type F/M); for Type S (UO₂), only supportive care.
> 5. Root cause analysis to be initiated within 7 days.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - Confusing **Equivalent Dose ($H_T$)** with **Effective Dose ($E$)** — students often forget $W_T$ multiplication.
> - Unit errors: forgetting to convert kBq → Bq (factor $10^3$) or mSv → Sv (factor $10^{-3}$).
> - Not mentioning **ALARA optimization** when discussing dose limit compliance — examiners deduct 1–2 marks.
> - Failing to cite **ICRP 103** and **AERB SC/G-3** in answers — this is mandatory for full marks.
> - In internal dose problems, students forget to specify **Type (F/M/S)** of inhaled material — examiners deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Health Physics Surveillance** = measurement + evaluation + control of doses.
- [x] **ALARA** = As Low As Reasonably Achievable (economic & social factors considered).
- [x] **Three Pillars of RP** = Justification, Optimization (ALARA), Dose Limitation.
- [x] **Dose Limit (Worker, whole body)** = **20 mSv/yr** (avg over 5 years).
- [x] **Dose Limit (Public)** = **1 mSv/yr**.
- [x] **Lens of eye (ICRP 2011)** = **20 mSv/yr** (averaged), 50 mSv in any year.
- [x] **Embryo/fetus** = **1 mSv** (declared pregnancy).
- [x] **TLD** is the most common routine dosimeter in India (LiF:Mg,Ti).
- [x] **OSL** = re-readable, no annealing, used in many BARC facilities.
- [x] **EPD** = real-time, alarm-based, supplementary to passive badge.
- [x] **Effective Dose formula** = $E = \sum W_T \cdot H_T$ — memorize $W_T$ values for Lungs(0.12), RBM(0.12), Thyroid(0.04), Gonads(0.08), Breast(0.12), Stomach(0.12), Colon(0.12), Liver(0.04), Bladder(0.04), Oesophagus(0.04), Skin(0.01), Bone surface(0.01), Brain(0.01), Salivary(0.01), Remainder(0.12).
- [x] **Internal Dose** computed via $E(50) = I \cdot e(50)$.
- [x] **ALI** = $DL / e(50)$ — Annual Limit on Intake.
- [x] **DAC** = $ALI / V_{annual}$ — Derived Air Concentration ($V$ = 2400 m³/yr).
- [x] **Stochastic effects** = cancer, leukaemia — no threshold.
- [x] **Deterministic effects** = burns, cataract, sterility — have threshold.
- [x] **Bioassay types** = In-vitro (urine/feces) and In-vivo (WBC, thyroid, lung count).
- [x] **AERB Codes** = AERB/NF/SC/MED-1 (medical), AERB/RF-MED-1 (personnel monitoring), AERB/SG/G-3 (dose limits).
- [x] **LNT model** = Linear No-Threshold model for stochastic risk.
- [x] **Investigation Level** = typically 30% of annual dose limit.
- [x] **Type S compounds** (e.g., UO₂) are retained in lungs for months → long-term dose.
- [x] **Type F compounds** (e.g., UF₆ hydrolysed) clear rapidly (days).
- [x] **CR-39** = track-etch dosimeter for neutrons.
- [x] **Inverse square law** = $\dot{X} \propto 1/d^2$ — double distance → quarter dose rate.
- [x] **HVL** = Half-Value Layer, the thickness reducing intensity to half.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — HEALTH PHYSICS SURVEILLANCE**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Safety in Medical Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT417 - NUCLEAR SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Safety in Medical Treatment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🩺 Nuclear Safety in Medical Treatment — Module 4

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medical Nuclear Safety (as per ICRP & IAEA BSS Framework):** The discipline of controlling ionizing radiation exposure of patients, medical staff, comforters, and the public during diagnostic, interventional, and therapeutic radiological procedures — while preserving the intended clinical benefit. It is governed by the **three fundamental principles of radiological protection**: **Justification, Optimization (ALARA), and Dose Limitation / Constraint**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, **Nuclear Safety in Medical Treatment** is the controlled application of ionizing radiation (X-rays, gamma rays, beta particles, alpha particles, protons, and neutrons) in three principal medical domains:

1. **Diagnostic Radiology** — imaging using X-rays, CT, fluoroscopy, mammography.
2. **Nuclear Medicine** — diagnostic (PET, SPECT) and therapeutic use of unsealed radionuclides (radiopharmaceuticals).
3. **Radiation Therapy (Radiotherapy)** — external beam therapy (LINAC, Co-60, proton therapy) and brachytherapy (sealed sources).

**Formal Definition (KTU/ICRP):**
> *Medical exposure* is exposure incurred by patients as part of their own medical or dental diagnosis or treatment; by individuals (other than occupationally exposed) knowingly and willingly helping in the support and comfort of patients; and by volunteers in research programmes.

The regulatory framework rests upon:
- **IAEA Safety Standards Series No. SSG-46** — Radiation Protection and Safety in Medical Uses of Ionizing Radiation.
- **ICRP Publication 103 (2007)** — Recommendations of the International Commission on Radiological Protection.
- **ICRP Publication 105 (2007)** — Radiological Protection in Medicine.
- **Atomic Energy (Radiation Processing of Food and Allied Products) Rules** and the **Atomic Energy (Radiation Protection) Rules, 2004** of India.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Surgical Scalpel" Principle 🔪**
> Imagine ionizing radiation in medicine as a **surgeon's scalpel**. Used by a trained, licensed surgeon inside a sterile, controlled operation theatre, it heals — sometimes it is the *only* thing that can save a life. But hand the same scalpel to an untrained person outside the theatre, or use it carelessly, and it becomes a lethal weapon. **Nuclear safety in medical treatment is the "sterile operation theatre + trained surgeon + safety checklist" that ensures the scalpel heals rather than harms.**

Three intuitions every KTU student must internalize:

- **The risk is *deliberate*, not accidental.** Unlike a nuclear power plant where radiation is a byproduct, in medicine, radiation is the *tool itself*. Therefore, safety is built into the *act of using* the tool, not merely around it.
- **The "patient" is the primary "radiation worker" in the room.** Paradoxically, patients receive far higher doses than any staff member. Justification of each procedure is therefore the cornerstone.
- **Dose is a double-edged sword.** Too little fails to treat cancer; too much causes secondary cancers, organ damage, or deterministic effects (skin burns, hair loss, radiation sickness). The art is in the *calibrated precision*.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Effective dose (E) — unit: Sievert (Sv)** = absorbed dose weighted by radiation type (w_R) and tissue sensitivity (w_T).
> - **Absorbed dose (D) — unit: Gray (Gy)** = energy deposited per unit mass (1 Gy = 1 J/kg).
> - **Equivalent dose (H_T) — unit: Sievert (Sv)** = absorbed dose in a tissue weighted by radiation type.
> - **Activity (A) — unit: Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration per second. *Historically* **1 Ci (Curie) = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**.
> - **Background radiation dose (global average) ≈ 2.4 mSv/year**.
> - **Public dose limit = 1 mSv/year** (planned exposure situations).
> - **Occupational dose limit = 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years**, with maximum **50 mSv in any single year**.
> - **Lens of the eye occupational limit = 20 mSv/year** (revised from 150 mSv by ICRP 2011).
> - **Embryo/Fetus occupational limit = 1 mGy equivalent dose** declared upon pregnancy notification.

### Typical Effective Doses from Common Medical Procedures (for intuition-building)

| Procedure | Typical Effective Dose (mSv) | Equivalent in Background |
|---|---|---|
| Chest X-ray (PA) | 0.02 | ~3 days |
| Dental X-ray | 0.005 | ~1 day |
| Mammography | 0.4 | ~2 months |
| Lumbar spine X-ray | 1.5 | ~7 months |
| CT Head | 2 | ~10 months |
| CT Chest | 7 | ~3 years |
| CT Abdomen/Pelvis | 10 | ~4 years |
| PET-CT (with ¹⁸F-FDG) | 25 | ~10 years |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Medical Radiation Protection

The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to articulate the ICRP's **three fundamental principles** in any medical safety question. Here is the deep theoretical breakdown:

### Pillar 1: **Justification** ✔️
- *Logic:* No medical exposure shall be carried out unless it produces a *net positive benefit* to the exposed individual.
- *Decision Level:* Decided by referring physicians in consultation with radiologists/nuclear medicine specialists.
- *Sub-categories:*
  - **Justification of a practice** (e.g., is CT screening justified for asymptomatic individuals?).
  - **Justification of an individual procedure** (e.g., is this specific CT justified for this specific patient?).
- *Regulatory mandate:* The practice of medical exposure must be *authorized* by the national regulatory body (in India: **AERB — Atomic Energy Regulatory Board**).

### Pillar 2: **Optimization of Protection (ALARA)** ⚖️
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALARA = "As Low As Reasonably Achievable"** — economic, social, and operational factors having been taken into account. This is the *engine* of day-to-day medical safety.

Operational sub-steps in medical context:
1. **Dose Constraint Setting** — A prospective upper bound on individual dose (e.g., for comforters: ≤ 5 mSv per episode; for pediatric CT: lower than adult).
2. **Diagnostic Reference Levels (DRLs)** — For diagnostic procedures, the 75th percentile of dose distributions observed in practice. *Not for individuals* — for *typical patients* on typical equipment.
3. **Design Constraints** — Equipment design, facility shielding, and procedural protocols.
4. **Operational Constraints** — Collimation, beam-on time minimization, protective shields (lead aprons, thyroid shields, lead-glass barriers), and personal protective equipment (PPE).

### Pillar 3: **Dose Limitation & Constraint** 🚧
- Applies to **occupationally exposed workers** and the **public**.
- *Does NOT apply to patients' medical exposures* (because the patient benefits directly; the dose is *prescribed*, not *limited*).
- However, *dose constraints* are set for **comforters, caregivers, and research volunteers**.

## 2.2 Categories of Medical Exposure (ICRP 105 / IAEA SSG-46)

A KTU-favorite question structure: *"Classify and discuss the different categories of medical exposure."* The four categories are:

1. **Exposure of patients** — for their own diagnosis or treatment.
2. **Exposure of comforters and caregivers** — knowingly and willingly helping patients (e.g., holding a child during X-ray). Constraint: ≤ 5 mSv per episode.
3. **Exposure of volunteers in biomedical research** — for medical research programmes (informed consent, dose constraints per ICRP 62).
4. **Exposure of workers (occupational)** — staff of radiology, nuclear medicine, radiotherapy departments. Standard occupational limits apply.

## 2.3 Medical Exposure Pathways: Sources & Hazards

| Modality | Source | Primary Hazard | Safety Barrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diagnostic X-ray (radiography, fluoroscopy, CT) | X-ray tube (50–140 kVp) | Skin dose (deterministic), cancer risk (stochastic) | Collimation, filtration, ALARA, shielding |
| Interventional radiology | X-ray tube (long fluoroscopy times) | Skin injuries, cataracts in operators | Dose monitoring, ceiling-suspended shields |
| Nuclear Medicine (diagnostic) | ⁹⁹ᵐTc, ¹⁸F, ¹³¹I, ⁶⁷Ga | Internal contamination, organ dose | Radiopharmacy controls, syringe shields, patient isolation |
| Nuclear Medicine (therapy) | ¹³¹I (thyroid), ⁸⁹Sr, ⁹⁰Y, ¹⁷⁷Lu | Very high local dose, contamination | Hospital isolation rooms, controlled patient discharge |
| External Beam RT | Co-60 γ, LINAC (6–25 MV X-rays), proton cyclotrons | Critical organ overdose, geometric miss | Treatment planning, IGRT, IMRT, multi-leaf collimators, interlocks |
| Brachytherapy | ¹⁹²Ir HDR, ⁶⁰Co, ¹²⁵I, ¹⁰³Pd | Source dislodgement, exposure of staff | Afterloading systems, source inventory, emergency response plan |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU 2024 Scheme exam answers involving numerical computation MUST reference formulas from this table. Memorize the units and the *boundary conditions*.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absorbed Dose | $D = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Energy (E) deposited per unit mass (m) | Gy = J/kg |
| 2 | Equivalent Dose | $H_T = \sum_R w_R \, D_{T,R}$ | Sum over radiation types of (w_R × tissue dose) | Sv |
| 3 | Effective Dose | $E = \sum_T w_T \, H_T$ | Sum over tissues of (w_T × equivalent dose) | Sv |
| 4 | Inverse Square Law | $I = I_0 \left(\dfrac{d_0}{d}\right)^{2}$ | Dose rate at distance d vs reference d₀ | Sv/h or mGy/h |
| 5 | Linear Attenuation | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Photon beam intensity after thickness x | I in same units as I₀ |
| 6 | Half-Value Layer (HVL) | $x_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Shielding thickness to halve intensity | cm or mm |
| 7 | Tenth-Value Layer (TVL) | $x_{1/10} = \dfrac{\ln 10}{\mu}$ | Shielding to reduce to 10% | cm or mm |
| 8 | Specific Gamma Ray Constant | $\Gamma = \dfrac{\dot{X} \, d^{2}}{A}$ | Exposure rate constant for a radionuclide | R·cm²/(mCi·h) or C·m²/(kg·s·Bq) |
| 9 | Dose Rate from Point Source | $\dot{H} = \dfrac{\Gamma \, A}{d^{2}}$ | Equivalent dose rate at distance d from source | mSv/h |
| 10 | Cumulative Activity (radiopharma) | $\tilde{A} = \int_0^{\infty} A(t)\,dt = \dfrac{A_0}{\lambda}$ | Total number of disintegrations in source organ | Bq·s |
| 11 | Cumulative Dose (radiopharma) | $D = \tilde{A} \times S$ | Where S = S-factor (mean absorbed dose per unit cum. activity) | Gy |
| 12 | Bystander / Public Inverse Square | $D_{public} = D_{patient,1m} \times \left(\dfrac{1\,m}{d_{public}}\right)^{2}$ | For discharge planning after radionuclide therapy | mSv |

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiation Weighting Factors (w_R) — Must Memorize:**
> - X-rays, γ rays, β⁻, β⁺: **w_R = 1**
> - Thermal neutrons: **w_R = 5**
> - Protons (>2 MeV): **w_R = 2**
> - α particles, heavy ions, fission fragments: **w_R = 20**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Medical nuclear safety is *not* an abstract regulatory exercise — it underpins:

- **Hospital design:** Lead-lined walls, controlled-access radiotherapy bunkers, hot labs for radiopharmacy.
- **Equipment design:** Beam collimators, automatic brightness control in fluoroscopy, last-image-hold, pulsed fluoroscopy, dose-area-product meters.
- **Software design:** Treatment Planning Systems (TPS) with double-redundancy interlocks (the lesson from the **Therac-25 accidents** of 1985–1987, where software race conditions caused massive overdoses — a mandatory case study in KTU Module 4).
- **Emergency preparedness:** Lost-source response, contamination control after radiopharmaceutical spills, evacuation plans for therapy patients.
- **Quality Assurance (QA) programmes:** Required by AERB Safety Code **AERB/RF-MED/SC-1 (Rev. 1)** and **AERB/SC/MED-4 (Radiotherapy)**.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests **numerical problem solving** in Part B questions. Below are the four most exam-relevant derivations and worked examples, every algebraic step shown explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Dose & Effective Dose (Exam Favourite)

### Problem Setup

A nuclear medicine patient receives an absorbed dose of **5 mGy to the thyroid** from Iodine-131 (β emitter, w_R = 1), and a separate absorbed dose of **2 mGy to the stomach** from Technetium-99m (γ emitter, w_R = 1). Additionally, the same patient receives **0.5 mGy to bone marrow from a CT scan** (X-rays, w_R = 1). Calculate the equivalent dose to each organ, then compute the effective dose using ICRP 103 tissue weighting factors.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the radiation type and weighting factors.**

From the ICRP 103 standard:
- Iodine-131 emits β⁻ particles → w_R = 1
- Technetium-99m emits γ photons → w_R = 1
- CT X-rays → w_R = 1

**Step 2: Compute Equivalent Dose for each tissue.**

The general formula:

$$
H_T = \sum_R w_R \, D_{T,R}
$$

For the **thyroid** (only I-131 contributes):

$$
H_{thyroid} = 1 \times 5 \, \text{mGy} = 5 \, \text{mSv}
$$

For the **stomach** (only Tc-99m contributes):

$$
H_{stomach} = 1 \times 2 \, \text{mGy} = 2 \, \text{mSv}
$$

For the **bone marrow** (only CT X-rays):

$$
H_{bone\,marrow} = 1 \times 0.5 \, \text{mGy} = 0.5 \, \text{mSv}
$$

**Step 3: Look up tissue weighting factors (w_T) from ICRP 103.**

$$
w_{thyroid} = 0.04, \quad w_{stomach} = 0.12, \quad w_{bone\,marrow} = 0.12
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Effective Dose formula.**

$$
E = \sum_T w_T \, H_T
$$

Expanding:

$$
E = w_{thyroid} \, H_{thyroid} + w_{stomach} \, H_{stomach} + w_{bone\,marrow} \, H_{bone\,marrow}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the numbers.**

$$
E = (0.04 \times 5) + (0.12 \times 2) + (0.12 \times 0.5)
$$

$$
E = 0.20 + 0.24 + 0.06
$$

$$
\boxed{E = 0.50 \, \text{mSv}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The patient's total weighted effective dose is **0.5 mSv**, which is roughly equivalent to ~2.5 months of natural background radiation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shielding Thickness Using Linear Attenuation

### Problem Setup

A radiation therapy bunker door is to be designed. The Co-60 source produces a dose rate of **100 mGy/h** at the door position (without shielding). Lead is the chosen shield material. The linear attenuation coefficient of lead for Co-60 γ rays is **$\mu = 0.66 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$**. Find the **lead thickness required to reduce the dose rate to 0.1 mGy/h** (an AERB-recommended design constraint for a controlled-access door).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the linear attenuation equation.**

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the knowns.**

- $I_0 = 100 \, \text{mGy/h}$
- $I = 0.1 \, \text{mGy/h}$
- $\mu = 0.66 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$
- $x = ?$

**Step 3: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$
\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x}
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{0.1}{100}\right) = -0.66 \times x
$$

$$
\ln(0.001) = -0.66 \, x
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$
-6.9078 = -0.66 \, x
$$

**Step 6: Solve for x.**

$$
x = \frac{6.9078}{0.66}
$$

$$
\boxed{x \approx 10.47 \, \text{cm of lead}}
$$

**Step 7 (Sanity check): Use the HVL formula as a verification path.**

The half-value layer is:

$$
x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.66} = 1.05 \, \text{cm}
$$

A reduction factor of 1000 = 10 decades. The number of HVLs required to reduce by a factor of $10^n$ is $\approx 3.32 n$. For $10^3$ reduction: $3.32 \times 3 = 9.96$ HVLs.

$$
x = 9.96 \times 1.05 = 10.46 \, \text{cm}
$$

**Both methods yield the same answer (≈ 10.5 cm of lead).** ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverse Square Law — Bystander Dose for ¹³¹I Therapy Patient

### Problem Setup

A patient has been administered **3.7 GBq (100 mCi) of I-131** for thyroid cancer therapy. A family member is asked to be a "comforter" during the patient's hospital stay. The family member will sit **0.5 m from the patient** for a duration such that the cumulative time-averaged exposure geometry suggests an effective dose at 1 m of **0.3 mSv/h** at the moment of measurement. Calculate the dose rate the comforter receives, and advise whether the dose constraint of 5 mSv per episode is exceeded if the stay is **6 hours long**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the inverse square law.**

$$
\dot{E}_{comforter} = \dot{E}_{1m} \times \left(\frac{1\,m}{d_{comforter}}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\dot{E}_{comforter} = 0.3 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\dot{E}_{comforter} = 0.3 \times 4 = 1.2 \, \text{mSv/h}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the cumulative dose over 6 hours.**

$$
E_{cum} = 1.2 \times 6 = 7.2 \, \text{mSv}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with ICRP dose constraint (5 mSv/episode).**

$$
7.2 \, \text{mSv} > 5 \, \text{mSv} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{EXCEEDED}
$$

**Step 5: Practical recommendation.**

Either reduce the contact time, increase the distance (sit ≥ 0.7 m away), or assign a different comforter. If the comforter must sit at 0.5 m for 6 hours, the exposure violates the **5 mSv dose constraint for comforters** set by ICRP 105.

**Required safe distance for 5 mSv in 6 hours:**

$$
\dot{E}_{max} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833 \, \text{mSv/h}
$$

$$
0.833 = 0.3 \times \left(\frac{1}{d}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
d^{2} = \frac{0.3}{0.833} = 0.36
$$

$$
\boxed{d \geq 0.6 \, \text{m}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Effective Half-Life & Patient Discharge Calculation

### Problem Setup

A patient administered **200 mCi (7.4 GBq) of I-131** is to be discharged from hospital. Regulatory guidance (US NRC, AERB, IAEA) typically permits discharge when the **retained activity falls below 1.2 GBq (33 mCi)** OR the **dose rate at 1 m falls below 0.05 mSv/h**. The physical half-life of I-131 is **8.02 days**, and the biological half-life (thyroid uptake followed by clearance) is approximately **7.6 days** (for a euthyroid adult; simplified). Calculate the **effective half-life** and the **earliest discharge time** assuming only physical decay (worst case for shielding design).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the effective half-life formula.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{T_{phys}} + \frac{1}{T_{biol}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$
\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{8.02} + \frac{1}{7.6}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = 0.1247 + 0.1316 = 0.2563 \, \text{day}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for T_eff.**

$$
T_{eff} = \frac{1}{0.2563} \approx 3.9 \, \text{days}
$$

**Step 4 (Worst case for discharge):** Use only physical half-life (T_eff = T_phys = 8.02 days) for conservative estimation.

The activity at time t:

$$
A(t) = A_0 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t / T_{eff}}
$$

We want $A(t) = 1.2 \, \text{GBq}$ from $A_0 = 7.4 \, \text{GBq}$:

$$
1.2 = 7.4 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t/8.02}
$$

$$
\frac{1.2}{7.4} = 0.5^{t/8.02}
$$

$$
0.1622 = 0.5^{t/8.02}
$$

**Step 5: Take the natural logarithm.**

$$
\ln(0.1622) = \frac{t}{8.02} \times \ln(0.5)
$$

$$
-1.8192 = \frac{t}{8.02} \times (-0.6931)
$$

**Step 6: Solve for t.**

$$
t = \frac{1.8192 \times 8.02}{0.6931}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 21.05 \, \text{days}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** For 7.4 GBq administered, the patient would need ~21 days of inpatient isolation (worst case) before discharge under the 1.2 GBq criterion. *In practice*, with biological clearance, this reduces to ~3–4 days, and the dose-rate criterion typically governs discharge.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Diagnostic vs Therapeutic Medical Exposure

| Parameter | Diagnostic (X-ray, CT, NM) | Therapeutic (RT, Brachy, ¹³¹I) |
|---|---|---|
| Dose range | mGy to tens of mGy | Gy levels (fractions of 1.8–20 Gy) |
| Risk paradigm | Stochastic (cancer induction) | Deterministic (cell kill) — but also secondary stochastic risk |
| Dose limit concept | **No** upper limit; use **DRLs** | **No** upper limit; use *prescription* dose |
| Justification | Highest scrutiny (every exam justified) | Clinical benefit presumed for life-threatening disease |
| Optimization | Image quality vs dose trade-off | Tumour control vs normal-tissue complication trade-off |
| Worker safety | Lower secondary hazard | Higher (HDR sources, neutron production in LINAC) |
| Public safety | Very low (after procedure) | High (patient is a "walking source" post-¹³¹I) |
